Address: 6 Sandringham Court 11 Dorset Road Belmont SM2 6NG
Name: Mrs Sheryl Moon
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to the proposed change of use from an ambulance station to a
place of worship on the grounds of traffic impact and public safety. This area already experiences
extremely high levels of congestion throughout the day, particularly at peak commuting times, and
the introduction of a use that is likely to generate significant additional vehicle movements at set
times, including evenings and weekends, would exacerbate these issues considerably. Of
particular concern is the close proximity of a nearby school, where increased traffic, on-street
parking, and pedestrian activity would heighten risks to children and families during drop-off and
pick-up times. The existing road infrastructure is already under strain, and the proposal does not
appear to adequately address the cumulative impact on highway safety, access, and parking
capacity. For these reasons, I believe the development would be detrimental to the safety and
wellbeing of the local community.

Name: Mr Kamran Ellahi
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a resident of Holland Avenue to express my full support for planning
application DM2025/01591.
As a neighbour, I believe the proposed community centre will provide significant benefits to local
residents. The centre will offer a welcoming space for community engagement, educational
activities, and social initiatives that will positively contribute to the wellbeing of families and
individuals in the area.
I am particularly encouraged by the parking management approach outlined in the application. The
proposed measures appear sensible and pragmatic, especially in seeking to avoid timing clashes
with drop-off and pick-up periods at Avenue Primary Academy. By managing event scheduling and
usage hours responsibly, the proposal demonstrates clear consideration for existing traffic
patterns and local residents. This proactive planning should help minimise any additional pressure
on parking within Holland Avenue and the surrounding roads.
Overall, I believe the development has been thoughtfully considered and will enhance community
cohesion while respecting the character and functioning of the neighbourhood.
I respectfully ask the Council to approve this application.

Name: Miss Rebecca Greig
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The site is located in an entirely residential area, as confirmed by the Council's own
report, which states the surroundings are "predominantly residential". A large, highcapacity
community hub and place of worship is fundamentally incompatible with the character, scale and
quiet nature of this neighbourhood, contrary to Sutton Local Plan Policies 28 (Character and
Design) and 29 (Protecting Amenity).
The Council has already identified serious concerns about noise and disturbance, especially from
up to 250 people leaving at 11pm and increased vehicle movements directly beside residents'
gardens. This would significantly harm residential amenity.
Transport accessibility is extremely poor, with a PTAL rating of 1a, meaning the location is not
suitable for a hightraffic use. The Council acknowledges this and has highlighted the likely onstreet
parking pressures and highway safety impacts, especially during Friday peak events, even after all
onsite spaces are used. This directly conflicts with Policies 36 and 37 of the Sutton Local Plan.
The proposed building would also appear visually out of place compared with the surrounding
domestic properties, failing to integrate with the established character of the area, contrary to
Policy 28.
Finally, there is no demonstrated local need for an additional facility of this nature. The applicant
already has access to a large venue with extensive parking less than one mile away, and there are
multiple mosques and community facilities within approximately 1-1.5 miles of Belmont. As such,
placing another intensive use in a quiet residential neighbourhood is unnecessary and unjustified.
For these reasons, I strongly object to the proposal and urge the Council to refuse any future
application.

Name: Ms Linda Smith
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to the application. The proposed site sits within a quiet residential area. This
area and surrounding streets already experience significant parking pressure due to the close
proximity to the Avenue primary school. We as residents already see unsafe parking especially at
school pick up and drop off times. In addition staff from the school park in the roads. The proposed
development does not appear to provide sufficient off street parking to accommodate the potential
numbers expected to be attending the centre. This is likely to lead to overspill parking in nearby
residential streets, leading to obstruction of driveways, reduced road safety, and increased
congestion.
In addition the change of use will generate an increase in noise from increased vehicles and large
numbers of people leaving the facility up to 11 pm at night.

Name: Mr Donald Cameron
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to this Planning Application as a mosque in this location will result in more traffic
on the local roads which means additional safety risks to pedestrians, a reduction in air quality,
more noise, and parking problems.
Currently, the traffic caused by parents of children at the Avenue Primary Academy presents
significant problems to pedestrians and local residents. Before and after school drop-off and pick-
up times, the local roads are packed with cars, with dozens of children trying to cross the road and
buses being blocked. The fumes from the cars, and the noise of engines, doors slamming, and
horns sounding are a constant nuisance. The parking around this time, due to the number of cars,
is appalling, with parents parking on single, double, and zig-zag yellow lines, grass verges, and
over residents' driveways.
It must be accepted that an increase in traffic of any magnitude would be completely
unacceptable. However, the applicant freely admits that 45% of their users will travel by car and
there will be an additional 69 cars in the area at peak times. There appears to be no attempt to
mitigate the presence of the additional traffic on the road, although the applicant does try to
demonstrate that there are sufficient parking spaces on local roads at all times, but these
calculations are flawed. As I have lived within the 400m boundary used in the survey for more than
30 years, I can confirm, by observation, that the parking utilisation in Avenue Road is greater than
100% (due to the amount of illegal parking) rather than the 49% that the applicant claims. It may
be 49% over the whole area of the survey, but there are obviously local hot-spots that invalidate
the figures used.
This means that the applicant has not met the Council's recommendations from the pre-application
submission regarding their concerns about the noise and disturbance that could be caused to the
surrounding residents and the impact that the proposal could have on the parking situation within
the surrounding streets.
As this is a residential area which has already seen an increase in housing development and the
size of the primary school, and is also increasingly used as a rat run to avoid the by-pass, the area
has already reached full capacity. This planning application should be refused.

Name: Mr Adnan Arab
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am a resident of Chelmsford Close and write to express my strong support for this
proposal. As a resident who lives close by I would walk to this venue as many other people in the
surrounding area would.
I welcome the re-use of the existing site and buildings for positive community purposes.
Community space in our area is increasingly limited, and accessible, flexible venues for local
groups are in short supply. This proposal would deliver a significant benefit to local residents.
As the organiser of a local cycling club, I know how challenging it is to secure suitable meeting and
activity space. A well-managed community centre would provide a valuable base for local groups,
promoting healthy lifestyles, social interaction, and stronger neighbourhood connections.
The proposed design is attractive and in keeping with the surrounding character. The plans appear
carefully considered, and the additional parking demonstrates practical regard for visitors and
neighbours.
The application aligns with the London Borough of Sutton Local Plan and the London Plan by
strengthening social infrastructure and supporting community cohesion. It provides much-needed
space in a sustainable location while enhancing the site's appearance.
Overall, I believe this proposal will deliver lasting benefits to the area and I respectfully request the
Council to approve it.

Name: Mr Mohammad Iftekhar
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a resident of York Road to express my strong support for planning
application DM2025/01591.
One of the aspects I value most about this proposal is that it will provide a community centre within
easy walking distance of my home. Having a local facility that residents can safely and
conveniently access on foot is a significant benefit, particularly for families, older residents, and
those who may not drive. It encourages local participation and reduces the need for unnecessary
travel.
I also greatly appreciate the wellbeing focus that the centre intends to bring to the area.
Community spaces that offer educational sessions, social activities, and wellbeing initiatives can
have a very positive impact on mental health, social connection, and overall quality of life. In
today's environment, accessible and inclusive community facilities are more important than ever.
In my view, this proposal represents a positive investment in our neighbourhood and will
strengthen community ties while providing practical local benefits.
I am pleased to support this application and respectfully ask the Council to approve planning
application DM2025/01591.

Name: Dr Aamir Iqbal
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a resident of Holland Avenue and as a medical doctor to express my
strong support for planning application DM2025/01591.
From a healthcare perspective, I particularly value the proposed medical and wellbeing initiatives
that the community centre intends to offer. Accessible health education sessions, preventative
health workshops, mental health awareness programmes, and community wellbeing activities can
play an important role in improving public health outcomes. Community-based initiatives often help
reach individuals who may not otherwise engage with traditional healthcare services, supporting
early intervention and reducing longer-term pressures on the health system.
In addition to the health benefits, I have reviewed the proposed plans and find the design to be
thoughtful and proportionate. The scheme appears well considered, with no excessive increase in
scale or height, and it respects the character of the surrounding residential area.
I am also reassured by the proposed parking management measures and the creation of
additional parking spaces. The approach outlined in the application demonstrates a responsible
and pragmatic effort to manage vehicle movements and minimise impact on neighbouring roads.
This shows clear consideration for existing residents and local traffic patterns.
Overall, I believe this proposal represents a positive and carefully planned development that will
provide tangible health and community benefits while remaining sensitive to its surroundings.
I respectfully ask the Council to approve planning application DM2025/01591.

Name: Mr D Idris
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly support this application.
My home is only a short walk from the site - just a few houses away - so I will not need to drive. As
a parent, this proposal is extremely important to my family. Having a safe, well-managed
community center nearby means my children can take part in positive activities without long
journeys, late-night travel, or additional traffic.
This development is also a clear improvement on the site's former 24-hour ambulance station use,
which generated noise and vehicle movements at all hours. A controlled, community-focused
facility is a far more appropriate and compatible use in a residential area.
I understand local concerns about parking and traffic, particularly around school hours. I am
reassured that the application directly addresses this through managed scheduling, parking
controls, and event planning designed to avoid peak times and minimize disruption.
The proposed building is modest, well designed, and in keeping with the surrounding homes. It will
enhance community life while respecting the character of the area.
Overall, I believe this is a positive and balanced proposal that will benefit local families while
protecting residential amenity.
I respectfully ask the Council to approve this application.

Name: Mr Ganganna Rayapati
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Objection to Planning Application DM2025/01591
Dear Planning Officer,
I wish to formally object to planning application DM2025/01591 on material planning grounds. My
concerns relate to highway safety, parking stress, residential amenity, and public safety.
1) School-Run Pedestrian Safety and Existing Peak Parking Stress
Avenue Primary Academy has a reported pupil roll of approximately 1,034. This generates very
high levels of pedestrian activity at drop-off and pick-up times, including young children, parents,
prams and buggies. There is also additional activity from after-school and weekend events.
The introduction of further peak-time arrivals and drop-offs, alongside vehicles circulating in
search of parking, would reduce visibility at crossings and junctions and materially increase risk to
children and other vulnerable pedestrians. The cumulative impact on highway safety is significant
and unacceptable.
2) Inadequate On-Site Parking and Predictable Overspill
The applicant's Transport Statement identifies the Friday prayer "changeover" period as the
busiest time and models a worst-case overlap scenario of:
Up to 80 vehicles associated with the overlap period
Only 14 on-site parking spaces
An assumption that 3 spaces may be used by staff, leaving only 11 spaces for attendees
Therefore, up to 69 vehicles potentially parking on surrounding residential streets
This level of predictable overspill would lead to obstruction, unsafe manoeuvring, blocked
driveways, pavement parking, reduced visibility at junctions and general harm to residential
amenity. The scale of overspill is not marginal - it is substantial and foreseeable.
3) Impact on Elderly and Mobility-Impaired Residents
Overspill parking and informal drop-off behaviour often result in blocked pavements and
pedestrians being forced into the carriageway. This disproportionately affects:
Elderly residents
Wheelchair users
Parents with pushchairs
Residents with mobility impairments
In a school-run environment with constrained streets, these impacts are particularly serious and
raise clear highway and public safety concerns.
4) Emergency Access and Response Resilience
The surrounding streets are constrained and functionally reliant on single routes of access.
Additional on-street parking, double parking, or queuing traffic would materially increase the risk of
delaying:
Emergency service vehicles
Refuse collection
Essential service access
This is not merely an inconvenience but a public safety concern.
5) Late Operating Hours and Residential Amenity
The Planning Statement indicates extended operating hours, including:
Community use between 07:00-22:00
Prayer use varying and potentially running until 11:00pm
Late evening dispersal - including raised voices, car doors closing, engines idling, and vehicles
circulating - is a well-recognised source of residential disturbance. This would result in sleep
disruption and loss of peaceful enjoyment of homes, particularly affecting elderly and vulnerable
residents.
6) Constrained Local Road Network and Cul-de-Sac Impacts
Dorset Road effectively acts as the primary vehicular access route to the site. Nearby residential
streets, including Balmoral Way, are cul-de-sacs with limited turning space.
In such layouts:
Drivers circulate when parking is unavailable
Three-point turns and reversing manoeuvres increase
Any obstruction affects the entire area due to lack of alternative routes
Even short peak surges can therefore have disproportionate impacts.

Name: Mr Rizwan Bashir
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
As a resident of Dorset Road, I have reviewed the BASMA Community Hub proposal
and wish to formally express my support for planning application DM2025/01591.
The project makes very good use of the former ambulance station site - replacing a building that
previously operated on a 24-hour basis, with associated vehicle movements and activity, with what
will now be a calm, community-based facility. I particularly appreciate that the site will no longer
function as a 24-hour operational premises, which in itself represents a positive change for
surrounding residents.
The proposed design is modest and carefully considered. It does not introduce a new upper floor,
but only a small roof adjustment to improve internal height. In my view, the plans demonstrate
sensitivity to neighbouring properties and will not result in overlooking or overshadowing of nearby
homes.
From what I understand, the daily activities will be small-scale - such as classes, wellbeing
sessions, and community drop-ins - rather than large events. Friday prayers are short in duration
and are being managed thoughtfully to avoid clashes with school-run times, which shows a
responsible approach to traffic and neighbourhood considerations.
Overall, I believe the new community hub will be a quiet, well-managed and responsible
neighbour, providing genuine benefit to local residents while improving the character and function
of the site.
I respectfully ask the Council to approve this application.

Name: Mr Hari Nepal
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Area is already busy due to school, this development will create further vehicle parking
chaos which will lead to road safety as well as noise issues to the nearby residents.

Address: Flat 8 Kiln House 7 Northdown Road Sutton SM2 6DY
Name: Mr Adnan Ahmed
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment: Support

Name: Ms Tazkera Sadeq
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Comment - Planning Application DM2025/01591
I am a local resident and a parent of multiple children at Avenue Primary School. I support this
application for the following reasons:
1. Since 2021, Friday congregational prayers organised by BASMA have taken place at a nearby
church hall without noticeable problems with noise, traffic, parking or safety. That location is closer
to Avenue Primary School's main entrance, the busy Belmont High Street and a more congested
traffic area, with less parking available. The proposed site is further away from these constraints
and includes its own on-site parking provision which is an improvement.
2. There is no congregational prayer during the school morning peak around 8:30am, presenting
no overlap at that busy morning time (including school and commuter traffic). Accordingly, no
material change to traffic conditions would arise at that time. The existing Friday prayer timing
already coincides with school hours at the current location and has not created issues, and bus
services continue to operate normally.
3. As a parent using the school regularly, I have been able to find parking when needed, and I
frequently observe parking wardens managing the area. Many pupils also travel by bus, and
services run reliably, indicating that traffic conditions remain manageable in practice.
4. The proposed place of worship and community facility is unlikely to generate concentrated or
constant daily activity, as most people are at work during weekdays. The main weekly gathering is
Friday congregational prayer, which has been established locally and operated in an orderly and
well-managed manner since 2021.
5. Friday prayers have been held at the local church since 2021 and managed successfully by
BASMA over several years without disturbance. Attendance is typically staggered across multiple
sittings and more than one congregation, naturally spreading arrival and departure times and
avoiding concentrated peaks.
6. BASMA has been part of the local community for several years and has worked positively with
local residents, the church and with the knowledge of the Council. As an established local
organisation experienced in managing activities responsibly, it is well placed to operate and
manage its own, much-needed, premises to serve the growing needs of local residents. BASMA
maintains clear, organised communication with its users and activities are conducted in a
structured, well-managed and respectful manner.
7. There is currently no nearby dedicated facility of this kind, and a once-weekly temporary
arrangement does not meet the needs of the growing community. A permanent, fit-for-purpose
space would better support women, families and older residents, particularly women who benefit
from accessible and appropriate community provision.
8. Community and social infrastructure that supports inclusion, wellbeing and local cohesion is
recognised within local and London planning policy, and this proposal contributes positively in that
context.
9. The facility will primarily serve the same local community already using BASMA's services,
many of whom attend on foot, including myself and my family. This represents continuity rather
than a new influx of visitors. The proposed site, with more appropriate space and on-site parking,
is likely to improve existing arrangements rather than create additional noise or traffic pressure.
10. The proposal also makes constructive use of a disused site that formerly operated as a 24-
hour ambulance station, a use which historically generated traffic and activity at all hours.
11. Based on what has already been operating locally, for local residents, for several years without
issue, I support the provision of this much-needed community facility in this location. It will provide
improved and more appropriate provision to meet the needs of the local community.
Taking all of the above into account, the proposal builds on a well-established and responsibly
managed local community presence and represents a proportionate and positive addition to local
community infrastructure. I therefore support this application.

Name: Mr Muhammad Siddiqui
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application

We are writing as residents of Dorset Road to express our support for planning application

Yours faithfully,

Name: Mrs Monika Chaubey
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
We, the residents of Sutton Council, call on London Borough of Sutton and Belmont ward
councillors to refuse Planning Application
DM2025/01591
for the former Ambulance Station on Dorset Road (SM2 6HX), Belmont, which proposes a change
of use from Sui Generis (Ambulance Station) to mixed Class F1 (Place of Worship) and Class F2
(Community Hub), with associated building works.Please submit an individual planning objection
to Sutton Council for
DM2025/01591
- it carries more weight than signatures alone. Focus on school safety, parking overspill,
traffic/drop-offs, noise/late hours, and emergency access.
We support appropriate community facilities in principle. However, we believe this proposal, in this
specific residential location with constrained access, will cause unacceptable harm to local
highway safety, parking, residential amenity, and emergency access unless refused.
Key reasons for objection (material planning grounds)1) School-run pedestrian safety and existing
peak parking stressAvenue Primary Academy has a reported pupil roll of 1,034, meaning the area
experiences very high pedestrian activity (children, parents, buggies) at drop-off/pick-up times,
with additional activity from optional after-school and weekend events. We are concerned that
extra peak-time arrivals, drop-offs and vehicles circulating for parking will reduce visibility at
crossings/junctions and increase risk to children and other pedestrians.
2) Inadequate on-site parking and predictable overspill into residential streetsThe applicant's own
Transport Statement identifies the Friday prayer "changeover" as the busiest period and models a
worst-case overlap scenario of:
* up to 80 vehicles associated with the overlap period;
* only 14 on-site spaces, with an assumption 3 may be used by staff (leaving 11 for attendees);
* therefore 69 vehicles potentially parking on surrounding residential streets in the worst case.We
believe this overspill will lead to obstruction, unsafe manoeuvres, blocked driveways, pavement
parking, reduced visibility and harm to residents' quality of life.
3) Impact on elderly and mobility-impaired residentsOverspill parking and drop-off behaviour can
force pedestrians into the carriageway, block pavements and make it harder for elderly residents,
wheelchair users and those with mobility issues to move safely. These impacts are particularly
serious in a school-run environment and on constrained streets.4) Emergency access and
response resilienceWith constrained streets and single-route reliance, additional on-street
parking/double-parking or queuing traffic increases the risk of delaying emergency vehicles and
refuse collection. This is a highway safety and public safety concern.5) Late operating hours and
residential amenity (noise and disturbance)The applicant's Planning Statement indicates long
operating hours, including community use 07:00-22:00 and prayer use varying and running up to
11pm. Late-evening dispersal (voices outside, car doors, engines idling, vehicles circulating) is a
common source of residential nuisance. We are concerned about sleep disturbance and loss of
peaceful enjoyment of homes, particularly affecting vulnerable and elderly residents.
6) Constrained local road network - single access and cul-de-sac impactsDorset Road effectively
acts as the only vehicular entry/exit to the site and nearby residential streets (including Balmoral
Way) are cul-de-sacs with limited turning space. In such layouts, even short peak surges have
disproportionate impacts: drivers circulate, undertake three-point turns/reversing, and any
obstruction affects the whole area because there is no alternative route.
7) Overreliance on management measures that may not be enforceable long-termThe proposed
mitigation relies heavily on behaviour management (e.g., marshals/management plans) rather
than physical capacity (parking/road space). We are concerned such measures are difficult to
enforce consistently over time, especially as attendance patterns and events change in future
years.
What we ask Sutton Council to doWe ask the Council to refuse DM2025/01591 due to
unacceptable impacts on highway safety, parking, residential amenity and emergency access.

up to 80 vehicles associated with the overlap period;
only 14 on-site spaces, with an assumption 3 may be used by staff (leaving 11 for attendees);
therefore 69 vehicles potentially parking on surrounding residential streets in the worst case.We
believe this overspill will lead to obstruction, unsafe manoeuvres, blocked driveways, pavement
parking, reduced visibility and harm to residents' quality of life.
3) Impact on elderly and mobility-impaired residents
Overspill parking and drop-off behaviour can force pedestrians into the carriageway, block
pavements and make it harder for elderly residents, wheelchair users and those with mobility
issues to move safely. These impacts are particularly serious in a school-run environment and on
constrained streets.
4) Emergency access and response resilience
With constrained streets and single-route reliance, additional on-street parking/double-parking or
queuing traffic increases the risk of delaying emergency vehicles and refuse collection. This is a
highway safety and public safety concern.
5) Late operating hours and residential amenity (noise and disturbance)
The applicant's Planning Statement indicates long operating hours, including community use
07:00-22:00 and prayer use varying and running up to 11pm. Late-evening dispersal (voices
outside, car doors, engines idling, vehicles circulating) is a common source of residential
nuisance. We are concerned about sleep disturbance and loss of peaceful enjoyment of homes,
particularly affecting vulnerable and elderly residents.
6) Constrained local road network - single access and cul-de-sac impacts
Dorset Road effectively acts as the only vehicular entry/exit to the site and nearby residential
streets (including Balmoral Way) are cul-de-sacs with limited turning space. In such layouts, even
short peak surges have disproportionate impacts: drivers circulate, undertake three-point
turns/reversing, and any obstruction affects the whole area because there is no alternative route.
7) Overreliance on management measures that may not be enforceable long-term
The proposed mitigation relies heavily on behaviour management (e.g., marshals/management
plans) rather than physical capacity (parking/road space). We are concerned such measures are
difficult to enforce consistently over time, especially as attendance patterns and events change in
future years.

Comment:
Dear Sutton Council Planning Team
I am writing in support of the planning application for the change of use of the former ambulance
center in our local area, which the local Muslim community has purchased with the intention of
converting it into a mosque and community facility.
I live locally and I strongly support this proposal. One of the main reasons is that this site is within
walking and cycling distance for many residents. A local mosque in an accessible location would
reduce the need for people to drive to places of worship further afield, which is what many families
currently have to do. In practical terms, this would help to reduce unnecessary car journeys, ease
pressure on surrounding roads, and support a more environmentally responsible way for people to
meet their faith and community needs.
For many Muslims, prayer in congregation is a core part of religious life. It is not only about
worship, but also about building a sense of connection, structure, and shared responsibility. A
local mosque provides a safe and welcoming place where people see one another regularly, look
out for one another, and develop stronger relationships across different ages and backgrounds.
This strengthens community cohesion and helps to reduce isolation, particularly for older people
and those who may otherwise feel disconnected.
It is also important to highlight that a well-run mosque is not just a place for prayer. It often
becomes a center for community support and local charity work. In recent years, I have seen
Muslim communities organise food drives, fundraising for local and international emergencies,
practical support for vulnerable families and projects that encourage volunteering and positive
involvement among young people. This kind of community spirit benefits the wider borough, not
only those who attend the mosque.
The former ambulance center is a sensible and appropriate building for this purpose. It is already
an established community site, and bringing it back into active use in this way is far more practical
than creating a new development. It also allows a previously underused building to become a
positive asset for the area.
I understand the Council will need to consider issues such as traffic, parking, and local impact.
However, I believe this proposal is reasonable and constructive, and I trust that the mosque
committee will continue to work responsibly with local residents and the Council to ensure the site
is managed in a considerate way.
For these reasons, I strongly support this planning application and I would be grateful if Sutton
Council would look upon it favorably.
Yours faithfully
Dr Fouzia Moolla
Cheam, Sutton

Address: 1 Stanstead Manor 59 Cheam Road Sutton SM1 2AY
Name: Mr Wasim Riyas
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
We acknowledge the concerns raised by local residents and recognise the importance
of safeguarding highway safety, residential amenity and emergency access. These are legitimate
material planning considerations and deserve careful assessment.
It is important to clarify at the outset that the former ambulance station has not been operational
for some time and no longer functions as an emergency facility. Any representations that assume
the continued operation of an ambulance centre should therefore be considered in light of the
site's current inactive status. When objectively assessed against national and local planning
policy, the submitted Transport Statement, the site's lawful fallback use, and the proposed
mitigation measures, the concerns raised do not demonstrate a level of planning harm that would
justify refusal. The evidence presented so far does not indicate severe residual cumulative impacts
on highway safety, parking, or residential amenity.
Common Objection: Increased arrivals during school drop-off/pick-up will endanger children.
Response:
The congregation has been operating since 2021 from a church site located closer to Avenue
Primary Academy than the proposed site. During this period, there have been no recorded safety
incidents, enforcement actions, or substantiated complaints relating to highway danger associated
with Friday prayer attendance. The proposed site on Dorset Road is situated further from the
school than the existing venue. As such, the relocation would not intensify school gate pressures
and may reasonably be expected to reduce any perceived interaction with school-run pedestrian
flows.
Importantly, no technical highway evidence has been submitted demonstrating that the proposal
would create an unacceptable risk to pedestrian safety. There is no Road Safety Audit identifying
a hazard, nor any objection from the Highway Authority indicating that the residual cumulative
impacts would be severe.
In planning terms, the former ambulance station benefits from a lawful Sui Generis use.
Historically, it generated 24-hour operational activity, emergency vehicle dispatches, staff shift-
change movements, and unpredictable high-speed departures. That lawful fallback position could
resume without requiring planning permission and would result in more frequent and less
predictable vehicle movements than the proposed managed and time-limited prayer use.
Under the National Planning Policy Framework, development should only be refused on highway
grounds where there would be an unacceptable impact on highway safety or where the residual
cumulative impacts would be severe. No such evidence has been demonstrated in this case.
Accordingly, the objection is speculative rather than evidence-based and does not meet the policy
threshold for refusal.
Common Objection: Inadequate On-Site Parking & Overspill
Refutation: The cited figure of 69 vehicles originates from our own Transport Statement and
represents a deliberately robust, worst-case overlap scenario during the busiest Friday prayer
period. This modelling assumes maximum theoretical attendance, minimal car-sharing, and a
temporary overlap between departing and arriving attendees within a short transition window. It is
important to emphasise that this represents a stress-tested assessment scenario rather than a
prediction of typical operational conditions. Such precautionary modelling is standard transport
practice and reflects transparency rather than an expectation of routine impact.
In practice, congregation patterns observed since 2021 at the existing venue demonstrate
materially different behaviour. Attendance is characterised by significant walking from nearby
residential areas, and use of public transport and when needed, significant car sharing. Arrival and
dispersal are naturally staggered rather than occurring as a single concentrated surge. During this
established period of operation closer to the school and within the same local highway network,
there have been no recorded enforcement actions or substantiated complaints evidencing
systemic parking harm. Real-world operational evidence therefore provides a more reliable
indicator of likely impact than a theoretical maximum scenario.
It is also relevant that Avenue Road and it's surrounding network currently accommodate regular
bus movements, including the S4 and S2 routes, without recorded obstruction or operational
difficulty. The continued functioning of scheduled bus services through the road network
demonstrates that the Road width and general layout are capable of accommodating routine
vehicular activity. There is no technical evidence before the Council indicating that the proposal
would compromise the safe passage of buses or emergency vehicles, nor that the residual
cumulative impact would reach the "severe" threshold set out in national planning policy.
Furthermore, on-street parking in residential areas is a normal and lawful component of suburban
street function. Planning policy does not require all parking demand to be contained within a site
boundary, particularly for community uses within established neighbourhoods. The relevant test is
not whether any overspill may occur, but whether the residual cumulative impacts would be
unacceptable or severe. No highway authority objection or technical safety audit has identified
such harm.
The applicant has also proposed, and is willing to accept by condition, a comprehensive Travel
Plan and Parking Management Strategy, including stewarding during peak periods, promotion of
sustainable travel modes, and monitoring mechanisms. Such measures are standard and
enforceable planning tools commonly applied to places of worship and community facilities across
the borough. Where impacts can be appropriately managed through condition, refusal would not
be proportionate.
When considered in the round - including the site's lawful fallback position as a 24-hour
operational ambulance facility generating unpredictable vehicle movements - the proposed
managed and time-limited use does not demonstrate severe residual cumulative impacts on
parking or highway safety. The evidence therefore does not substantiate refusal on parking
grounds.
Emergency Access & Response
Refutation: We recognise that concerns have been raised regarding the potential impact of on-
street parking on emergency access and response times. The safe and unobstructed passage of
emergency vehicles is a fundamental public safety consideration and is rightly treated as a
material planning matter.
However, it is important to consider the historic and lawful use of the site. The premises operated
as an ambulance station - an emergency response facility - generating 24-hour operational
activity, including staff shift changes and the dispatch of emergency vehicles at speed. The local
highway network was therefore previously deemed suitable for regular emergency vehicle
movements associated directly with life-saving operations. In comparison, the proposed use would
generate time-limited and predictable peak movements of significantly lower intensity and urgency
than those historically associated with an operational ambulance base.
There is no evidence before the Council that the highway layout has materially changed in a way
that would now render emergency access unsuitable. Nor has any technical objection been raised
by the Highway Authority or emergency services indicating that the proposal would compromise
response resilience. In planning terms, concerns regarding emergency access must be supported
by professional highway assessment or demonstrable evidence of likely obstruction. Speculative
assertions, in the absence of technical analysis, do not meet the policy threshold required to justify
refusal.
Furthermore, the surrounding road network currently accommodates routine vehicular activity,
including scheduled bus services, without recorded operational difficulty. There is no objective
evidence to suggest that the managed and time-limited nature of the proposed use would result in
sustained obstruction or materially increase the risk of delayed emergency response.
When assessed objectively against its lawful fallback position and in the absence of technical
evidence demonstrating unacceptable impact, the proposal does not give rise to a substantiated
emergency access concern sufficient to warrant refusal.
Operating 07:00-22:00(and occasionally 23:00) and Residential Amenity
Refutation: We acknowledge the concerns raised regarding operating hours and the potential for
late-evening disturbance to neighbouring residents. The protection of residential amenity, including
safeguarding against unacceptable noise and disturbance, is a core planning consideration and
warrants careful assessment.
The proposed hours of community use between 07:00 and 22:00 are consistent with the operation
of many community facilities across London boroughs. Places of worship, community halls, and
similar Class F1 and F2 uses commonly operate until 22:00, which is widely regarded within
planning practice as a standard and reasonable closing time for community activities in residential
areas. Such hours do not, in themselves, constitute evidence of unacceptable impact.
Concerns have also been expressed regarding dispersal noise during evening periods. It is
important to distinguish between transient and routine activity - such as people conversing, car
doors closing, or vehicles departing - and forms of noise typically associated with commercial
nightlife uses. The proposal does not involve amplified music and licensed late-night
entertainment. Planning policy and environmental health practice differentiate between significant
noise nuisance and ordinary residential comings and goings. Brief dispersal activity, particularly
when managed, does not automatically equate to material harm.
It is also relevant that the congregation has been operating from St John's Church for
approximately three years, including occasional use extending to 22:00-23:00, without recorded
noise complaints, enforcement action, or substantiated evidence of residential disturbance. This
established operational history provides tangible real-world evidence that the nature of the use
does not inherently give rise to unacceptable amenity impacts.
Should the Council consider it necessary, operating hours and a Noise Management Plan can be
secured by condition. Such conditions are standard, enforceable, and proportionate planning tools
used to ensure ongoing protection of residential amenity. Where impacts can be appropriately
controlled through condition, refusal would not be justified.
Finally, it is material to consider the site's lawful fallback position. As an operational ambulance
station, the premises historically functioned on a 24-hour basis, including emergency vehicle
dispatches involving sirens and rapid response movements. Such activity would have been
materially louder and more intrusive than the managed, time-limited community use now
proposed. In comparison, the proposed use represents a significantly reduced intensity of
disturbance.
When assessed objectively, and in light of established operational evidence, enforceable
mitigation measures, and the site's historic lawful use, the proposal does not demonstrate a level
of noise or residential impact that would warrant refusal on amenity grounds.
Common Objection: Constrained Road Network and Cul-de-Sac Character
Refutation: Concerns have been raised that Dorset Road and surrounding streets, including
Balmoral Way, function as a constrained residential network with limited turning space and
effectively single-route access, such that even short peak surges may have disproportionate
impacts. While the cul-de-sac character of parts of the network is acknowledged, this
characteristic alone does not render a site unsuitable for community use.
Many suburban community facilities, including places of worship, nurseries and community halls,
are located within cul-de-sacs or semi-enclosed residential streets across London boroughs.
Planning policy does not prohibit such uses in these settings; rather, the assessment turns on
whether the highway network can safely and functionally accommodate anticipated movements
without severe residual impact.
In this case, there is no technical evidence demonstrating that the local network lacks sufficient
capacity to absorb short-duration peak movements. The busiest period identified in the Transport
Statement relates to a temporary overlap window during Friday prayer, lasting approximately 60-
90 minutes. This does not represent sustained all-day congestion, but a predictable and time-
limited peak. Short-lived increases in vehicular activity do not equate to systemic highway failure.
Furthermore, the lawful fallback position must be considered. As an operational ambulance
station, the site historically generated 24-hour vehicle movements, including emergency
dispatches requiring rapid departure and priority access through the same road network. Those
movements were inherently less predictable and more intensive than the managed, time-limited
peaks now proposed. If the network was suitable for emergency fleet operations, it cannot
reasonably be characterised as fundamentally incapable of accommodating a controlled
community use.
The suggestion that drivers will circulate excessively, undertake unsafe manoeuvres, or create
persistent obstruction remains speculative in the absence of modelling or recorded evidence from
existing operations. The congregation has been operating nearby since 2021 without
substantiated reports of systemic highway harm, despite being located closer to sensitive
receptors. Real-world experience is, therefore, a material indicator of likely impact.
There is no capacity assessment, traffic count data, or modelling before the Council demonstrating
that Dorset Road or adjoining streets operate at or near capacity during existing peak periods. In
the absence of such evidence, it cannot reasonably be concluded that short-duration additional
movements would result in network failure or unsafe conditions.
Importantly, the policy test is not whether additional vehicles may enter the network at peak times,
but whether the residual cumulative impacts would be severe. No technical objection from the
Highway Authority has been identified concluding that the cul-de-sac nature of the road results in
an unacceptable safety risk or functional failure.
Conclusion:
When the proposal is assessed in the round, the key question for the Council is whether the
development would give rise to unacceptable impacts, having regard to national and local planning
policy.
First, in relation to highway matters, the National Planning Policy Framework is clear that
development should only be refused on transport grounds where there would be an unacceptable
impact on highway safety, or where the residual cumulative impacts would be severe. No technical
evidence has been presented demonstrating that the proposal would meet this threshold. There is
no highway authority objection identifying severe impact, no Road Safety Audit evidencing danger,
and no professional assessment concluding that emergency access would be compromised. The
concerns raised, while understandable, remain speculative rather than evidence-based.
Secondly, it is necessary in planning law to consider the lawful fallback position. The site
previously operated as a 24-hour ambulance station, generating emergency dispatches, staff shift
changes, and rapid-response vehicle movements, including sirens at unsocial hours. That use
could lawfully resume without further planning permission. By comparison, the proposed use
involves predictable, time-limited peak periods, significantly reduced intensity of vehicle
movements, and no night-time emergency operations. It is objectively quieter, more controlled,
and more limited in scope than the historic lawful use. The assessment of impact must be
undertaken against that realistic fallback baseline.
Thirdly, there is an established and ongoing local need. The congregation is not a new or
speculative use; it already exists and has been operating from premises closer to the school since
2021 without substantiated enforcement action or recorded safety incidents. The proposal does
not introduce a new congregation into the area, but instead formalises and relocates an existing
use to a site further removed from sensitive receptors. The real-world operational history is
therefore an important material consideration.
Finally, the proposed use falls within Class F1 and F2, which are intended to support community,
cultural and social infrastructure. Community facilities promote social cohesion, provide structured
youth engagement, reduce isolation, and support integration within established neighbourhoods.
Planning policy at both national and local levels supports the retention and provision of community
facilities unless demonstrable harm clearly outweighs the public benefit. In this case, the asserted
harms have not been substantiated to the policy threshold required for refusal.
For these reasons, when objectively assessed against planning policy, transport evidence, the
lawful fallback position, and the established operational history, the proposal does not give rise to
severe residual cumulative impacts on highway safety. The Local Highway Authority has not
objected on grounds of highway safety, parking, emergency access, or residential amenity. The
material considerations support approval.

Name: Mr Ramesh Mareguddi
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Objection to DM2025/01591 - material planning reasons
1) Highway safety risk in a highly sensitive area (school-run + pedestrians)
- The proposal sits within an area of very high pedestrian activity, including children and parents
with buggies, because Avenue Primary Academy has 1,034 pupils.
- School activity extends beyond normal pick-up: the school's published wraparound offer provides
childcare until 5:45pm (term time), extending parent pick-ups and child crossings into the early
evening.
- Any increase in peak traffic, drop-offs, turning, and on-street parking will materially increase
pedestrian safety risk at already busy times.
2) Constrained road network and limited resilience (single access / cul-de-sacs)
- Dorset Road functions as a constrained access route to the site and nearby roads; Balmoral Way
is a cul-de-sac with limited turning space. Increased overspill parking and "circling" traffic leads to
more reversing/3-point turns and reduced visibility-this is a classic highway safety concern in cul-
de-sac layouts.
- Any obstruction (double-parking, drop-offs, deliveries) can disproportionately affect flow and
emergency/refuse access because there are limited alternative routes.
3) Peak intensity is high for a residential street location (capacity + late dispersal)
- The applicant's Fire Strategy states: "Total maximum capacity for the building = 297 persons."
- The Council's pre-application feedback (repeated in applicant documents) explicitly notes "up to
250 attendees might depart around 11 pm" for Friday prayers / large community events.
- These are significant peaks for a residential street with limited capacity and sensitive receptors
(families, elderly residents).
4) Parking provision is demonstrably insufficient and applicant's own "worst case" relies on heavy
on-street parking
- The Transport Statement models a worst-case Friday changeover and says there could be "up to
80 vehicles" parked within the site car park and on-street locally.
- It confirms 14 on-site spaces, assumes 3 used by staff, leaving 11 for attendee remaining 69
vehicles"** would park on surrounding streets in the worst case.
- This is not a minor overspill - it is a material increase in on-street parking demand which risks:
- blocked driveways and pavements,
- reduced junction visibility,
- conflict with school-run movement,
- obstruction risk for emergency/refuse vehicles.
5) The "overlap" assumptions indicate frequent peak pressure, not occasional blips
- The Transport Statement assumes overlap of: "all 160 visitors from Session 1 and 90 visitors
from Session 2... at the same time."
- It also references "240-250 attendees... across the Friday Prayer sessions per Friday."
- These figures demonstrate the proposal is capable of generating regular, repeatable peak that all
streets are not designed to absorb.
6) Residential amenity harm from late-evening dispersal and associated noise
- The pre-app concern referenced by the applicant is specifically about late dispersal around
11pm.
- Even if internal noise is managed, late dispersal typically creates external disturbance (voices,
car doors, engines idling, vehicles searching for spaces). In a quiet residential area this is a
material amenity issue.
7) Cumulative impacts are not properly addressed (other venues already create peaks on/near
Dorset Road)
This proposal is being assessed as if the background network is "neutral", but nearby venues
already generate arrivals/departures and event peaks.
- Sutton Bowling Club states it has over 500 members and is open "8.00am until late seven days
per week."
- Downs Lawn Tennis Club advertises hall hire that can cater for up to 80 people.
- Downs Lawn Tennis Club also advertises Friday evening jazz events (e.g., 06 Mar 2026, 19:00-
23:00 at 50 Holland Ave, SM2 6HU).
These are foreseeable overlapping peaks (evenings/weekends and Friday evenings in particular).
The highways evidence should test cumulative event overlap, not just this proposal in isolation.
8) Risk of net increase in trips (not a true "relocation")
- The application does not demonstrate that activity at the current venue will permanently cease or
that similar use will not continue there under another group. Without any binding "offset", there is a
credible risk the area ends up with two active venues, producing a net increase in trips and
parking pressure rather than displacement.
9) Equality / vulnerable residents - access and safety
- Overspill parking and pavement obstruction disproportionately affects elderly residents, those
with mobility impairments, and families with pushchairs by forcing people into the carriageway and
reducing safe crossing visibility. This is relevant to safe access and residential amenity.
What I'm asking the Council to do
I respectfully request council to Refuse the application due to unacceptable highway safety,
parking stress, and residential amenity impacts, especially given the applicant's own worst-case
on-street parking reliance and late-evening dispersal concerns.

Name: Mrs Shilpa Prasad
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
We, the residents of Sutton Council, call on London Borough of Sutton and Belmont
ward councillors to refuse Planning Application
DM2025/01591
for the former Ambulance Station on Dorset Road (SM2 6HX), Belmont, which proposes a change
of use from Sui Generis (Ambulance Station) to mixed Class F1 (Place of Worship) and Class F2
(Community Hub), with associated building works.
We support appropriate community facilities in principle. However, we believe this proposal, in this
specific residential location with constrained access, will cause unacceptable harm to local
highway safety, parking, residential amenity, and emergency access unless refused.
Key reasons for objection (material planning grounds)1) School-run pedestrian safety and existing
peak parking stressAvenue Primary Academy has a reported pupil roll of 1,034, meaning the area
experiences very high pedestrian activity (children, parents, buggies) at drop-off/pick-up times,
with additional activity from optional after-school and weekend events. We are concerned that
extra peak-time arrivals, drop-offs and vehicles circulating for parking will reduce visibility at
crossings/junctions and increase risk to children and other pedestrians.
2) Inadequate on-site parking and predictable overspill into residential streetsThe applicant's own
Transport Statement identifies the Friday prayer "changeover" as the busiest period and models a
worst-case overlap scenario of:
* up to 80 vehicles associated with the overlap period;
* only 14 on-site spaces, with an assumption 3 may be used by staff (leaving 11 for attendees);
* therefore 69 vehicles potentially parking on surrounding residential streets in the worst case.We
believe this overspill will lead to obstruction, unsafe manoeuvres, blocked driveways, pavement
parking, reduced visibility and harm to residents' quality of life.
3) Impact on elderly and mobility-impaired residentsOverspill parking and drop-off behaviour can
force pedestrians into the carriageway, block pavements and make it harder for elderly residents,
wheelchair users and those with mobility issues to move safely. These impacts are particularly
serious in a school-run environment and on constrained streets.4) Emergency access and
response resilienceWith constrained streets and single-route reliance, additional on-street
parking/double-parking or queuing traffic increases the risk of delaying emergency vehicles and
refuse collection. This is a highway safety and public safety concern.5) Late operating hours and
residential amenity (noise and disturbance)The applicant's Planning Statement indicates long
operating hours, including community use 07:00-22:00 and prayer use varying and running up to
11pm. Late-evening dispersal (voices outside, car doors, engines idling, vehicles circulating) is a
common source of residential nuisance. We are concerned about sleep disturbance and loss of
peaceful enjoyment of homes, particularly affecting vulnerable and elderly residents.
6) Constrained local road network - single access and cul-de-sac impactsDorset Road effectively
acts as the only vehicular entry/exit to the site and nearby residential streets (including Balmoral
Way) are cul-de-sacs with limited turning space. In such layouts, even short peak surges have
disproportionate impacts: drivers circulate, undertake three-point turns/reversing, and any
obstruction affects the whole area because there is no alternative route.
7) Overreliance on management measures that may not be enforceable long-termThe proposed
mitigation relies heavily on behaviour management (e.g., marshals/management plans) rather
than physical capacity (parking/road space). We are concerned such measures are difficult to
enforce consistently over time, especially as attendance patterns and events change in future
years.
What we ask Sutton Council to doWe ask the Council to refuse DM2025/01591 due to
unacceptable impacts on highway safety, parking, residential amenity and emergency access.

Name: Nimit Shishodia
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
FORMAL FORENSIC OBJECTION
Application: DM2025/01591
Site: Former Ambulance Station, Dorset Road, Belmont SM2 6JH
This objection is submitted on clear material planning grounds. It directly addresses the applicant's
Transport Statement, Fire Strategy, and supporting representations, and demonstrates that the
proposal fails to satisfy the statutory and policy tests required for approval.
1. Legal Framework and Policy Threshold
Under paragraph 115 of the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF):
Development should only be refused on highways grounds if there would be an unacceptable
impact on highway safety, or the residual cumulative impacts on the road network would be
severe.
The applicant repeatedly asserts that objections are "speculative". However:
- The applicant's own modelling identifies substantial peak parking demand beyond on-site
capacity.
- The site is located within a constrained residential network with known sensitive receptors.
- Weekly peak congregation of up to 240-297 persons is acknowledged in the submitted
documents.
The planning question is not whether absolute gridlock occurs, but whether predictable, repeatable
peak impacts produce severe cumulative stress in context.
Severity must be assessed relative to:
- Road typology (residential cul-de-sac environment),
- Sensitive land uses (primary school with 1,034 pupils),
- Existing background event venues,
- Available on-site capacity.
When assessed proportionately in this localised context, the threshold is engaged.
2. The "Lawful Fallback" Argument is Legally Misapplied
The applicant relies heavily on historic use as a 24-hour ambulance station.
This reliance is materially flawed for four reasons:
(a) Realistic Prospect Test
Case law establishes that fallback must represent a realistic and genuine prospect of
implementation - not a theoretical abstraction.
No evidence has been submitted that:
- The NHS or emergency authority intends to recommence use.
- The building remains configured for operational emergency dispatch.
- Operational contracts remain in place.
Absent such evidence, the fallback argument carries limited weight.
(b) Operational Characteristics Are Fundamentally Different
Emergency fleet dispatch:
- Trained drivers.
- Controlled exit protocols.
- Short dwell time.
- Dispersed trip patterns.
- Non-congregational use.
Congregational use:
- Simultaneous arrival/departure.
- High dwell-time concentration.
- Private vehicle dominance.
- Circulation searching behaviour.
- Pedestrian clustering.
These are not comparable transport typologies.
Using emergency dispatch as a baseline materially understates the behavioural and parking
implications of a congregation-based land use.
(c) Intensification vs Historical Use
Even if fallback were realistic, the assessment must consider intensity and pattern, not abstract
vehicle numbers.
Weekly peak congregation of 200-250 attendees in a 60-90 minute overlap window represents
concentrated loading not analogous to historic dispatch patterns.
3. The Applicant's Own Transport Evidence Demonstrates Structural Overspill
The Transport Statement identifies:
- 14 on-site spaces.
- 3 staff spaces.
- 11 available attendee spaces.
- Up to 80 vehicles during overlap.
- 69 vehicles reliant on surrounding streets.
This is not marginal overflow. It represents approximately 86% of peak demand displaced to
residential streets.
The applicant describes this as "stress testing". However:
- Friday prayer is weekly.
- The overlap scenario is foreseeable and repeatable.
- The Fire Strategy confirms maximum building capacity of 297 persons.
Planning assessment must consider credible maximum utilisation - particularly where the building
is being physically extended to facilitate increased occupancy.
4. Failure to Properly Assess Cumulative Impact
The assessment fails to model cumulative peaks with:
- Sutton Bowling Club (500+ members),
- Downs Lawn Tennis Club (event hire up to 80 attendees),
- Scheduled Friday evening events.
Cumulative impact assessment must evaluate overlapping peak windows.
The Transport Statement isolates this proposal from the surrounding event ecosystem. That is a
methodological weakness.
Where predictable overlap exists, cumulative modelling is required.
5. Cul-de-Sac Constraints and Network Resilience
Dorset Road and Balmoral Way exhibit:
- Limited turning geometry,
- On-street parking reliance,
- Absence of distributed egress routes,
- Residential frontage with direct driveway access.
In cul-de-sac typology, congestion risk does not require high traffic volume - it requires constrained
manoeuvring space.
Foreseeable behavioural consequences include:
- Multi-point turns,
- Reversing into pedestrian space,
- Circulatory "parking search" loops,
- Pavement overhang parking,
- Junction visibility impairment.
In proximity to a primary school with extended wraparound operations, these effects are amplified.
The absence of a Road Safety Audit specifically modelling congregation dispersal in this street
typology is notable.
6. Residential Amenity - Late Evening Dispersal
The applicant acknowledges potential operation until 23:00.
Planning law recognises that residential amenity harm is not confined to amplified music.
Dispersal-related impacts include:
- Engine noise,
- Door closures,
- Conversational clustering,
- Vehicle searching,
- Idling emissions.
Weekly late-evening dispersal of 200+ attendees is materially different from incidental residential
comings and goings.
The ambulance fallback comparison is inapposite; emergency dispatches are sporadic, not
scheduled congregation events.
7. Vulnerable User Impact
Overspill parking and circulation behaviour create disproportionate risk to:
- Elderly residents,
- Mobility-impaired individuals,
- Children walking from Avenue Primary Academy,
- Families with pushchairs.
This engages both highway safety and residential amenity considerations.
8. Absence of Legally Binding Relocation Offset
The application assumes displacement from an existing venue.
No planning condition or legal agreement secures:
- Permanent cessation of the existing use,
- Prevention of dual operation,
- Restriction on maximum attendance below fire capacity.
Without such safeguards, net trip intensification remains a credible risk.
9. Conditions Cannot Cure Structural Capacity Deficit
While Travel Plans and stewarding are proposed, these measures:
- Cannot create physical parking capacity,
- Cannot prevent lawful on-street parking,
- Cannot eliminate circulation behaviour,
- Cannot eliminate cumulative event overlap.
Where structural capacity deficit exists (69 vehicles overspill at peak), conditions may mitigate but
cannot neutralise impact.
OVERALL CONCLUSION
When objectively assessed:
- The proposal relies heavily on surrounding residential streets to absorb event-scale
congregation.
- Peak congregation is weekly, predictable, and repeatable.
- The site sits within a constrained cul-de-sac typology adjacent to a large primary school.
- The fallback comparison is legally weak and operationally incomparable.
- Cumulative modelling is incomplete.
- Structural overspill parking is acknowledged by the applicant's own evidence.
Taken together, these factors demonstrate a credible risk of unacceptable highway impact and
severe cumulative stress within the meaning of the NPPF.
The Council is therefore respectfully requested to:
REFUSE planning permission on the grounds of unacceptable highway safety risk, severe
cumulative parking stress, and material harm to residential amenity.

Name: Mrs Atya Sherwani
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a local resident of more than twenty years to express my enthusiastic
support for the proposed redevelopment of the former ambulance station on Dorset Road. I live at
York Road, SM2 6HN, and having walked past this site for years , I firmly believe that the current
plans represent a thoughtful and high-quality use for a piece of land that has sat vacant for far too
long. From the perspective of an immediate neighbour, the design appears remarkably sensitive to
the established character of Belmont. The architectural approach does not just fill a gap; it
enhances the street scene by replacing a redundant, utilitarian structure with a building that feels
proportionate in scale and sympathetic to the surrounding residential aesthetic.
The environmental considerations within the application are particularly impressive and form a
major part of my support. The integration of enhanced greenery and the protection of ecological
value on-site will provide a much-needed visual lift to this corner of the neighbourhood. Rather
than a harsh, paved-over footprint, the proposal introduces soft landscaping that will help the site
blend naturally with the verdant quality of our local roads. This focus on biodiversity and high-
quality outdoor space shows a commitment to creating a sustainable environment that benefits the
wider community, not just the eventual users of the building.
The social infrastructure and community need addressed by this application cannot be overstated.
There is a clear and pressing requirement for modern, accessible facilities in Sutton that cater to
inclusive local provision, and this site is perfectly situated to meet those needs. The accessibility
features built into the design ensure that the facility will be welcoming and usable for all members
of the public, which is a significant material planning benefit. It is heartening to see a proposal that
prioritises social value while remaining entirely compatible with the residential context of the area,
working with the grain of the neighbourhood rather than against it.
When looking at the overall planning balance, the benefits of this scheme far outweigh any minor
changes to the local setting. The anticipated activity levels appear realistic for a site of this nature
and compatible with our quiet residential life. By bringing a derelict site back into a meaningful,
community-focused use, the borough is securing a facility that will serve Belmont for generations.
The project is a well-rounded and sustainable piece of urban planning that respects the privacy
and amenity of neighbours while delivering a vital public asset. I fully support this application and
hope to see it approved.

Name: Mr Safeer Butt
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Re: Planning Application DM2025/01591
Address: Ambulance Station, Dorset Road, Belmont, SM2 6JH
Proposal: Change of use from Sui Generis (Ambulance Station) to mixed use Classes F1 (Place of
Worship) and F2 (Community Hub)
I write in strong support of this application.
This proposal represents a positive and inclusive development that would provide significant social
and community benefits to the local area.
1. Community Integration & Demographics
The proposed use as a Place of Worship and Community Hub aligns well with the local
demographic profile and the evolving needs of the area. Belmont and the surrounding
neighbourhoods are home to a diverse and growing community. A facility such as this promotes:
Social cohesion
Inter-faith and intercultural dialogue
Community engagement across generations
A shared community space strengthens integration rather than division and provides a welcoming
focal point for residents of all backgrounds.
2. Walking Distance & Sustainable Location
The site is located within walking distance of residential areas, reducing reliance on vehicles.
Many attendees are likely to walk, particularly:
Elderly residents
Families with children
Local residents living nearby
This supports sustainable transport objectives and minimises traffic impact.
3. Benefits for Elderly Residents
For older members of the community, especially those who may be socially isolated, having a
local focal point is extremely valuable. The proposal offers:
A safe and accessible meeting space
Daytime activities and community gatherings
Opportunities for volunteering and social participation
Such facilities are proven to improve wellbeing and reduce isolation.
4. Youth Programs & Educational Classes
The inclusion of F2 Community Hub use will enable:
Youth engagement programmes
Children's educational classes
After-school and weekend activities
Cultural and language education
Community workshops and support sessions
These services benefit the whole community, not only worshippers.
5. Proven Local Precedent - No Parking or Traffic Issues
Nearby church facilities have operated for many years without material parking or traffic problems
affecting neighbouring schools or residents. This demonstrates that:

A valuable asset for community integration
Support for elderly residents and young people
A sustainable, walkable local facility
