Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Sir/Madam,
I am writing to express my full support for Planning Application DM2025/01591. I am a local
resident and have been part of the community for several years.
I believe this proposal will provide significant benefits to the local area. The development will offer
valuable community space, including health and wellbeing programmes, inclusive facilities, and
activities designed to bring people together. In particular, the dedicated space and activities for
women will provide an important and positive contribution to inclusivity within our community.
The proposal also includes educational programmes and activities for children, which will support
young people's development in a safe and structured environment. These types of facilities are
greatly needed locally and will serve families in a constructive and meaningful way.
From a planning perspective, the building is modest in scale and in keeping with the character of
the surrounding area. It represents a low-intensity use compared to many alternative forms of
development that could be permitted on the site. I do not believe it will cause significant traffic,
light, or noise disturbance.
The scheme has carefully considered transport and access. The inclusion of 12 on-site parking
spaces plus 2 disabled bays will help manage vehicle demand responsibly. Cycle racks and
encouragement of sustainable travel are also positive elements of the proposal. It is reassuring
that Friday prayers are being staggered to avoid conflict with local school pick-up times,
demonstrating thoughtful planning and consideration for neighbours.
I also welcome that greenery is being maintained as part of the development, helping preserve the
visual quality of the area.
Overall, I believe this application represents a well-considered, community-focused proposal that
will benefit local residents while respecting the character of the neighbourhood.
I respectfully ask the Council to approve Planning Application DM2025/01591.

Address: Flat 17 Ashwood Park 69 Grange Road Sutton SM2 6SP
Name: Mr Zain Tahir
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly support this planning application for the change of use from an Ambulance
Station to a mixed-use Place of Worship (Class F1) and Community Hub (Class F2), including the
proposed extensions and facilities.
I moved to this area two years ago, and during that time I've felt that the community could really
benefit from a dedicated space like this. There are many families and individuals here, but limited

charitable activities.
The trustees have been very welcoming, responsible, and thoughtful in their approach. From my
interactions with them, it's clear they care deeply about being good neighbours and contributing

The proposed improvements, including cycle storage and refuse facilities, show good planning

valuable asset to the neighbourhood and should be approved.
For these reasons, I strongly support granting planning permission.

Name: Mr Faisal Khan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a resident of Bawtree Close to express my support for this planning
application. It is introducing a community-focused facility that will greatly improve the physical and
mental well-being of a large number of local residents.
I welcome the fact that the previous busy operational facility on the site is now being replaced by a
modest, community-focused development. The proposed centre will be significantly quieter and
much more in keeping with the surrounding residential area.
The proposal also includes additional parking provision and sustainable travel methods e.g. the
cycle store. This demonstrates consideration for local residents and will help minimise any impact
on surrounding roads. The majority of people attending to such community-focused buildings tend
to walk, which is another positive impact to the local area.
In terms of design, I find the development to be in line with the aesthetics of the area. The scale
and layout appear proportionate and well thought-out, ensuring that the building integrates
comfortably within the existing streetscape.
Overall, I believe this proposal represents a positive change for the neighbourhood, providing a
well-managed and community-focused facility that is sympathetic to its surroundings. I respectfully
ask the Council to approve planning application.

Name: Mr Shakil M

Comment:
I am writing to offer my support for the Planning Application ref:DM2025/01591
As a resident of Burdon Lane, living less than a mile from the proposed site, I consider this an
ideal and accessible location for a community facility. Its proximity means it can be easily reached
on foot by myself and many others in the surrounding neighbourhood.
I am pleased to see a proposal that combines a place of worship with space for community events
and activities. Centres like this contribute significantly to social cohesion, providing a respectful
and organised setting for learning, gatherings, and positive community engagement.
The development also brings potential economic and social benefits. Construction will create
shortterm employment opportunities, and once the centre is operational, roles such as security
staff and traffic marshals during prayer times could offer ongoing local jobs. Additionally, the
centre is likely to encourage volunteering among residents and young people, helping to build civic
pride and community participation.
Regarding traffic, attendance will mainly coincide with prayer times, which generally fall outside
the busiest commuting periods. This naturally limits any overlap with peak traffic. Moreover, most
worshippers will be local to the borough and already familiar with the area's parking rules and
traffic restrictions.
In summary, I believe this proposal represents a thoughtful and beneficial addition to the
community, offering social, cultural, and economic value. I respectfully urge the Council to approve
the Planning Application.

Name: Mr Surajit Deb Roy
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to DM2025/01591 because the proposal would cause unacceptable harm to
highway safety, parking stress, and residential amenity in a constrained residential area,
particularly during school-run and evening peak periods.
This is a school-run environment with high pedestrian movement. Any increase in drop-offs,
circulating traffic, and on-street parking will reduce visibility and materially increase risk to children
and pedestrians.
The applicant's Transport Statement identifies significant overspill, with a worst-case scenario
referring to up to 80 vehicles locally. Only 14 on-site spaces are proposed (with an assumption
that 3 may be staff spaces), leaving up to 69 vehicles to park on surrounding residential streets.
This level of overspill would lead to blocked driveways, pavement obstruction, unsafe
manoeuvres, and obstruction risks.
We regularly experience:
- Heavy parking during school drop-off and pick-up
- Poor visibility at junctions due to parked cars
- Vehicles undertaking three-point turns and reversing in narrow or cul-de-sac roads
- Pavements obstructed by vehicles
- Difficulty for buggies, elderly residents, and wheelchair users
For the reasons above, I respectfully request that Sutton Council refuse application
DM2025/01591 due to unacceptable highway safety, parking, and residential amenity impacts.

Name: Mrs Uzma Mobin
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
This place will benefit the local community as well as the wider community. It will be a
great place for education, cohesion and acceptance of people from all classes, colours and
creeds.

Name: Mr Arijit GhoshDastidar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am a resident and a school-run parent residing at UK.
I object to DM2025/01591 because the proposal would cause unacceptable harm to highway
safety, parking stress, and residential amenity in a constrained residential area, particularly during
school-run and evening peaks.
I live at 25 Holland Avenue, SM2 6HW which is very near to the site. I regularly use the roads
adjacent to the site in dispute in Belmont. I regularly experience heavy parking during school drop-
off/pick-up. The pavements remain blocked by vehicles. There is always very poor visibility at the
junctions near to the site due to parked cars. On several instances, drivers doing three-point
turns/reversing in the adjacent cul-de-sac roads cause extremely unsafe and vulnerable situation
for kids and family travelling through the area.
Last week I saw an elderly woman was experiencing difficulty in travelling along the pavement with
her own wheel chair due to increased vehicular traffic on the road adjacent to the site. On several
instances, I have seen young families face extreme difficulty while travelling with kids and buggies
along the road and pavements adjacent to the site due to increased traffic and vehicular
movements.
The area is a school-run environment and already has high pedestrian movement. Any increase in
drop-offs, circulating traffic, and on-street parking will reduce visibility and increase risk to children
and pedestrians.
At school run time, near Dorset road and Balmoral way junction and in areas adjacent to the site,
children frequently cross and cars already struggle and visibility is blocked. Usually busy cars
mount the kerb.
The applicant's own Transport Statement indicates significant overspill:
Worst case refers to up to 80 vehicles locally.
TRANSPORT_STATEMENT-6060376
Only 14 on-site spaces (with an assumption 3 may be staff), leaving 69 vehicles on-street in the
worst case.
This level of overspill would lead to blocked driveways/pavements, unsafe manoeuvres and
obstruction risks.
My driveway / my neighbour's driveway is often blocked already on week days. Cars already park
close to corners, making it hard to see pedestrians.
The applicant's Fire Strategy states a maximum building capacity of 297 persons.
FIRE_STATEMENT_FIRE_STRATEGY_DO...
The Design & Access / Planning Statement references that up to 250 attendees might depart
around 11pm for Friday prayers / large community events.
Late dispersal in a residential area can cause disturbance (voices, car doors, engines idling,
vehicles searching for parking).
Noise travels in the evening and residents are disturbed by existing noise source already.
Local streets include cul-de-sacs and limited turning space, so extra parking/circulation leads to
reversing, three-point turns, and obstruction. Any double-parking or drop-off stopping can cause
disproportionate congestion.
Vehicles often have to reverse or do three-point turns near the junction of Doreset Road and
Balmoral Way.
Nearby venues already create evening/weekend traffic and parking demand. The proposal should
be assessed against cumulative event overlap, not in isolation.
I request Sutton Council to refuse DM2025/01591 outright due to unacceptable highway safety,
parking and amenity impacts.

Name: Dr Sahar Khan

Comment:
I fully support the planning application DM2025/01591
As a local resident of over 10 years I feel that there is a strong need for a centre which will benefit

As a woman, the provision of a space walking distance from my home will encourage myself as
well as many women in the neighbourhood to be able to attend women friendly programs related

or public transport.
With regards to traffic issues I personally do not believe this is a factor as most people in the area

Also, the timings of prayers have been specifically designed to avoid congestion/traffic especially

I would ask the council to approve the application.

Name: Mrs Monika Jha
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly object to this proposal due to its location within a quiet residential area.
The proposed site is in very close proximity to Avenue Primary School, which experiences
significant traffic and pedestrian activity throughout the day, particularly during drop-off and pick-
up times. Introducing additional vehicles and on-street parking in this already congested area
would create serious safety concerns, especially for young children who walk to and from the
school.
As residents living very close to the proposed site, with children who attend Avenue Primary
School, we would be directly and adversely affected by the increased traffic, parking pressures,
and potential security risks. The presence of young children and elderly residents in the immediate
vicinity further heightens these concerns. The safety and wellbeing of the local community must be
prioritised.
I would also like to highlight that many of the residents expressing support for this proposal do not
live in close proximity to the site and would not experience its day-to-day impact. In contrast, those
of us who live nearby would be directly exposed to the increased traffic, congestion, and
associated risks.
For these reasons, I respectfully urge the Council to reject this proposal in order to protect the
safety, character, and quality of life of the immediate residential community.

Name: Mr Mohammad Usman Sadjid
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Case Officer,
My name is Mohammad Usman Sadjid, and I have been a resident of the local area since 2019. I
am writing to express my full support for Planning Application DM2025/01591 relating to the
proposed conversion of the former Sutton Ambulance Station on Dorset Road into a community
and worship facility.
1. Understanding of the Site and Its Previous Use
The former Sutton Ambulance Station operated as a 24-hour emergency service facility, with
vehicle movements and operational activity occurring at all times of day and night. This level of
activity was part of the established character of the site for many years.
In comparison, the proposed community and worship use represents a significantly lower-intensity
and more predictable use of the building. Activity will be concentrated at specific times and
managed in an organised manner, rather than the unpredictable nature of emergency response
operations historically associated with the site.
2. First-Hand Experience of Responsible Operation
I regularly attend Friday prayers (Jumaa) and other activities organised by BASMA, and I have
seen first-hand how responsibly and respectfully the congregation operates.
Friday prayers have been effectively staggered to avoid conflict with school pick-up times and
peak local traffic periods. This demonstrates a proactive and cooperative approach to managing
impact on the surrounding area.
Over the years I have attended, I have observed orderly arrival and departure patterns, respectful
behaviour towards neighbours, and a strong emphasis on maintaining good relations within the
wider community.
3. Sustainable Travel and Local Accessibility
The proposal includes 12 on-site parking spaces, including 2 disabled bays, alongside cycle racks
that encourage sustainable travel.
From my own experience, many attendees walk, car-share, or use public transport. Arrival and
departure times are naturally staggered, reducing pressure on surrounding streets.
My family and I have previously discussed the benefit of having a permanent community and
worship facility within reasonable walking distance of our home. A location such as this supports
sustainable travel patterns and reduces reliance on vehicles, which is positive for both residents
and the wider environment.
I do not believe the development will result in significant traffic, light, or noise concerns.
4. Community Benefit and Social Cohesion
There is a clear and genuine need for this facility.
Through attending BASMA, I have personally experienced the sense of community, belonging,
and positive engagement it fosters. I have seen people from diverse cultural and ethnic
backgrounds come together in a respectful and unified environment. It strengthens social cohesion
and promotes understanding across communities.
The proposed centre will provide:
Dedicated space and programmes for women,
Educational activities and structured learning for children,
Community wellbeing initiatives,
A safe and inclusive environment for families.
As a parent, I value the positive environment and sense of community that BASMA provides,
something I had not previously experienced before becoming involved. This facility would allow
those benefits to continue and grow in a stable, permanent location.
5. Appropriate and Modest Use of the Site
The building is modest in scale and appropriate for the surrounding area. The proposal maintains
greenery and respects the character of the site while bringing a currently unused building back into
productive community use.
It represents a low-intensity and community-focused development, fully aligned with the principles
of sustainable and socially beneficial planning.
Conclusion
As a local resident since 2019 and a regular attendee who has witnessed the operation and its
positive impact first-hand, I strongly believe this proposal is:
Appropriate for the site,
Responsibly managed,
Community-serving in nature, and
Unlikely to generate significant adverse impact.
For these reasons, I respectfully urge the Council to approve Planning Application DM2025/01591.
Yours faithfully,
Mr Mohammad Usman Sadjid
23 Gomshall Road, Cheam, Sutton, Surrey, SM2 7JZ

Comment:

This centre would give young people a chance to take part in group activities like football,
badminton, table tennis, and hikes while fostering a sense of community open to everyone.
I believe the local area would greatly benefit from these opportunities. Providing spaces for young
people to socialise and form healthy relationships can help reduce the risk of anti social behaviour,
support mental health, and combat loneliness, all which are increasingly prevalent in young people
today.

Name: Mr Khurshed Alam
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am a local resident and I would like to register my support for planning application
DM2025/01591.
In my view, this is a sensible re-use of an existing site that previously served a public function, and
it seems reasonable for it to continue in a community-serving role rather than remaining potentially
unused or vacant/abandoned. The proposal is for a mixed-use community hub and place of
worship, and it appears intended to operate as a community venue with regular activities rather
than being limited to one purpose.
What I see as positive is the wider community use described in the submitted information. It
includes provision aimed at different groups, such as activities for children and young people,
mothers and toddlers support, and programmes for older residents, alongside broader community
sessions. Having this type of facility locally can be beneficial in practical terms because it gives
people access to organised activities and support within the borough, rather than needing to travel
further away for similar services.
From a day-to-day perspective, the documents also suggest that access and on-site arrangements
have been considered, including parking and cycle storage, and an approach to managing how
the site is used at busier times. Based on my experience of the immediate area, the main pressure
points on the roads are typically the school-run periods in the early morning and mid-afternoon.
Outside of those times the area is generally quieter, which reduces the likelihood of routine
community use causing noticeable traffic issues.
Overall, I consider the proposal to be a reasonable use of an existing site that would bring clear
community benefit, and I support Sutton Council granting approval.

The proposed services, including children's education support, youth engagement, family

Another important benefit is that the centre will be within walking distance of my home. This

Overall, I strongly believe this project will have a positive social, educational, and cultural impact
on our community. I fully support this application and hope it will be approved.
Thank you for considering my views.

The application proposes only 10 car parking spaces and for a facility who are anticipating a

The insufficiency has already been acknowledged by the applicants themselves, as evidenced by

For these reasons, I believe the application will have a significant negative impact on local
residents and existing transport and access arrangements, and I therefore object to it in its current
form.

represents a balanced and positive outcome for the neighbourhood.
The inclusion of additional parking spaces is another sensible feature of the plans, helping to
manage demand and minimise impact on surrounding roads.
Furthermore, I appreciate that the greenery and landscaping are being maintained as part of the
proposal, which contributes positively to the appearance of the site and the wider streetscape.
Overall, I believe this application represents a well-considered and beneficial development for the
area. I respectfully ask the Council to approve planning application.

From a planning perspective, the building is modest in scale and appears in keeping with the
surrounding area. The proposed use is low-intensity compared to many alternative forms of

It is also positive that existing greenery is being maintained, helping the development integrate
sensitively into its surroundings. The measures outlined, including staggered Friday prayer times
to avoid conflict with local school pick-up periods, demonstrate thoughtful planning and
consideration for neighbours.
Overall, I do not believe the proposal will cause significant traffic, light or noise issues. Instead, it

Yours faithfully,

provide a welcoming environment for people of all ages and backgrounds, encouraging social
interaction, support networks and community cohesion. Facilities like this are greatly needed in our

The proposed extensions and adjustments to the building appear proportionate and thoughtfully

Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
In my view, this represents a thoughtful and pragmatic re-use of a site that has long
fulfilled a public function. It seems both logical and desirable for the premises to continue serving
the community, rather than risk falling into vacancy or neglect. The proposal outlines a mixed-use
community hub and place of worship, conceived not as a single-purpose venue but as a vibrant,
multi-faceted space hosting regular activities for a broad cross-section of local residents.
Of particular merit is the emphasis on inclusive community provision set out in the submitted
materials. The scheme proposes activities and support tailored to a range of groups, including
children and young people, mothers and toddlers, and older residents, alongside wider community
sessions open to all. Access to facilities of this nature within the borough offers tangible benefits: it
enables residents to participate in organised activities and access support services locally,
reducing the need to travel elsewhere for comparable opportunities.
On a personal level, my husband and I do not have relatives in the UK, and facilities of this kind
are especially important to us. A centre such as this would provide a vital space in which to build
strong social connections, feel supported, and develop meaningful relationships within the local
community. Beyond individual benefit, it would also foster understanding, harmony, and positive
interaction among people from diverse backgrounds, strengthening the social fabric of the area as
a whole.
From a practical standpoint, the documentation indicates that due consideration has been given to
site access and operational management, including parking arrangements, cycle storage, and
strategies for managing busier periods. In my experience of the immediate area, traffic pressures
are largely concentrated around school drop-off and pick-up times in the early morning and mid-
afternoon. Outside these peak windows, the surrounding roads are generally calmer, which
suggests that routine community use is unlikely to generate significant traffic disruption.
Overall, I regard the proposal as a sensible and constructive use of an existing site, one that
promises clear and meaningful community benefit. I therefore support Sutton Council granting
approval.

Cycle racks are a great addition supporting sustainable travel.
I am also pleased to see that existing greenery is being maintained as part of the scheme, which

On balance, this development will not cause significant issues in terms of traffic, noise or light. The

I respectfully urge the planning committee to approve this application.
Yours faithfully
Mona Ahmed-Surty

Comment:

I am a local resident and a father living in the area, and I am actively involved in community life

The change of use from a former ambulance station to a mixed-use Place of Worship (Class F1)

Overall, I consider this to be a well-balanced and community-focused proposal that makes good

Council to approve this application.

Name: Mr Rashed Karim
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a local resident of Belmont to express my strong support for planning
application DM2025/01591, submitted by the local community for the conversion of the former
ambulance garage into a community centre and place of worship.
This application matters deeply to me, and I would ask the Council to consider not just the
planning merits but the genuine community need that underpins it.
Belmont and the wider Sutton borough is home to a significant and long-established community,
yet our residents, including many families with young children, currently have no dedicated local
facility for islmaic education, faith-based wellbeing activities, or community gathering. While other
faith communities in the area are well served by churches and other places of worship, Muslim
residents have had to travel considerable distances or go without. This application addresses that
gap in a measured and responsible way.
On the planning merits themselves, I am satisfied that the proposal is sound. The design is
sympathetic to the character of the surrounding area, modest in scale, well-considered in
appearance, and unlikely to detract from the neighbourhood's aesthetic. The conversion of an
existing building, rather than new construction, is itself a sensitive and proportionate approach.
The proposed programmes, Islamic education for children and young people, wellbeing initiatives,
and broader community activities, will provide structured, positive opportunities for residents of all
ages and backgrounds. A well-run community centre of this kind does not serve only one group; it
strengthens the social fabric of the whole neighbourhood by giving young people in particular a
safe, purposeful environment.
I respectfully urge the Council to approve this application. It represents exactly the kind of
thoughtful, community-led development that Sutton should be proud to support, filling a real and
long-standing need, in a building that fits its surroundings, run by a community that has
demonstrated care and responsibility in how it has brought this proposal forward.

Name: Mr F Burney
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a local resident and a parent of a young family to express my strong
support for planning application DM2025/01591.
Having reviewed the proposals, I believe this development represents a thoughtful and much-
needed investment in our neighborhood. My support is based on the following key points:
1. Essential Provisions for Families and Youth
As a parent, I am particularly impressed by the focus on education and childcare. The inclusion of
a crèche and a dedicated kids' program addresses a significant gap in local services. Furthermore,
Sutton's youth facilities have long lacked adequate investment; the proposed youth-focused
spaces provide a safe, constructive environment for the next generation.
2. Health, Sports, and Well-being
The emphasis on physical and mental health is a standout feature of this application. I am very
supportive of the:
- Sports and well-being facilities: Providing local options for physical activity.
- Health and well-being sessions: Offering structured support for the community's overall health.
- Publicly available defibrillator: This is a vital, life-saving addition to our street that provides peace
of mind for all residents.
3. Inclusive and Sensitive Design
The layout of the center-thoughtfully divided into three distinct halls (a dedicated women's space,
a multi-use community space, and a prayer hall)-demonstrates a commitment to inclusivity. I
specifically value the dedicated space for women, which ensures that all members of our
community feel comfortable and represented.
4. Consideration for the Neighborhood
The applicant has clearly gone to great lengths to mitigate any impact on local infrastructure:
- Traffic Management: I am pleased to see that prayer timings have been staggered to avoid any
overlap with the busy pick-up and drop-off times at Avenue Primary Academy.
- Parking & Sustainable Transport: The addition of dedicated parking spaces and bike racks is a
proactive approach to managing local road use.
- Operational Hours: Transitioning from a former 24-hour facility to a community hub with restricted
hours is a significant win for local residents.
5. Aesthetic and Environmental Integration
The design is modest and sympathetic, aligning well with the existing character of the street
scene. I also appreciate the commitment to maintaining existing greenery and plants, ensuring the
site remains soft and integrated into the suburban landscape.
6. Alignment with Strategic Policy
This application is strongly supported by both local and regional planning frameworks:
- London Plan (2021) Policy S1 (Social Infrastructure): The proposal provides high-quality,
inclusive social infrastructure that meets a documented local need. It specifically follows the
London Plan's encouragement for the co-location of services and the shared use of facilities to
use land more efficiently.
- London Plan (2021) Policy S3 & S5: By providing dedicated childcare (S3) and sports/recreation
facilities (S5), the project directly contributes to the "Healthy Streets" and "Good Growth"
objectives.
- Sutton Local Plan (2018) Policy 25: This development fulfills the council's objective to protect and
enhance community facilities. It ensures that the borough's social infrastructure keeps pace with
its growing population.
- Sutton Local Plan Policy 33 & 36: The retention of greenery and focus on site landscaping aligns
with the borough's commitment to urban greening and biodiversity.
In conclusion, I believe this center will be a fantastic asset to our community. It offers a balanced
approach to modernizing our local amenities while remaining deeply respectful of its neighbors. I
urge the planning committee to approve this application.

Address: Bay Cottage 5 Park Road Cheam SM3 8QB
Name: Mr Iftikhar Ahmed
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Committee Members,
I am writing as a long-standing resident of this community to express my unequivocal and strong
support for planning application DM2025/01591, which proposes the establishment of a much-
needed community centre within our neighbourhood. I firmly believe this development will deliver
significant and tangible benefits, aligning closely with the principles of sustainable communities
and local wellbeing promoted by both national and local planning policies, including those outlined
by Sutton Council.
The provision of a dedicated community centre is a vital initiative that will profoundly enhance the
social infrastructure of our area. I am particularly encouraged by the commitment to creating an
inclusive space, with facilities and programmes open to all residents, irrespective of background.
This aligns perfectly with the National Planning Policy Framework's (NPPF) emphasis on fostering
strong, vibrant, and healthy communities, and Sutton Council's strategic objectives for community
cohesion and resident empowerment.
I am especially supportive of the proposed health and wellbeing programmes, including dedicated
health clinics and proactive community health initiatives. By delivering preventative healthcare,
educational workshops, and wellbeing sessions locally, this centre will significantly improve access
to crucial services for individuals and families. This directly contributes to reducing health
inequalities and promoting healthier lifestyles, which are key priorities within Sutton Council's
Local Plan and its commitment to improving public health outcomes for its residents.
Regarding potential concerns often raised in planning deliberations, particularly around school
rush and parking, I wish to provide important context from a local resident's perspective. It is
crucial to recognise that the primary users of this proposed community centre will largely comprise
existing members of our community, many of whom are already engaging with local facilities,
including schools. The parents and carers currently involved in the daily school drop-off and pick-
up routines are precisely the individuals who will benefit most from, and utilise, this new facility.
Therefore, the centre is not anticipated to generate significant new traffic or parking demand from
outside the immediate area, but rather to serve and consolidate existing community activity within
a well-designed and accessible local hub. This approach promotes walking, cycling, and short
local trips, contributing to Sutton Council's sustainability goals by reducing reliance on private
vehicles for essential services.
Having thoroughly reviewed the submitted plans, I am confident that the design is both
proportionate and thoughtfully conceived, ensuring harmonious integration with the surrounding
area. The scale, appearance, and architectural approach appear entirely in keeping with the
established residential character of the neighbourhood. This careful consideration for local context
demonstrates adherence to good design principles, a cornerstone of effective planning policy,
ensuring the development integrates respectfully without detriment to local amenity.
In conclusion, I believe this proposal represents an outstanding opportunity to create a valuable
community asset that will foster social inclusion, improve health outcomes, and enhance the
overall quality of life for all residents. It is a development that is demonstrably sensitive to its
surroundings while providing significant and lasting community benefit. I respectfully and strongly
urge the Planning Committee to approve planning application DM2025/01591, allowing our
community to realise the immense potential of this project.
Thank you

Name: Mrs Saba Ahmad
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Planning Application DM2025/01591 - Letter of Support
Dear Sir/Madam,
I am writing to express my strong support for planning application DM2025/01591.
As a mother of three young children, this proposal would make a meaningful difference to our
family.
We currently attend Basma at its existing location, particularly for Friday prayers, and I have
consistently been impressed by how responsibly and thoughtfully it is managed. Prayer times are
staggered to prevent overcrowding, services are well organised and there have been no
noticeable issues with congestion or disruption. In my experience, Basma makes clear efforts to
minimise impact on the surrounding area.
I understand that concerns are sometimes raised around traffic or noise with developments of this
nature. However, based on our regular attendance, services are structured and time-limited, and
the management actively works to ensure smooth arrival and departure. The proposed location,
with improved accessibility, would in fact make attendance easier and more efficient for families
like ours, including elderly relatives who help with childcare.
For our family, this development is about far more than convenience. We do not live near
extended family, and having access to a local place of prayer provides a sense of belonging and
stability that is deeply important. Research has consistently shown that when children grow up
feeling connected to a community, it strengthens their confidence and sense of identity. This
means that being able to practise our faith locally allows them to understand who they are, feel
proud of their heritage and build friendships with peers who attend the same school (the Avenue
Academy). Again research has consistently shown that such experiences shape self-esteem and
emotional resilience in ways that benefit not only our own children but the wider group of young
people growing up in the area.
The new location would also greatly support our elderly parents, who often assist with school pick-
ups and childcare. Easier access would allow them to facilitate prayers and remain actively
involved in passing on family tradition across generations.
In my view, the proposal is appropriate in scale and design and would provide long-term social,
cultural, and wellbeing benefits to many local families. I respectfully request that Sutton Council
approves this application.

Name: Mr manish bhalla
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to the proposed planning application on the following grounds:
The National Planning Policy Framework (December 2024) requires developments to promote
social interaction and healthy communities, and to ensure they do not undermine community
cohesion (paras 96 and related). This should be considered. This proposal risks the opposite.
The borough needs neutral, multi-use community spaces (e.g. halls, youth centres, recreational
venues) that serve residents of all backgrounds and faiths equally, rather than a specialised facility
for one group. Prioritising shared infrastructure would better foster genuine unity.
The borough already has multiple functional/operational mosques in operation.
This appears significantly disproportionate relative to the local Muslim population (based on
census/demographic data).
At the same time, other faith groups - with similar proportion of population - have no dedicated
place of worship at all.
Further provision for one group raises serious concerns about fairness, equitable resource
allocation, and balanced treatment of all faith communities.
A fact-finding exercise should be done to check proportion of local population of different faiths
and how many places of worship already exist before approving any new ones.
UK policy has moved away from failed multiculturalism towards active integration and assimilation.
The Casey Review (2016) warned of "parallel lives," residential clustering, and segregation risks in
some communities.
Additional large or concentrated faith facilities frequently become focal points that encourage
clustering, leading within a few years to enclaves with:
- reduced everyday mixing between groups
- diminished shared British identity
- the very ghettoisation the Review cautioned against.
An example is in nearby Morden where a big mosque has seen local demography change in last
decade.
In our beautiful borough, already experiencing demographic pressures and change, approving
another mosque is likely to entrench separation rather than build inclusive cohesion - directly
contrary to NPPF goals for unified, integrated places.
References -
### 1. National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF) - December 2024
- *Official GOV.UK page*: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-planning-policy-
framework--2
- *Direct PDF (full document)*:
https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/67aafe8f3b41f783cca46251/NPPF_December_202
4.pdf (published 12 December 2024)
*Relevant section *:
*Paragraph 96* states:
"Planning policies and decisions should aim to achieve healthy, inclusive and safe places which:
a) promote social interaction, including opportunities for meetings between people who might not
otherwise come into contact with each other...
b) are safe and accessible, so that crime and disorder, and the fear of crime, do not undermine the
quality of life or *community cohesion*...
c) enable and support healthy lives..."
*Paragraph 98* also covers community facilities (explicitly including "places of worship") and
requires planning to "plan positively for the provision and use of shared spaces, community
facilities... to enhance the sustainability of communities" and "take into account... the delivery of
local strategies to improve... social and cultural well-being for *all sections of the community*."
### 2. Casey Review (2016) - "The Casey Review: a review into opportunity and integration"
- *Official GOV.UK page*: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/the-casey-review-a-review-
into-opportunity-and-integration
- *Direct PDF (full 200+ page report)*:
https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/5a80c4fded915d74e6230579/The_Casey_Review_
Report.pdf (published 5 December 2016 by Dame Louise Casey)
The review explicitly warns about "parallel lives", residential clustering, segregation risks, and how
concentrated faith facilities (especially large mosques) can accelerate enclave formation and
reduce everyday mixing. Key relevant sections include Chapters 7-8 (on segregation and religion),
Chapter 9 (concentrations of Muslim/Pakistani communities), and Annex A (legacy of "parallel
lives" from earlier reports). It directly supports your points on integration over multiculturalism and
the risks of further specialised facilities.
### 3. Latest Census - Religion in Sutton Borough (2021 Census)
This is the most recent official data (2021; the next full census is 2031).
*Total usual residents in Sutton: **209,602*
*Full religion breakdown (2021)*:
| Religion | Number | Percentage |
|-------------------|-----------|------------|
| Christian | 95,983 | 45.8% |
| No religion | 67,779 | 32.3% |
| Muslim | 14,722 | 7.0%
| Hindu | 14,663 | 7.0% |
| Buddhist | 1,814 | 0.9% |
| Other | 1,249 | 0.6% |
| Jewish | 407 | 0.2% |
| Sikh | 449 | 0.2% |
| No answer | 12,536 | 6.0% |
*Sources* (official ONS data):
- Interactive ONS visualisation with % changes:
https://www.ons.gov.uk/visualisations/censusareachanges/E09000029
- Detailed numbers: ONS-derived tables (e.g., via citypopulation.de cross-reference)

Name: Miss Jaine Schwarz
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly object to the proposed development at the former Sutton Ambulance Station
on Dorset Road. The scale, intensity, and nature of this proposal make it wholly inappropriate for
its setting and would cause significant, lasting, and unacceptable harm to the surrounding
residential community.
1. A fundamentally incompatible use in a 100% residential area
This is a quiet neighbourhood made up entirely of homes, flats, and gardens. Introducing a
highcapacity place of worship and community hub-operating long hours, hosting highturnover
events, and attracting large volumes of people-would radically and permanently disrupt the
tranquillity of the area. This type of facility simply does not belong in such a location.
2. Visually overbearing and completely out of character
The proposed extensions and increased building height would create a structure that is visually
dominant and entirely out of keeping with the domestic scale of neighbouring properties. It would
erode the established look, feel, and rhythm of the neighbourhood and introduce a
commercialscale presence in a suburban street.
3. No unmet need-this development is unjustified and excessive
Multiple similar facilities already exist within 1-1.5 miles of this site, including those used by the
applicant. There is no shortfall in provision and no evidence to justify positioning a highintensity
community centre in a suburban location with extremely poor public transport accessibility (PTAL
1a). This development is unnecessary, overconcentrated, and in the wrong place.
4. Serious noise and latenight disturbance
The Council's own report acknowledges that the proposal could result in raised noise and
disturbance, including the dispersal of up to 250 people at 11pm. This level of activity is wholly
unsuitable for a residential street and would severely undermine neighbours' ability to enjoy
peace, privacy, and sleep. This is a direct threat to residential amenity.
5. Unacceptable parking pressure and traffic impact
The proposal relies heavily on onstreet parking, with demand expected to exceed onsite capacity
and spill into surrounding roads. Combined with poor public transport links, this will cause
congestion, reduce highway safety, and significantly inconvenience residents, particularly during
peak event times.
6. Damaging cumulative impact on community character
Taken together, the increased noise, traffic, footfall, lateevening activity, and intense use would
fundamentally alter the character of the neighbourhood. This development would replace a calm,
suburban residential environment with a highintensity activity hub, harming the area's cohesion,
identity, and quality of life.
Conclusion
This proposal is unnecessary, inappropriate, and harmful.
It threatens residential amenity, undermines the established suburban character of the area, and
introduces a scale and intensity of use that the location simply cannot support.
For these reasons, I strongly urge the Council to refuse the proposed development.

Dear Sir/Madam,
I am writing to express my strong support for Planning Application DM2025/01591.
I am 67 years old and have lived locally for many years. At this stage of life, having accessible

The centre would also create opportunities for intergenerational connection, allowing older

Having such a facility within walking distance reduces reliance on transport and ensures that older

I firmly believe this centre would enhance wellbeing, reduce isolation, and strengthen community
bonds in the locality. I respectfully ask the Council to give favourable consideration to Planning
Application DM2025/01591.
Yours faithfully,
Amna Khan

Name: Mr Thulasi Peddineni
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Reason for Objection:
I am writing to object to the proposed conversion of the former ambulance station into a religious
centre. While I recognize the value of community facilities, the specific nature of this site and its
location within a quiet residential area make this change of use inappropriate for the following
reasons:
Impact on Residential Amenity (Noise and Disturbance):
Unlike its previous use as an emergency service base (which had intermittent and predictable
activity), a religious centre typically generates concentrated periods of high activity, often during
evenings and weekends when residents expect quiet. The noise from large gatherings,
arrivals/departures, and amplified sound would cause significant "noise nuisance" to the
immediate neighbors.
Highways Safety and Parking Pressure:
The surrounding residential streets are narrow and already experience high parking demand. A
religious centre attracts a large number of visitors simultaneously. The current site does not
appear to have sufficient off-street parking to accommodate a full congregation, which will lead to
overspill parking on residential roads, blocking driveways and creating hazards for emergency
access and pedestrians.
Change in Character of the Area:
This is a quiet, established residential neighborhood. The intensification of use-transforming a low-
occupancy service building into a high-capacity public assembly point-is a significant "material
change of use" that is inconsistent with the tranquil character of the area defined in the Sutton
Local Plan.
Inadequate Access: The access points designed for ambulance vehicles may not be safe for the
high volume of pedestrian traffic, including families and children, that a community centre attracts,
leading to potential road safety issues.
I request that the Planning Committee conducts a full Noise Impact Assessment and a Transport
Statement before any decision is made, and I urge the Council to refuse this application to protect
the living conditions of the local residents.

Name: Mr Shameer Subedar
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Re: Letter of Support - Planning Application DM2025/01591
Change of use from Sui Generis (Ambulance Station) to mixed use Classes F1 (Places of
Worship) and F2 (Community Hub), involving the erection of a part one, part two storey front and
side extension, increase in height to part of building and provision of cycle and refuse stores.
Dear Planning Officer,
I am writing to express my strong support for planning application DM2025/01591. I am a local
resident living within walking distance of the proposed site, and I believe this development would
bring meaningful and lasting benefits to our community.
Accessibility and Local Need
As someone who currently travels to Sutton or the MCWAS (Carshalton) mosque to access
appropriate faith facilities, I am acutely aware of the gap this proposal would fill. Having a facility of
this nature within walking distance for myself and my family would be genuinely transformative,
reducing the need to travel and making regular attendance far more practical.
Multifaith and Community Use
I strongly welcome the inclusion of a multifaith hall and function centre. This is not simply a single-
use facility - it is designed to be open and welcoming to all members of the community. By making
the hall available for hire and community events, the development actively encourages cross-
community use and will help foster cohesion amongst residents of different backgrounds. The
neutral and welcoming aesthetic of Option B's proposed façade further supports this ambition,
presenting a building that is approachable and inclusive rather than exclusive.
Youth Provision
I particularly welcome the youth centre initiative included within the plans. Dedicated, accessible
youth provision is something that is notably lacking in this area. I have three children, and this
centre would give them a safe, structured environment in which to meet other young people from
the local community - something that is genuinely difficult to find nearby at present. The fact that a
community-led organisation is taking the initiative to provide this space, at no cost to the public
purse, should be recognised and supported.
Provision for Women
I also welcome the dedicated activities and spaces for women included in the proposal. Accessible
health, wellbeing and social programmes designed specifically around women's needs address a
real demand in the local area and will provide an important resource for many residents who may
otherwise lack access to such provision.
Educational Programmes for Children
The inclusion of educational programmes for children is another significant community benefit.
Supplementary learning opportunities support children's development and help ease pressure on
already stretched school resources.
Sustainable Travel and Parking
The proposal includes 12 on-site parking spaces and 2 dedicated disabled bays, which I consider
adequate and appropriate for a community use of this nature. I am especially pleased to note the
provision of generous cycle storage, which actively encourages sustainable travel. Given that the
facility will serve many local residents who can walk or cycle, I do not consider traffic generation to
be a significant concern. I am also reassured that Friday prayers are being staggered specifically
to avoid conflict with school collection times - a thoughtful and considerate approach that
demonstrates the applicants' awareness of their responsibilities to the wider neighbourhood.
Scale, Design and Greenery
The proposed building is modest in scale and in keeping with the character of the local area.
Option B's façade is particularly well considered - refreshing and visually appealing without being
overbearing. The retention of existing greenery is also to be welcomed and helps ensure the
development maintains a pleasant relationship with its surroundings.
Alternative Uses and Pressure on Local Services
I would also ask the Council to consider what would happen to this site if the application were
refused. There is a real risk that a vacant former ambulance station could be sold for residential
development - potentially a large block of flats or additional housing. While I do not oppose
housing in principle, our local services - schools, GP surgeries, community provision and transport
- are already under considerable pressure. This proposal, by contrast, serves the existing
community without adding to that pressure. It is a low-intensity use that gives something back
rather than simply adding demand.
I am satisfied that this development will not give rise to significant issues of traffic, noise or light
pollution, and that the applicants have made every effort to be considerate neighbours.
I therefore respectfully urge the Council to grant planning permission for application
DM2025/01591. This is a thoughtful, community-minded proposal that will deliver real and lasting
benefits for residents across the local area, and I believe it deserves your full support.

Name: Mrs Lekha Hanumanthaiah
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Subject: Objection to Proposed Mosque Development in Sutton
Dear Council Members,
I am writing to formally object to the proposed construction of a mosque at Dorset Road. My
concerns are based on practical and community planning grounds, which I believe warrant serious
consideration.
1. Traffic and Parking Impact
The proposed site is located in an area that already experiences significant traffic congestion,
particularly during school hours and peak commuting times. The addition of a place of worship that
attracts large gatherings-especially on Fridays and religious festival days-would further exacerbate
traffic flow and parking shortages. Nearby residential roads are not designed to accommodate
such levels of vehicular activity.
2. Proximity to Schools and Residential Zones
The site is close to several schools and residential properties. Increased traffic and crowds during
prayer times could create safety concerns for children, pedestrians, and local residents. The noise
and activity may also disturb the quiet character of surrounding neighbourhoods.
3. Adequate Existing Facilities
There are already several mosques and Islamic centres located within a reasonable distance of
Sutton. These established venues are meeting the needs of the existing Muslim community in the
wider area. It is therefore questionable whether this additional development is necessary or
justified based on current local demographics.
4. Community Balance and Infrastructure
Developments of this nature must consider the capacity of local infrastructure and the composition
of the immediate community. Sutton's planning policy should ensure sustainable development that
reflects genuine local need rather than contributing to overdevelopment and strain on shared
public spaces.
I respectfully ask the Council to refuse planning permission for this proposal on the basis of these
material concerns related to traffic, safety, and planning policy compliance.
Thank you for your time and for considering the views of local residents.
Yours sincerely,
Lekha

Name: Mr Vivek Sharma
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to the proposed planning application on the following grounds:
The National Planning Policy Framework (December 2024) requires developments to promote
social interaction and healthy communities, and to ensure they do not undermine community
cohesion (paras 96 and related). This should be considered. This proposal risks the opposite.
The borough needs neutral, multi-use community spaces (e.g. halls, youth centres, recreational
venues) that serve residents of all backgrounds and faiths equally, rather than a specialised facility
for one group. Prioritising shared infrastructure would better foster genuine unity.
The borough already has multiple functional/operational mosques in operation.
This appears significantly disproportionate relative to the local Muslim population (based on
census/demographic data).
At the same time, other faith groups - with similar proportion of population - have no dedicated
place of worship at all.
Further provision for one group raises serious concerns about fairness, equitable resource
allocation, and balanced treatment of all faith communities.
A fact-finding exercise should be done to check proportion of local population of different faiths
and how many places of worship already exist before approving any new ones.
UK policy has moved away from failed multiculturalism towards active integration and assimilation.
The Casey Review (2016) warned of "parallel lives," residential clustering, and segregation risks in
some communities.
Additional large or concentrated faith facilities frequently become focal points that encourage
clustering, leading within a few years to enclaves with:
- reduced everyday mixing between groups
- diminished shared British identity
- the very ghettoisation the Review cautioned against.
An example is in nearby Morden where a big mosque has seen local demography change in last
decade.
In our beautiful borough, already experiencing demographic pressures and change, approving
another mosque is likely to entrench separation rather than build inclusive cohesion - directly
contrary to NPPF goals for unified, integrated places.

One of local resident,
Shrikant N
