Teachers use the area to park all day, while parents block Holland Avenue, Dorset Rd, York Road.
If you add to this problem by approving the development, you might have an issue where
emergency services will struggle to gain access to this area.

Address: Flats 131 To 139 Westmoreland Drive Sutton SM2 6AJ
Name: Mr Sultan Rahi
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I urge the Planning Committee to approve application DM2025/01591. As a local
resident, I see this centre as a vital consolidation of existing community activity, not a generator of
new, external traffic. By serving local families mostly ''after'' existing school-run routines, it
promotes freedom of worship, religious expression, community harmony and inclusivity by
servicing all people in the community with health & well-being projects as a community hub.
Also Having reviewed the plans, I am confident the scale and design are proportionate and
harmonious with the surrounding residential area. I recommend the Planning Committee to
approve this valuable community asset as the project offers immense value for local cohesion. It is
a well-considered project that fits seamlessly into our community.

Name: Mr Prasant SINGH
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment: Increased traffic as nearby school and also heath and safety for school children

Address: 58 Carew Court Basinghall Gardens Sutton SM2 6AS
Name: Ms Bushra Ali
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my strong support for the proposed community centre
(DM2025/01591), which I believe aligns perfectly with good planning principles.
Addressing concerns about parking and school runs and noise pollution or disturbance, I believe
the planning has sufficient details that highlights the understanding of those concerns and keeping
that in mind the community hub seeks to respect and crowd control accordingly with all
programmes predominantly arranged after peak traffic hours. The facility acts as a local hub for
existing residents. The proposal demonstrates high-quality, contextual design that respects the
local character as well. By approving this, the council will be enabling a project that promotes &
enhances community health and harmony, and provides a necessary asset for our neighbourhood.

Name: Miss Catherine Fellows
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am seriously concerned and worried about the former ambulance station being turned
into a place of worship / community hub. As someone who has lived in Balmoral Way for 15 years,
I have witnessed first hand the overwhelming parking situation during the morning, lunch time and
pickup time due to The Avenue primary school where it can take 15-20 minutes to get out of the
road properly with the congestion from traffic. There is already a lack of suitable parking for The
Avenue school and parents (understandably) park all alongside Balmoral Way throughout the day.
Having a place of worship / community hub with multiple services / congregations throughout the
day would cause a severe issue with primary school drop offs / pick ups and the general
congestion in the surrounding area. The application is also not in line with the Sutton Plan 2031
and the Mayor of London's London plan, and we note that there is an underestimation of required
parking spaces at the site (only 11) vs expected visitors of min. 69 people (and presumably it
could be even more than that at any point in time) for each service / visit at this proposed
community centre / worship hub.
We are seriously worried about the effects on the local community and the primary school which is
at the heart of our community. There is simply not enough space around this ambulance station,
when the road is a two-way system with cars parked already on either side, particularly during
Monday-Friday when children are in school.

Name: Dr Sohail Ansari
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally express my strong support for planning application
DM2025/01591.
As a doctor who has lived and worked in Sutton for many years, I have had the privilege of serving
local families across generations. Through both my professional work and personal life in the
borough, I have seen first-hand how important accessible community spaces are to the health and
wellbeing of residents.
In my view, this proposal represents a positive and meaningful investment in the local community.
The provision of a dedicated community facility will support not only faith-based activities but also
wider social, educational and wellbeing initiatives. Community centres play an important
preventative role in public health. They reduce social isolation, provide structured activities for
young people, offer informal support networks for families, and create safe environments for
constructive engagement. These factors are closely linked to improved mental health outcomes,
stronger family cohesion and greater social stability.
From my understanding of the area, the proposal appears proportionate in scale and appropriate
to its surroundings. It would provide improved facilities within walking distance for many residents,
encouraging more sustainable travel patterns and reducing unnecessary reliance on private
vehicles. Importantly, the development would formalise and enhance community activity that
already exists within Sutton, allowing it to operate in a more suitable and organised setting.
Having lived in Sutton for many years, I care deeply about maintaining the character, safety and
harmony of the area. I would not support a proposal that I believed would undermine those values.
On the contrary, I believe this application aligns with the principles of community-focused
development and would contribute positively to local life.
For these reasons, I respectfully urge Sutton Council to approve planning application
DM2025/01591.

Name: Dr Daniyal Ansari
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally support planning application DM2025/01591 and to encourage
the Council to look positively upon this proposal.
For the past three years, members of this congregation have already been praying and gathering
locally on a regular basis. During that time, these activities have taken place with minimal
complaints and without any significant disruption to neighbouring residents. This sustained period
of responsible operation provides reassurance that the community understands its obligations and
conducts itself respectfully within a residential environment.
The proposed community centre would provide a more appropriate and structured setting for
activities that are already taking place. Rather than creating something new in principle, it
formalises and improves an established presence, allowing gatherings to occur in a facility
designed to accommodate them properly. This brings greater organisation, clearer management
and long-term stability.
As a doctor who has lived in Sutton for many years, I have seen how important strong community
networks are to overall wellbeing. In my professional experience, spaces that encourage positive
engagement, mutual support and structured activities contribute meaningfully to mental health,
social cohesion and family resilience. Accessible local facilities help reduce isolation and provide
constructive outlets for young people and families.
In my view, the proposal is proportionate and suitable for the area. It supports genuine local needs
while remaining consistent with the character of the neighbourhood. Having been part of this
borough for many years, I would not support an application that I believed would negatively affect
the community. On the contrary, I believe this development would enhance it by providing a stable
and beneficial focal point for residents.
For these reasons, I respectfully request that Sutton Council approves planning application
DM2025/01591.

Name: Mr Sanjay Sharma
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to the proposed development due to the significant and
disproportionate impact it is likely to have on our immediate residential community. The projected
increase in traffic-potentially 70-80 additional vehicles during peak overlap-poses serious road
safety concerns, particularly given the proximity of Avenue Primary Academy and its pupils, where
congestion already affects visibility and safe drop-off and pick-up for our children. As a nearby
resident, I already experience difficulty accessing and parking near my home at busy times, and
the addition of this level of traffic would materially affect my ability to safely enter and exit my
property and go about daily routines without delay or obstruction.
The late-night dispersal of attendees to this place of worship would inevitably result in noise,
engines idling, car doors slamming, and vehicles circling for parking, causing disturbance to
families, elderly neighbours, and especially residents of the nearby care home; this would directly
affect my household's ability to enjoy reasonable peace and quiet in the evenings. Furthermore,
the scale and height of the proposed extensions would be visually overbearing and wholly out of
character with the surrounding domestic properties, fundamentally altering the residential nature
and outlook of the area in which I live.

Name: Dr Sahlah Sohail
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my support for planning application DM2025/01591.
As a doctor currently working within the NHS and living in Sutton, I see daily the pressures that
many individuals and families face, particularly in relation to mental health, social isolation and
access to supportive community networks. In clinical practice, we increasingly recognise that
health outcomes are not determined by medical treatment alone, but also by social connection,
belonging and preventative community engagement.
For the past three years, this congregation has already been gathering locally for prayer and
community activities. These gatherings have taken place with minimal complaints and without
significant disruption, demonstrating that the group operates in a considerate and responsible
manner. The proposed community centre would simply provide a more suitable and structured
environment for activities that are already established within the area.
From a healthcare perspective, accessible community spaces can have a measurable positive
impact on mental wellbeing. They offer opportunities for peer support, reduce loneliness, provide
routine and stability, and create safe spaces for discussion and guidance. Such protective factors
are strongly associated with improved mental health outcomes and reduced strain on primary and
secondary care services.
Importantly, a dedicated community facility would also create opportunities to deliver community
health workshops. These could include sessions on mental health awareness, chronic disease
prevention, healthy lifestyle education and support for carers and vulnerable groups. Bringing
health education directly into community settings improves engagement, particularly among
populations who may be less likely to access traditional healthcare pathways.
Having worked within the NHS for many years, I am acutely aware of the importance of
preventative and community-based interventions. In my view, this proposal aligns with those
principles and would strengthen the local support infrastructure in Sutton.
For these reasons, I respectfully ask Sutton Council to approve planning application
DM2025/01591.

Name: Dr Saba Faruqui
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my support for planning application DM2025/01591.
As a long-term Sutton resident who also works locally as a doctor, I approach developments such
as this with careful consideration. I understand the importance of protecting residential character,
road safety and neighbourly amenity. However, I also believe that sustainable communities require
accessible spaces that bring people together in a structured and positive way.
This congregation has been gathering locally for approximately three years. During that time, the
activity has taken place with minimal complaints and without any significant safety or parking
incidents being brought to wider attention. That period of operation is, in my view, relevant. It
suggests that the scale and pattern of use are manageable within the local context.
Some concerns have been raised regarding traffic and parking pressures. While these issues
always merit scrutiny, it is important to distinguish between theoretical peak scenarios and lived
experience. The existing pattern of gatherings has not resulted in any demonstrable breakdown in
road safety or neighbourhood function. Where activity is predictable and time-limited, communities
often adapt without long-term harm.
There have also been concerns about residential amenity. In my observation, the gatherings have
been orderly and respectful. A permanent, properly managed facility is likely to improve
organisation and oversight further, rather than diminish it.
Beyond addressing concerns, I believe the proposal offers tangible benefits. Community centres
provide a focal point for social interaction, youth engagement and informal support networks. In
modern urban settings, where isolation is increasingly common, these shared spaces are
valuable. From my professional experience, social connection and structured community
involvement are protective factors for mental wellbeing and resilience, particularly among young
people and older residents.
This proposal does not introduce an entirely new activity to the area. It regularises and formalises
an existing one within a more suitable setting. In doing so, it offers the opportunity to strengthen
community cohesion while remaining proportionate in scale.
For these reasons, I believe the application represents a balanced and constructive development,
and I respectfully request that Sutton Council grants approval.

Address: Flat 4 Tegan Court Tegan Close Sutton SM2 6GS
Name: Mr Sandeep Jaiswal
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I would like to object to this development for several reasons.
Firstly, the impact on Road Safety and Traffic Congestion is a primary concern; the area already
experiences high volumes of traffic during school drop-off and pick-up times, and the introduction
of a high-capacity community building will create hazardous conditions for pupils and residents
alike.
Secondly, the application appears to have Insufficient Parking Provision, which will inevitably lead
to overspill onto narrow residential streets, obstructing emergency access and resident driveways.
Thirdly, the Noise and Disturbance generated by early morning and late-night arrivals and
departures is incompatible with a quiet residential neighborhood and will negatively affect the living
conditions of those in the immediate vicinity

Name: Mr Siraj Shaik
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Sutton Council,
I am writing as a local resident to express my support for the proposed planning application
DM2025/01591.
Beyond its religious function, I strongly support the wider community benefits outlined in the plans.
The proposed health and wellbeing programmes along with the multi-purpose spaces will make
the facility inclusive and valuable to residents of different ages and backgrounds.
As a young adult living locally, I am particularly encouraged by the prospect of activities and
events that would enable me to connect with like-minded people in my age group within the
community - something that is currently limited but would be greatly enhanced by a shared local
venue.
I appreciate that traffic concerns have been raised by some residents, and these are valid
considerations. However, the supporting documents set out clear mitigation measures, including
staggered prayer times on the busiest day (Friday) to avoid school pick-up periods. On other days,
attendance is expected to be significantly lower, and the proposed on-site parking together with
available street parking should be more than sufficient.
Overall, I believe the proposal has carefully addressed these issues and will provide a positive and
well-managed addition to the neighbourhood.
Therefore, I respectfully request the the Sutton Council approves this application.
Yours Faithfully,
Siraj Shaik

Name: Mr Randeep Donapati
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to planning application DM2025/01591.
The proposal would cause unacceptable harm to highway safety, parking stress, and residential
amenity in an already constrained residential area, particularly during school drop-off/pick-up times
and evening peak hours.
Highway safety and school-run concerns
This area is a school-run environment with high pedestrian activity, including children, parents,
and elderly residents. During peak times, Dorset Road and surrounding streets already experience
heavy traffic congestion and limited visibility.
Any increase in visitors, drop-offs, servicing vehicles, or circulating traffic linked to a community
hub and place of worship would significantly increase risk to pedestrians, especially children.
I regularly experience:
Heavy parking and double parking during school drop-off and pick-up times
Vehicles stopping suddenly or mounting pavements
Reduced visibility at junctions due to parked cars
An intensification of use at this site would worsen these conditions and create an unsafe
environment.
Parking stress
On-street parking in the area is already under severe pressure. Residents frequently struggle to
park near their homes, especially in the evenings.
The proposal does not adequately demonstrate how additional parking demand from visitors,
events, or peak-time use would be managed without overspill into surrounding residential streets.
This would lead to:
Increased congestion
Obstruction of driveways
Reduced access for emergency and service vehicles
Impact on residential amenity
The change of use would introduce higher levels of activity, noise, and disturbance in what is
primarily a residential area. Increased footfall, vehicle movements, and evening use would
negatively affect the quiet enjoyment of nearby homes.
Conclusion
For the reasons above, I ask Sutton Council to refuse planning application DM2025/01591 due to
its unacceptable impact on:
Highway safety
Parking stress
Residential amenity
particularly in a sensitive school-run and residential environment.
Thanks & regards,
Randeep

Address: Flat 4 Tegan Court Tegan Close Sutton SM2 6GS
Name: Mrs Manjushree Jaiswal
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally register my objection to this planning application based on
several critical planning concerns. My primary objection relates to Overdevelopment and
Character; the proposed scale and intensity of use for this site are disproportionate to a quiet
residential setting and would significantly alter the established character of the neighborhood.
Furthermore, the Cumulative Impact on Highway Safety is a major concern, particularly given the
site's proximity to a school; the additional vehicle movements at peak times will exacerbate
existing congestion and create an unsafe environment for children and pedestrians. Additionally,
the proposal lacks a comprehensive Noise Mitigation Plan, and the frequent movement of people
and vehicles during unsociable hours-specifically early mornings and late evenings-will cause a
significant loss of residential amenity and privacy for those living directly adjacent to the site.
Finally, the Inadequate On-site Parking will force visitor vehicles onto already narrow surrounding
roads, creating a persistent obstruction for emergency services and local residents alike. On these
grounds, I believe the application in its current form is unsuitable for this location.

Name: Mr Abhinav Goel
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment: This will increase congestion in our quite neighbourhood.

Name: Dr Abdussamad Ansari
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a local resident and as a doctor to express my support for planning
application DM2025/01591.
Over the past three years, our congregation has been meeting regularly at a nearby location,
closer to Avenue Primary Academy, without any substantiated complaints, enforcement action, or
safety incidents. This track record demonstrates that regular congregational activity can coexist
responsibly with the surrounding residential area. The proposed new site would be slightly further
from the school, yet continues to offer a controlled, time-limited, and managed environment,
meaning it is unlikely to increase any existing risks.
Community facilities such as this play an important role beyond their immediate purpose. They
provide a place for social connection, mutual support, and structured activity, all of which
contribute positively to mental and social wellbeing. For families, older residents, and other
community members, having a safe, accessible, and organised venue encourages engagement,
reduces isolation, and fosters cohesion. It also creates opportunities for health and wellbeing
initiatives, such as workshops or community education sessions, which benefit the wider
neighbourhood.
Concerns regarding parking, traffic, and residential amenity are understandable. However, over
the past three years, comparable gatherings have taken place without observable disruption or
objection from the Local Highway Authority. Bus routes such as the S4 continue to operate safely,
and on-street parking and network circulation have functioned without issue. This real-world
operational experience demonstrates that the proposal can be accommodated without severe or
unacceptable impact.
In my professional and personal view, the proposed development is appropriately scaled,
responsibly managed, and aligned with the needs of the local community. It offers tangible social
and wellbeing benefits while maintaining compatibility with surrounding residential streets.
I therefore respectfully request that Sutton Council approve planning application DM2025/01591.

Address: Flat 4 Tegan Court Tegan Close Sutton SM2 6GS
Name: Miss Khushi jaiswal
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to this application, as the proposed development
represents an unsuitable intensification of use for a site located within a sensitive residential and
school zone. My primary concern is the Pedestrian and Highway Safety risk; the introduction of a
high-occupancy facility in such close proximity to a school will place undue stress on the existing
road infrastructure, creating significant safety hazards for children during peak hours. Furthermore,
the application fails to address the Loss of Residential Amenity that will occur due to the increased
noise, light pollution, and general disturbance associated with frequent, large-scale arrivals and
departures at all hours of the day. The Inadequate Parking Strategy provided in the proposal is
also deeply concerning, as it will inevitably result in displaced parking on narrow neighboring
streets, compromising access for residents and emergency services. Finally, the Scale and
Character of the proposed building are inconsistent with the surrounding domestic architecture,
resulting in an overbearing presence that negatively impacts the visual amenity of the area. For
these reasons, I believe the proposal is contrary to local planning policies regarding sustainable
development and neighborhood protection

Name: Mr Mazhar Sarwar
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I support the Planning Application reference number DM2025/01591. I have been living
in Cheam/Sutton area for over fifteen years and we need community centre/facilities for health and
wellbeing of the people living in the community. The building is in the local area and is within easy
reach for people living in the Cheam and Sutton area. The community centre/facility will also be
used for prayers. The Friday payers will be staggered so that school pick up times are not
impacted. this will not cause traffic or noise issues in the area. In view of the needs of the local
community I kindly ask the Council to approve this Application for the betterment of the people of
Sutton and surrounding area.

Name: Mrs Anjali Goel
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Since the school is nearby, there will be parking issues. Parents may have difficulty
finding parking spaces, and residents could also experience problems due to increased traffic in
the area.

Name: Mr Shantanu Suwarnkar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application

Name: Mr Nitin PRAKASH
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
It is a place being used for fire suppression and community service. Don't need any
community based prayer area. We need to accept the country and culture as it is rather than
changing it. Let the country be great as it is and not change it to destroy the image and sanctity!

Name: Mr Shantanu Suwarnkar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I have to visit this area often. There will be too much traffic and noise which will
significantly disrupt the quality of life of the residents.

Name: Mrs Humaira Sarwar
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I support this application. I have been living in Cheam for over 15 years. we need
community centre for health and wellbeing of people in our community. The building is in the local
area and easy to reach for the people in the Sutton area.the community centre will also be used
for Friday payers. The Friday prayer times wil be such as to not conflict with school pick ups. This
will not cause traffic or noise issues. I kindly as the Council to approve this application.

Address: Flat 6 Bridge House Bridge Road Sutton SM2 6NE
Name: Mr Sreekanth Pasuladi
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The proposed development is located in close proximity to a school. This area already
experiences significant congestion during school drop-off and pick-up times. The introduction of a
Place of Worship and Community Hub is likely to:
Generate high volumes of traffic at peak times, including evenings and weekends.
Result in additional on-street parking pressures.
Increase the risk of unsafe parking (double parking, pavement parking, obstruction of visibility
splays).
Compromise pedestrian safety, particularly for school children and parents.
Unlike the former ambulance station use, which operated in a controlled and professional capacity,
a Place of Worship and Community Hub could attract large gatherings at specific times, leading to
concentrated surges in traffic that the surrounding highway network is not designed to
accommodate.
This intensification of use is likely to conflict with highway safety policies within the Local Plan and
the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF), which seek to ensure safe and suitable access
for all users.

Name: Mr Zayn Razvi
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application

Name: Mrs Piali Das
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The traffic in that area will become worse.
There is a primary school stone's throw distance away.Parents already find it difficult to park their
cars for drop off and pick up.
Residents always complain about the traffic due to the school. So any more traffic will cause
further problems for the residents.
Noise will be another big issue for residents and the care home nearby.

Name: Mrs Naga Patcha
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I have raised following objections
* Inaccurate Representation of Usage: The application suggests that the proposal is simply a
relocation of existing activities from Belmont Church. However, this is misleading. Currently, the
church is only utilized for Friday afternoon prayers. Because the Islamic faith requires five daily
prayers, seven days a week, the transition to a dedicated mosque will significantly increase the
frequency and intensity of site usage compared to the current arrangement.
* Access Constraints: There are currently only three points of access to the old fire station, which
are insufficient to handle the projected increase in traffic and footfall.
* Proximity to Avenue School and Local Infrastructure: The site is located near Avenue School, an
area that already suffers from severe parking shortages and high traffic congestion. Introducing a
full-time place of worship would place an unsustainable "stress" on the local roads. Furthermore,
the increased vehicle flow raises significant concerns regarding the safety and security of children
in the immediate vicinity. Given these combined factors, the location is not an ideal or safe choice
for a mosque.

Name: Mrs Hima Puchakayala
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment: I object this proposal

Name: Mrs Kaumudi Madnurkar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to this proposal based on the following significant
concerns regarding highways safety and residential amenity:
Severe Parking Shortfall & Displacement:The provision of only 14 on-site spaces is fundamentally
inadequate for the documented peak surge of 250 people. The applicant's "worst-case" scenario
acknowledges that 69 vehicles will be displaced onto narrow residential roads, with up to 80
additional vehicles circulating at once. This will result in dangerous levels of congestion and
"cruising" for spaces.
Child Safety (Avenue Primary Academy): With over 1,000 pupils nearby, the influx of traffic during
peak overlap periods creates an unacceptable risk. Increased roadside parking will severely
reduce visibility for children at crossing points and hinder the safe flow of school-run traffic.
Impact on Vulnerable Residents: The site is in close proximity to a care home and elderly
neighbours. Obstruction of pavements and driveways by displaced vehicles poses a direct threat
to the accessibility and safety of these vulnerable groups.
Late-Night Noise Disturbance: Dispersal at 11:00 PM will cause significant sleep disturbance
through engine noise, door slamming, and raised voices in a quiet residential setting, where sound
echoes and carries late at night.
Cumulative Impact: Dorset Road is already at capacity due to existing clubs and venues. This
proposal ignores the cumulative pressure on the local highway network, which cannot sustain
another high-occupancy use without reaching a point of total gridlock.
I urge the planning committee to refuse this application on the grounds of highways safety and the
unacceptable impact on local residential amenity.

Name: Mrs Karthiga Kaliaperumal
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Our community is designated as a residential zone intended to provide a peaceful and
low-traffic living environment. The proposed development may lead to increased traffic congestion,
parking shortages, noise, and strain on local infrastructure, which could significantly impact the
quality of life for residents.
Our objection is based solely on planning, zoning, and community impact concerns-not on
religious grounds. We respectfully request that the relevant authorities reconsider this proposal
and explore alternative locations more suitable for such a facility.

Address: 12 Lincoln Terrace York Road Cheam SM2 6HE
Name: Mr Saifullah Taj
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I whole heartedly support the application DM2025/01591 for a community hall and place
of worship that will serve the locality.
I understand that as part of maintaining the property over the last year, a program of tree planting
was initiated. This saw over half a dozen trees (cherry trees, fig tree, cherry blossom) planted to
augment the existing foliage. In addition to this, rose beds and daffodils have also been
introduced. Other ideas that have been floated for the future are that of a herb garden...
The ecology and bio diversity of the area is to be valued. These activities which were partly carried
out by volunteers and partly by professionals have demonstrated that this can be a space where
different generations and backgrounds can share their love for nature and gardening.
It is envisaged that there will be a number of facilities within the complex including a community
hall for the general public, facilities for Muslim women etc. After the local British Muslim community
had been hiring general purpose halls to facilitate their community and religious needs and rights,
the time has now come for them to have their own facility having demonstrated there is a need
which exceeds the current provision.
Having lived in Sutton for over 25 years, I am sure this project will be of benefit to the borough. I
am hopeful/ looking forward to this visionary project with facilities benefitting the local community
materially, socially and spiritually.

Name: Mr HASSAN SHAIK
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am a local resident who moved to the area relatively recently, and I support this
application.
When I first moved here, I did not know anyone locally. Through BASMA and the weekly Friday
prayers, I was able to connect socially very quickly and feel part of the community. This has been
very important for me and my family.
I am also a parent of 4 children 3 of them study at Avenue Primary School. I can do the school run
both by car and on foot, and I have not experienced problems finding parking. The morning period
is naturally the busiest for traffic due to school and work travel, but there are no prayers at that
time, so the proposed centre would not add pressure then.
In the afternoons, traffic is generally lighter as children do not all leave at once. Many stay for
after-school clubs or childcare, and congestion, parking and noise are never as intense as the
morning peak. From my experience, buses run normally and on time, and I have not seen
disruption to traffic flow.
The current Friday prayers have been managed by BASMA calmly and respectfully for several
years. Most activity takes place indoors, people arrive and leave in a short and orderly way, and I
have not experienced noise or disturbance.
Most attendees are local residents, many arriving on foot, and the proposed site is an
improvement on the current temporary arrangement as it is better suited and has its own parking.
BASMA has been a positive and responsible part of the local community for years.
I believe this centre will benefit local residents and help families like mine feel connected and
supported in the area, and I hope the Council will approve this application as it is in the best
interests of Worcester road residents.

Name: Mr Sachin Madnurkar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to this proposal based on the following significant
concerns regarding highways safety and residential amenity:
Severe Parking Shortfall & Displacement:The provision of only 14 on-site spaces is fundamentally
inadequate for the documented peak surge of 250 people. The applicant's "worst-case" scenario
acknowledges that 69 vehicles will be displaced onto narrow residential roads, with up to 80
additional vehicles circulating at once. This will result in dangerous levels of congestion and
"cruising" for spaces.
Child Safety (Avenue Primary Academy):With over 1,000 pupils nearby, the influx of traffic during
peak overlap periods creates an unacceptable risk. Increased roadside parking will severely
reduce visibility for children at crossing points and hinder the safe flow of school-run traffic.
Impact on Vulnerable Residents: The site is in close proximity to a care home and elderly
neighbours. Obstruction of pavements and driveways by displaced vehicles poses a direct threat
to the accessibility and safety of these vulnerable groups.
Late-Night Noise Disturbance: Dispersal at 11:00 PM will cause significant sleep disturbance
through engine noise, door slamming, and raised voices in a quiet residential setting, where sound
echoes and carries late at night.
Cumulative Impact: This Road is already at capacity due to existing clubs and venues. This
proposal ignores the cumulative pressure on the local road network, which cannot sustain another
high-occupancy use without reaching a point of total gridlock.
I urge the planning committee to refuse this application on the grounds of highways safety (NPPF
Para 115) and the unacceptable impact on local residential amenity.

Name: Mr Aguilane Periasoamy
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Our community is designated as a residential zone intended to provide a peaceful, low-
traffic, and safe living environment. The proposed development is likely to increase traffic
congestion, parking shortages, and noise levels, particularly during prayer times, events, and large
gatherings.
Additionally, there is a school and an elderly care home located nearby. Increased traffic,
congestion, and crowd movement may create safety concerns for children and senior citizens. We
are also concerned about potential environmental impacts, including increased noise, air pollution
from vehicle traffic, and strain on local infrastructure.
Our objection is based solely on planning, zoning, environmental, and community impact

facility.

community.

Name: Mrs ZAINAB SULTANA
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am a local resident who moved to the area relatively recently, and I support this
application.
When I first moved here, I did not know anyone locally. Through BASMA and the weekly Friday
prayers, I was able to connect socially very quickly and feel part of the community. This has been
very important for me and my family.
I am also a parent of 4 children. 3 of them study at Avenue Primary School. I can do the school
run both by car and on foot, and I have not experienced problems finding parking. The morning
period is naturally the busiest for traffic due to school and work travel, but there are no prayers at
that time, so the proposed centre would not add pressure then.
In the afternoons, traffic is generally lighter as children do not all leave at once. Many stay for
after-school clubs or childcare, and congestion, parking and noise are never as intense as the
morning peak. From my experience, buses run normally and on time, and I have not seen
disruption to traffic flow.
The current Friday prayers have been managed by BASMA calmly and respectfully for several
years. Most activity takes place indoors, people arrive and leave in a short and orderly way, and I
have not experienced noise or disturbance.
Most attendees are local residents, many arriving on foot, and the proposed site is an
improvement on the current temporary arrangement as it is better suited and has its own parking.
BASMA has been a positive and responsible part of the local community for years.
I believe this centre will benefit local residents and help families like mine feel connected and
supported in the area, and I hope the Council will approve this application as it is in the best
interests of Worcester road residents.

Comment:
I wish to object to this application due to concerns regarding traffic generation, parking

Approving a full-time place of worship and community hub at this site is likely to significantly

There are limited access points to the site, and the local road network does not appear capable of

Planning decisions should properly consider the cumulative long-term impact on traffic, parking,
and the character of the area before granting permission.

Name: Ramya Donthi Nagaraju
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
- Increased Traffic & Road Safety Risks
1) The proposed development is likely to generate a substantial increase in vehicle movements in
surrounding residential streets. It is estimated that 70 or more vehicles could seek parking on
nearby roads, with the potential for up to 80 vehicles present locally during peak overlap periods
(for example, when one event finishes and another begins).
This level of additional traffic would:
Intensify congestion on already narrow residential streets
Lead to unsafe parking on corners, pavements, and across driveways
Increase vehicle turning movements and reversing in confined spaces
Create hazards for pedestrians, cyclists, and local residents
Such pressure on local infrastructure significantly raises the risk of minor collisions, near misses,
and blocked emergency access routes.
- School Safety Concerns
Avenue Primary Academy serves approximately 1,034 pupils, resulting in substantial pedestrian
and vehicle activity during morning drop-off and afternoon pick-up periods.
The addition of event-related traffic would:
Increase congestion during peak school travel times
Reduce sightlines for drivers and pedestrians due to on-street parking
Heighten the risk of children stepping into traffic from between parked vehicles
Complicate safe crossing for families walking to and from school
Children are particularly vulnerable road users. Even small increases in traffic and parking density
can materially elevate the risk of accidents in school zones.
- Serious Noise and Late-Night Disturbance
Events involving up to 250 attendees, dispersing around 11:00pm, would likely create repeated
late-night disturbances.
This disturbance is not limited to amplified sound from within the venue, but includes:
Groups congregating and talking loudly outside
Car engines idling and revving
Doors slamming
Vehicles circling repeatedly while searching for parking spaces
Car stereos and horns
Noise at this time of night travels further and is more intrusive due to lower ambient background
sound. Repeated weekly or regular events would have a cumulative negative impact on residents'
sleep, wellbeing, and quality of life.
- Impact on Vulnerable Residents
The area includes a nearby care home and a significant number of elderly residents, who are
particularly sensitive to disruption.
The likely impacts include:
Blocked access for carers, ambulances, and essential services
Increased difficulty for residents with limited mobility navigating obstructed pavements
Heightened anxiety caused by congestion and late-night activity
Greater health impact from sleep disturbance
For elderly and vulnerable individuals, even moderate increases in noise and access obstruction
can have disproportionate effects on physical and mental wellbeing.
- Visually Overbearing and Out of Character
The proposed extensions and increased building height would result in a structure that is:
Visually dominant within the streetscape
Out of scale with neighbouring two-storey domestic properties
Inconsistent with the established residential character of the area
The development would alter the street's appearance from a predominantly domestic setting to
one defined by a larger, more imposing institutional-style building. This would undermine the visual
cohesion of the neighbourhood and negatively affect residential amenity.

Address: Flats 140 To 148 Westmoreland Drive Sutton SM2 6AJ
Name: Mr Wahab Riaz
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
As a resident and stakeholder of Sutton, I formally submit this statement in support of
the proposed community hub and place of worship. I believe this application represents an
exemplary use of a brownfield site and provides significant public benefit. The site is a former
ambulance station, currently vacant and underutilised. Redeveloping this "brownfield" site for
community use directly supports National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF) goals to prioritise
the reuse of previously developed land over greenfield sites.
Also There is a demonstrated local demand for multi-purpose community spaces. This hub will
foster social inclusion, provide health and wellbeing benefits, and serve as a vital local asset for a
range of demographics, including families and the elderly.
Contrary to concerns about new traffic, this facility is designed to serve the existing local
population. By providing a hub within walking and cycling distance for nearby residents and
school-run parents, the project reduces the need for private vehicle trips, directly supporting
Sutton's sustainability and "Healthy Streets" targets. Nevertheless any traffic would be after peak
hours not affecting the neighbourhood.
Looking at the proposed design it shows respect of the residential character of the area. Its scale
and architectural approach are proportionate to the surrounding streetscape, ensuring that the
development integrates respectfully without harming local amenity.
This project represents a significant private investment into community infrastructure. It provides a
"place of worship" (Class F1) and a community hub without requiring public funding, while still
contributing to the borough's social fabric.
So This proposal transforms a redundant site into a vibrant community anchor. I respectfully urge
the Planning Committee to approve application DM2025/01591, as it is demonstrably in
accordance with both local and national planning policy.

Name: Mr Giridharan Wijayasingam
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
1. Significant Traffic Generation and Road Safety Risks
The proposal is expected to generate substantial additional traffic, with estimates suggesting over
70 vehicles attending at peak times, and potentially up to 80 vehicles locally during overlapping
arrival and departure periods.
The surrounding roads are residential in nature and are not designed to accommodate this volume
of traffic. The likely consequences include:
Road congestion and obstruction of access routes
Vehicles blocking junctions and driveways
Increased risk to pedestrians
Reduced emergency vehicle access
Such levels of vehicle movement would materially harm highway safety and residential amenity.
2. School Safety Concerns - Avenue Primary Academy
The site is in close proximity to Avenue Primary Academy, which accommodates approximately
1,034 pupils. The additional traffic associated with this development would significantly increase
risks during school drop-off and pick-up times.
Increased vehicle circulation, parking searches, and informal stopping would create hazardous
conditions for children, parents, and staff. The potential conflict between high pedestrian volumes
and increased traffic is a serious safeguarding concern.
3. Noise and Late-Night Disturbance
The proposed use could involve gatherings of up to 250 people, with activity extending to
approximately 11:00 pm.
This raises serious concerns regarding:
Noise from large groups dispersing
Raised voices and general congregation outside the building
Vehicle engines idling and repeated circulation in search of parking
Car doors slamming late at night
This level of activity would represent a significant departure from the current quiet residential
character of the area and would materially harm neighbouring residents' right to peaceful
enjoyment of their homes.
4. Impact on Vulnerable Residents
There are vulnerable and elderly residents living nearby who may be particularly affected by
increased noise, disturbance, and traffic. The sustained intensity of use could have adverse
effects on wellbeing and quality of life for those in close proximity.
5. Overdevelopment and Harm to Character of the Area
The proposed building appears visually dominant and out of character with the surrounding
residential properties. Its scale and massing would overwhelm the existing built environment and
fundamentally alter the character of the area.
The proposal represents overdevelopment of the site and fails to respect the established
residential setting.
Conclusion
For the reasons set out above - including highway safety risks, school safeguarding concerns,
noise disturbance, impact on vulnerable residents, and harm to local character - I respectfully
request that this planning application be refused.
Thank you for your consideration.

Dear Sir

- Youth engagement
- Cultural programmes
- Educational tuition/ after school clubs

Comment:

Name: Dr Afshan Jamil
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my support for Planning Application DM2025/01591. I have lived
in Sutton for 14 years, I work locally as a GP, and I am also a parent of three teenagers who are
growing up in this community.
Over the years, Sutton has been a place my family and I are proud to call home. However, I have
increasingly seen, both professionally and personally, the shortage of accessible, affordable
indoor community spaces. Many structured activities for young people, educational support
sessions, and health-focused workshops rely on limited or costly venues. This proposal would help
address that gap in a practical and proportionate way.
In my role as a GP, I regularly see the impact of social isolation, anxiety, low mood and lack of
safe youth provision. Elderly residents struggling with loneliness, new parents needing support
networks, and teenagers lacking structured engagement are common themes in primary care.
Community hubs that promote connection, belonging and purposeful activity play a significant
preventative role in supporting mental and physical wellbeing.
As a parent of three teenagers, I understand first-hand the importance of safe, local and
supervised environments. There are currently no youth clubs in the immediate area. A managed
space offering educational programmes, youth engagement, and positive social interaction would
be of genuine benefit, not only to my own children, but to many local families.
I also particularly welcome the inclusion of dedicated space and activities for women, which are
limited locally, especially culturally sensitive community-based wellbeing programmes.
From a planning perspective, the building is modest in scale and in keeping with the surrounding
residential area. It represents a low-intensity use compared with other potential developments.
The inclusion of 12 on-site parking spaces and 2 disabled bays, along with cycle racks, shows
appropriate consideration for access and sustainable travel. The staggering of Friday prayers to
avoid school pick-up times further demonstrates sensitivity to local traffic patterns. I therefore do
not anticipate significant traffic, noise or light disturbance. The retention of greenery is also a
positive aspect of the design.
Overall, I believe this proposal represents a thoughtful and much-needed community investment
that will strengthen social cohesion and support preventative health and wellbeing in Sutton.
I respectfully ask the Council to approve this application.

Name: Mr Shailendra Kumar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Re: Planning Application DM2025/01591 - Objection
I formally object to this application on highway safety and traffic grounds.
1. Traffic Congestion & Highway Safety
The proposed change of use to a Place of Worship and Community Hub will materially increase
vehicle movements, particularly during concentrated service/event times. Dorset Road and
surrounding residential streets already experience peak congestion. The proposal risks:
- Increased queuing and unsafe turning movements
- Illegal or obstructive parking
- Reduced emergency vehicle access
- No robust Transport Assessment has demonstrated that the local road network can safely
accommodate this uplift.
2. Proximity to Avenue Primary School - Child Safety Risk
Avenue Primary School is located within approximately 1km and shares the same local access
routes. Peak worship/event times are likely to overlap with school drop-off and pick-up periods.
This creates foreseeable risks:
- Conflict between high pedestrian volumes (children) and additional vehicle traffic
- Unsafe drop-off behaviour, which is already a nightmare during peak times
- Increased road-crossing hazards
- The application does not adequately address safeguarding of vulnerable road users.
3. Lack of Parking & Traffic Mitigation
There is insufficient clarity on:
- Dedicated parking provision
- Managed drop-off zones
- Event traffic management strategy
- Controlled dispersal plans
- Without enforceable mitigation, the proposal would result in unacceptable highway and
pedestrian safety impacts.
Conclusion
Given the significant unresolved highway safety concerns - particularly regarding children - I
request that Sutton Council refuse the application unless a full Transport Assessment and
enforceable mitigation strategy clearly demonstrate no adverse impact on traffic flow or child
safety.

Address: 23 Elizabeth House Elizabeth drive Banstead Sm7 2fe
Name: Ms Susie Sriranganathan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to the above planning application.
My father resides on Balmoral Way and I visit him regularly to assist with his day-to-day needs. I
am therefore very familiar with the traffic conditions and access issues on this road.
Balmoral Way already experiences significant congestion, particularly during school peak times in
the mornings and afternoons. At these times, vehicle movement is extremely restricted and
visibility is compromised.
In addition, the road is frequently used for commuter parking by individuals accessing Belmont
railway station and nearby bus routes. This results in extended on-street parking throughout the
day, reducing available road width and making manoeuvring difficult.
The cumulative impact of:
- School peak-time congestion
- Commuter parking
- Narrow residential road layout
already creates challenging and at times unsafe conditions.
Of particular concern is emergency access. Should an ambulance, fire engine, or other emergency
vehicle need to enter Balmoral Way, current parking patterns and congestion would make access
extremely difficult, if not impossible. This raises serious public safety concerns for residents, many
of whom are elderly.
If this development proceeds, it will inevitably attract additional vehicles from surrounding areas,
whether residents, visitors, service providers, or delivery vehicles. The increased traffic and
parking demand would significantly exacerbate an already problematic situation and result in
unacceptable highway pressure and disruption.
In my view, the proposal fails to adequately consider:
- Highway safety
- Emergency vehicle access
- Existing peak-time congestion
- The cumulative impact on a constrained residential road
I respectfully request that these concerns be fully assessed before any decision is made.

weekends, and special religious events, may substantially increase traffic volume and on-street
parking pressure.
Of particular concern is the immediate proximity of the site to a primary school. The area is already
heavily congested during school drop-off and pick-up times, creating safety risks for children and
pedestrians. Additional traffic and parking demand could further compromise road safety, increase
the likelihood of accidents, and obstruct emergency vehicle access.
I respectfully request that the council carefully assess:
The adequacy of the traffic impact assessment
Parking capacity and overspill risk into residential streets
Pedestrian safety measures, especially for school children
Noise and crowd management plans
The cumulative impact on existing local infrastructure
I ask that these factors be thoroughly reviewed before any approval is granted, and that
consideration be given to alternative locations better suited to accommodate higher visitor
volumes without affecting residential safety and daily life.
Thank you for considering my concerns.

Name: Mr Jai santhosh Paatur
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a local resident to strongly object to the proposed conversion of the
former ambulance station into a religious place of worship in this core residential and quiet area.
The site is located in a predominantly residential neighborhood with a nearby school, and the
change of use is unfit for such a sensitive location. My objections are based on material planning
considerations, including increased traffic, pollution, noise, and incompatibility with the surrounding
area, as outlined below.
1. Increased Traffic and Highway Safety Concerns.
The conversion to a religious place would likely attract large gatherings, especially during services,
events, or festivals, leading to a significant increase in vehicle traffic in this quiet residential street.
This could result in congestion, inadequate parking, and safety risks for pedestrians, including
schoolchildren from the nearby school. The area already has limited road capacity, and additional
vehicles would exacerbate these issues, potentially causing accidents or blocking emergency
access. This is contrary to local planning policies on sustainable transport and highway safety.
2. Noise and Disturbance
Religious activities often involve amplified sound systems, calls to prayer, group gatherings, and
extended hours of operation, which would generate excessive noise in an otherwise peaceful
residential zone. With a school nearby, this could disrupt the learning environment for children and
disturb residents' quality of life, particularly during evenings or weekends. Such disturbances
would harm the amenity of the area, violating policies aimed at protecting residential tranquility
from noise nuisance.
4. Unfit for Change to Community/Religious Use and Impact on Area Character.
The site is in the heart of a quiet residential neighborhood, not suited for a community or religious
facility that could draw crowds from outside the area. This change of use is incompatible with
existing residential and educational surroundings, potentially altering the character of the locale
from a calm, family-oriented space to one with commercial-like activity. It may also strain local
infrastructure without providing benefits to the immediate community, contravening local plan
policies on appropriate land use and preserving neighborhood identity.

Name: Mr Tashfin Osmani
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Ms Davidson,
I write in support of Planning Application DM2025/01591 in respect of the Ambulance Station,

In built form terms, the proposed part one, part two storey extensions and limited height increase
appear proportionate and modest. The scale and design are consistent with the surrounding
residential context and do not represent overdevelopment of the site.
The use proposed is, in my view, relatively low intensity compared to alternative forms of
development that could lawfully come forward on the site. The provision of 12 on-site parking
spaces, including 2 disabled bays, together with cycle racks, demonstrates that transport and
accessibility considerations have been properly addressed. The retention of greenery further
ensures the site remains visually appropriate within its setting.
It is also noted that Friday prayers will be staggered to avoid conflict with local school pick-up
times, reflecting a proactive and responsible approach to traffic management. On this basis, I do
not consider that the proposal would give rise to significant or unacceptable traffic, noise, or light
impacts.
For the above reasons, I respectfully request that the Council grants approval for Planning
Application DM2025/01591.
Yours sincerely,
Tashfin Osmani

Name: Ms Y Jiang
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
We would like to raise an objection to the planning application, as the proposals present
several significant concerns for residents living in this quiet, residential area. While the application
suggests that the center may attract around 120 people, it is not realistic or practical to restrict
attendance at Friday prayers. The Council therefore has to consider the genuine worstcase
scenario: that the building could reach its full capacity of approximately 250 people, with activities
continuing until the proposed closing time of 11pm.
This raises a number of issues that would directly affect the well-being and safety of the local
community:
1) Noise and LateEvening Disturbance
This neighborhood is home to families, older residents, and people who work early mornings. The
prospect of up to 250 people leaving the site at 11pm would inevitably create significant noise and
disruption at a time when the area is normally quiet. This level of lateevening activity is not
compatible with the character of the surrounding residential streets.
2) Traffic and Highway Safety
The site is located just 100 meters from a large Avenue Primary Academy with over 1,000 pupils.
During school dropoff and pickup times, the surrounding roads already experience heavy
congestion, high pedestrian activity, and limited visibility. Introducing a facility that could attract up
to 250 additional visitors-particularly during Friday prayers, which overlap with school finishing
times-would create severe traffic pressure and increase the risk of accidents. The combination of
high pedestrian volumes, parked cars, and increased vehicle movements would be chaotic and
unsafe.
3) Parking Pressure
Parking in the area is already extremely limited, especially during school hours and evenings. The
application does not demonstrate how it will accommodate the parking needs of such a large
number of visitors. Over-spill parking onto surrounding streets would place additional strain on
residents, obstruct driveways, and create further safety hazards for children and families walking
to and from the nearby school.
4) Cumulative Impact on the Community
When considering noise, traffic, parking, schoolrelated congestion, and latenight activity together,
the cumulative impact on the neighborhood would be considerable. The scale and intensity of the
proposed use are not in keeping with the residential setting, and the application does not provide
adequate mitigation to protect the amenity, safety, and quality of life of those living nearby.
Therefor we ask the Council to refuse DM2025/01591 due to unacceptable impacts on highway
safety, parking, residential amenity, emergency access and cumulative Impact on the community.

Address: 1 Kenilworth Terrace 89 Worcester Road Sutton SM2 6JQ
Name: Dr M Alamu
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Planning Department
London Borough of Sutton
Civic Offices, St Nicholas Way
Sutton, SM1 1EA
RE: Planning Application Support - DM2025/01591
Site Address: Ambulance Station, Dorset Road, Belmont, SM2 6JH
Proposal: Change of use from Sui Generis (Ambulance Station) to mixed use Classes F1 (Places
of Worship) and F2 (Community Hub) involving the erection of a part one, part two storey front and
side extension, increase in height to part of building and provision of cycle and refuse stores.
Dear Planning Officer,
I am writing to express my strong support for the above planning application to transform the
former Ambulance Station on Dorset Road into a mixed-use Place of Worship (Class F1) and
Community Hub (Class F2).
I am writing this letter strictly in my personal capacity as a local resident; however, my views are
deeply informed by my experience serving in a governance capacity within the local education
sector.
I see firsthand the pressing need for supplementary community infrastructure in this borough, and
I support this application on the following material planning grounds:
1. Direct Support for Local Educational Growth and SEN Provision
The F1/F2 designation allows this site to act as a vital supplementary space for educational growth
in the borough. With the new Sutton special school slated to open in the near future, this
Community Hub could serve as an invaluable auxiliary space. It has the immense potential to be
utilised for the assessment of base provision-offering a neutral, welcoming environment for Special
Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND) assessments, early interventions, and parent-support
meetings outside of a formal, school setting.
2. Partnerships with Sutton's Local Academy Trusts
Through my governance experience, I am acutely aware of the pressure on local school facilities
and the constant need for accessible, neutral community partnership spaces. High-performing
local educational networks across the borough-such as the Greenshaw Learning Trust (GLT), LEO
Academy Trust, and Cirrus Academy Trust, etc.- frequently require off-site venues to facilitate
pupil outreach, after-school mentoring, and localised family support programs. By approving this
F1/F2 Community Hub, the Council will be providing a safe, dedicated space that these
mainstream educational trusts can partner with.
3. Addressing the Shortfall in Youth Provision and Supplementary Education (e.g., 11-Plus, 6th
formers, etc )
It is well documented that severe budget pressures and funding cuts have forced the closure of
open-access, Council-run youth centres across Sutton. Consequently, the borough now relies
heavily on the voluntary and community sectors to fill this critical gap. Approving this Community
Hub will provide a safe, dedicated space for young people across a wide age spectrum. For
younger pupils, it can serve as a vital venue for 11-plus tuition; given Sutton's highly competitive
grammar school system, there is a massive local demand for affordable, community-based spaces
to host these preparation groups. Furthermore, for older youths, it will serve as an essential
environment where 6th formers, apprentices, and those in vocational training can access study
support, mentoring, and positive social engagement during evenings and weekends.
4. Alignment with Local Healthcare Objectives - Epsom and St Helier University Hospitals NHS
Trust & The Royal Marsden Hospital
A dedicated Community Hub (Class F2) at this location presents a vital opportunity to support the
preventative health and community care objectives of both the Epsom and St Helier University
Hospitals NHS Trust and The Royal Marsden Hospital, which is located right here in Belmont. The
NHS relies heavily on community infrastructure for "social prescribing"-directing vulnerable
residents, including the elderly and those recovering from long-term illnesses, to local support
groups to combat isolation, loneliness, and cognitive decline.
This site is perfectly positioned to host elderly day clubs, dementia support cafes, well-being drop-
in sessions, and provide a quiet, accessible space for outpatient support groups and families
navigating care at the nearby Royal Marsden.
5. Relieving Pressure on Existing Oversubscribed Community Assets
It is well known that existing community hubs in the borough, such as the Thomas Wall Centre,
are frequently overbooked and operating at maximum capacity. Approving this new hub in
Belmont will provide a much-needed, localised backup facility. When the Thomas Wall Centre is
fully booked, this site can serve as an essential overspill location for vital services like general
after-school tuition, specifically benefiting Belmont families who currently have to travel into central
Sutton for these activities.
6. Sustainable Transport, Environmental Impact, and Waste Management
The site benefits from excellent public transport accessibility due to its close proximity to Belmont
Railway Station and highly connected local bus networks. Specifically, the S2 bus runs directly via
Dorset Road-passing the secondary gate of Avenue Primary School and routing through York
Road-while the S4 service travels along Holland Avenue, traversing the Dorset Road junction
before continuing via Avenue Road to its final destination. This exceptional connectivity, alongside
dedicated cycle stores, actively promotes sustainable travel.
Furthermore, the dedicated refuse stores align perfectly with the Sutton borough objective of
waste recycling, ensuring that segregated waste can be processed into green, usable products.
The modernisation of this building also presents the opportunity to deliver an eco-friendly facility
utilising modern, solar-powered components, delivering a significantly reduced carbon load
compared to the previous 24/7 ambulance depot.
7. Regeneration of a Redundant Site, Visual Amenity, and Local Character
Rather than leaving a vacant commercial building that could fall into disrepair, this proposal brings
a brownfield site back into active, positive use. The new design blends perfectly into the ambiance
of the neighbouring properties. Upgrading the current utilitarian structure with the proposed part-
one and part-two storey extensions will modernise the site without compromising the existing local
architecture, positively contributing to the visual amenity and overall streetscape of Dorset Road.
8. Protection of Residential Amenity (Compared to Existing Use)
When assessing potential noise or traffic impacts on neighbouring properties, it is highly material
to compare the proposal against the site's existing established use. As a Sui Generis Ambulance
Station, the site inherently accommodates 24/7 operations, emergency sirens, large vehicle
movements, and unsociable shift patterns. A transition to an F1/F2 Community Hub will introduce
more structured, predictable hours of operation, resulting in a net-reduction in 24/7 noise and
vehicular disruption for the immediate residential neighbours.
Conclusion
This proposal turns an obsolete building into a vibrant, multi-functional community asset. Whether
it is supporting Sutton's major academy trusts, hosting local 11-plus tuition and 6th form study
groups, providing vital backup capacity for the Thomas Wall Centre, facilitating local NHS and
Royal Marsden support groups, or simply reducing the 24/7 disruption of the previous ambulance
depot, this development represents a massive net-benefit to Belmont and the wider borough.
I strongly urge the planning committee and officers to recommend this application for approval.
Yours faithfully
Dr Alamu
Local Resident (SM2, I live within the vicinity of the proposed site )

Name: Mr Avdhesh Chauhan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to the proposed planning application on the following grounds:
The National Planning Policy Framework (December 2024) requires developments to promote
social interaction and healthy communities, and to ensure they do not undermine community
cohesion (paras 96 and related). This should be considered. This proposal risks the opposite.
The borough needs neutral, multi-use community spaces (e.g. halls, youth centres, recreational
venues) that serve residents of all backgrounds and faiths equally, rather than a specialised facility
for one group. Prioritising shared infrastructure would better foster genuine unity.
- The planning proposal will further hugely worsen the already deteriorated parking and traffic
situation on daily basis on surrounding roads. Residents in this area already face a tough
challenge due to large primary school and ever busy roads that serve the exit from Sutton South
side to M25 which are shared by local buses S2 and S4. The new planning proposal will severely
accentuate the prevailing traffic, safety, parking and noise situation.
To Summarise, I would kindly request planning team to Reject the new proposed plans of this
application for this facility.

Name: Mrs Shehnaz Shaikh
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I have been a resident of Fairfax Avenue for many years, and as an older member of
the community, I would like to express my support for this proposal.
At this stage of my life, having accessible local spaces where people can come together is
incredibly important. It gives me a chance for gentle exercise, health and wellbeing sessions, and
simple social interaction can make a meaningful difference to older residents like myself. Many
elderly people experience isolation, and a welcoming community centre within walking distance
would provide valuable companionship and engagement.
I am also reassured by the thoughtful planning that has gone into the proposal. The inclusion of
dedicated parking spaces and cycle racks shows that practical considerations have been taken
seriously. This helps ensure that both visitors and neighbours are accommodated responsibly.
I believe the building itself has been designed with sensitivity. It appears modest in scale and in
keeping with the surrounding residential properties, which is important to those of us who value
the character of our area.
Lastly, I see this centre as a positive and considerate addition to the neighbourhood - one that will
support wellbeing, reduce loneliness and strengthen community ties while respecting the local
environment. I therefore fully support the application.

Name: Mrs Dheepa Wijayasingam
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a resident who lives very close to the proposed development site. My
family and I pass through this area several times every day, and I feel strongly that the practical
impact of this proposal on daily life in our neighbourhood has not been fully appreciated.
While I understand the importance of community facilities, I have serious concerns about the
consequences this development would have on road safety, congestion, noise, and the overall
character of our residential area.
First and foremost, traffic is already a significant issue. The roads surrounding the site regularly
become congested, particularly because of Avenue Primary Academy, which has over 1,000
pupils. During school drop-off and pick-up times, the area becomes a bottleneck. It can take
several minutes just to pass through a short stretch of road due to parked cars, vehicles
manoeuvring, and heavy pedestrian movement.
Adding an estimated 70-80 vehicles during peak times, particularly where arrivals and departures
overlap, would inevitably worsen this congestion. The cumulative effect would not just be
inconvenience - it could create dangerous conditions. Increased vehicle circulation, informal
parking, and cars searching for spaces would significantly heighten the risk to children and
pedestrians.
Of particular concern is emergency access. When the road is already gridlocked at school times, it
is difficult to see how ambulances or other emergency vehicles would be able to pass through
quickly. This raises genuine health and safety concerns for residents.
In addition, the proposed capacity of up to 250 people, with activity potentially continuing until
around 11PM, would introduce a level of late-evening noise and disturbance that is entirely
different from the current character of the area. The dispersal of large groups, raised voices, car
doors slamming, engines idling, and vehicles circulating in search of parking spaces would
significantly affect the quiet enjoyment of neighbouring homes.
There are also vulnerable and elderly residents living nearby who may be particularly impacted by
increased noise and ongoing disturbance.
Finally, the scale and visual presence of the proposed building appear out of keeping with the
surrounding residential properties. The development feels disproportionate to the character of the
area and would dominate what is currently a relatively low-key neighbourhood setting.
For all of these reasons - traffic congestion, school safety, emergency access concerns, noise
disturbance, impact on vulnerable residents, and harm to the character of the area - I respectfully
ask that the Council refuse this application.
Thank you for taking the time to consider the views of local residents.

I also appreciate that the centre is in close proximity to my home. Having a community facility

Furthermore, I welcome the parking arrangements and the decision to stagger Friday prayer times

Overall, I believe the centre will be a valuable addition to the area, offering inclusive facilities and

Comment:

These factors reduce operational risk and enhance the likelihood of positive community outcomes.
I particularly welcome the inclusion of health and wellbeing initiatives, educational programmes

Investment in community infrastructure often supports stronger local networks and more resilient

consideration of accessibility and sustainable transport - important factors in ensuring responsible

From a governance standpoint, clear planning and structured operations reduce uncertainty and
support long-term community value. The proposal appears to balance community benefit with
