Name: S Bhargava
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to the above planning application. While the provision of
community facilities is generally supported in principle, this specific proposal fails to meet the
stringent requirements for community cohesion and social integration set out in the National
Planning Policy Framework (NPPF) December 2024 and supported by the findings of the 2016
Casey Review.
1. Failure to Meet NPPF (2024) Requirements for Social Interaction
Under Paragraph 96(a) of the NPPF (Dec 2024), planning decisions must aim to achieve healthy,
inclusive, and safe places that "promote social interaction, including opportunities for meetings
between people who might not otherwise come into contact with each other."
The current proposal is designed as a specialized, single-use faith facility. By its nature and scale,
it does not facilitate the broad-spectrum social mixing required by the Framework. Instead of
creating a "shared space," it risks creating a "closed space." Furthermore, Paragraph 98 requires
that planning for shared spaces must benefit "all sections of the community." Given that this
proposal serves a narrow demographic, it fails the "all sections" test.
2. Risk of Social Segregation (The Casey Review)
The Casey Review (2016), which remains a cornerstone of UK integration policy, warns explicitly
against the formation of "parallel lives." The Review highlights how concentrated faith facilities can
accelerate residential clustering and reduce everyday mixing between different ethnic and
religious groups.
The proposed development, due to its [size/intensity of use], risks consolidating a faith-based
enclave in this part of Sutton. This directly contradicts the Borough's duty to foster a cohesive
community where residents from different backgrounds interact regularly. The Casey Review
argues that integration is a "two-way street"; this proposal, however, represents a "one-way" use
of land that offers little to the wider non-aligned community.
3. Disproportionality Relative to Local Need (2021 Census)
According to the 2021 Census data for Sutton, the religious landscape of the borough is highly
diverse but also significantly secular:
Christian: 45.8%
No Religion: 32.3%
Muslim: 7.0%
Hindu: 7.0%
With nearly one-third (32.3%) of Sutton's population identifying as having "No Religion," there is a
demonstrable and greater strategic need for neutral, secular community spaces that serve the
entire population. Granting a large-scale, single-faith facility for a demographic representing 7% of
the borough is disproportionate, especially if that facility does not provide significant, guaranteed
access for the 93% of residents who do not share that faith. In addition there are already faith
worship places (6 in total) available in the borough)
The proposal in its current form undermines the "Cohesion" mandate of the NPPF 2024 and
ignores the integration warnings of the Casey Review. It fails to reflect the actual demographic
needs of Sutton as evidenced by the 2021 Census. I urge the Council to refuse this application
and instead prioritize land uses that offer genuine, inclusive interaction for all residents.

Name: Mrs S Siddiqui
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application

Name: Mrs Vanita Sharma
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to the proposed planning application on the following grounds:
The National Planning Policy Framework (December 2024) requires developments to promote
social interaction and healthy communities, and to ensure they do not undermine community
cohesion (paras 96 and related). This should be considered. This proposal risks the opposite.
The borough needs neutral, multi-use community spaces (e.g. halls, youth centres, recreational
venues) that serve residents of all backgrounds and faiths equally, rather than a specialised facility
for one group. Prioritising shared infrastructure would better foster genuine unity.
The borough already has multiple functional/operational mosques in operation.
This appears significantly disproportionate relative to the local Muslim population (based on
census/demographic data).
At the same time, other faith groups - with similar proportion of population - have no dedicated
place of worship at all.
Further provision for one group raises serious concerns about fairness, equitable resource
allocation, and balanced treatment of all faith communities.
A fact-finding exercise should be done to check proportion of local population of different faiths
and how many places of worship already exist before approving any new ones.
UK policy has moved away from failed multiculturalism towards active integration and assimilation.
The Casey Review (2016) warned of "parallel lives," residential clustering, and segregation risks in
some communities.
Additional large or concentrated faith facilities frequently become focal points that encourage
clustering, leading within a few years to enclaves with:
- reduced everyday mixing between groups
- diminished shared British identity
- the very ghettoisation the Review cautioned against.
An example is in nearby Morden where a big mosque has seen local demography change in last
decade.
In our beautiful borough, already experiencing demographic pressures and change, approving
another mosque is likely to entrench separation rather than build inclusive cohesion - directly
contrary to NPPF goals for unified, integrated places.

Name: Mr Suresh Rajasekaran
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
My objection is based on the following material planning grounds:
1. Lack of Demonstrable Need
There are already several established community centres and halls operating within the SM2
postcode and surrounding Belmont area. These venues provide space for meetings, classes,
worship, and community activities.
No clear evidence has been presented to demonstrate an unmet need for an additional facility of
this scale.The proposal represents avoidable overprovision that would place further strain on local
roads and residential amenity.
2. Traffic and Highway Safety
Dorset Road and the surrounding streets are quiet residential roads that are not designed to
accommodate significant increases in traffic. A place of worship and community hub is likely to
generate concentrated vehicle movements, particularly during evenings and weekends. Events,
gatherings and organised activities may result in simultaneous arrivals and departures, creating
congestion and increased risk to pedestrians.
There are schools in close proximity to the site. Additional traffic during school drop-off and pick-
up times would raise serious highway safety concerns. Overspill parking on surrounding
residential roads is highly likely if on-site provision proves insufficient, potentially obstructing
driveways, narrowing carriageways and restricting emergency access.
3. Impact on Residential Amenity
The proposed use represents a substantial intensification compared to the former ambulance
station. The regular congregation of large groups, combined with evening and weekend activities,
would materially alter the quiet character of the area. Extended hours of use could lead to
disturbance during evenings and weekends, undermining the peaceful residential environment
currently enjoyed.
4. Scale, Bulk and Design
The proposal includes part one- and part two-storey extensions and an increase in height to part
of the building. This raises concerns regarding over development of the site and visual impact on
the street scene. The increased scale and massing may appear out of keeping with the
surrounding residential character and could result in overshadowing or loss of outlook for
neighboring properties.

It is clear that the positives far outweigh the negatives and are in line with planning policies.
There are a number of objections from people far away but as someone who will live opposite the

Comment:
I am writing as a resident of Holland Avenue and a mother of two children to express my

Name: Mr awanish kumar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Re: Planning Application DM2025/01591 - Objection
I formally object to this application on highway safety and traffic grounds.
1. Traffic Congestion & Highway Safety
The proposed change of use to a Place of Worship and Community Hub will materially increase
vehicle movements, particularly during concentrated service/event times. Dorset Road and
surrounding residential streets already experience peak congestion. The proposal risks:
Increased queuing and unsafe turning movements
Illegal or obstructive parking
Reduced emergency vehicle access
No robust Transport Assessment has demonstrated that the local road network can safely
accommodate this uplift.
2. Proximity to Avenue Primary School - Child Safety Risk
Avenue Primary School is located within approximately 1km and shares the same local access
routes. Peak worship/event times are likely to overlap with school drop-off and pick-up periods.
This creates foreseeable risks:
Conflict between high pedestrian volumes (children) and additional vehicle traffic
Unsafe drop-off behaviour
Increased road-crossing hazards
The application does not adequately address safeguarding of vulnerable road users.
3. Lack of Parking & Traffic Mitigation
There is insufficient clarity on:
Dedicated parking provision
Managed drop-off zones
Event traffic management strategy
Controlled dispersal plans
Without enforceable mitigation, the proposal would result in unacceptable highway and pedestrian
safety impacts.
Conclusion
Given the significant unresolved highway safety concerns - particularly regarding children - I
request that Sutton Council refuse the application unless a full Transport Assessment and
enforceable mitigation strategy clearly demonstrate no adverse impact on traffic flow or child
safety.

Name: Mr Sreedhar Ravula
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to object to the proposed conversion of the former ambulance station into a
religious centre. While I recognize the value of community facilities, the specific nature of this site
and its location within a quiet residential area make this change of use inappropriate for the
following reasons:
Impact on Residential Amenity (Noise and Disturbance): Unlike its previous use as an emergency
service base (which had intermittent and predictable activity), a religious centre typically generates
concentrated periods of high activity, often during evenings and weekends when residents expect
quiet. The noise from large gatherings, arrivals/departures, and amplified sound would cause
significant "noise nuisance" to the immediate neighbors.
Highways Safety and Parking Pressure: The surrounding residential streets are narrow and
already experience high parking demand. A religious centre attracts a large number of visitors
simultaneously. The current site does not appear to have sufficient off-street parking to
accommodate a full congregation, which will lead to overspill parking on residential roads, blocking
driveways and creating hazards for emergency access and pedestrians.
Change in Character of the Area: This is a quiet, established residential neighborhood. The
intensification of use-transforming a low-occupancy service building into a high-capacity public
assembly point-is a significant "material change of use" that is inconsistent with the tranquil
character of the area defined in the Sutton Local Plan.
Inadequate Access: The access points designed for ambulance vehicles may not be safe for the
high volume of pedestrian traffic, including families and children, that a community centre attracts,
leading to potential road safety issues.
I request that the Planning Committee conducts a full Noise Impact Assessment and a Transport
Statement before any decision is made, and I urge the Council to refuse this application to protect
the living conditions of the local residents.

Name: Mr Pagdalu Rajat Naidu
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to this application on the grounds of highway safety and traffic
impact.
1. Traffic Congestion and Road Safety
The proposed change of use to a Place of Worship and Community Hub is likely to generate a
substantial increase in vehicle movements, particularly during peak service and event times.
Dorset Road and the surrounding residential streets already experience congestion during busy
periods. The development is therefore likely to result in:
Longer queues and hazardous turning manoeuvres
Illegal or inconsiderate parking
Restricted access for emergency vehicles
No comprehensive Transport Assessment has been provided to demonstrate that the existing
road network can safely accommodate this additional traffic.
2. Proximity to Avenue Primary School - Risk to Children
Avenue Primary School is situated within approximately 1km of the site and relies on the same
local access routes. Service and event peak times are likely to coincide with school drop-off and
pick-up periods.
This overlap creates foreseeable safety concerns, including:
Increased conflict between higher volumes of vehicles and child pedestrians
Unsafe drop-off and pick-up practices
Greater risks for children crossing roads
The application fails to adequately address the protection of vulnerable road users.
3. Insufficient Parking and Traffic Management Measures
There is a lack of detail regarding:
Provision of dedicated parking spaces
Managed drop-off and pick-up arrangements
A clear event traffic management plan
Controlled dispersal procedures after services or events
In the absence of firm and enforceable mitigation measures, the proposal would lead to
unacceptable impacts on both highway safety and pedestrian welfare.
Conclusion
In light of the significant and unresolved highway safety concerns - particularly those affecting
children - I respectfully request that Sutton Council refuse this application unless a full Transport
Assessment and binding mitigation strategy clearly demonstrate that there will be no detrimental
effect on traffic conditions or child safety.

Name: Mr Salhin Langhi
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Re: Planning Application DM2025/01591
I am writing to express my strong support for Planning Application DM2025/01591.
I am 28 years old and have grown up in Belmont, spending my childhood and early adult life in the
immediate vicinity of this site. I attended The Avenue Primary School and later Overton Grange
School, and much of my upbringing was rooted in this neighbourhood. Having lived here for nearly
three decades, with close family still based locally, I remain deeply connected to the area and
continue to participate in community life. This longstanding connection gives me a clear
understanding of both the character of Belmont and the needs of its residents.
From my perspective as someone who grew up locally, a community-focused facility of this kind
would have been incredibly beneficial to many young people in the area. The proposed change of
use from the former ambulance station (Sui Generis) to a mixed-use facility under Classes F1
(Place of Worship) and F2 (Community Hub) represents a thoughtful and positive evolution of the
site. Rather than remaining underutilised, the building would become a place that actively serves
and brings together the local community.
The plans outline a range of clear social benefits, including wellbeing initiatives, youth education
programmes, and wider community activities accessible to all residents. Of particular importance
is the provision of dedicated space and structured activities for women, offering a secure and
supportive environment for engagement and personal development. The youth-focused elements
are equally valuable, providing mentorship, guidance, and constructive opportunities that can help
foster confidence, discipline, and stronger community ties.
In terms of design and scale, the proposed extensions appear proportionate and respectful of the
surrounding residential context. The part one, part two-storey additions and modest height
increase do not seem excessive for the plot and are consistent with the character of nearby
buildings. It is also worth noting that the intensity of use associated with this proposal is
comparatively modest when considering alternative redevelopment possibilities for the site.
Traffic and parking considerations have clearly been addressed within the application. The
inclusion of 12 on-site parking spaces, including disabled bays, alongside cycle storage and
encouragement of sustainable travel, reflects careful planning. The proposed staggering of Friday
prayer times to avoid overlap with nearby school pick-up periods demonstrates awareness of local
patterns and a willingness to mitigate potential congestion. Overall, I do not believe the
development would create unreasonable levels of noise, traffic, or disturbance beyond what is
typical for a community facility.
I am also pleased to see that landscaping and greenery are being retained, ensuring the
development integrates sympathetically into the surrounding streetscape rather than appearing
overly dense.
Having seen Belmont evolve over the years, I believe this proposal would provide meaningful,
long-term benefits. It would offer structured support for young people, inclusive space for residents
of all backgrounds, and opportunities that strengthen connections across the community.
For these reasons, I respectfully urge the Council to approve Planning Application DM2025/01591.

Address: 1 Sandringham Court 11 Dorset Road Belmont SM2 6NG
Name: Ms Victoria France
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to submit an objection to the proposed development at the former ambulance
station site on the grounds of clarity of use, potential intensification of activity, and the resulting
impact on traffic, parking, residential amenity, and inclusive community access.
When I attended the Open Day, the proposal was presented primarily as a community centre with
allocated times for prayer. Based on this, I understood that the community centre would operate in
a similar way to the community centre I currently attend, where rooms are hired out for small
classes and activities typically involving around four or five people per session, usually generating
only two or three cars at a time. On that basis, I viewed the proposal positively and supported the
idea of a genuine community hub. My understanding at the time was that community use would
form the principal function of the building, with prayer use incorporated alongside it.
However, following discussions within the community and my own research regarding BASMA, I
have become concerned that there will be significantly larger numbers of people attending
activities and prayers than I had originally understood. I acknowledge that regular prayer use,
including larger Friday prayers, was discussed at the Open Day; however, while it was indicated
that most attendees would walk from surrounding streets, the locations of people submitting
supporting comments indicate that many attendees live outside the immediate local area and will
therefore be travelling to the site by car, increasing car movements and parking demand beyond
what would normally be expected of a neighbourhood community facility.
There is also concern that the building's future use may evolve to operate primarily as a mosque
and place of worship rather than remaining a mixed-use community facility. This distinction is
important from a planning perspective because differing primary uses can generate materially
different patterns and intensities of activity.
The surrounding area already experiences significant congestion during school drop-off and
collection periods, and any increase in concentrated attendance could exacerbate existing traffic
pressures, parking demand, and associated disturbance within what is otherwise a quiet
residential environment. In addition, experience from other mixed-use applications indicates that
community-use elements can diminish over time once permission for a place of worship has been
established. While this may not be the applicant's intention, planning decisions should consider
long-term operational outcomes as well as initial proposals, and it is therefore important that the
intended primary use, anticipated attendance levels, traffic impacts, and the balance between
community and worship use are clearly defined and, where necessary, secured through
appropriate planning conditions to prevent future intensification beyond what has currently been
presented to residents.
I would also ask the Council to consider how equal and inclusive access to the proposed
community facilities will be ensured, particularly with regard to women's participation. My
understanding from discussions with individuals familiar with the organisation, together with
publicly available material such as event photographs, is that participation in some existing
activities appears to be predominantly male. While I fully respect religious practice, a development
described and supported as a community facility should demonstrably serve the whole local
community on an equal basis. It is therefore important that women have meaningful, regular, and
equivalent access to community spaces and activities, and that the community-use element
operates in a genuinely inclusive manner consistent with equality expectations for publicly
accessible community facilities.

2. Addressing Social Isolation (Policy 25): At my age, easy access to local community facilities is
essential for my mental and physical wellbeing. This centre will provide a "lifetime neighbourhood"
hub where I can meet others, avoiding the isolation that many seniors in SM2 face.

that many of us intend to access the site on foot or by local bus. Unlike the previous ambulance
use, the centre's arrivals are predictable and will be managed by marshals to ensure no driveways
are ever blocked.
4. Equality and Inclusion: Under the Public Sector Equality Duty, the Council has a responsibility to
support facilities that serve minority groups and the elderly.
Approval of this site would demonstrate Sutton's commitment to an inclusive community.
I urge the Council to approve this application so that we can bring this building back into a use that
truly serves the residents of Belmont and Sutton.
Yours faithfully,
Mrs Selmah Tulsidas

Address: Flat 68 Sutton Park House 15 Carshalton Road Sutton SM1 4GN
Name: Mr Khalid Langhi
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing in strong support of Planning Application DM2025/01591.
I am a lifelong local resident who grew up in Belmont, specifically on Balmoral Way (SM2 6PD),
where the former ambulance station was quite literally visible from my bedroom window. Although
I have recently moved nearby, I remain closely connected to the area, with my parents and
siblings still living adjacent to the site. I continue to attend and walk to community events in
Belmont, including weekly congregations at St John's Community Hall. I am now 26 years old and
have lived in this area my entire life, attending The Avenue Primary School and Overton Grange
School for my secondary and sixth form studies.
Having grown up here as a young person, I strongly feel that a community hub of this nature would
have been a valuable asset to many of us. The proposal to change the use from Sui Generis
(Ambulance Station) to a mixed-use facility under Classes F1 (Place of Worship) and F2
(Community Hub) presents a meaningful opportunity to further strengthen what is already a
supportive and close-knit community.
The proposed development offers clear community benefits, including health and wellbeing
programmes, educational activities for children, and inclusive facilities that serve the wider
community regardless of religion. The dedicated space and activities for women are particularly
important to me personally, as I want my mother, sister and wife to feel comfortable, welcomed
and supported within their own local community. Providing a safe and inclusive environment for
women to gather, learn and access support is a significant benefit of this proposal. The
educational programmes for young people will offer constructive outlets, mentorship and guidance,
which are essential for youth development and community cohesion.
In terms of scale and design, the building remains modest and in keeping with the character of the
surrounding area. The part one, part two-storey extension and height increase appear
proportionate and appropriate for the site. Importantly, this proposal represents a relatively low-
intensity use compared to other potential forms of redevelopment that could occur on this site.
Concerns around traffic and congestion have clearly been considered. The provision of 12 on-site
parking spaces, including 2 disabled bays, demonstrates thoughtful planning. In addition, cycle
racks and sustainable travel provisions encourage environmentally responsible access. The
staggering of Friday prayer times to avoid conflict with nearby school pick-up hours is a
responsible and proactive measure that reflects genuine engagement with the local context. I do
not believe the proposal will result in significant traffic, noise or light disturbance beyond what
would reasonably be expected for a community facility.
It is also worth noting that the site previously operated as a working ambulance station. Growing
up, I regularly witnessed flashing lights and heard sirens at all hours of the day and night. In
comparison, this proposed community use would be far less disruptive than the historic
emergency service operations that once took place there.
I also welcome the retention of greenery on the site, ensuring that the development integrates
respectfully into its surroundings rather than appearing overdeveloped.
Having witnessed Belmont grow from a quieter residential area into a thriving and supportive
neighbourhood, I believe this proposal would be a positive uplift for the area. It will provide
structure, guidance and opportunity for young people, create inclusive community space, and
strengthen social ties across generations.
For these reasons, I respectfully ask the Council to approve Planning Application DM2025/01591.

Name: Mr Rashid Khan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a nearby and local resident of SM2 5NQ, a postcode in very close
proximity to the application site, to express my wholehearted and unreserved support for the
proposed Belmont and Sutton Muslim Assembly (BASMA) community facility at the former Sutton
Ambulance Station on Dorset Road, Sutton.
I am a local resident with deep roots in this community. My wife works locally in the SM2 area, and
our children attend Avenue Primary School and Harris Academy, both within this community. Our
household is entirely embedded in and dependent upon the health, cohesion and vibrancy of this
local community. It is from this perspective, as a parent, a neighbour, and a local stakeholder, that
I offer this letter of support.
From reading the Transport Statement and Travel Plan, I am reassured that traffic and parking
have been carefully considered. The proposal provides 14 onsite car spaces (including disabled
and EV bays) and secure cycle parking, and commits to marshalled onsite parking management
and promotion of walking, carsharing and public transport. Surveys show that most existing
BASMA users already walk, with over half of Friday attendees arriving on foot and only a minority
driving. Parking beat surveys in nearby streets recorded daytime utilisation at under 50%, even on
Fridays, indicating spare capacity and that overspill parking is unlikely to be severe.
As a parent, schoolrun safety is paramount to me. I welcome the clear commitment to stagger
Friday prayer times so they do not clash with Avenue dropoff and pickup, and to use stewards to
supervise vehicle movements during the busiest periods, so Dorset Road will not be affected. The
hailandride S2 and S4 bus services and the short walk from Belmont Station also mean many
visitors can come without a car, consistent with Sutton Local Plan Policies 35-37 on sustainable
travel and appropriate parking.
The proposal reuses an existing brownfield public service site with a modest extension, improved
landscaping, cycle parking and onsite car parking. Technical reports show that most existing users
already walk, that parking stress on surrounding streets is well below capacity, and that staggered
Friday prayer times and marshalled onsite parking will avoid conflict with school dropoff and
pickup. Noise assessment work demonstrates that activities inside a wellinsulated, singlestorey
building will remain below existing background levels and will be quieter than the former 24hour
ambulance use.
Safe Spaces and Youth Programmes:
--------------------------------------
As a father of my children growing up in Sutton, I am acutely aware of the lack of structured, safe,
after-school spaces for young people in our area. The proposed BASMA facility would provide
exactly the kind of safe, supervised, and nurturing environment that young people need,
particularly in the SM2/Belmont corridor where provision is limited. Youth programmes delivered
through community facilities of this kind have a well-evidenced impact on diverting young people
from anti-social behaviour, building confidence, and fostering a sense of belonging. My children,
along with their classmates from Avenue and Harris, would directly benefit from having structured
youth activities within walking distance of home and school.
Education and Structured Learning:
-------------------------------------
The facility proposes to operate under Use Class F1 (Learning and Non-Residential Institutions),
which reflects BASMA's explicit commitment to education. not only religious education, but wider
social learning, literacy, and life skills programming. As a parent who cares deeply about my
children's educational enrichment beyond the school day, I strongly welcome a community
resource that provides structured educational activities in a local, accessible and trusted setting.
Educational community centres of this kind form part of the wider educational ecosystem that
complements schools like Avenue and Harris Academy.
Wellbeing, Women's Support and Elderly Engagement:
--------------------------------------------------------
My wife, who works locally, is one of many women in our community who would benefit from the
wellbeing and support networks that BASMA intends to provide. Women's support initiatives,
health and wellbeing sessions, and informal peer networks are genuinely life-changing for
residents who may be isolated, working long hours, or managing caring responsibilities. Similarly,
our community has a significant elderly population who would benefit enormously from regular,
accessible engagement activities, reducing loneliness and social isolation in a borough where
these are recognised public health challenges.
Inter-Community Interaction and Social Cohesion:
---------------------------------------------------
I wish to specifically address the concerns raised by some local residents about this facility
creating division. I believe strongly that the opposite is true. Community facilities run by well-
established charities like BASMA, which has operated Friday prayers at St John's Community Hall
on Northdown Road for several years with no adverse impact whatsoever on neighbours, serve as
bridges between communities, not barriers. A permanent, well-designed, purpose-fit facility
enables BASMA to open its doors to the wider community for inter-faith dialogue, shared events,
and cross-community partnerships. A formal facility is far more capable of hosting such inter-
community interaction than a temporary hired hall.
3 halls are proposed - 1 for ladies, 1 for prayers and 1 for multi-use which is for all communities
There will be a publicly available defibrillator which is a vital life saving device.
Addressing Loneliness and Isolation:
--------------------------------------
The borough's own public health data recognises loneliness and isolation as significant challenges
in Sutton. Community facilities that provide structured activities, social clubs, hobby groups,
support circles, classes, are among the most cost-effective public health interventions. The
proposed BASMA centre, with its range of planned social programmes, directly addresses this.
The repurposing of a vacant former ambulance station into an active community hub is exactly the
type of development the Sutton Local Plan's social infrastructure policies are designed to
encourage and protect.
Planning Merits:
-----------------
From a planning perspective, I note that the proposal is for a change of use and refurbishment, not
new build. The existing structure is already well-established on Dorset Road. The comprehensive
suite of supporting documents submitted, including a Transport Statement, Parking Survey, Noise
Assessment, Fire Strategy, and Biodiversity Net Gain Assessment, demonstrates the
thoroughness and professionalism of this application. I urge the Planning Authority to give full
weight to these technical submissions when assessing the application.
I respectfully urge the Planning Authority to grant planning permission for this application. This
facility will make a genuine, lasting, positive difference to the lives of residents like me, my family,
and our neighbours in SM2 and across the Sutton Borough.
Yours faithfully,
Rashid Khan

Name: Ms Shabnam Dutta

Name: Mr Ash Garg
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to the proposed planning application on the following grounds:
The National Planning Policy Framework (December 2024) requires developments to promote
social interaction and healthy communities, and to ensure they do not undermine community
cohesion (paras 96 and related). This should be considered. This proposal risks the opposite.
The borough needs neutral, multi-use community spaces (e.g. halls, youth centres, recreational
venues) that serve residents of all backgrounds and faiths equally, rather than a specialised facility
for one group. Prioritising shared infrastructure would better foster genuine unity.
The borough already has multiple functional/operational mosques in operation.
This appears significantly disproportionate relative to the local Muslim population (based on
census/demographic data).
At the same time, other faith groups - with similar proportion of population - have no dedicated
place of worship at all.
Further provision for one group raises serious concerns about fairness, equitable resource
allocation, and balanced treatment of all faith communities.
A fact-finding exercise should be done to check proportion of local population of different faiths
and how many places of worship already exist before approving any new ones.
UK policy has moved away from failed multiculturalism towards active integration and assimilation.
The Casey Review (2016) warned of "parallel lives," residential clustering, and segregation risks in
some communities.
Additional large or concentrated faith facilities frequently become focal points that encourage
clustering, leading within a few years to enclaves with:
- reduced everyday mixing between groups
- diminished shared British identity
- the very ghettoisation the Review cautioned against.
An example is in nearby Morden where a big mosque has seen local demography change in last
decade.
In our beautiful borough, already experiencing demographic pressures and change, approving
another mosque is likely to entrench separation rather than build inclusive cohesion - directly
contrary to NPPF goals for unified, integrated places.

Name: Mrs Abira Rashid
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my strong support for the above planning application. As a
woman who lives and works locally in the SM2/Sutton area, and as a mother of my children
attending local schools, I write from a perspective that is both professional and deeply personal. I
am a resident of SM2 5NQ, and this proposed facility, if approved, will directly serve my family and
our wider community.
From reading the Transport Statement and Travel Plan, I am reassured that traffic and parking
have been carefully considered. The proposal provides 14 onsite car spaces (including disabled
and EV bays) and secure cycle parking, and commits to marshalled onsite parking management
and promotion of walking, carsharing and public transport. Surveys show that most existing
BASMA users already walk, with over half of Friday attendees arriving on foot and only a minority
driving. Parking beat surveys in nearby streets recorded daytime utilisation at under 50%, even on
Fridays, indicating spare capacity and that overspill parking is unlikely to be severe.
As a parent, schoolrun safety is paramount to me. I welcome the clear commitment to stagger
Friday prayer times so they do not clash with Avenue dropoff and pickup, and to use stewards to
supervise vehicle movements during the busiest periods, so Dorset Road will not be affected. The
hailandride S2 and S4 bus services and the short walk from Belmont Station also mean many
visitors can come without a car, consistent with Sutton Local Plan Policies 35-37 on sustainable
travel and appropriate parking.
The proposal reuses an existing brownfield public service site with a modest extension, improved
landscaping, cycle parking and onsite car parking. Technical reports show that most existing users
already walk, that parking stress on surrounding streets is well below capacity, and that staggered
Friday prayer times and marshalled onsite parking will avoid conflict with school dropoff and
pickup. Noise assessment work demonstrates that activities inside a wellinsulated, singlestorey
building will remain below existing background levels and will be quieter than the former 24hour
ambulance use.
Women's Support and Wellbeing Initiatives:
---------------------------------------------
As a working mother managing the demands of employment, childcare, and family life, I know
from personal experience how difficult it is to find time and accessible spaces for personal
wellbeing. The proposed BASMA facility has explicitly committed to delivering women-focused
support programmes and wellbeing initiatives. These are not peripheral add-ons, they are a vital
community service. Many women in our area, particularly those from South Asian and Muslim
backgrounds, face social isolation, language barriers, and lack of culturally appropriate support
services. A community centre of this kind, managed by a well-respected charity with a proven
track record in Sutton, provides the trusted, accessible, local resource that fills a genuine gap.
Safe Spaces for Children and Structured Activities:
-----------------------------------------------------
As a parent of children at Avenue Primary and Harris Academy, I am keenly aware that after-
school provision in the SM2 area is stretched and in high demand. The proposed community
centre would provide supervised, structured after-school activities for children in a safe and
stimulating environment. Youth clubs, homework clubs, sports sessions, arts activities, all of these
can be housed within a facility like this. This is particularly important for children growing up in a
diverse area: structured activities in inclusive, welcoming spaces build social skills, resilience, and
community bonds that last a lifetime.
Education Beyond the Classroom:
-----------------------------------
I work in a professional capacity and understand the value of lifelong learning. The F1 use class
proposed for this facility encompasses educational programmes that go well beyond religious
instruction. Adult education, English language classes, digital literacy workshops, and vocational
guidance can all be delivered from this site. For women who may have paused careers, for new
arrivals to the borough, and for young people bridging school and employment, these programmes
are transformative. I strongly welcome the educational dimension of this proposal.
Elderly Engagement and Combating Loneliness:
--------------------------------------------------
My own experience of caring for older relatives has made me acutely aware of the loneliness
epidemic among elderly residents. Our area has a significant elderly population, many of whom
live alone and lack regular social contact. Community facilities that offer regular, welcoming,
accessible social programmes, coffee mornings, health talks, gentle exercise classes, social clubs,
are invaluable in addressing loneliness and social isolation. The proposed BASMA facility is
perfectly positioned to serve this role in the SM2 and Belmont community.
Inter-Community Interaction:
------------------------------
I want to speak directly to concerns about whether this facility serves all communities. BASMA has
operated Friday prayers at St John's Community Hall on Northdown Road for several years, with
no disruption to neighbours, no community tension, and no adverse impact on the local area. A
permanent, purpose-fit facility at Dorset Road will allow BASMA to build on this track record and to
open its doors more widely, hosting community events, inter-faith activities, and public
programmes that benefit all residents regardless of background. Diversity of community provision
strengthens a borough; it does not weaken it.
3 halls are proposed - 1 for ladies, 1 for prayers and 1 for multi-use which is for all communities
There will be a publicly available defibrillator which is a vital life saving device.
Support for People Who May Be Lonely or Isolated:
-----------------------------------------------------
The postcode SM2 5NQ, like much of the Belmont area, contains a significant proportion of
residents who are at risk of social isolation: elderly people, new mothers, recently arrived families,
people with disabilities, and those dealing with mental health challenges. Community facilities like
the proposed BASMA centre are among the most powerful tools available to local communities to
address these issues outside of statutory services. I urge the Planning Authority to recognise the
substantial public benefit this facility will deliver.
I offer my wholehearted support for this application and respectfully request that the Planning
Authority grant planning permission.
Yours faithfully,
Abira Rashid

Name: Mr Muhammad Azeem Khan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am a student currently residing at an address in SM2 5NQ and write to express my
strong support for the proposed BASMA community facility at the former Sutton Ambulance
Station on Dorset Road. As a young person living in this community, I write with a particular
perspective on what this facility would mean for residents of my generation, and for the long-term
future of this neighbourhood.
The proposal reuses an existing brownfield public service site with a modest extension, improved
landscaping, cycle parking and onsite car parking. Technical reports show that most existing users
already walk, that parking stress on surrounding streets is well below capacity, and that staggered
Friday prayer times and marshalled onsite parking will avoid conflict with school dropoff and
pickup. Noise assessment work demonstrates that activities inside a wellinsulated, singlestorey
building will remain below existing background levels and will be quieter than the former 24hour
ambulance use.
Youth Programmes and Structured Activities:
-----------------------------------------------
As a student, I have seen firsthand how critical structured, accessible, and affordable activities are
for young people in Sutton. The provision of youth programmes, whether educational workshops,
mentoring, sports, arts, or social clubs, gives young people direction, builds confidence, and
creates the kind of community bonds that help individuals thrive. The SM2 and Belmont area has a
growing young population, yet community provision specifically designed for young people is
severely limited. The proposed BASMA facility, with its explicit focus on youth engagement, fills
this gap directly. I would personally benefit from and enthusiastically participate in the programmes
this centre aims to deliver.
Education and Supporting Students:
-------------------------------------
Students like me often require support that goes beyond university or college provision, mentoring,
study spaces, career guidance, and peer networks. A community facility that offers educational
programming and structured learning support is invaluable to young people navigating higher
education, employment applications, and professional development. I am especially supportive of
the Use Class F1 (Learning and Non-Residential Institutions) dimension of this proposal, which
reflects the centre's ambition to be a genuine educational hub for the whole community.
Wellbeing and Mental Health:
------------------------------
Student life is not without its pressures, and isolation during periods of study is a growing concern
nationally. A local community centre within walking distance provides an accessible, welcoming
environment where young people can take a break, socialise, engage in wellbeing activities, and
find peer support. The wellbeing initiatives proposed by BASMA, including fitness sessions and
health programmes already being trialled by the community, are particularly relevant to students
and young adults who often fall through the gaps of formal support services.
Inter-Community Interaction and Social Cohesion:
----------------------------------------------------
I am part of a generation that genuinely values diversity, mutual understanding, and cross-
community dialogue. I believe that community centres run by established charities like BASMA,
which has served this community with professionalism and care since 2020, are among the most
effective platforms for fostering inter-community interaction. A permanent, well-resourced facility
on Dorset Road would allow BASMA to host open events, cultural exchanges, and community
partnerships that reach well beyond its own congregation, strengthening the social fabric of the
entire SM2 area.
3 halls are proposed - 1 for ladies, 1 for prayers and 1 for multi-use which is for all communities
There will be a publicly available defibrillator which is a vital life saving device.
Supporting People Who May Be Lonely or Isolated:
-----------------------------------------------------
Living in a residential area and studying, I see around me many neighbours, particularly elderly
residents and those living alone, who lack regular social contact and structured social
engagement. Community facilities that offer regular, friendly activities are the backbone of
community wellbeing. I fully support the ambition of this facility to be a place where isolated
residents of all ages can find connection, support, and a sense of belonging.
Repurposing a Vacant Site for Community Benefit:
----------------------------------------------------
The former ambulance station on Dorset Road has been vacant since its closure. From a planning
perspective, the repurposing of a redundant civic building into an active community facility is
precisely the kind of sustainable, socially beneficial development that the Sutton Local Plan and
the National Planning Policy Framework encourage. Rather than allowing a community asset to
fall into disuse or be converted to less publicly beneficial uses, this application proposes to breathe
new life into the site in a way that serves thousands of residents.
I write as a young resident and student who has a genuine stake in the future of this community. I
urge the Planning Authority to grant planning permission for this application, which will deliver
lasting benefits for people of all ages, backgrounds, and walks of life in Sutton.
Yours faithfully,
Azeem Khan

Name: Mrs Aruna GUPTA
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to raise a objection to the proposed planning application on the following grounds
while respecting the communities to have dedicated spaces for worship.
The National Planning Policy Framework (December 2024) states that development should
support social interaction, promote healthy communities, and avoid harming community cohesion
(see paragraph 96 and related guidance). These principles are highly relevant in this case, and the
current proposal risks undermining them rather than supporting them.
There are already several established and operational mosques within the borough. When
considered alongside available census and demographic information, the level of provision
appears disproportionately high in comparison to the size of the local relevant community. In
contrast, other faith groups of similar size do not have equivalent dedicated places of worship.
Approving further provision for one group therefore raises concerns about fairness, balanced
provision of facilities, and equitable treatment across different faith communities. A thorough
review of local demographic data and existing places of worship should be undertaken before any
further decisions are made.
National policy discussions have increasingly emphasised the importance of integration. The
Casey Review highlighted risks such as segregation, residential clustering, and the development
of "parallel lives" in some areas. Large or concentrated single-faith facilities can, in some
circumstances, become focal points that encourage this kind of clustering over time.
In a borough that is already experiencing demographic change and pressure on local
infrastructure, approving an additional large faith facility risks reinforcing separation rather than
strengthening inclusive cohesion. This would run counter to the objectives set out in the National
Planning Policy Framework for creating integrated, inclusive communities.

Name: Ms Kasturi Biswas
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to the proposed conversion of the former Ambulance Station on
Dorset Road into a mosque and community hall.
My concerns are based on material planning considerations as follows:
1. Traffic and Highway Safety
Dorset Road is a narrow residential road that already experiences congestion, particularly during
school drop-off and pick-up times for Avenue Primary Academy. The proposal indicates gatherings
of up to 250 people, which could generate significant additional vehicle movements and parking
demand.
The surrounding roads do not have the capacity to safely accommodate this level of traffic.
Increased on-street parking will reduce visibility at junctions and create risks for pedestrians,
especially children walking to and from school.
2. Impact on School Safety
With over 1,000 pupils attending the nearby primary school, the area is extremely busy at peak
times. Additional event-based traffic will increase the likelihood of dangerous situations, including
blocked pavements, restricted sightlines, and vehicles mounting curbs.
I have personally witnessed congestion on Dorset Road during school hours, and adding further
traffic would significantly worsen an already challenging situation.
3. Noise and Disturbance
Late evening events finishing around 11pm would result in noise from people dispersing, car
engines, doors slamming, and vehicles circulating in search of parking. This would materially harm
the residential amenity of neighbouring homes.
This is a quiet residential area, and the scale of activity proposed is not in keeping with its
character.
4. Overdevelopment and Character of the Area
The proposed extensions and increased building height would create a structure that is visually
dominant and out of keeping with the surrounding domestic properties. The development
represents over-intensification of a constrained site within a residential setting.
For the reasons above, the proposal would result in unacceptable harm to highway safety,
residential amenity, and the character of the area. I respectfully request that the Council refuse
this application.

Name: Mr Swapnil Bhonsle
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I write to formally object to planning application DM2025/01591 for the change of use of the former
ambulance station at 18 Dorset Road to a mixed-use Place of Worship (Class F1) and Community
Hub (Class F2), together with extensions and increased height.
My objection is made on clear material planning grounds, as set out below.
1. Conflict with Policies Protecting Residential Amenity
The application site is located within a predominantly residential area characterised by low-
intensity uses. The proposed development represents a significant intensification of activity,
particularly during evenings and weekends, when religious services and community events
typically occur.
The likely frequency of gatherings, vehicular arrivals/departures, and associated activity would
materially harm neighbouring residential amenity through increased noise, disturbance, general
activity, and light pollution. The absence of detailed operational controls (including enforceable
limits on hours, event frequency, and maximum occupancy) gives rise to substantial uncertainty
regarding the real-world impacts.
Development plan policies require that proposals do not result in unacceptable harm to the living
conditions of neighbouring occupiers. In this case, the scale and nature of the proposed use is
incompatible with the quiet residential character of the immediate area.
2. Severe Impact on Highway Safety and Parking Stress
The application site is not located within a town centre, nor is it supported by infrastructure
appropriate for a high-intensity community destination use. A place of worship and community hub
will predictably generate concentrated peak demand before and after services and events.
Local streets are constrained and already experience on-street parking pressure. The proposal
fails to robustly demonstrate that:
On-site parking provision (if any) is sufficient to meet peak demand;
Overspill parking will not occur in surrounding residential roads;
Pedestrian and vehicle conflict points will not increase; and
Emergency access will remain uncompromised during large gatherings.
Highway safety is a material consideration of significant weight. Where parking demand materially
exceeds supply, and overspill parking is likely, the resulting congestion and obstruction can
constitute sustainable grounds for refusal. The application does not contain a sufficiently detailed
or independently verified transport assessment demonstrating that impacts would be neutral or
acceptable.
3. Unsustainable and Inappropriate Location
Planning policy directs high footfall community uses toward accessible, sustainable locations such
as town centres or sites well served by public transport and designed for visitor accommodation.
This site sits within a residential context without the infrastructure normally associated with a
regional or sub-regional worship facility. The proposal would create a destination venue within a
residential street network not designed to accommodate concentrated visitor flows. This runs
counter to sustainable spatial planning principles and risks creating ongoing land use conflict.
4. Harm to Character and Overdevelopment
The proposal includes physical extensions and increased height in addition to the material change
of use. When combined with intensified activity levels, the cumulative effect is a fundamental shift
in the function and character of the site.
The development would introduce a level of built form and operational intensity inconsistent with
the established pattern of development. This constitutes overdevelopment of the site in practical
planning terms, particularly when assessed against the prevailing residential grain and scale.
5. Absence of Robust Mitigation and Enforcement Mechanisms
The application lacks sufficient detail regarding:
Maximum capacity of the venue;
Frequency and scale of services/events;
Traffic marshals or controlled parking strategies;
Noise management plans;
Servicing and refuse management arrangements;
Mechanisms to prevent ad hoc large-scale events.
Without clearly defined, enforceable conditions supported by evidence, there is a material risk of
incremental intensification beyond what is assessed at application stage. The Planning Authority
must assess the proposal based on its worst-case realistic operational scenario, not aspirational
management statements.
Given the scale of likely impacts and the absence of demonstrably effective mitigation, the
proposal fails to provide adequate certainty that residential amenity, highway safety, and local
character would be protected.
For the reasons outlined above, the proposal conflicts with key development plan principles
relating to residential amenity, highway safety, sustainable location, and character. I respectfully
request that planning permission be refused.
Yours faithfully,

Name: Mr Manav Verma
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to the above-referenced planning application. I have been
a local resident for the last 10 years, currently live in a close vicinity of the site address on Northey
Avenue, having previously been a resident of a flat adjoining the boundary of the site address in
2019-20.
While I recognize the importance of providing community infrastructure, this specific proposal
conflicts with several key requirements of the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF)
December 2024 and Sutton Local Plan 2018, and raises significant concerns regarding long-term
community integration and equitable resource allocation.
1. Conflict with NPPF Paragraphs 96 and 98 (Social Interaction & Cohesion)
The NPPF (2024) is clear that planning decisions must aim to achieve "healthy, inclusive and safe
places." Specifically:
- Paragraph 96(a) requires developments to "promote social interaction... between people who
might not otherwise come into contact with each other."
- Paragraph 98 emphasizes the need for shared spaces and community facilities that serve all
sections of the community.
By proposing a specialized facility for one specific faith group, this development risks undermining
these goals. It creates a "destination" for a specific demographic, which limits the "chance contact"
between diverse residents that is essential for a unified borough of Sutton.
2. Integration Risks and the Casey Review (2016)
The Casey Review warned of the risks of "parallel lives" and residential clustering. The creation of
large, concentrated faith facilities often acts as a catalyst for "enclave" formation, leading to:
- Reduced everyday mixing between different ethnic and religious groups.
- Diminished shared British identity and social integration.
- The very "ghettoisation" cautioned against in national social policy.
Experience in nearby areas, such as Morden, demonstrates how significant demographic shifts
and "clustering" often followed after the establishment of large-scale, single-use faith centers. In a
borough already facing demographic pressures, the Council should prioritize integrated
infrastructure that brings people together rather than facilities that entrench separation.
Similar single faith demographic shifts have also been seen in the last 20-30 years across London
boroughs namely Tooting, Tower Hamlet, Newham, Barking and Dagenham, Waltham Forest,
Brent, Ealing and Hounslow. Trends are also evident across the country: Watford, Birmingham,
Bradford, Leeds and many more. I request that the Council conduct a formal study across these
boroughs and counties to assess how a single faith centre creates a gradual and structural dilution
of diversity of communities.
3. Conflict with Sutton Local Plan (2018) - Policies 21 and 23
The Council's own Local Plan emphasizes the need for social infrastructure that fosters integration
rather than separation:
- Policy 21 (Social Infrastructure): This policy requires the Council to ensure that social
infrastructure is provided in a way that is "sustainable" and "inclusive." By approving an additional
specialized facility for a group that is already well-served (as outlined below), the Council risks
failing its duty to ensure a balanced distribution of community assets for all sections of the
community.
- Policy 23 (Environmental Protection and Impact): This policy addresses the need for
developments to be appropriate to their location. Given the existing concentration of faith-specific
facilities in the immediate area (as outlined below), a further single-use mosque would entrench a
"mono-cultural" use of land, which directly contradicts the goal of maintaining the diverse,
suburban character of Sutton.
4. Disproportionate Allocation relative to Census Data
According to the 2021 ONS Census data for London borough of Sutton, the demographic
breakdown is as follows:
Religion. % of Population. Existing Provision
Christians 45.8% (95,983 residents) Multiple operational churches
No religion 32.3% (67,779 residents) -
Muslim. 7.0% (14,722 residents) Multiple operational mosques
Hindu. 7.0% (14,663 residents) Zero
Other Faiths
Combined. 1.0%. Zero
Granting further specialized provision for a group that already possesses multiple functional sites-
while other groups of identical or similar size have no dedicated worship space-is a failure of
equitable resource allocation. I request that the Council conduct a formal fact-finding exercise to
audit existing places of worship against the actual local population needs before making a
determination
5. Evidence of Existing Provision (Disproportionality)
Planning decisions must be evidence-led and based on an objective assessment of local need.
The 2021 Census confirms that the Muslim and Hindu populations in Sutton are identical in size
(7.0%). However, the borough and its immediate borders already feature a significant number of
functional masjid and Islamic centres, including:
- Sutton Central Masjid (25 Carshalton Rd, Sutton SM1 4LF)
- Sutton Islamic Centre (62 Oakhill Rd, Sutton SM1 3AG)
- Muslim Cultural & Welfare Association of Sutton (Wentworth Hall, 80 Ruskin Rd, Carshalton SM5
3DH)
- Masjid Abu Bakar Al-Siddiq (The Market, Wrythe Ln, Rosehill SM5 1JQ)
- The Baitul Futuh Mosque and the Morden Islamic Community Centre (Massive regional facilities
in Morden).
Granting permission for another facility while other faith groups (and the 32.3% of residents with
"No Religion") lack adequate neutral, shared community spaces is a clear breach of the NPPF
Paragraph 98 requirement to "plan positively for the provision of shared spaces... for all sections
of the community."
6. Socio-Economic Considerations & Land Use
While the British Muslim community makes a material annual contribution to the UK economy,
ONS data indicates that local Muslim residents often face higher rates of economic inactivity
(51.4%) and housing deprivation compared to other groups.
Under Sutton Local Plan Policy 21, the council should prioritize infrastructure that addresses these
specific socio-economic needs for the entire community. A neutral, multi-use community hub
(vocational training, youth services, or recreational space) would provide greater "leveling up"
benefits and foster integration, whereas a specialized religious facility risks encouraging residential
clustering and the "enclave" formation cautioned against in the Casey Review (2016).
The comparative Economic Data from ONS Census 2021 is provided below for ready reference.
Conclusion
Under NPPF Paragraph 96 (December 2024), the Council has a duty to ensure that planning
decisions do not "undermine community cohesion." The clustering of specialized faith facilities, as
warned by the Casey Review, leads to parallel lives rather than integrated ones.
I urge the Planning Committee to refuse this application and reflect the diversity of the borough
and serves all residents equally, regardless of faith or background.
Yours sincerely
Manav Verma
Comparative Economic Data (ONS Census 2021)
When comparing economic activity and household wealth across community groups, the data
shows distinct patterns:

1. Employment Rate:
- Hindus: (16-64)Higher: ~75%-80%
- Christian: Moderate: ~70%
- Muslim: Lower: 51.4%
2. High-Income Households
- Hindus: Highest: ~41% earn £1k+ weekly
- Christian: Average: ~30% earn £1k+ weekly
- Muslims: Lower: ~18% earn £1k+ weekly
3. Housing Tenure
- Hindus: High owner-occupation
- Christian: High owner-occupation
- Muslims: High social renting (26.6%)
4. Economic Activity
- Hindus: Very high
- Christian: Stable
- Muslim: High % of students/home-carers

Name: Mr Suresh Rajasekaran
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
My objection is based on the following material planning grounds:
1. Lack of Demonstrable Need
There are already several established community centres and halls operating within the SM2
postcode and surrounding Belmont area. These venues provide space for meetings, classes,
worship, and community activities.
No clear evidence has been presented to demonstrate an unmet need for an additional facility of
this scale.The proposal represents avoidable overprovision that would place further strain on local
roads and residential amenity.
2. Traffic and Highway Safety
Dorset Road and the surrounding streets are quiet residential roads that are not designed to
accommodate significant increases in traffic. A place of worship and community hub is likely to
generate concentrated vehicle movements, particularly during evenings and weekends. Events,
gatherings and organised activities may result in simultaneous arrivals and departures, creating
congestion and increased risk to pedestrians.
There are schools in close proximity to the site. Additional traffic during school drop-off and pick-
up times would raise serious highway safety concerns. Overspill parking on surrounding
residential roads is highly likely if on-site provision proves insufficient, potentially obstructing
driveways, narrowing carriageways and restricting emergency access.
3. Impact on Residential Amenity
The proposed use represents a substantial intensification compared to the former ambulance
station. The regular congregation of large groups, combined with evening and weekend activities,
would materially alter the quiet character of the area. Extended hours of use could lead to
disturbance during evenings and weekends, undermining the peaceful residential environment
currently enjoyed.
4. Scale, Bulk and Design
The proposal includes part one- and part two-storey extensions and an increase in height to part
of the building. This raises concerns regarding over development of the site and visual impact on
the street scene. The increased scale and massing may appear out of keeping with the
surrounding residential character and could result in overshadowing or loss of outlook for
neighboring properties.

Name: Mr Aman Narang
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Increased traffic and parking problems. It promotes one particular religious belief and
space will not be used for welfare of all communities.

Name: Mr Carlos Abreu

Name: Mr Joydeep Acharjee
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly object to application reference DM2025/01591 for the proposed change of use
from Sui Generis (Ambulance Station) to mixed use Classes F1 (Place of Worship) and F2
(Community Hub), including extensions and increased building height.
Firstly, this proposal results in the unjustified loss of essential emergency service infrastructure.
Ambulance stations form part of critical public service provision and are fundamental to
safeguarding community health and safety. The permanent loss of such a facility conflicts with the
overarching objectives of the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF), which supports the
retention and development of accessible community facilities and requires planning decisions to
promote healthy and safe communities. There appears to be no clear evidence that the loss of this
emergency infrastructure would not harm local service capacity or response times. Once lost, such
facilities are extremely difficult to re-provide within the same locality.
Secondly, the proposal raises serious highway safety and congestion concerns. Under the NPPF
and local development plan policies, development should not result in severe cumulative transport
impacts or compromise highway safety. A place of worship and community hub is likely to
generate significant peak-time vehicle movements, particularly during evenings, weekends and
event-based gatherings. Dorset Road and surrounding streets are residential, relatively narrow,
and already constrained, particularly during school drop-off and pick-up times and periods of high
park usage. The increased intensity of use, combined with the proposed extensions and increase
in building height, risks overdevelopment of the site and an unacceptable impact on the character
and functioning of the area.
The area has limited off-street parking availability, meaning visitors will inevitably rely on on-street
parking. This would reduce effective carriageway width, restrict visibility, and potentially obstruct
emergency vehicle access. This is especially concerning given the proximity to a school and public
park, where pedestrian movements-including children-are high. Planning policy requires safe and
suitable access for all users, including pedestrians, cyclists, and disabled persons. Increased
congestion and parking pressure would disproportionately impact elderly and mobility-impaired
residents, undermining inclusive access principles.
Furthermore, the proposal appears inconsistent with policies that seek to protect existing
community and infrastructure uses unless it can be clearly demonstrated that they are no longer
required or viable. No compelling justification has been provided to outweigh the public safety and
infrastructure concerns associated with the loss of the ambulance station.
For the reasons outlined above - loss of critical emergency infrastructure, unacceptable highway
and parking impacts, harm to residential amenity, and conflict with national and local planning
policy objectives - I respectfully request that the Council refuse this application in the interests of
public safety, accessibility, and sustainable development.

Name: Mr Omar Ismail
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I am writing as a local resident of Sandy Lane, Cheam, and as a father raising a young family in
this neighbourhood, to formally record my strong support for planning application DM2025/01591.
I have taken the time to review all of the planning documents submitted with this application,
including the Transport Statement by Caneparo Associates and the Environmental Noise Survey
by Hann Tucker Associates, as well as a number of the comments submitted both for and against
the proposal. Having done so, I am satisfied that this is a well-considered, policy-compliant
application that will benefit the local community. I urge the London Borough of Sutton to grant
planning permission.
Brownfield reuse and planning principle
The former ambulance station has stood vacant since the NHS declared it surplus to operational
requirements. This modest brownfield site sits in the heart of a residential neighbourhood, and
bringing it back into active, community-serving use is exactly what the National Planning Policy
Framework (NPPF, paragraph 124) and the London Plan encourage. The proposed development
makes efficient use of previously developed land and avoids any encroachment on greenfield or
protected land.
It is worth considering what this site replaces. An ambulance station operated here on a 24-hour
basis, with large vehicles, sirens, reversing bleepers, and crew movements at all hours of the day
and night. A calm, time-limited community and faith facility is, in practical amenity terms, a clear
improvement for neighbouring residents, including those of us on Sandy Lane.
Policy compliance
Sutton Local Plan Policy 26 supports the provision and retention of community facilities, and Policy
33 recognises places of worship and community centres as essential social infrastructure. The
NPPF at paragraph 97 explicitly requires planning authorities to plan positively for community
facilities, including places of worship. This application is in clear and direct conformity with these
policy requirements. Refusal would need to be justified against a strong policy presumption in
favour of community facility provision.
Addressing a real and demonstrable local need
As a local Muslim resident raising a young family, I can speak directly to the need this facility
addresses. The Muslim community in Sutton has grown substantially and existing provision is
severely over-capacity. The nearest existing facilities are in Sutton town centre and Carshalton, all
over two miles from the surrounding area. At present, I have to drive to reach any of them. The
proposed facility at Dorset Road is a c.15 minute walk from my home. That is the difference
between a facility my family can realistically use on foot or by bike and one that requires a car
journey every time.
A permanent, purpose-built local facility at Dorset Road would eliminate those car journeys for my
family and many others in the area, and provide an accessible base for the community. The
planning statement demonstrates this need convincingly with reference to demographic data and
evidence of demand.
As a parent, the community hub element of this proposal is just as important to me as the faith
provision. Educational programmes, family activities, wellbeing sessions, social drop-ins, and
interfaith events will be open to all residents of the Belmont Ward. Having a safe, welcoming
space within walking distance where my family and our neighbours can participate in community
life is something this area currently lacks. This is the kind of inclusive, locally-rooted infrastructure
that makes a neighbourhood cohesive and liveable.
Design and impact on the area
The proposed works are modest. The extension is a part one, part two storey addition with a
limited height increase to part of the existing building. There is no new upper floor of the kind that
would generate overlooking or overshadowing concerns. The scheme includes external greening,
improved access arrangements, dedicated cycle storage, and refuse stores, all of which improve
the presentation and function of the site. I have no concerns about the visual impact of this
development on Dorset Road or the surrounding residential streets.
Transport and parking
I have reviewed the Transport Statement and Travel Plan in detail. I am aware that some objectors
have raised concerns about parking, but the submitted evidence does not support those concerns.
The Transport Statement includes a parking survey conducted within a 400-metre radius of the
site during a real Friday at 13:00, the peak prayer period. That survey found 269 on-street parking
spaces in the surrounding area, with only 134 occupied at baseline: a utilisation rate of just 49.8%,
well below the 85% parking stress threshold used by the London Borough of Sutton.
Even under the worst-case scenario modelled in the Transport Statement, adding 66 additional
visitor vehicles during Friday prayer changeover, on-street utilisation would rise to only 75.5%,
leaving 66 spaces still available. The Council's own parking stress threshold would not be reached
unless a further 25 vehicles beyond the worst case were added. The evidence is clear: there is no
material impact on residents' parking.
The modal split data reinforces this. Based on actual travel surveys of the existing BASMA
congregation, 50% of Friday prayer attendees are predicted to travel on foot, only 11.7% drive
alone, and 20% car-share as driver. This is a community that predominantly walks to worship. As I
noted above, my own family would be among those walking or cycling rather than driving, and the
application includes dedicated cycle storage to support exactly that.
I also note a point that objectors appear to have missed: the former ambulance station had 21
parking spaces (18 external and 3 internal). The development reduces on-site parking from that
baseline, not increases it. The existing yellow keep-clear lines near the site access will also be
removed, freeing up additional kerbside capacity for residents.
The suggestion that Friday prayers will clash with the school run at Avenue Primary Academy is
factually incorrect. Friday prayer changeover runs from approximately 12:30 to 12:55. The school
run peaks are 08:30 to 09:00 and 15:00 to 15:30. There is no overlap. The daily use of the facility,
including classes, drop-ins, and wellbeing sessions, is small-scale and spread across the day.
Noise
The Environmental Noise Survey and Noise Impact Assessment by Hann Tucker Associates is
thorough. The consultants conducted a real-world background noise survey using calibrated
sound level meters, then assessed noise breakout from the building using measured sound
pressure levels from the BASMA community at their current venue. This was a worst-case
amplified speech scenario, not a quiet session.
The conclusion is straightforward: external noise levels at the nearest noise-sensitive receptors
would not exceed the existing background noise level, even during the worst-case Friday prayer
peak. The noise reaching neighbouring properties from this facility is predicted to be quieter than
the existing ambient environment. This meets the Local Authority's criteria.
The report also concludes that the anticipated noise impact of the proposed use will be lower than
the historic operational profile of the ambulance station, which was a 24-hour facility with sirens,
engine idling, and crew movements at all hours. Operating hours for the proposed use are
restricted to 07:00 to 23:00, and the planning statement does not schedule events to run routinely
until that hour. The concerns raised by objectors about late-night noise are not substantiated by
the acoustic evidence.
Conclusion
This proposal is good planning. It brings a brownfield site back into use, provides community
infrastructure that is needed and policy-supported, delivers a sensitive design, and addresses
potential impacts thoroughly. The Transport Statement demonstrates that parking capacity is more
than adequate, that the school-run clash alleged by objectors does not exist, and that the modal
split is overwhelmingly sustainable. The noise assessment confirms that the proposed use will be
quieter than the former ambulance station and will not exceed background noise levels at
neighbouring properties. As a resident and a father raising a family on Sandy Lane, I welcome this
application and strongly support the grant of planning permission for DM2025/01591.
Yours faithfully,
Omar Ismail
95 Sandy Lane, Sutton, SM2 7EP
