Name: Dr Nida Ismail
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I am writing as a local resident of Sandy Lane, Cheam, and as a practising GP, to express my
wholehearted support for planning application DM2025/01591. I am particularly pleased to see a
proposal of this kind come forward in our neighbourhood, and I hope the Council will give it the
positive decision it clearly merits.
The case for community facilities in Belmont
Belmont is a predominantly residential ward that would benefit greatly from improved community
infrastructure. The former ambulance station on Dorset Road has lain vacant since the NHS
declared it surplus to requirements, and it represents a significant missed opportunity in the heart
of our neighbourhood. This application proposes to transform it into a mixed-use Community Hub
and Place of Worship, a use that will serve residents of all backgrounds with educational, social,
and health and wellbeing activities, alongside faith provision for the local Muslim community.
The proposed plan fits in squarely with the objective of both the National Planning Policy
Framework Sutton's own Local Plan. This is not a marginal or speculative use. It is what planning
policy is designed to encourage.
A public health perspective
As a GP, I see the consequences of social isolation, poor mental health, and limited community
access in my patients every day. The evidence linking community participation to improved health
outcomes is well established and is reflected in NHS England's emphasis on social prescribing as
a frontline intervention. Loneliness and social isolation are recognised risk factors for
cardiovascular disease, depression, cognitive decline, and premature mortality, particularly among
elderly residents, new mothers, and those from minority ethnic communities who may face
additional barriers to accessing mainstream services.
This proposal directly addresses those barriers. A permanent, locally-accessible community hub
offering wellbeing sessions, educational programmes, social drop-ins, and family activities is
exactly the kind of preventative infrastructure that reduces demand on GP surgeries, A&E
departments, and mental health services. From a public health standpoint, this facility will benefit
the health and wellbeing of Belmont's residents, and I would urge the planning authority to give
this weight in its decision.
The particular value of this facility for women, families, and elderly residents
I want to draw the planning officer's attention to an aspect of this proposal that deserves specific
recognition: the application explicitly proposes dedicated facilities for women, elderly residents,
and families. As a woman and a mother living locally, I know first-hand how limited the accessible,
safe, and faith-appropriate community provision is in this area. Many women, particularly those
who are elderly, who have young children, or who do not drive, are effectively excluded from
community and faith participation because no suitable local facility exists. This proposal directly
addresses that exclusion.
In my clinical experience, women from faith communities who lack accessible local provision are
disproportionately affected by isolation and its health consequences. Providing a permanent,
welcoming, locally-accessible space for women's groups, family activities, and wellbeing sessions
is a real social benefit that will improve quality of life for some of the most under-served residents
in the borough.
The site is ideal and the design is appropriate
The former ambulance station is a brownfield site: previously developed, currently vacant, and
confirmed surplus to NHS requirements. Its reuse for community benefit, rather than residential or
commercial development, is something to be welcomed. The NPPF and London Plan strongly
support the productive reuse of previously developed land, and this proposal delivers that
alongside a clear community benefit.
The proposed physical works are modest and carefully considered: a part one, part two storey
extension to the front and side of the existing building, a limited height increase to improve internal
functionality, new cycle and refuse stores, and external greening. The design has been prepared
sensitively, with no new upper-floor additions that would affect privacy or outlook for neighbours.
The application also delivers Biodiversity Net Gain improvements and a full package of
environmental assessments, demonstrating that the project has been properly thought through.
Transport, parking, and noise
I have read the Transport Statement and the Environmental Noise Survey submitted with this
application and I am satisfied that both parking capacity and noise impact have been properly
assessed. The Transport Statement demonstrates that on-street parking in the surrounding area
has sufficient spare capacity to absorb the modest additional demand, and that Friday prayer
times do not overlap with the school run at Avenue Primary Academy. The noise assessment
confirms that the proposed use will not exceed background noise levels at neighbouring properties
and will be quieter than the former 24-hour ambulance station. I do not consider it necessary to
repeat the detailed figures here, as the submitted reports speak for themselves, but I am content
that the concerns raised by some objectors on these grounds are not supported by the evidence.
I would also respectfully note that I expect the Council to assess this application on its planning
merits, as I am sure it will. The proposed use is clearly policy-supported, the technical evidence
addresses the concerns that have been raised, and the community benefit is substantial.
Conclusion
This is a thoughtfully prepared, clearly needed, and strongly policy-supported application. It will
bring lasting benefit to Belmont, provide a welcoming space for women, families, and elderly
residents who currently lack accessible local provision, and transform a vacant brownfield site into
a real community asset. As both a local resident and a GP, I can attest to the pressing need for
this facility and the positive impact it will have on the health and wellbeing of our community. I urge
the London Borough of Sutton to grant planning permission for DM2025/01591.
Yours faithfully,
Nida Ismail
95 Sandy Lane, Sutton, SM2 7EP

Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my full support for the planning application to convert the former
ambulance centre in Belmont into a community space for prayers and meetings.
This facility is genuinely needed within the local community. The Muslim community in Belmont
has grown over the years, yet there is currently no suitable, purpose-built space to accommodate
prayers and community gatherings. At present, members meet in limited space at St John Hall,
which is not designed for this purpose and does not adequately serve the needs of a progressive
and growing community.
The building has been unused for some time. Its conversion into a community space represents a
positive and constructive use of an otherwise dormant site. It would be far more beneficial for the
area to have a managed, community-led facility than to see the site sold for high-density
residential development, which could create significantly greater and more permanent traffic and
parking pressures.
Concerns have been raised regarding traffic, parking and safety, particularly given the nearby
primary school. These matters can be managed through appropriate planning conditions, clear
parking guidance and responsible stewardship by the community. Prayer times are structured and
predictable, and peak usage would not coincide with school drop-off and pick-up times on most
weekdays. A community facility of this nature is unlikely to generate the same continuous daily
traffic as alternative commercial or residential developments.
Importantly, this project promotes inclusion, cohesion and collaboration within Belmont. Providing
a safe and dedicated space for community gathering strengthens social responsibility and
encourages constructive engagement with the wider neighbourhood. Rejecting such an application
risks creating unnecessary division rather than fostering mutual understanding.
I respectfully urge the planning authority to consider the long-term social benefit this facility will
bring and to approve the application.

Comment:
Objection to Proposed Development - Former Sutton Ambulance Station, Dorset Road
I strongly object to the proposed development at the former Sutton Ambulance Station on Dorset
Road. The scale, intensity, and nature of this proposal are wholly inappropriate for a quiet, entirely
residential area and would cause unacceptable harm to local amenity and neighbourhood
character.
The site is surrounded solely by homes and gardens. Introducing a highcapacity place of worship
and community hub, operating long hours and attracting large numbers of visitors, is
fundamentally incompatible with this setting and would permanently disrupt the residential
character of the area.
The proposed extensions and increased height are visually overbearing and entirely out of
keeping with the domestic scale of neighbouring properties, resulting in a dominant and intrusive
presence within a suburban street.
There is no demonstrated unmet need for this development. Similar facilities already exist within
close proximity, and the site is poorly served by public transport (PTAL 1a), making it an
unsuitable location for a highintensity use.
The Council's own assessment acknowledges the risk of significant noise and latenight
disturbance, including largescale dispersal of visitors late in the evening. This would seriously
undermine residents' ability to enjoy peace, privacy, and sleep.
The proposal also relies heavily on onstreet parking, which would lead to congestion, parking
stress, and highway safety concerns, to the detriment of existing residents.
Taken together, the noise, traffic, footfall, and lateevening activity would fundamentally alter the
character of the neighbourhood and significantly reduce residents' quality of life.

Name: Mrs N Chandrasekaran

Address: 30 Sackville Mews Sackville Road Sutton SM2 6HS
Name: Mr Dhaval Rathod
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Planning Objection - Former Sutton Ambulance Station, Dorset Road
I object to the proposed development on the grounds that it represents an inappropriate and
incompatible use within a wholly residential area and would result in significant harm to residential
amenity and local character, contrary to established planning policy.
The proposal introduces a highintensity place of worship and community use into a location
characterised entirely by lowdensity residential development. The scale, hours of operation, and
expected levels of attendance are incompatible with the surrounding land use and would materially
alter the established residential character of the area.
The proposed extensions and increase in building height would result in a visually dominant and
overbearing form of development, out of keeping with the prevailing domestic scale, massing, and
appearance of neighbouring properties, causing harm to townscape character.
There is no demonstrated unmet need for this development. Similar facilities exist within close
proximity, and the site is located in an area of very poor public transport accessibility (PTAL 1a),
making it unsuitable for a highcapacity use reliant on large numbers of visitors.
The proposal raises serious concerns regarding noise and disturbance, particularly from
lateevening activities and largescale dispersal of attendees, which would result in unacceptable
harm to neighbouring occupiers' living conditions.
In addition, the development would lead to unacceptable parking stress and traffic impact, due to
insufficient onsite parking provision and reliance on surrounding residential streets, adversely
affecting highway safety and residential convenience.
Taken cumulatively, the intensity of use, noise, traffic, parking pressure, and extended hours of
operation would cause substantial and lasting harm to residential amenity and neighbourhood
character.
Conclusion
The proposal fails to respect the character of the area, does not adequately protect residential
amenity, and represents an unsustainable form of development in this location.
For these reasons, the application should be refused.

Name: Mr Ashok Srinivasan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application

Name: Mr Hammyue Khan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly support this proposal to reuse the former ambulance centre.
I live within a few minutes walk of the site, and this new community facility would be a real benefit
to local residents. As someone who has recently moved to the area with a young family, having a
safe, welcoming space within walking distance would make it much easier to meet others locally
and take part in community activities.
I also understand a defibrillator is available on site, which would be a valuable and much needed
resource for the neighbourhood.
The current temporary arrangement at St John's Church is not a suitable long term solution, so
providing a dedicated, permanent space is important. This is a sensible and positive reuse of an
existing building.
The proposal appears in keeping with the character of the area and should have minimal, if any,
impact on the streetscape or skyline.

Name: Mr Gaber Langhi
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my strong support for Planning Application DM2025/01591.
I have been a homeowner on Balmoral Way since 1993 and have lived directly next to the former
ambulance station for over three decades. During that time, I have seen Belmont change
considerably - families grow, new neighbours arrive, schools expand, and the community evolve.
While the area has developed in many positive ways, one thing that has consistently been lacking
is a dedicated, well-organised community facility that can properly serve local residents of all ages.
Having lived beside the site for so many years, I am very familiar with its history and its impact on
the immediate surroundings. When it operated as an ambulance station, there were regular
vehicle movements, flashing blue lights and sirens at all hours, including late at night and early in
the morning. That was understandably part of its essential function. In comparison, the proposed
use as a Place of Worship and Community Hub represents a far more predictable and moderate
use of the site.
The location of the building is, in my view, ideal for local residents. I personally walk to community
events that have been held in Belmont and have done so regularly over the past several years,
and I know many neighbours who do the same. The site is perfectly placed within the heart of the
community. It is literally just down the road from where current activities take place at St John's
Community Hall. Residents like myself value being able to walk to local events, and this proposal
simply builds on an existing pattern of community activity in the area. I do not believe it would
introduce anything materially different from what residents are already used to, nor would it cause
disturbance beyond normal and reasonable community use.
I believe there is a genuine and growing need for a facility like this in Belmont. Over the years, I
have seen the local population increase and become more diverse, yet community infrastructure
has not expanded at the same pace. This proposal would provide structured activities, educational
programmes for children, youth engagement initiatives, health and wellbeing support, and
dedicated space for women's activities. These are not luxuries - they are essential components of
a healthy and cohesive community.
Importantly, the building extensions are modest and appear proportionate to the existing structure.
The design remains in keeping with the surrounding residential character and does not represent
overdevelopment. The retention of greenery and the inclusion of cycle storage also demonstrate
thoughtful planning.
Concerns about parking and traffic have clearly been addressed through the provision of 12 on-
site parking spaces, including 2 disabled bays. Given my long-term experience of living
immediately next to the site, I do not believe this proposal will cause significant traffic, noise or
disturbance beyond what the area has historically accommodated.
What stands out most to me is the opportunity this development presents. For many years, I have
watched young people in the area grow up without a dedicated local hub where they can receive
guidance, participate in organised programmes and feel a sense of belonging. A well-managed
community centre and place of worship would provide structure, support and positive engagement,
which ultimately benefits the entire neighbourhood.
Belmont has always been a strong community, but it now needs facilities that reflect its growth and
future. After more than 30 years living next to this site, I believe this proposal is a positive and
responsible use of the land.
I respectfully ask the Council to approve Planning Application DM2025/01591.

The proposed site is already located in an area experiencing significant traffic

off and pick-up times. In addition, the presence of recreational facilities such as tennis courts and
an elderly residents' golf facility further contributes to regular vehicle circulation, parking demand,
and vulnerable pedestrian activity.
The introduction of a community hall, temple, mosque, church, or any similar assembly use would
inevitably generate additional peak-time traffic, particularly during school running time. This would
intensify existing congestion, increase on-street parking stress, reduce road visibility, and heighten
risks to school children, elderly residents, and other pedestrians.
Given the current cumulative traffic impact and the sensitive nature of surrounding uses (school
and elderly facilities), this location is not suitable for further high-intensity community or assembly-
based development.

Name: Mrs Prajakta J
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment: Object

Address: 26 Lyndhurst Court Grange Road Sutton SM2 6SR
Name: Mr Sreekanth Iyengar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The proposed development is likely to generate a substantial increase in vehicle
movements in
surrounding residential streets. It is estimated that 70 or more vehicles could seek parking on
nearby roads, with the potential for up to 80 vehicles present locally during peak overlap periods
(for example, when one event finishes and another begins).
This level of additional traffic would:
Intensify congestion on already narrow residential streets
Lead to unsafe parking on corners, pavements, and across driveways
Increase vehicle turning movements and reversing in confined spaces
Create hazards for pedestrians, cyclists, and local residents
Such pressure on local infrastructure significantly raises the risk of minor collisions, near misses,
and blocked emergency access routes.
- School Safety Concerns
Avenue Primary Academy serves approximately 1,034 pupils, resulting in substantial pedestrian
and vehicle activity during morning drop-off and afternoon pick-up periods.
The addition of event-related traffic would:
Increase congestion during peak school travel times
Reduce sightlines for drivers and pedestrians due to on-street parking
Heighten the risk of children stepping into traffic from between parked vehicles
Complicate safe crossing for families walking to and from school
Children are particularly vulnerable road users. Even small increases in traffic and parking density
can materially elevate the risk of accidents in school zones.
- Serious Noise and Late-Night Disturbance
Events involving up to 250 attendees, dispersing around 11:00pm, would likely create repeated
late-night disturbances.
This disturbance is not limited to amplified sound from within the venue, but includes:
Groups congregating and talking loudly outside
Car engines idling and revving
Doors slamming
Vehicles circling repeatedly while searching for parking spaces
Car stereos and horns
Noise at this time of night travels further and is more intrusive due to lower ambient background
sound. Repeated weekly or regular events would have a cumulative negative impact on residents'
sleep, wellbeing, and quality of life.
- Impact on Vulnerable Residents
The area includes a nearby care home and a significant number of elderly residents, who are
particularly sensitive to disruption.
The likely impacts include:
Blocked access for carers, ambulances, and essential services
Increased difficulty for residents with limited mobility navigating obstructed pavements
Heightened anxiety caused by congestion and late-night activity
Greater health impact from sleep disturbance
For elderly and vulnerable individuals, even moderate increases in noise and access obstruction
can have disproportionate effects on physical and mental wellbeing.

Name: Mr Mokshith Rayapati
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to the proposed planning permission due to serious
concerns regarding its negative impact on traffic, safety, and the well-being of local residents.
The proposed development will significantly worsen traffic conditions in an area that already
experiences substantial congestion, particularly during school peak hours. The nearby Avenue
School accommodates approximately 1,100 students. During morning drop-off and afternoon pick-
up times, the roads are already heavily congested, with vehicles queueing and limited safe
crossing points for children. Any additional traffic generated by this development would further
increase the risk of accidents and endanger students and parents.
Furthermore, the area is home to a large elderly population and is located near Golf Club, which
also generates regular vehicle movement throughout the day. Increased traffic flow will make road
access more difficult and potentially hazardous for elderly residents who may have limited mobility
and require safer pedestrian access.
This is a predominantly residential neighbourhood with narrow roads not designed to
accommodate high volumes of additional traffic. Increased congestion will lead to:
Reduced road safety for pedestrians, especially children and elderly residents
Increased air pollution and noise levels
Difficulty for emergency vehicles to access properties
Reduced quality of life for residents
Given these serious concerns, I strongly urge the planning authority to reject this application. The
existing infrastructure does not have the capacity to safely manage further traffic increases, and
the safety and well-being of the community must be prioritised.

Name: Mr Mohammad Hussain Azami

Comment:
I would like to confirm my strong support for this development. I believe it will provide a

I have reviewed the plans and spoken to the team leading the project. The inclusion of dedicated
community space for young people is particularly important, as there is a shortage of youth
facilities in Sutton. This proposal directly addresses that need by providing structured and
supervised activities in a safe environment.
In addition, the proposed wellbeing events, sporting activities and organised community
programmes will deliver much-needed services for the community, by the community. Having a
permanent, well-managed space will ensure these initiatives are properly coordinated and
accessible.
With an ever-growing elderly population, providing safe and welcoming facilities for older residents
is equally important. Spaces that encourage connection and reduce isolation have a meaningful
and lasting social impact.
I am confident that this centre will bring real, measurable value to the area and respectfully
request that this application receives your support.

Name: Mr Ahmed Khan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a resident of Heath Drive to express my strong support for the planning
application.
As a local resident living close to the proposed site, I have carefully reviewed the details of the
application and believe it represents a well-considered, responsible, and beneficial development
for the area.
In particular, I welcome the provision of substantial on-site parking, including the 12 standard
spaces and dedicated disabled bays. This demonstrates a clear commitment to managing traffic
responsibly and minimising any potential impact on surrounding residential streets. Adequate on-
site parking is essential for developments of this nature, and the proposal appears to address this
appropriately.
I also appreciate the applicant's proactive approach to managing Friday prayer times to avoid
overlap with nearby school pick-up periods. This shows a thoughtful and cooperative effort to
reduce congestion and disruption at peak times and reflects genuine engagement with local
concerns.
The proposed centre will provide a wide range of positive community benefits, including
educational programmes, youth activities, wellbeing initiatives, and opportunities for social
engagement. Facilities of this kind can play a vital role in strengthening community cohesion,
promoting wellbeing, and offering constructive activities for residents of all ages. A structured and
managed community space is far preferable to informal or unmanaged gatherings and can
contribute positively to the social fabric of the neighbourhood.
Furthermore, the design of the building appears modest, proportionate, and sympathetic to the
character of the surrounding area. Based on the submitted plans, it does not appear to be
overbearing or out of keeping with neighbouring properties and should integrate well into the local
streetscape.
Importantly, this proposal demonstrates clear efforts to address common planning concerns such
as parking, traffic management, accessibility, and neighbourhood impact while delivering
meaningful and long-term benefits to the local community.
For these reasons, I believe the application represents a balanced, thoughtful, and community-
focused development. I respectfully urge the Council to grant planning permission.

Name: Mr Aguilane Periasoamy
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to this application on planning grounds.
While community facilities are important, this proposal represents an inappropriate and over-
intensive non-residential use within a quiet, established residential area. The scale and
institutional nature of the building would be out of character with the surrounding domestic
properties and would undermine the prevailing residential character.
The proposed F1/F2 use would introduce concentrated peak-time activity, including vehicle
movements, parking demand and congregation of attendees, which is materially different from
typical residential activity (Use Class C3). This would result in unacceptable harm to neighbouring
amenity through noise, disturbance, and general activity levels inconsistent with a quiet residential
street.
There are also significant highway safety concerns. The surrounding roads are residential in
nature and not designed to accommodate institutional-scale peak traffic flows. Insufficient on-site
parking is likely to lead to overspill parking, obstruction of driveways, congestion, and reduced
road safety.
The site is located approximately 500 metres from a primary school. The surrounding streets
already experience congestion and informal parking during school drop-off and collection times.
The additional peak traffic associated with religious services and community events would
exacerbate cumulative highway pressures and increase risks to pedestrian safety, particularly for
young children.
In addition, there is a retirement/elderly care facility within 500 metres of the site. Increased traffic
volumes, parking congestion, and heightened activity levels would adversely affect vulnerable
elderly residents who rely on safe and low-traffic environments.
Taken together, the proposal would result in cumulative impacts on highway safety and residential
amenity that conflict with the Development Plan and national policy set out in the Town and
Country Planning Act 1990 and the National Planning Policy Framework.
For these reasons, I respectfully request that planning permission be refused.

Name: Mr Gulam Kibria Chowdhury
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
We are writing as a family from Dorset Road to voice our strong support for planning
application DM2025/01591.
Having been long-term residents in the vicinity of the former ambulance station, we have direct
experience of its previous 24-hour operation, which entailed constant vehicle movement and
activity. We are therefore particularly enthusiastic about the proposition to replace this
continuously busy facility with a more modest, community-oriented development.
This change promises a significantly reduced impact on noise and traffic, and we believe it will
integrate far more harmoniously with our residential surroundings.
We also commend the inclusion of additional parking in the proposal. This thoughtful provision
addresses a practical concern, offering a proactive solution to manage demand and alleviate
parking pressures on local roads.
From an aesthetic standpoint, the proposed development strikes us as entirely appropriate for the
area. The designs appear well-proportioned and sensitively considered, ensuring the new
structure will complement the existing streetscape seamlessly.
In conclusion, we view this application as a genuinely positive step for our neighbourhood. It
transitions an intensive operational site into a calm, community-focused asset that genuinely
enhances the area's character.
We respectfully urge the Council to grant approval for planning application DM2025/01591.

Name: Dr Mahbobur Choudhury
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I have loved working and living in Sutton for almost 25 years, and I am very pleased to
see this new proposed development which my family and I support without any reservations.
Sutton is changing and becoming a wonderful place for people from diverse backgrounds working
together to make a great community with rich values. The Council has a duty to look after the
needs of the people who live and work in the borough. The muslim community of Belmont and
Cheam have used St John's Church Hall for many years without any issues. Indeed, I booked the
hall for an event some 20 years ago.
There is now a need for the local muslim community to have a purpose built, larger space for
worship (including holding wedding vows), recreation, place for the youth to enjoy activities (social,
sports, education) and a place to get positive guidance on life. The venue will also be open to non-
muslims to attend functions and events.
The concerns regarding highway safety and noise levels are overstated. Fewer people will be
attending the venue than attending the Avenue school. The times of attending the venue will not
coincide with the opening and closing times of the school. Friday is usually the peak attendance
between 1pm and 2pm. This attendance has already been in place for many years at the nearby
St John's Church Hall, which is a more congested venue. Other times only a dozen or so people
will probably attend and most like myself will probably walk to the site. The concern regarding
noise is overstated. A simple visit to any such establishment in London will confirm this. The
attendees are very peaceful and only soft drinks are allowed in the premises with no music
allowed.
The site location for a place of worship is no different to residential roads in which most of the
churches in the borough are located. The building design will add character and value to a site that
has been neglected for a long time. I am sure any changes the Case Officer feels objectionable
can be accommodated by the developer.
I hope the Case Officer will look at all the benefits this development will provide the local
community and approve the application.

I am writing to object very strongly to the Ambulance Station being turned into a a

Then we have the parents who park every week day for drop off and pick up. They often park

Friday is chaotic with traffic everywhere which often means the bus is delayed. At least we have
some peace on Saturdays and Sundays. The extra and various activities taking place at the
enlarged Ambulance Station with almost no parking, doesn't bear thinking about. It will be busy

The second cause for concern is the increased noise. We have the noise from the school which

When we moved here in 2000, this was a quiet and peaceful road. Since then the quality of life for
us has declined exponentially as the school has increased in size. The idea of a 'prayer room'
opening and further increasing parking and noise is very depressing and surely unnecessary.
There is a prayer room not far from here in the old karate hall and yet another in Oakhill Road.
How many do we need? I see this as unnecessary, unreasonable and unfair and leading to a
reduced quality of life for the local residents.
Yours faithfully
Mrs. MP Standerwick. 68, York Road.

Name: Mr Adithya Kantheti
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I am writing to formally object to the above planning application for the proposed place of worship
at Sui Generis (Ambulance station).
My objection is based on the following material planning considerations:
Traffic Impact and Road Safety
The proposed development is expected to generate significant vehicular traffic, particularly during
peak worship times which are likely to coincide with existing rush hour periods. The surrounding
road network already experiences congestion during morning and afternoon peak hours. The
additional traffic volume may exacerbate congestion, increase journey times, and reduce overall
road safety.
Proximity to Primary School
The site is adjacent to / in close proximity to a primary school. During school drop-off and pick-up
times, the area is already heavily congested with parents, buses, and pedestrians. The
introduction of additional traffic during similar time periods raises serious concerns regarding
pedestrian safety, especially for young children. Increased vehicle movements, parking pressure,
and potential illegal parking could heighten the risk of accidents.
Parking Pressure and Overspill
The surrounding residential streets have limited parking capacity. There is a high likelihood of
overspill parking into nearby residential roads during services and events, which would
inconvenience residents and restrict access for emergency vehicles.
Noise and Disturbance
Regular gatherings, special events, and increased footfall may result in noise and general
disturbance to the surrounding residential neighborhood, particularly during early mornings,
evenings, or weekends.
Suitability of Location
Given the scale and expected visitor numbers, this site may not be appropriate for such a
development without significant infrastructure upgrades or a comprehensive traffic management
strategy.
I respectfully request that the council carefully consider these concerns and require a detailed
traffic impact assessment, parking management plan, and road safety evaluation before
determining this application.
Thank you for considering my representation.

Name: Mrs Soumya Chowdary Vemuri
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Sir/Madam,
I am writing to raise formal concerns regarding the proposed development of a place of worship
within our residential neighbourhood.
The proposed location is directly adjacent to a Avenue primary school, which already experiences
considerable congestion during morning drop-off and afternoon pick-up periods. The addition of a
facility expected to draw significant numbers of visitors particularly during peak worship times,
weekends, and religious events could intensify traffic levels and create heightened safety risks for
young children and pedestrians.
In addition, the surrounding roads are primarily residential and not designed to accommodate
sustained high traffic volumes. Increased vehicle movements, on-street parking, and potential
congestion may negatively affect access for residents, emergency services, and school transport.
The cumulative impact on local infrastructure and daily community life should be carefully
evaluated.
I respectfully request that the council thoroughly review the traffic impact assessment, parking
provisions, pedestrian safety measures, and any proposed mitigation plans before making a
decision. Consideration should also be given to whether the site is appropriate for a development
of this scale given its proximity to residential housing and a school.
My objection is based solely on planning, traffic management, and public safety concerns, and I
trust these matters will be given careful consideration.

Name: Mr Tanmay Karkare
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The application site is located within an area that already experiences significant traffic
congestion and peak-time pressure on the local highway network. The immediate vicinity includes
a large primary school with a pupil capacity exceeding 1,000. This generates substantial
pedestrian and vehicular activity during morning drop-off and afternoon pick-up periods. At these
times, the surrounding roads are frequently congested, parking availability is extremely limited,
visibility at junctions is reduced, and conditions for pedestrians - particularly children and
accompanying parents - are compromised.
In addition, nearby recreational facilities, including tennis courts and a golf facility primarily used by
elderly residents, generate consistent vehicular movements throughout the day and into the
evening. These facilities attract both regular visitors and individuals who may have reduced
mobility, thereby increasing the sensitivity of the area in terms of highway safety and pedestrian
protection.
The proposed use as a place of worship or community assembly (whether temple, mosque,
church, hall, or similar) would likely give rise to concentrated patterns of arrival and departure,
particularly during evenings, weekends, and religious or community events. Such uses
characteristically generate peak and overlapping traffic flows rather than dispersed movements.
This would place additional strain on an already constrained road network.
The cumulative impact would likely include intensified congestion, increased on-street parking
pressure, a higher risk of obstructive or unsafe parking practices, reduced access for emergency
vehicles, and a material deterioration in road safety conditions for schoolchildren, elderly users of
nearby facilities, and other pedestrians.
When assessed in the context of existing conditions and the sensitive surrounding land uses, the
site is not considered suitable for further high-intensity, assembly-based development. The
proposal would materially worsen highway safety conditions and adversely affect the amenity of
neighbouring residents.

Name: Mrs Ayesha Uddin
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly support the above application, as it will bring significant benefits to the local
community for several important reasons.
It's vital that there is community support for our ethnically diverse population, whose needs are
varied and evolving. It is essential that we encourage the development of worship and community
spaces that can respond to these needs and help address local inequalities. Our growing and
diverse population requires a dedicated worship and community centre that can promote social
connection, support mental wellbeing, and help reduce loneliness.
The proposed worship timings are typically in the mid-afternoon and do not conflict with school
hours, with attendees dispersing before school pick-up times. Additionally, existing traffic
regulations are already in place to manage congestion, and the local community has historically
complied with these arrangements responsibly.
This facility would also provide invaluable support to elderly residents, offering them a welcoming
space to socialise, reduce isolation, and access community-based assistance tailored to their
frailty and wellbeing needs.
Furthermore, it would complement local hospitals and existing community services by helping to
identify, channel, and support vulnerable individuals within a community setting, thereby
expanding the overall reach and effectiveness of local support systems.
For these reasons, I firmly believe this application deserves approval.

Name: Mr Sadananda Ganna
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
1. Existing Traffic Congestion and Peak-Time Pressure
The proposed site is located in an area already experiencing substantial traffic congestion and
acute peak-time pressure. The surrounding road network frequently operates at or near capacity,
particularly during school drop-off and pick-up hours, when vehicle queues, limited manoeuvring
space, and restricted visibility are common.
2. Impact of Nearby Primary School
The immediate vicinity includes a primary school with a pupil capacity exceeding 1,000 students.
This generates exceptionally high volumes of pedestrian and vehicular activity during morning and
afternoon peak periods. Large numbers of children, parents, pushchairs, and cyclists use the
surrounding pavements and crossings, creating a highly sensitive highway environment.
Any further intensification of traffic would materially increase risks to child safety and exacerbate
existing congestion.
3. Cumulative Impact from Recreational and Elderly Facilities
In addition to the school, the area is served by recreational facilities including tennis courts and an
elderly residents' golf facility. These uses generate steady vehicle circulation and parking demand
throughout the day and into the evening.
Importantly, the presence of elderly users increases the vulnerability profile of the area, as many
may have reduced mobility or slower crossing speeds, requiring heightened highway safety
considerations.
4. Traffic Generation from Assembly-Based Use
The proposed introduction of a community hall, temple, mosque, church, or similar assembly use
would result in concentrated arrival and departure patterns, particularly during evenings,
weekends, and special events.
Unlike residential development, assembly uses typically create simultaneous peak traffic
movements, leading to:
Intensified congestion
Increased on-street parking stress
Obstructive and unsafe parking practices
Reduced visibility at junctions
Impeded access for emergency vehicles
This pattern of traffic generation would compound existing pressures rather than operate
independently of them.
5. Highway Safety and Residential Amenity
When considered cumulatively, the existing high pedestrian activity, vulnerable users (children and
elderly residents), and established congestion levels demonstrate that the site is unsuitable for
further high-intensity assembly use.
The proposal would:
Materially worsen highway safety conditions
Increase risks to pedestrians
Diminish residential amenity
Undermine the safe operation of the surrounding road network

Name: Mr Daniel Aksentic
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my strong support for the proposed change of use of the
Ambulance Station on Dorset Road in Belmont to a mixed-use facility including a Place of Worship
and Community Hub.
This proposal would provide much-needed community space for residents in Belmont and the
wider London Borough of Sutton. Facilities that bring people together for worship, social activities,
and support services are vital for community cohesion, wellbeing, and inclusion. Re-using an
existing building rather than leaving it underused is also a positive and sustainable approach.
The proposed extensions appear proportionate and in keeping with the surrounding area, and the
inclusion of cycle parking and refuse storage demonstrates thoughtful planning. A community hub
will benefit families, older residents, and young people alike by providing a safe and welcoming
local venue for events, support groups, and educational activities.
I hope that Polly Davidson and the planning committee will give favourable consideration to this
application, as it will bring lasting social value to the neighbourhood.
Thank you for considering my comment.

Name: Mrs Sujatha Boddu
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The proposed site is situated within an area already subject to substantial traffic
congestion and acute peak-time pressure. The immediate vicinity includes a primary school with a
pupil capacity in excess of 1,000, generating exceptionally high volumes of pedestrian and
vehicular movement during morning drop-off and afternoon pick-up periods. These times are
characterized by congestion, limited parking availability, reduced visibility at junctions, and
increased vulnerability for children and accompanying parents.
In addition, nearby recreational facilities, including tennis courts and an elderly residents' golf
facility, contribute to sustained vehicle circulation throughout the day and evening. These uses
attract both regular visitors and older residents, many of whom may have reduced mobility,
thereby heightening the sensitivity of the area in terms of highway safety and pedestrian
protection.
The introduction of a community hall, temple, mosque, church, or any comparable assembly use
would inevitably result in concentrated arrival and departure patterns, particularly during evenings,
weekends, and religious or community events. Such uses typically generate peak, overlapping
traffic flows rather than dispersed movements. This would significantly intensify existing
congestion, exacerbate on-street parking stress, increase instances of obstructive or unsafe
parking, compromise emergency vehicle access, and materially reduce road safety for
schoolchildren, elderly residents, and other pedestrians.
When assessed cumulatively, the existing pressures and the sensitive character of surrounding
uses clearly demonstrate that the site is unsuitable for further high-intensity, assembly-based
development. The proposal would materially worsen highway safety conditions and adversely
affect residential amenity.

Address: Bay Cottage 5 Park Road Cheam SM3 8QB
Name: Mrs Farzana Khan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I wanted to say how much I support the new community centre being built in Belmont.
As a grandmother living nearby, it would mean so much to have somewhere local and safe to walk
to where older people can meet, talk, and feel connected, especially as it is too easy nowadays to
feel alone. Travelling further away isn't always easy at my age, so having something within
walking distance would make a real difference.
I'm also really looking forward to the community side of it. With my grandchildren, I'm hopeful it will
help us meet other families who live nearby and build new friendships. The idea of finally feeling
part of a local community excites me, as that hasn't always been easily available before.
I believe it will bring people of all ages together and be such a positive addition to Belmont.

Name: Mrs Monali Mohite

Name: Mr Braja Nayak
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Subject: Objection to Planning Application DM2025/01591 - Change of Use to Place of
Worship and Community Hub, Former Ambulance Station, Dorset Road, Belmont (SM2 6HX)
Dear Sir/Madam,
I am writing to formally object to planning application DM2025/01591, which proposes the change
of use of the former ambulance station on Dorset Road, Belmont, to a mixed-use place of worship
and community hub.
As a resident living in close proximity to the site, I have significant concerns regarding the impact
of this development on highway safety, parking, residential amenity, and the overall character of
the area.
1. Highway Safety, Traffic Impact, and School Proximity
The site is located very close to Avenue Primary School, where traffic congestion is already
severe during school drop-off and pick-up times. The proposed development is likely to generate
substantial additional vehicle movements that would coincide with peak school hours, significantly
increasing risks to children, parents, and pedestrians.
The surrounding roads are residential and constrained, with limited turning space and visibility.
Increased congestion and unsafe manoeuvres could compromise road safety and hinder access
for emergency vehicles.
2. Inadequate Parking Provision
The proposal appears to provide limited on-site parking relative to anticipated attendance levels.
This is likely to result in overspill parking on nearby residential streets, particularly during school
hours, exacerbating existing parking pressures and creating hazardous conditions.
Blocked driveways, reduced visibility at junctions, and restricted access for refuse collection and
emergency services would have a serious impact on residents and the safe operation of the area.
3. Impact on Residential Amenity
The scale and intensity of use associated with a place of worship and community hub could result
in increased noise, disturbance, and general activity, particularly during early mornings, evenings,
weekends, and special events. This would detract from the quiet enjoyment of neighbouring
homes.
Increased footfall and vehicle movements may also affect privacy and the overall sense of safety
within the neighbourhood.
4. Overdevelopment and Character of the Area
The proposed use represents a significant intensification compared to the previous use of the site.
The level of activity generated would be out of keeping with the predominantly residential
character of Dorset Road and surrounding streets.
5. Reliance on Management Measures
The application appears to rely heavily on operational management measures (such as
attendance control and parking management) rather than providing sufficient physical
infrastructure to mitigate impacts. Such measures may be difficult to enforce consistently over
time.
Conclusion
While I recognise the importance of community facilities, I believe that this proposal, in its current
form, would result in unacceptable harm to highway safety-particularly given its proximity to
Avenue Primary School-parking conditions, residential amenity, and the character of the local
area.
I respectfully request that the Council refuse planning application DM2025/01591, or require
substantial revisions to address the concerns outlined above.
Yours faithfully,
Braja Nayak

Name: Mr Hamza Taimuri
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my strong support for Planning Application DM2025/01591.
I have lived in Sutton for over 20 years, and having grown up in this community, I care deeply
about its character, safety and welcoming atmosphere. It is a peaceful area with very little noise
pollution, and it has always been a place where people from diverse backgrounds live respectfully
alongside one another. I believe this proposal aligns with those values and will contribute
positively to our neighbourhood.
In my view, the proposed building is modest in scale and in keeping with the surrounding area. It
does not appear overbearing or out of character, and the design respects the existing streetscape.
Compared to many alternative developments that could be brought forward, this represents a low-
intensity use that is unlikely to place undue pressure on local infrastructure.
The proposal also provides clear community benefits. The inclusion of health and wellbeing
programmes, educational programmes for children, and dedicated activities and space for women
will create meaningful opportunities for local residents. These types of inclusive facilities
strengthen community ties and provide safe, structured environments for learning, support and
social connection.
I am reassured by the practical measures included within the application. The provision of 12 on-
site parking spaces plus 2 disabled bays demonstrates that access has been properly considered.
The inclusion of cycle racks also supports sustainable travel choices. I also welcome the fact that
existing greenery is being maintained, helping to preserve the pleasant character of the area.
It is particularly positive that Friday prayers are being staggered to avoid conflict with local school
pick-up times. This shows consideration for neighbours and proactive planning to minimise
disruption.
Based on the information provided, I do not believe the proposal will cause significant traffic, light
or noise issues. Given the modest scale of the building and the planned management
arrangements, I consider it to be an appropriate and responsible development for this location.
For these reasons, I respectfully ask the Council to approve Planning Application DM2025/01591.

Name: Mrs NEELAM BAGHEL
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to the above-referenced planning application. I live in this
area from last 10 years.
The new planning permission will increase lot of traffic and as its near the school it will create a

Name: Mr Aneesh Myneni
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to the proposed planning application on the following grounds:
Limited Access Points:
The former fire station has just three access points. These appear insufficient to accommodate the
anticipated rise in vehicle movements and pedestrian traffic associated with a full-time place of
worship.
Impact on Avenue School and Surrounding Infrastructure:
The proposed site is close to Avenue School, in an area that already experiences notable traffic
congestion and limited parking availability. The introduction of a regularly operating mosque could
intensify these existing pressures on local roads. Additionally, increased traffic volumes may
heighten concerns about pedestrian safety, particularly for schoolchildren nearby. Taken together,
these considerations suggest that the location may present challenges in terms of capacity and
road safety.

Name: Mr Murali Myneni
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to the proposed change of use from an ambulance station to a
place of worship due to concerns regarding traffic generation and public safety.
The surrounding area already experiences substantial congestion, particularly during peak
commuting periods. A development that is likely to attract additional vehicle movements at
predictable times - including evenings and weekends - would add further pressure to an already
constrained road network.
The site's proximity to a nearby school is of particular concern. Increased traffic flow, higher
demand for on-street parking, and greater pedestrian activity could intensify safety risks for
children, parents, and local residents, especially during school drop-off and collection times.
The existing infrastructure appears to be operating near capacity, and the application does not
sufficiently demonstrate how the cumulative impacts on highway safety, site access, and parking
provision would be mitigated.
Integration and Long-Term Community Impact
National policy discussions in recent years have increasingly focused on integration and the
importance of shared civic identity. The The Casey Review highlighted concerns in some areas
about residential clustering and communities living "parallel lives." While each locality is different,
planning authorities are encouraged to consider the long-term social impacts of developments that
may concentrate single-faith activity in particular neighbourhoods.
In some locations, significant new religious infrastructure has coincided with noticeable
demographic shifts over time. For example, parts of Morden have experienced changes in local
population patterns in the decade following the establishment of a large mosque. Whether or not
such changes are directly linked, they demonstrate the importance of undertaking careful impact
assessment before coming to a decision.
For these reasons, I believe the proposed development would have an adverse effect on road
safety and the overall wellbeing of the local community.

Name: Dr Raana Dar
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
This will be so convenient as it's local. I strongly support for it to be converted into a
mosque. We have done so much effort and collected funds it will be a shame if this doesn't go

Name: Mrs Hira Saeed
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a local resident to express my support for planning application
DM2025/01591.
As a mother living in the area, I believe the proposed community centre would provide a valuable
and much-needed local facility for families. Having a safe, welcoming space within walking
distance where children can attend educational programmes and structured activities is a real
benefit to the neighbourhood.
I particularly welcome the inclusion of dedicated space and activities for women, including
wellbeing and fitness sessions. Accessible facilities that support women's health and social
connection are extremely important, especially for those balancing childcare and family
responsibilities.
The proposal also includes sensible practical measures such as on-site parking, disabled bays
and cycle storage, which demonstrate that the development has been carefully planned. From my
own experience in the area, I do not believe the centre will cause significant traffic or disruption.
Activities have been operating nearby for several years without noticeable issues, and Friday
prayers are staggered to avoid conflict with school pick-up times.
Overall, I feel this represents a positive and well-managed addition to the local area. It supports
families, promotes wellbeing and strengthens community ties while remaining appropriate in scale
and character for a residential neighbourhood.
I respectfully ask the Council to approve this application.

But the proposed location of development is far from it.

I have been reading some of the objections & oddly enough from people who are outside this
