Name: Mr Asad Raja
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
As someone who lives locally, I want to add my voice in support of planning application
DM2025/01591. I think this proposal will be good for the neighbourhood.
The proposed works are modest: a part one, part two storey extension with a limited height
increase to the existing building. There is no new upper floor that would create overlooking or
overshadowing. The scheme includes external greening, cycle storage, and improved refuse
arrangements, all of which improve the appearance of the site.
The Muslim community in Sutton has grown considerably and existing facilities are over-capacity.
Many residents currently have to travel substantial distances by car to access suitable provision. A
local facility at Dorset Road would meet a clear and demonstrable need.
Preventing health problems is cheaper and more effective than treating them. Community hubs
that offer wellbeing activities, social connection, and educational programmes are proven to
reduce demand on the NHS. This proposal delivers exactly that.
I would also note that this is not just a place of worship. The community hub element is open to all
residents and will provide educational programmes, wellbeing sessions, social drop-ins, and
interfaith activities. The benefits extend well beyond any single community.
The BNG assessment meets the statutory requirement, and the external greening will improve the
appearance and ecological value of a site that currently offers very little in either regard. I welcome
the environmental improvements included in this proposal.
DM2025/01591 is a strong application that will benefit the whole community. I ask the London
Borough of Sutton to approve it.

Name: Mr Pramod Mahajan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I write to formally object in the strongest possible terms to planning application DM2025/01591
concerning the proposed change of use and extensions at the former Ambulance Station, Dorset
Road, Belmont.
My objection is based on serious concerns relating to highway safety, traffic generation, impact on
vulnerable children, overdevelopment of the site, and unsuitability of the location for the proposed
intensified mixed use.
**1. Severe Traffic and Highway Safety Concerns**
The proposal would intensify the use of this site significantly. A place of worship and community
hub typically generate concentrated peak-time traffic, particularly during evenings and weekends.
Dorset Road and the surrounding residential streets are not designed to accommodate high visitor
volumes or event-based traffic surges.
The site is located close to the busy A217, a major and heavily trafficked route. Vehicles entering
and exiting the site in higher numbers will add pressure to an already constrained highway
environment, increasing congestion and the risk of collisions.
Furthermore, Brastead Close is not a through road. Increased traffic is likely to result in turning
movements, reversing, and informal manoeuvring within narrow residential streets, creating
additional danger and disturbance for existing residents.
**2. Proximity to Avenue Primary Academy - Safeguarding Concerns**
The site lies in close proximity to Avenue Primary Academy. This is a critical concern.
School drop-off and pick-up times already generate substantial pedestrian and vehicle activity in
the immediate area. Introducing a use that could attract significant numbers of visitors, including
during overlapping hours, will materially increase risk to young and vulnerable children.
The safety of children must be given paramount consideration. Increased traffic movements, on-
street parking pressure, and larger gatherings of adults in close proximity to the school raise
legitimate safeguarding and public safety concerns.
**3. Overdevelopment and Scale**
The proposal includes a part one, part two-storey front and side extension and an increase in
height to part of the building. This represents a substantial intensification compared to the former
ambulance station use.
The cumulative impact of:
* Change of use to mixed F1 and F2
* Increased built mass and height
* Higher footfall and visitor numbers
* Additional servicing and refuse activity
amounts to overdevelopment of a constrained residential site.
**4. Parking Stress and Residential Amenity Impact**
Places of worship frequently generate parking overspill into surrounding residential roads when
on-site capacity is insufficient. This area is already sensitive due to school-related parking
demand.
Overspill parking would:
* Obstruct driveways
* Reduce visibility at junctions
* Impede emergency vehicle access
* Increase neighbour conflict and nuisance
Cycle storage provision does not mitigate the realistic expectation that most visitors will arrive by
car.
**5. Noise and Disturbance**
Community hubs and places of worship can involve amplified speech, evening events, gatherings,
and high visitor turnover. This is materially different from the previous ambulance station use and
would erode the quiet residential character of the area.
**6. Inappropriate Location for Intensified Assembly Use**
This site sits within a residential context with constrained access arrangements. It is not positioned
within a town centre, high street, or area designed to accommodate large-scale community
assembly uses. The proposal is therefore fundamentally unsuited to its surroundings.
---
In summary, this application presents:
* Significant highway safety risks
* Direct impact on vulnerable school children
* Overdevelopment of a constrained site
* Increased congestion near a major road (A217)
* Residential amenity harm
* Inadequate infrastructure to support the scale of use proposed
For these reasons, I respectfully request that the Council refuse this application.

Name: Mrs Nandini Balakumar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Summary Objection
1.The area already suffers from heavy congestion due to the nearby primary school, with parents
parking across residential roads; any additional visitor traffic will worsen an already unsafe
situation.
2. The claim that most attendees will arrive by bicycle is unrealistic, especially for older visitors,
meaning cars will inevitably be used.
3. No dedicated parking is provided, so visitors will spill onto surrounding streets, increasing
congestion and reducing access for residents.
4. The roads are narrow and not designed for the increased vehicle movements associated with a
public gathering space. At the end of Dorset Road there is a block only giving access to
emergency vehicles .
5. Noise and disturbance will increase due to regular gatherings, including evenings and
weekends, affecting nearby homes.
6.The proposal shifts the area from a residential setting to a more commercial/institutional use,
which is unsuitable for this location.
7. Many supporters listed on the application appear to live outside the borough, suggesting the
facility will attract nonlocal users and further increase traffic.

Name: Mr Apparao Nagandla
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to the above planning application for the proposed place
of worship at Sui Generis (Ambulance station).
My objection is based on the following material planning considerations:
Traffic Impact and Road Safety:
The proposed development is expected to generate significant vehicular traffic, particularly during
peak worship times which are likely to coincide with existing rush hour periods. The surrounding
road network already experiences congestion during morning and afternoon peak hours. The
additional traffic volume may exacerbate congestion, increase journey times, and reduce overall
road safety.
Proximity to Primary School :
The site is adjacent to / in close proximity to a primary school. During school drop-off and pick-up
times, the area is already heavily congested with parents, buses, and pedestrians. The
introduction of additional traffic during similar time periods raises serious concerns regarding
pedestrian safety, especially for young children. Increased vehicle movements, parking pressure,
and potential illegal parking could heighten the risk of accidents.
Parking Pressure and Over-spill :
The surrounding residential streets have limited parking capacity. There is a high likelihood of over
spill parking into nearby residential roads during services and events, which would inconvenience
residents and restrict access for emergency vehicles.
Need of the Prayer room/site:
We already have so many prayer sites in and around Sutton within short distance, how many more
we needed? I think it is time to stop giving permissions to any type of religious prayer rooms in the
residential area.
Noise and Disturbance:
Regular gatherings, special events, and increased footfall may result in noise and general
disturbance to the surrounding residential neighborhood, particularly during early mornings,
evenings, or weekends.
Suitability of Location:
Given the scale and expected visitor numbers, this site may not be appropriate for such a
development without significant infrastructure upgrades or a comprehensive traffic management
strategy.

Name: Mrs Kavitha N
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This development is likely to increase traffic congestion and noise near the school and
throughout the surrounding residential neighborhoods

Name: Mr Venkatesh Pachipulusu
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Planning decisions must be evidence-led and based on an objective assessment of
local need.
The 2021 Census confirms that the Muslim and Hindu populations in Sutton are identical in size
(7.0%). However, the borough and its immediate borders already feature a significant number of
functional masjid and Islamic centres, including:
- Sutton Central Masjid (25 Carshalton Rd, Sutton SM1 4LF)
- Sutton Islamic Centre (62 Oakhill Rd, Sutton SM1 3AG)
- Muslim Cultural & Welfare Association of Sutton (Wentworth Hall, 80 Ruskin Rd, Carshalton SM5
3DH)
- Masjid Abu Bakar Al-Siddiq (The Market, Wrythe Ln, Rosehill SM5 1JQ)
- The Baitul Futuh Mosque and the Morden Islamic Community Centre (Massive regional facilities
in Morden).
Granting permission for another facility while other faith groups (and the 32.3% of residents with
"No Religion") lack adequate neutral, shared community spaces is a clear breach of the NPPF
Paragraph 98 requirement to "plan positively for the provision of shared spaces... for all sections
of the community."

Address: 45 Kew Crescent North Cheam Sutton SM3 9RU
Name: Mr Karthik K
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Its a quiet residential place. So in order to secure the quiet and calm around the place, I
would object and suggest that a land appropriately bigger in size is provided outside this
residential place. Instead of trying to convert a place thats already in place, its better to build
outside in a new upcoming area.

Name: Dr Mansoora Arshad
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my full support for the planning application to convert the former
ambulance centre in Belmont into a community space for prayers and meetings.
This facility is genuinely needed within the local community. The Muslim community in Belmont
has grown over the years, yet there is currently no suitable, purpose-built space to accommodate
prayers and community gatherings. At present, members meet in limited space at St John Hall,
which is not designed for this purpose and does not adequately serve the needs of a progressive
and growing community.
The building has been unused for some time. Its conversion into a community space represents a
positive and constructive use of an otherwise dormant site. It would be far more beneficial for the
area to have a managed, community-led facility than to see the site sold for high-density
residential development, which could create significantly greater and more permanent traffic and
parking pressures.
Concerns have been raised regarding traffic, parking and safety, particularly given the nearby
primary school. These matters can be managed through appropriate planning conditions, clear
parking guidance and responsible stewardship by the community. Prayer times are structured and
predictable, and peak usage would not coincide with school drop-off and pick-up times on most
weekdays. A community facility of this nature is unlikely to generate the same continuous daily
traffic as alternative commercial or residential developments.
Importantly, this project promotes inclusion, cohesion and collaboration within Belmont. Providing
a safe and dedicated space for community gathering strengthens social responsibility and
encourages constructive engagement with the wider neighbourhood. Rejecting such an application
risks creating unnecessary division rather than fostering mutual understanding.
I respectfully urge the planning authority to consider the long-term social benefit this facility will
bring and to approve the application.

Name: Mrs Aktar Khan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
This will be a very useful addition to the local community and area. Benefitting the
neighbourhood. Providing community gatherings, prayers, education, improved health and mental
well being for the vulnerable. The area is close to many to allow walking. there's plenty of parking
spaces within the centre. The centre can also help keep youths busy with educational and leisure
activities rather than wander around in streets that could lead to anti social behaviour.
With an aging population, elderly members can walk to improve their health and fitness. Attend for
gatherings, make friends and prayers too.
As an experienced high school teacher in Sutton, I can say this will definatly help with community
development. I love living in Cheam Sutton.
I look forward to seeing this approved soon by Sutton Council.

Name: Mrs Sabina Osmani
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Ms Davidson,
I am writing to support Planning Application DM2025/01591 for the Ambulance Station, Dorset
Road, Belmont SM2 6JH.
I have lived locally for over 12 years and care deeply about ensuring development strengthens,
rather than disrupts, our community. Having reviewed the proposal, I support the change of use to
a mixed Class F1 (Place of Worship) and Class F2 (Community Hub) facility, together with the
proposed extensions.
The application offers meaningful social value. In particular, the provision of dedicated space and
programmes for women, alongside educational activities for children and wider health and
wellbeing initiatives, will provide inclusive and supportive facilities that are often limited locally.
Spaces that encourage participation, learning and connection are especially important for women
and families.
The physical changes proposed are modest and appear in keeping with the surrounding
residential area. In my view, the use is relatively low intensity compared with other potential
developments. The 12 on-site parking spaces, including 2 disabled bays, along with cycle racks
and the retention of greenery, demonstrate a considered and responsible approach to accessibility
and sustainability. The staggering of Friday prayers to avoid school pick-up times further reflects
sensitivity to local concerns.
Overall, I do not believe the proposal will result in any significant traffic, noise or light impacts, and
I respectfully ask that the Council approves the application.
Kind Regards,
Sabina Osmani

Name: Mr Mehdi Girach
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am a local resident writing in support of the proposed change of use from the former
ambulance station on Dorset Road to a mixed-use Place of Worship (Class F1) and Community
Hub (Class F2).
This proposal represents the positive reuse of an existing site. The building is already in
community-related use, and this application seeks to continue serving the public through worship,
education, and broader community activities. Retaining and improving an existing structure is
preferable to vacancy or deterioration and aligns with sustainable development principles.
Traffic and School Pick Up Concerns:
The daily prayer times are spread throughout the day and do not coincide with school drop off and
pick up times. The short duration of prayers and the distribution of attendance across five daily
prayer times mean that visitor flow is staggered rather than concentrated.
In addition:
- Attendance at daily prayer is typically modest, with larger gathering mainly limited to Friday
lunchtime.
- Friday prayers occur during working hours, when general residential parking pressure is lower.
- The proposal includes cycle storage, encouraging sustainable travel.
- Many worshippers are likely to be local residents who will walk.
The traffic profile of a place of worship differs significantly from venues that generate concentrated
evening or weekend surges.
Community Benefit and Youth Engagement:
The inclusion of Class F2 (Community Hub) use is important. The property will not function solely
as a place of worship, but as a structured environment for:
- Youth activities
- Educational support and mentoring
- Social development programmes
- Community events and wellbeing initiatives
Providing constructive, supervised spaces for young people strengthens social cohesion and
reduces antisocial behaviour. A well-managed community hub benefits the wider area, not just
congregants.
Community Resilience and Social Infrastructure:
Modern planning increasingly recognises the importance of social infrastructure, facilities that
strengthen community networks and resilience.
Places of worship and community hubs often:
- Coordinate volunteer networks
- Provide charitable initiatives
- Offer mental wellbeing support
- Assist vulnerable residents during periods of hardship
The COVID 19 pandemic highlighted the importance of trusted local institutions. Facilities like this
serve as stabilising anchors within neighbourhoods, reducing pressure on statutory services and
strengthening social cohesion.
This proposal enhances Belmont's social infrastructure in a structured, managed way.
Public Health and Wellbeing:
Places of worship and community hubs contribute to mental health and wellbeing by reducing
loneliness and fostering belonging. Social isolation is a recognised public health issue. A well
managed, welcoming space where people can gather responsibly supports preventative wellbeing
outcomes.
Planning is not only about buildings, it is about creating healthy communities.
Environmental Sustainability and Localism:
The site serves a local catchment. Many worshippers are likely to live within walking distance.
Encouraging local access to worship and community facilities reduces the need for longer
journeys elsewhere.
The inclusion of cycle storage demonstrates commitments to sustainable transport. Reusing an
existing building also reduces the carbon impact associated with demolition and full
redevelopment.
Established Community and Worship Use in the Area:
It is also relevant that places of worship already operate within the wider Belmont area,
demonstrating that such uses are compatible with the surrounding residential character. The
proposal therefore does not introduce an unfamiliar land use, but continues an established pattern
of community and faith based facilities serving local residents.
Noise and Disturbance:
There is no proposal for externally amplified calls to prayer. Prayer services are orderly and quiet.
Worshippers typically arrive and leave in a respectful and gradual manner, and there is no reason
to assume prolonged outdoor congregation or disturbance.
Compared with other uses permitted within Class F1 or F2, a place of worship is relatively low
impact in terms of noise and disturbance.
Investment and Site Improvement:
The proposal includes a part one, part two-storey extension and improvements that will enhance
the appearance and functionality of the building. Provision for refuse and cycle storage indicates
thoughtful design and responsible operation. This is a long-term investment into a currently
underutilised site.
Economic and Civic Contribution:
Faith and community institutions often:
- Employ local contractors
- Support local suppliers
- Organise charitable fundraising and outreach
This contributes positively to the local micro-economy and civic life.
Conclusion:
This proposal represents:
- Sustainable reuse of an existing building
- Managed activity patterns
- Youth and community development opportunities
- Strengthening of local social infrastructure
- Investment in the built environment
- Support for wellbeing and cohesion
- Low-impact operational characteristics
For these reasons, I respectfully urge the Council to approve this application.

Name: Mr Brian Godley
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Sir
I am writing to formally object to the proposed increase in parking on nearby residential roads
associated with the new mosque community hub.
According to the proposal, this development is expected to generate an additional 70 vehicles with
a further 80 vehicles during peak overlap times. This level of increase is wholly unsuitable for the
surrounding roads and will have a serious and lasting impact on safety, accessibility and residents
quality of life.
I am severely sight impaired. For me, roads with heavy on-street parking presents an extreme and
immediate danger. Parked vehicles significantly reduce visibility, force pedestrians to cross
between cars and obscure approaching traffic. Attempting to cross road in these conditions is not
merely inconvenient - it is hazardous and places me at constant risk of serious injury.
Blocked and narrowed roads also prevent safe navigation along pavements and crossings.
Increased traffic congestion drivers searching for parking, and vehicles mounting pavements
further compound these dangers. The proposal fails to adequately consider the needs of disable
residents, which is deeply concerning.
In addition to daytime risks, the night time impact must be taken seriously. the movement of up to
250 people leaving the site, with engines running doors slamming and vehicles manoeuvring late
into the evening will create excessive noise and disturbance. For someone with a visual
impairment increased night time traffic also dramatically heightens danger, as visibility is further
reduced. and hazards are harder to detect.
The development will fundamentally change the character and safety of the area. The current road
infrastructure is not designed to support this volume of additional parking or traffic, and no
adequate mitigation has been proposed to address the risks posed to pedestrians, particularly
those with disabilities and children.
I respectfully urge the planning authority to reconsider this proposal, give proper weight to
disability access and children safety concerns, and reject any plans that would result in increased
on-street parking in this area.
Thank you for taking the time to consider this objection.
Yours faithfully
Brian Godley
9 Balmoral Way
Belmont SM26PD

Address: 11 Lincoln Terrace York Road Cheam SM2 6HE
Name: Mr Vineet Bhati
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I wish to formally object to planning application DM2025/01591.
My objection is based on material planning considerations, in particular highway safety, parking
stress, pedestrian safety, and the impact on residential amenity arising from the site's close
proximity to a primary school and surrounding residential properties.The proposed development is
located in close proximity to a primary school. The intensified use of the site is likely to generate
increased vehicle movements and pedestrian footfall, particularly during peak school drop-off and
pick-up times. This raises serious concerns regarding highway safety, congestion, and the safety
of children, parents, and other pedestrians using the surrounding streets and pavements. The
proposal therefore conflicts with Sutton Local Plan Policy P26 (Transport and Parking), which
seeks to ensure that development does not result in unacceptable impacts on highway safety, and
with NPPF paragraphs 110 and 111, which require developments to provide safe and suitable
access for all users and to avoid severe residual impacts on the road network.
The application does not demonstrate that sufficient on-site parking is provided to accommodate
the scale and nature of the proposed use. This is likely to result in overspill parking on nearby
residential streets, leading to obstruction of pavements, reduced visibility at junctions, and
additional safety risks, particularly for school children. This is contrary to Sutton Local Plan Policy
P26 and NPPF paragraph 110, which emphasise the importance of safe, well-managed movement
and parking arrangements.
The proposal would also significantly increase pedestrian activity in an area already subject to
high levels of pedestrian movement associated with the nearby primary school. The application
fails to adequately assess the impact of this increased footfall on pedestrian safety, pavement
capacity, and the overall safety of the local environment for children. This conflicts with Sutton
Local Plan Policy P26 and NPPF paragraph 92, which seeks to promote safe, inclusive, and
healthy communities, particularly for children and vulnerable users.
In addition, the intensified use of the site is likely to result in increased noise and disturbance from
arrivals, departures, and associated activities, particularly during evenings and weekends. This
would adversely affect the living conditions of nearby residents and is contrary to Sutton Local
Plan Policy P14 (Residential Quality) and NPPF paragraph 185, which require development to be
appropriate for its location and not give rise to unacceptable levels of noise or disturbance.
Overall, the proposal represents an over-intensification of use that is not appropriate for this
predominantly residential location. It fails to respect the character of the area or safeguard
residential amenity, contrary to Sutton Local Plan Policies P12 (Design) and P14, and NPPF
paragraph 130, which requires development to be sympathetic to local character and establish a
safe and high-quality environment.
For the reasons set out above, I respectfully request that the Council gives careful consideration to
these material planning objections when determining this application.

Name: Mr Alagarsamy Kuppamuthu
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Subject: Objection to Planning Application DM2025/01591
Address: Ambulance Station, Dorset Road, Belmont, SM2 6JH
Proposal: Change of use from Sui Generis (Ambulance Station) to mixed use Classes F1 (Place of
Worship) and F2 (Community Hub) with extensions and height increase
Dear Ms Davidson,
I write to formally object to planning application DM2025/01591 relating to the Ambulance Station
on Dorset Road, Belmont.
My objection is based on material planning considerations, particularly the impact on highway
safety, traffic congestion, parking stress, residential amenity, and community safety.
1. Traffic Generation and Highway Safety
The proposed change of use from a former ambulance station to a mixed-use Place of Worship
(Class F1) and Community Hub (Class F2) will significantly intensify activity at the site.
Places of worship and community hubs typically generate concentrated peak-time traffic,
particularly:
Evenings, afternoon and weekends
Religious gatherings and events
Community functions and celebrations
Dorset Road and the surrounding residential streets already experience congestion, particularly
during school drop-off and pick-up times. The additional vehicle movements associated with this
proposal would materially worsen:
Traffic congestion
Road safety conditions
Pedestrian safety
Access for emergency vehicles
This intensification is inappropriate in a predominantly residential area already operating under
constrained highway conditions.
2. Parking Stress and Overspill
The area currently suffers from significant parking pressure, especially during school hours. The
proposed development does not appear to provide sufficient on-site parking to accommodate peak
attendance levels.
As a result, there is a high likelihood of:
Overspill parking into surrounding residential streets
Obstruction of driveways
Illegal or inconsiderate parking
Increased risk to children and parents walking to and from school
This would have a direct and ongoing adverse impact on residential amenity and highway safety.
3. Increased Footfall and Community Impact
The proposed use would draw visitors from outside the immediate neighbourhood. While
community facilities are important, the scale and nature of this mixed-use proposal raise concern
regarding:
Increased footfall in a quiet residential area
Congregation of large numbers of people before and after events
Noise and disturbance from gatherings
Loss of residential character
The regular presence of unfamiliar visitors and high turnover of attendees changes the character
and dynamics of what is currently a relatively settled residential environment.
4. Scale and Massing
The proposal includes a part one, part two-storey extension and an increase in building height.
This represents a substantial intensification compared to the previous use and may result in:
Overdevelopment of the site
Visual harm to the streetscape
Increased dominance within a residential context
The cumulative impact of increased scale and intensified use must be properly assessed.
Conclusion
In summary, the proposed development would:
Exacerbate existing traffic congestion
Create significant parking stress
Increase risks to school children and pedestrians
Harm residential amenity
Intensify use beyond what is appropriate for this location
For these reasons, I respectfully request that planning permission for application DM2025/01591
be refused.
Yours sincerely,
Alagarsamy Kuppamuthu
30 Avenue Road
SM2 6JB

Name: Miss Nabiha Noor
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
As a Sutton resident for over seven years, I write in strong support of Planning
Application DM2025/01591. I believe this development represents a significant positive step for
our evolving community.
The proposed centre will provide:
1. Community space for children, women, and men, including recreation and youth activities
(social, sports, education). The planned educational activities for children and a dedicated safe
space for women will provide an invaluable platform for females in the community to connect and
support each other.
2. A place for community wellbeing events and positive guidance, promoting health, social
connection, and personal development for residents of all ages.
3. A dedicated local space for worship.
Crucially, this venue will also be open to non-Muslims for various functions and events, and I
eagerly anticipate the many other communal engagements that will be facilitated, fostering a
peaceful, safe, and vibrant community for all.
The centre's location being within safe walking distance for many residents will strongly encourage
locals to walk rather than drive, reducing reliance on cars and helping to strengthen
neighbourhood relationships. Most attendees are expected to walk to the site.
Concerns regarding highway safety and noise levels appear to be overstated. It is important to
note that the site previously operated as a 24-hour ambulance station with continuous vehicle
movements. The proposed community centre, with its clearly defined and more limited opening
hours, represents a positive improvement, likely resulting in lower overall noise and activity levels
compared to its previous use. Attendance will be lower than the nearby Avenue School, and peak
times (e.g., Friday 1-2 pm) will not coincide with school opening/closing hours. This peak
attendance already occurs at the more congested St John's Church Hall. Similarly, noise concerns
are unfounded; attendees are peaceful, only soft drinks are permitted, and no music will be
played, consistent with similar establishments operating hours.
I commend the responsible parking management measures described in the application, including
staggering Friday prayers and avoiding school drop-off and pick-up times. This demonstrates a
considerate approach to traffic management. Overall, I do not believe the proposal will cause
significant traffic, light, or noise issues.
For these reasons, I respectfully urge the Council to approve Planning Application DM2025/01591,
recognising the significant and diverse benefits it will bring to the local community.

By opening up a new source of frantic activity and sizeable traffic to this site in question, for

Planning body needs to observe concerns around unnecessarily imperilling the lives of residents,

Allowing high volume footfall into the site in question drives coach and horses through the

I drive through the stretch often, and have started avoiding driving past it owing to the utter chaos

Importantly, there are several places of worship/community centres in the vicinity for the said faith,

The drawbacks of the proposal far outweigh its merits.

Lastly, and importantly, many of the comments for supporting this application have out-of-borough

Name: Mrs Janet Kelly
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application

Name: Mr Andrew Fellows
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Commenter Type:
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment Reasons:
Comment: I am resubmitting my objection with a 2nd version after the Council only today put up
notices near the application site to give residents time to comment. I wish to formally object to the
above planning application, given the events of the last few days, the clear signal that there is a
race to submit supporting and objecting comments on the Council website and the fact that local
residents are not being consulted in full. I do support the provision of facilities for the entire
community in Sutton and Belmont but it is clear this proposal fails to meet those requirements,
such as those as set out in the community cohesion and social integration stipulations, set out in
the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF) dated December 2024 and supported by the
findings of the 2016 Casey Review which holds precedence here. This application is for the benefit
of one faith group only, not for the wider Belmont community and certainly not for residents of
Dorset Road, Avenue Road, York Road, Holland Avenue, Belmont High Street, Belmont Road,
Queens Avenue, Kings' Road, Cross Road and Balmoral Way who will all suffer from both
increased vehicular traffic in the area, as well as increased usage of currently inadequate public
transport (whether bus or train), should the application succeed. Many objectors have pointed out,
as do I, that is not a question of NIMBYS (Not in my backyard) and/or house price or other societal
concerns to be absolutely clear, but a question of what happens to a quiet residential area which is
already over-stretched in terms of infrastructure and overstretched in terms of transportation flows
and unacceptable parking, as recognised by the Council. Also, we have not been informed in any
way, until the Council put up two notices on lampposts which are easily overlooked, today 23rd
February 2026, of what was being planned and only have 21 days to object. This time is
insufficient given the thoroughness with which the applications has not only sought professional
advice, but also received pre-application feedback from the Council. It seems the residents of
Belmont do not matter and will not be given sufficient time to respond. I would further state that,
should the Council approve the application, it would represent a failure to meet the NPPF (2024)
Requirements for Social Interaction. Under Paragraph 96(a) of the NPPF (Dec 2024), planning
decisions must aim to achieve healthy,inclusive, and safe places that "promote social interaction,
including opportunities for meetings
between people who might not otherwise come into contact with each other," this means people of
all faiths, not just one. The current proposal is designed as a specialized, single-use, single-faith
facility. By its nature and the planned scale, the facility does not facilitate the broad spectrum of
social mixing and interaction, as required by the Framework cited above and will not do so. The
planning application, when one reads the detail, also refers to visitors being welcome and groups
of up to 250, (I assume it will be more than this!), being present for Friday prayers and for special
events. The number of parking spaces available for that group is woefully low. Furthermore , I
request the Council to note that, instead of creating a "shared space," a successful application
risks creating a "closed space." The NPPF's Paragraph 98 requires
that planning for shared spaces must benefit "all sections of the community." Given that this
proposal serves a narrow demographic, it fails the "all sections" test of the NPPF in totality.
2. There is an even greater risk of Social Segregation (The Casey Review). The Casey Review
(2016), which remains a cornerstone of UK integration policy, warns explicitly
against the formation of "parallel lives" or ghettos. The Review highlights how concentrated faith
facilities can accelerate residential clustering and reduce everyday mixing between different ethnic
and religious groups.The proposed development, due to its [size/intensity of use], risks
consolidating a single faith-based enclave with ever larger numbers driving to it in this entirely
residential part of Belmont and Sutton. This directly contradicts the Borough's duty and legal
responsibility, in my view, to foster a cohesive community where residents from different
backgrounds interact regularly and to preserve residential environments. The Casey Review also
argues that integration is a "two-way street"; this proposal, however, represents a "one-way" use
of land that offers little to the wider non-aligned community or other other religious groups in the
area.
3. Disproportionality Relative to Local Need (2021 Census)
According to the 2021 Census data for Sutton, the religious landscape of the borough is highly
diverse, which I welcome being married to someone who is of another ethnic group, but also
significantly secular, (unfortunately in my view!):
Christian: 45.8%
No Religion: 32.3%
Muslim: 7.0%, (Belmont in 2021 was 9.43% only in the specific ward)
Hindu: 7.0%
With nearly one-third, (32.3%) of Sutton's population identifying as having "no Religion," and other
groups such as the Hindu community being underserved, there is a demonstrable and greater
strategic need for neutral, secular community spaces that serve the entire population of the
Belmont ward and the London Borough of Sutton, if one were to grant such applications. Granting
a large-scale, single-faith facility for a demographic representing 7% of the borough/9.43% of the
Belmont ward admittedly is totally disproportionate, especially if that facility does not provide
significant, guaranteed access for the 93% of residents who do not share that faith or have
another faith to use that local facility. In addition there are already faith/worship places (6 in total)
available in the borough of Sutton, representing more than adequate provision for the 7%, as well
as further large-scale provision in Morden.The proposal in its current form undermines the
"Cohesion" mandate of the NPPF 2024 and ignores the integration warnings of the Casey Review.
It fails to reflect the actual demographic needs of Sutton as evidenced by the 2021 Census. On
this basis alone, the application should be rejected. However, my main point is not around
integration but parking and traffic and woeful infrastructure in the London Borough of Sutton and in
Belmont particularly, a mere few miles or km from the centre of our capital city.
5. Problems with Traffic and Parking and Infrastructure. Furthermore, the traffic flow, the parking
situation from drop-off and pick-ups from the Avenue Primary Academy, (for which we have
evidence of today only and are collecting further evidence), demonstrates clearly double-parking,
parking on double yellow lines, parking in front of people's driveways, traffic chaos, and the
inherent danger to schoolchildren being brought to and from school. Clearly, the school children
and their parents cannot take responsibility for the traffic situation and they cannot find parking
elsewhere to bring their (often young) children to school, and these children in today's time cannot
walk to school alone, it is far too dangerous. To then impose further traffic and potential danger to
those children is quite frankly unacceptable and the Council would not be fulfilling its duty to all
residents of Sutton and to those schoolchildren, if the traffic, traffic flow, parking situation, lack of
adequate infrastructure in Belmont and Sutton, lack of controlled parking zones, (which we should
have in this area and for which I intend to apply), and inadequate consideration of resident's
wishes are simply ignored. The assertions made in the application have not been tested, in
respect of surveys of parking done at midnight, and only on one Friday, this is frankly insufficient,
and it would be remiss, if not to say negligent, of the Council not to conduct its own independent,
impact survey on parking, traffic flows, effects on the already overstretched situation with Avenue
Primary parents' parking at drop-off and pick-up times, examining the situation at those times, (for
which I have taken 67 photos today alone), and the wishes of the majority of local residents, (that
is those in Dorset Road and the roads adjoining Dorset Road), many of whose children walk to
school to have safe, undisturbed access for their children, as well as the desire of residents for
peace and quiet. This is why we moved here. We already face a chaotic situation in terms of traffic
flow at such times and the Council should not permit this to get worse.
6. The Environmental Health Protection Act of 1990 also requires the Council to protect the health
of residents of the Borough and its wards and illegal, inconsiderate and disrespectful parking, as
witnessed today during school drop-offs and pick-ups, (which is no fault of the Avenue Primary
and its parents), already stretch the tolerance and our physical infrastructure, (which is outdated)
to its maximum.
7. I would request the Council, in addition to the requests above, to carry out an environmental
impact assessment before any planning application is approved to look at the effects of Friday
prayers, weekly prayers, late-night sessions, blocked driveways, double and illegal parking and
the potential effects of overloading the locality which already suffers from an intolerable parking
and traffic situation. The local area simply cannot handle the planned number - and I believe this is
a low estimate - number of worshippers which might be expected and the effect on the roads listed
above will be totally unacceptable, given we know what 'overloading' looks like from the lack of
infrastructure, (road parking and access), for the Avenue School alone.
8. This planning application will make a bad situation worse for all in the Borough and I request the
Council planning committee to reject the application in its entirety and to order relevant
investigations into the traffic, parking and environmental impacts, should the Council have doubts.

Address: 36 Foxley Court Christchurch Park Sutton SM2 5UE
Name: Mrs Mamta Verma
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to the above-referenced planning application. I have been
a local resident for the last 10 years, having previously been a resident of a flat adjoining the
boundary of the site address in 2019-20.
While I recognize the importance of providing community infrastructure, this specific proposal
conflicts with several key requirements of the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF)
December 2024 and Sutton Local Plan 2018, and raises significant concerns regarding long-term
community integration and equitable resource allocation.
1. Conflict with NPPF Paragraphs 96 and 98 (Social Interaction & Cohesion)
The NPPF (2024) is clear that planning decisions must aim to achieve "healthy, inclusive and safe
places." Specifically:
- Paragraph 96(a) requires developments to "promote social interaction... between people who
might not otherwise come into contact with each other."
- Paragraph 98 emphasizes the need for shared spaces and community facilities that serve all
sections of the community.
By proposing a specialized facility for one specific faith group, this development risks undermining
these goals. It creates a "destination" for a specific demographic, which limits the "chance contact"
between diverse residents that is essential for a unified borough of Sutton.
2. Integration Risks and the Casey Review (2016)
The Casey Review warned of the risks of "parallel lives" and residential clustering. The creation of
large, concentrated faith facilities often acts as a catalyst for "enclave" formation, leading to:
- Reduced everyday mixing between different ethnic and religious groups.
- Diminished shared British identity and social integration.
- The very "ghettoisation" cautioned against in national social policy.
Experience in nearby areas, such as Morden, demonstrates how significant demographic shifts
and "clustering" often followed after the establishment of large-scale, single-use faith centers. In a
borough already facing demographic pressures, the Council should prioritize integrated
infrastructure that brings people together rather than facilities that entrench separation.
Similar single faith demographic shifts have also been seen in the last 20-30 years across London
boroughs namely Tooting, Tower Hamlet, Newham, Barking and Dagenham, Waltham Forest,
Brent, Ealing and Hounslow. Trends are also evident across the country: Watford, Birmingham,
Bradford, Leeds and many more. I request that the Council conduct a formal study across these
boroughs and counties to assess how a single faith centre creates a gradual and structural dilution
of diversity of communities.
3. Conflict with Sutton Local Plan (2018) - Policies 21 and 23
The Council's own Local Plan emphasizes the need for social infrastructure that fosters integration
rather than separation:
- Policy 21 (Social Infrastructure): This policy requires the Council to ensure that social
infrastructure is provided in a way that is "sustainable" and "inclusive." By approving an additional
specialized facility for a group that is already well-served (as outlined below), the Council risks
failing its duty to ensure a balanced distribution of community assets for all sections of the
community.
- Policy 23 (Environmental Protection and Impact): This policy addresses the need for
developments to be appropriate to their location. Given the existing concentration of faith-specific
facilities in the immediate area (as outlined below), a further single-use mosque would entrench a
"mono-cultural" use of land, which directly contradicts the goal of maintaining the diverse,
suburban character of Sutton.
4. Disproportionate Allocation relative to Census Data
According to the 2021 ONS Census data for London borough of Sutton, the demographic
breakdown is as follows:
Religion. % of Population. Existing Provision
Christians 45.8% (95,983 residents) Multiple operational churches
No religion 32.3% (67,779 residents) -
Muslim. 7.0% (14,722 residents) Multiple operational mosques
Hindu. 7.0% (14,663 residents) Zero
Other Faiths
Combined. 1.0%. Zero
Granting further specialized provision for a group that already possesses multiple functional sites-
while other groups of identical or similar size have no dedicated worship space-is a failure of
equitable resource allocation. I request that the Council conduct a formal fact-finding exercise to
audit existing places of worship against the actual local population needs before making a
determination
5. Evidence of Existing Provision (Disproportionality)
Planning decisions must be evidence-led and based on an objective assessment of local need.
The 2021 Census confirms that the Muslim and Hindu populations in Sutton are identical in size
(7.0%). However, the borough and its immediate borders already feature a significant number of
functional masjid and Islamic centres, including:
- Sutton Central Masjid (25 Carshalton Rd, Sutton SM1 4LF)
- Sutton Islamic Centre (62 Oakhill Rd, Sutton SM1 3AG)
- Muslim Cultural & Welfare Association of Sutton (Wentworth Hall, 80 Ruskin Rd, Carshalton SM5
3DH)
- Masjid Abu Bakar Al-Siddiq (The Market, Wrythe Ln, Rosehill SM5 1JQ)
- The Baitul Futuh Mosque and the Morden Islamic Community Centre (Massive regional facilities
in Morden).
Granting permission for another facility while other faith groups (and the 32.3% of residents with
"No Religion") lack adequate neutral, shared community spaces is a clear breach of the NPPF
Paragraph 98 requirement to "plan positively for the provision of shared spaces... for all sections
of the community."
6. Socio-Economic Considerations & Land Use
While the British Muslim community makes a material annual contribution to the UK economy,
ONS data indicates that local Muslim residents often face higher rates of economic inactivity
(51.4%) and housing deprivation compared to other groups.
Under Sutton Local Plan Policy 21, the council should prioritize infrastructure that addresses these
specific socio-economic needs for the entire community. A neutral, multi-use community hub
(vocational training, youth services, or recreational space) would provide greater "leveling up"
benefits and foster integration, whereas a specialized religious facility risks encouraging residential
clustering and the "enclave" formation cautioned against in the Casey Review (2016).
The comparative Economic Data from ONS Census 2021 is provided below for ready reference.
Conclusion
Under NPPF Paragraph 96 (December 2024), the Council has a duty to ensure that planning
decisions do not "undermine community cohesion." The clustering of specialized faith facilities, as
warned by the Casey Review, leads to parallel lives rather than integrated ones.
I urge the Planning Committee to refuse this application and reflect the diversity of the borough
and serves all residents equally, regardless of faith or background.
Yours sincerely
Mamta Verma
Comparative Economic Data (ONS Census 2021)
When comparing economic activity and household wealth across community groups, the data
shows distinct patterns:
Metric (ONS 2021)
1. Employment Rate:
- Hindus: (16-64)Higher: ~75%-80%
- Christian: Moderate: ~70%
- Muslim: Lower: 51.4%
2. High-Income Households
- Hindus: Highest: ~41% earn £1k+ weekly
- Christian: Average: ~30% earn £1k+ weekly
- Muslims: Lower: ~18% earn £1k+ weekly
3. Housing Tenure
- Hindus: High owner-occupation
- Christian: High owner-occupation
- Muslims: High social renting (26.6%)
4. Economic Activity
- Hindus: Very high
- Christian: Stable
- Muslim: High % of students/home-carers

Address: 4 Heyward Court Overton Road Sutton SM2 6JY
Name: Mrs uzma shirwani
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly support Application DM2025/01591
I have lived in this area for 31 years, and in this time I have seen very few establishments that
allow for the community to come together, meet each other and get to know one another.
This is not just about a building, number of people, or parking spaces. It is about the community.
I see the former ambulance site as an ideal location for a community centre that could host free
health check sessions for residents, mother and toddler groups for young parents, and structured
activities for young people, particularly as there is currently no dedicated youth centre in the
surrounding area.
Through BASMA's existing activities such as weekly Friday prayers, monthly talks, community
dinners during Ramadan, and sports sessions for both men and women, strong friendships have
formed and a welcoming and inclusive local network has developed among people who previously
did not know one another. A permanent community centre would allow this positive work to
continue in a more organised and accessible setting.
These activities reflect a genuine commitment to supporting all parts of the community including
elders, young people and families. Programmes such as women's wellbeing sessions, children's
activities and family-focused events are designed to provide a safe and supportive environment for
local residents.
Importantly, the proposed centre would also include a publicly accessible defibrillator on site. This
would be available for use in medical emergencies and would provide an additional community
health resource for the surrounding neighbourhood.
I also wish to reassure local neighbours and the nearby primary school that the centre would offer
positive facilities for the community. It would be a place where young people can take part in
structured activities, women can attend fitness sessions, and older residents can enjoy social
gatherings such as coffee mornings.
The former ambulance station operated on a twenty-four-hour basis, with emergency vehicles
regularly arriving and leaving. A community hub would not operate around the clock and would not
involve constant vehicle movement, resulting in significantly less disturbance to neighbouring
residents.
Moreover, the site would only approach its maximum capacity of 250 during the Friday
congregational prayer. At other times of the day, attendance for regular activities and daily prayers
would be significantly lower. This indicates that demand on parking would be limited for the
majority of the week and that there would not be continuous levels of noise or activity during
daytime hours.
There are also convenient bus routes in the area, and many local members would be able to walk
to the centre.
Overall, I believe this centre would have a positive impact on the wider community, and I hope the
council will view this application as a constructive step for the future.
I kindly ask that this application be approved.
Kind regards,
Uzma Shirwani

Address: Apartment 13 Sovereign Apartments 328 High Street Sutton SM1 1AP

Comment:

The programmes they provide are vital to our local community. Their core focus is bringing people

In addition, the group organises health and fitness classes, which are particularly beneficial for
women. These sessions provide not only physical health benefits but also a safe and supportive
environment where participants feel comfortable attending classes and engaging openly with
people they know and trust.
It is important to emphasise that this group has been running classes and community programmes
in the same area for a considerable time, hiring the local church hall. Therefore, this is not a new
or unfamiliar activity, despite concerns that may have been raised. Their presence has been
consistent, responsible, and positively received by many residents.
Granting this approval would ensure the continuation of a safe, inclusive space that strengthens
community cohesion. The centre also has plans to organise integration tours and community-wide
programmes open to all local residents. These initiatives will further promote understanding,
inclusivity, and fraternity within the Sutton community.
For these reasons, I strongly support this application and believe it will bring lasting social value to
the area.

Name: Mr anthony burnell
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I write to formally object to planning application DM2025/01591 for the proposed
mosque/community center
My objection is based on material planning considerations, specifically highways impact, parking
provision, residential amenity, and noise.
-Traffic Generation, adding to school and bowling club traffic
Under the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF), development should only be approved
where the residual cumulative impacts on the road network are not severe. The proposed use is
likely to generate concentrated peaks of traffic, particularly on Fridays and during religious
festivals and upward of 5 times a day.
The surrounding highway network consists of predominantly residential streets and cul de sacs,
that already experience congestion. Increased vehicle movements at peak times raise concerns
regarding pedestrian safety, obstruction, and access for emergency vehicles. I request that a full
Transport Assessment and robust trip-generation data be scrutinised carefully.
-Parking Provision
The application does not appear to demonstrate adequate on-site parking to meet likely demand.
Inadequate provision risks overspill parking on nearby residential streets, which would adversely
affect residential amenity and highway safety.
Local Plan policies typically require development to provide appropriate parking provision and not
result in unacceptable on-street parking stress. I request that the council assess compliance with
adopted parking standards and consider a parking management condition if approval is
contemplated.
-Noise and Residential Amenity
The NPPF and local development plan policies seek to protect existing residential amenity from
unacceptable noise impacts. The scale of gatherings and associated activity may give rise to noise
from vehicle arrivals/departures, congregation outside the premises, and any amplified sound.
If any external call to prayer or amplified sound is proposed, this could materially affect
neighbouring residents' quiet enjoyment of their homes. A detailed Noise Impact Assessment
should therefore be required. If approved, conditions should restrict external amplification and
control hours of use to safeguard amenity.
-Intensity of Use
The proposed scale and intensity of the development may be out of keeping with the character of
the surrounding residential area. The cumulative impact of traffic, parking, and noise would

Comment:
Hi,
I want to register my objection over few major concerns specific to that area and local community.
1. Traffic Congestion and Safety Risks Near the School:
The proposed site is located in very close proximity to a local school, an area that already
experiences significant congestion during peak dropoff and pickup times. Introducing an additional
use that is likely to generate concentrated footfall and vehicle movements-particularly where peak

The road layout provides limited space for safe manoeuvring, including threepoint turns, and any
increase in vehicle volume will heighten the risk of obstruction, reduced visibility, and potential
accidents involving schoolchildren and other pedestrians. This raises serious safeguarding
concerns that must be carefully evaluated.
Please consider the traffic related to local amenities like Tennis club and bowling club.
2. Loss of an Essential Emergency and Community Service
The current premises serve as a base for ambulance and emergency support services, which
provide critical coverage for residents across the area. Removing this facility would represent a
direct reduction in essential emergency response capacity, impacting the wellbeing and safety of
all members of the community.
In planning policy terms, this constitutes the loss of a vital community asset. Such services are
difficult to relocate without negative operational impact, and once lost, they are unlikely to be
reinstated. It would be far more beneficial to retain or replace this type of universally accessible
facility-one that supports and brings together all residents through essential health and emergency
provision.
3. Impact on Community Cohesion and Balanced Use of Local Infrastructure
Introducing a highintensity use into an already constrained location risks placing additional
pressure on local infrastructure, parking availability, and neighbouring residential amenities.
Community facilities should enhance cohesion and inclusivity; therefore, any replacement for the
ambulance space should ideally serve the broadest crosssection of local residents and address
widely shared needs.
For these reasons, I respectfully request that the planning authority carefully reconsider this
proposal in light of its significant implications for public safety, emergency response provision, and
the overall suitability of this location for a highdensity use.
Thanks

Name: Mr Jayakeerthi Rangaiah
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to the above-referenced planning application. I lived in
SM2 postcode for 8 years and moved to my current address 5 years ago, but have a lot of friends
in and around the area where this construction is proposed.
I fully recognize the importance of providing community infrastructure, however specific proposal
conflicts with several key requirements of the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF)
December 2024 and Sutton Local Plan 2018, and raises significant concerns regarding long-term
community integration and equitable resource allocation.
1. Conflict with NPPF Paragraphs 96 and 98 (Social Interaction & Cohesion)
The NPPF (2024) is clear that planning decisions must aim to achieve "healthy, inclusive and safe
places." Specifically:
- Paragraph 96(a) requires developments to "promote social interaction... between people who
might not otherwise come into contact with each other."
- Paragraph 98 emphasizes the need for shared spaces and community facilities that serve all
sections of the community.
By proposing a specialized facility for one specific faith group, this development risks undermining
these goals. It creates a "destination" for a specific demographic, which limits the "chance contact"
between diverse residents that is essential for a unified borough of Sutton.
2. Integration Risks and the Casey Review (2016)
The Casey Review warned of the risks of "parallel lives" and residential clustering. The creation of
large, concentrated faith facilities often acts as a catalyst for "enclave" formation, leading to:
- Reduced everyday mixing between different ethnic and religious groups.
- Diminished shared British identity and social integration.
- The very "ghettoisation" cautioned against in national social policy.
Experience in nearby areas, such as Morden, demonstrates how significant demographic shifts
and "clustering" often followed after the establishment of large-scale, single-use faith centers. In a
borough already facing demographic pressures, the Council should prioritize integrated
infrastructure that brings people together rather than facilities that entrench separation.
Similar single faith demographic shifts have also been seen in the last 20-30 years across London
boroughs namely Tooting, Tower Hamlet, Newham, Barking and Dagenham, Waltham Forest,
Brent, Ealing and Hounslow. Trends are also evident across the country: Watford, Birmingham,
Bradford, Leeds and many more. I request that the Council conduct a formal study across these
boroughs and counties to assess how a single faith centre creates a gradual and structural dilution
of diversity of communities.
3. Conflict with Sutton Local Plan (2018) - Policies 21 and 23
The Council's own Local Plan emphasizes the need for social infrastructure that fosters integration
rather than separation:
- Policy 21 (Social Infrastructure): This policy requires the Council to ensure that social
infrastructure is provided in a way that is "sustainable" and "inclusive." By approving an additional
specialized facility for a group that is already well-served (as outlined below), the Council risks
failing its duty to ensure a balanced distribution of community assets for all sections of the
community.
- Policy 23 (Environmental Protection and Impact): This policy addresses the need for
developments to be appropriate to their location. Given the existing concentration of faith-specific
facilities in the immediate area (as outlined below), a further single-use mosque would entrench a
"mono-cultural" use of land, which directly contradicts the goal of maintaining the diverse,
suburban character of Sutton.
4. Disproportionate Allocation relative to Census Data
According to the 2021 ONS Census data for London borough of Sutton, the demographic
breakdown is as follows:
Religion. % of Population. Existing Provision
Christians 45.8% (95,983 residents) Multiple operational churches
No religion 32.3% (67,779 residents) -
Muslim. 7.0% (14,722 residents) Multiple operational mosques
Hindu. 7.0% (14,663 residents) Zero
Other Faiths
Combined. 1.0%. Zero
Granting further specialized provision for a group that already possesses multiple functional
siteswhile
other groups of identical or similar size have no dedicated worship space-is a failure of
equitable resource allocation. I request that the Council conduct a formal fact-finding exercise to
audit existing places of worship against the actual local population needs before making a
determination
5. Evidence of Existing Provision (Disproportionality)
Planning decisions must be evidence-led and based on an objective assessment of local need.
The 2021 Census confirms that the Muslim and Hindu populations in Sutton are identical in size
(7.0%). However, the borough and its immediate borders already feature a significant number of
functional masjid and Islamic centres, including:
- Sutton Central Masjid (25 Carshalton Rd, Sutton SM1 4LF)
- Sutton Islamic Centre (62 Oakhill Rd, Sutton SM1 3AG)
- Muslim Cultural & Welfare Association of Sutton (Wentworth Hall, 80 Ruskin Rd, Carshalton SM5
3DH)
- Masjid Abu Bakar Al-Siddiq (The Market, Wrythe Ln, Rosehill SM5 1JQ)
- The Baitul Futuh Mosque and the Morden Islamic Community Centre (Massive regional facilities
in Morden).
Granting permission for another facility while other faith groups (and the 32.3% of residents with
"No Religion") lack adequate neutral, shared community spaces is a clear breach of the NPPF
Paragraph 98 requirement to "plan positively for the provision of shared spaces... for all sections
of the community."
6. Socio-Economic Considerations & Land Use
While the British Muslim community makes a material annual contribution to the UK economy,
ONS data indicates that local Muslim residents often face higher rates of economic inactivity
(51.4%) and housing deprivation compared to other groups.
Under Sutton Local Plan Policy 21, the council should prioritize infrastructure that addresses these
specific socio-economic needs for the entire community. A neutral, multi-use community hub
(vocational training, youth services, or recreational space) would provide greater "leveling up"
benefits and foster integration, whereas a specialized religious facility risks encouraging residential
clustering and the "enclave" formation cautioned against in the Casey Review (2016).
The comparative Economic Data from ONS Census 2021 is provided below for ready reference.
Conclusion
Under NPPF Paragraph 96 (December 2024), the Council has a duty to ensure that planning
decisions do not "undermine community cohesion." The clustering of specialized faith facilities, as
warned by the Casey Review, leads to parallel lives rather than integrated ones.
I urge the Planning Committee to refuse this application and reflect the diversity of the borough
and serves all residents equally, regardless of faith or background.
Yours sincerely
Jayakeerthi Rangaiah

Address: 32 non suchcourtavenue Ewell Ewell, Surrey KT17 2RU
Name: Mrs soumya hegde
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
1. Impact on Residential Character, Traffic and Safety
Belmont is a well-established, quiet residential neighbourhood. I lived in the area for over eight
years and, although I now reside nearby in Ewell, I continue to visit frequently due to close
community ties.
The introduction of a large community hall or place of worship would significantly alter the peaceful
nature of the area and undermine its residential character. Dorset Road already experiences
heavy traffic during peak hours, and this development would inevitably increase congestion,
parking pressure, and road safety risks.
Of particular concern is the close proximity of a primary school. Increased vehicle and pedestrian
traffic associated with the proposed use would create additional safety hazards for children
walking to and from school.
There are also nearby sports and recreational facilities that already contribute to traffic levels.
Further intensification of use would make local roads increasingly difficult to navigate and
unsuitable for a quiet suburban environment.
For these reasons, I strongly object to this proposal and respectfully request that the Council
prioritise the preservation of residential amenity and public safety.
2. Community Cohesion and Integration Considerations (Casey Review 2016)
The Casey Review (2016) highlighted the risks of residential and cultural segregation, often
described as the development of "parallel lives." It warned that large, concentrated single-faith
facilities can contribute to social clustering, which may lead to:
- Reduced day-to-day interaction between different communities
- Weakened social integration and shared civic identity
- The formation of segregated enclaves
Experiences in nearby areas such as Morden, as well as across several London boroughs
including Tooting, Tower Hamlets, Newham, Barking and Dagenham, Waltham Forest, Brent,
Ealing and Hounslow, demonstrate how significant demographic clustering has followed the
establishment of large-scale, single-use religious centres.
Similar trends are evident nationally in towns and cities such as Watford, Birmingham, Bradford
and Leeds.
In a borough already facing pressures on infrastructure and social cohesion, the Council should
prioritise developments that bring diverse communities together through shared, neutral spaces
rather than facilities that risk reinforcing separation.
I respectfully request that the Council undertake a formal assessment of the long-term community
impacts observed in comparable areas before approving additional single-faith developments.
3. Conflict with Sutton Local Plan (2018) - Policies 21 and 23
The proposed development appears to conflict with the Council's own planning framework:
Policy 21 - Social Infrastructure
This policy requires social infrastructure to be sustainable, inclusive and balanced across
communities. Approving additional specialised facilities for groups that are already well-served
risks creating disproportionate provision and undermines equitable access to community
resources.
Policy 23 - Environmental Protection and Impact
Developments must be appropriate to their location and character. Given the existing
concentration of faith-specific facilities within the surrounding area, further intensification of single-
use religious provision would contribute to a monocultural land use pattern that is inconsistent with
Sutton's diverse suburban character.
4. Disproportionate Provision in Relation to Local Demographics
According to the 2021 Office for National Statistics Census for the London Borough of Sutton, the
religious composition is approximately:
- Christian - 45.8% (95,983 residents) - multiple churches
- No religion - 32.3% (67,779 residents) - limited neutral community spaces
- Muslim - 7.0% (14,722 residents) - multiple mosques
- Hindu - 7.0% (14,663 residents) - no dedicated worship facilities
- Other faiths combined - approximately 1%
Despite Muslim and Hindu populations being of equal size, only one group benefits from multiple
dedicated facilities. Granting further specialised provision without addressing this imbalance
represents inequitable resource distribution.
I respectfully request that the Council conduct a borough-wide audit of existing places of worship
and shared community facilities against demographic need before determining this application.
5. Evidence of Existing Provision
Planning decisions must be guided by objective assessments of need. Current provision already
includes several operational Islamic centres within Sutton and nearby areas, including:
- Sutton Central Masjid - Carshalton Road
- Sutton Islamic Centre - Oakhill Road
- Muslim Cultural & Welfare Association of Sutton - Wentworth Hall, Carshalton
- Masjid Abu Bakar Al-Siddiq - Rosehill
- Baitul Futuh Mosque and Morden Islamic Community Centre (major regional facilities)
In contrast, other faith groups and over one-third of residents who identify with no religion lack
access to neutral, shared community infrastructure.
Approving further specialised provision without addressing this imbalance is inconsistent with the
National Planning Policy Framework's requirement to plan positively for inclusive shared spaces
for all sections of the community.

Name: Dr T Baksh
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I would like to record my support for application DM2025/01591. The proposal to
convert the former 24 hour ambulance station into a community hub and place of worship is
exactly the kind of development this area needs.
While the faith provision addresses a specific need in the Muslim community, the community hub
is for all people of different backgrounds and a range of activities proposed, from education to
wellbeing to social events, means this facility will benefit the whole neighbourhood.
Community hubs like the one proposed here are exactly the kind of preventative infrastructure that
the NHS social prescribing model relies on. Providing a local space for wellbeing sessions, drop-
ins, and community activities reduces pressure on GP surgeries and mental health services.
Moreover, the scale of the proposed works is restrained. The extension does not dominate the site
or the street scene, and the height increase is limited to improving the internal functionality of the
building. The external greening will soften the appearance of the site.
It is worth remembering what this site was before. An ambulance station operated here around the
clock, with emergency vehicles coming and going at all hours, sirens, reversing bleepers, engine
noise, and crew changeovers through the night. A community facility with defined operating hours
is a significant step down in terms of noise and disruption for neighbours.
I'd also add that some objectors have raised concerns about noise from prayers and events. The
acoustic evidence shows these concerns are not well-founded. Even at the worst case, noise at
neighbouring properties stays below background levels. The building is well-insulated and the
hours are restricted.
It is also worth mentioning that sutton's own Local Plan identifies community centres and places of
worship as essential social infrastructure under Policy 33. This proposal is exactly what those
policies were written to support. The NPPF requires authorities to plan positively for these uses.
Additionally, the former ambulance station operated 24 hours a day. The proposed facility has
restricted hours. This is a straightforward improvement in terms of the impact on neighbours
during unsociable hours.
In addition, the Belmont area lacks the kind of community infrastructure that other parts of the
borough take for granted. This proposal would provide educational programmes, wellbeing
sessions, social drop-ins, and faith provision, all within walking distance for local residents.
Furthermore, half of all attendees at Friday prayers walk, and fewer than 12% drive alone. The
modal split for this facility is overwhelmingly sustainable. The proposed cycle storage will
encourage even more sustainable travel. This is far better than many other developments the
Council approves.
I'd also add that many women in faith communities are unable to access existing facilities because
they are too far away, not accessible by public transport, or do not provide appropriate spaces.
This proposal solves all three of those problems.
It is also worth mentioning that the planning system requires decisions to be made on material
grounds. This application is policy-compliant, supported by thorough evidence, and addresses a
clear community need. That should be what determines the outcome.
I support the grant of planning permission for DM2025/01591. This is the right use for this site, at
the right time, and it deserves a positive outcome.
