Name: Mr Vijay R
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Comment
1. Existing Traffic Congestion and Peak-Time Pressure
The proposed site is situated within an area that already experiences significant traffic congestion
and pronounced peak-time pressure. The surrounding highway network regularly operates at, or
close to, capacity, particularly during school drop-off and pick-up periods. At these times, vehicle
queuing, constrained manoeuvring space, and limited visibility are frequent and well-established
conditions.
2. Impact of Nearby Primary School
The site lies in close proximity to a primary school with a pupil capacity in excess of 1,000. This
results in exceptionally high levels of pedestrian and vehicular movement during the morning and
afternoon peak periods. Substantial numbers of children, parents, pushchairs, and cyclists use the
adjacent pavements and crossing points, creating a highly sensitive and vulnerable highway
environment.
Any further increase in traffic activity in this location would be likely to materially increase risks to
child safety and further worsen existing congestion.
3. Cumulative Impact from Recreational and Elderly Facilities
In addition to the school-related traffic, the area is also served by recreational facilities, including
tennis courts and a golf facility used by elderly residents. These uses generate ongoing vehicle
movements and parking demand throughout the day and into the evening.
The presence of elderly users is a particularly important consideration, as reduced mobility and
slower crossing times may increase vulnerability and require enhanced highway safety
safeguards.
4. Traffic Generation from Assembly-Based Use
The proposed introduction of a community hall, temple, mosque, church, or other assembly-based
use is likely to generate concentrated arrival and departure patterns, particularly during evenings,
weekends, and special events.
Unlike residential development, assembly uses typically result in simultaneous peak traffic
movements, which can contribute to:
Increased congestion
Greater pressure on on-street parking capacity
Obstructive or unsafe parking behaviour
Reduced visibility at junctions
Restricted access for emergency vehicles
Accordingly, the traffic impacts associated with this form of development would not operate in
isolation, but would instead add to and intensify the existing pressures on the local highway
network.

Name: Dr B Wu
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Statement to Planning Committee - Objection to Planning Application DM2025/01591
Chair, Members of the Committee,
Thank you for the opportunity to comment. I am addressing you today as a local resident to outline
why this application, in its current form, would have a significant and harmful impact on our
community. My concerns relate to noise, traffic, parking, safety, and the cumulative impact of the
proposed use, Inaccurate Representation of Usage - all of which conflict with key policies in the
Sutton Local Plan and the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF).
1. Noise and Late Evening Disturbance
The applicant suggests attendance of around 120 people, but in reality it is not practical to restrict
numbers for Friday prayers. The Council must therefore consider the true maximum capacity of
around 250 people, with activities running until 11pm.
This is a quiet residential area. The dispersal of up to 250 people at 11pm would cause
unavoidable noise and disturbance.
Relevant policies:
- Sutton Local Plan Policy 29 (Amenity) - requires protection of residents from noise and
disturbance.
- NPPF Paragraphs 130(f) and 185(a) - developments must safeguard residential amenity and be
appropriate for their location.
2. Traffic and Highway Safety
The site sits within 100 meters from a primary school with over 1,000 pupils. At school drop off and
pick up times, the roads are already heavily congested, with high pedestrian activity and limited
visibility.
Adding up to 250 additional visitors - particularly during Friday prayers, which may overlap with
school finishing times - would create serious safety risks for children and families.
Relevant policies:
- Policy 34 (Transport and Development) - development must not create or worsen highway
hazards.
- NPPF Paragraph 111 - development should be refused if it results in an unacceptable impact on
highway safety.
3. Parking Pressure
Parking in the area is already extremely limited. The application does not demonstrate how it will
accommodate the parking needs of such a large number of visitors.
Over-spill parking would obstruct driveways, reduce visibility, and increase risks for pedestrians -
particularly schoolchildren.
Relevant policies:
- Policy 37 (Parking) - requires adequate on site parking and avoidance of harmful over-spill.
- NPPF Paragraphs 105-106 - developments must consider local parking pressures and avoid
adverse impacts.
4. Cumulative Impact on the Community
When noise, late night activity, school related congestion, parking pressure, and safety risks are
considered together, the cumulative impact is substantial.
The scale and intensity of the proposed use are not compatible with the residential character of
the area.
Relevant policies:
- Policy 1 (Sustainable Growth) - development must be appropriate to its context.
- Policy 28 (Character and Design) - requires development to respect the surrounding area.
- NPPF Paragraph 92 - planning should support safe, healthy, and inclusive communities.
5. Inaccurate Representation of Usage
The application suggests that the proposal is simply a relocation of existing activities from Belmont
Church. However, this is misleading. Currently, the church is only utilized for Friday afternoon
prayers. Because the Islamic faith requires five daily prayers, seven days a week, the transition to
a dedicated mosque will significantly increase the frequency and intensity of site usage compared
to the current arrangement.
Conclusion
This application conflicts with multiple Local Plan and NPPF policies. It would introduce significant
noise, traffic, parking stress, and safety risks - particularly given the proximity of a large primary
school.
For these reasons, I respectfully ask the Committee to refuse this Planning Application
DM2025/01591.
Thank you.

Comment:
I am writing to formally register my strong objection to planning application
DM2025/01591 on the grounds that the proposed development represents an unsustainable
intensification of use that is fundamentally incompatible with the residential character of the local
area. My primary concern pertains to Highways and Pedestrian Safety; the applicant's transport
assessment appears to significantly underestimate the "trip generation" and peak-time traffic
volume associated with a place of worship of this scale. The surrounding residential infrastructure
is already operating at capacity, and the inevitable "overspill" parking onto narrow neighboring
streets will create hazardous bottlenecks, obstructing access for emergency services and delivery
vehicles while endangering local school children and elderly pedestrians. Furthermore, the
proposed parking provision is mathematically insufficient to accommodate the expected number of
worshippers, which will lead to a permanent and detrimental shift in the local parking equilibrium.
Beyond traffic, the proposal constitutes a severe Loss of Amenity for immediate residents. The
transition from the site's current use to a high-occupancy community hub will result in a substantial
increase in ambient noise levels, specifically regarding the "comings and goings" of visitors during
sensitive early morning and late evening hours. This includes the cumulative noise of car doors,
idling engines, and congregating groups, which will shatter the quiet enjoyment of nearby gardens
and bedrooms. From a Design and Character perspective, the scale, massing, and height of the
proposed structure are out of proportion with the established street scene. The development
appears "cramped" and overbearing, violating Policy 28 of the Sutton Local Plan, which requires
new developments to respect the local context and visual amenity. The height of the building will
likely result in a significant loss of natural light (overshadowing) and a direct loss of privacy for the
occupants of adjoining properties due to overlooking windows.
Finally, the environmental impact of such a large-scale construction on a constrained site has not
been adequately mitigated. The increase in non-porous surfaces raises concerns regarding local
surface water drainage and potential flood risks. The proposed development does not offer
sufficient public benefit to outweigh the significant harm it will cause to the safety, privacy, and
character of this neighborhood. For these reasons-centered on traffic congestion, noise pollution,
and architectural incongruity-I urge the Planning Committee to refuse this application in its entirety
and protect the integrity of our residential community.

Name: Mr Ashish Pawargi
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
We, the residents of Sutton Council, call on London Borough of Sutton and Belmont
ward councillors to refuse Planning Application
DM2025/01591
for the former Ambulance Station on Dorset Road (SM2 6HX), Belmont, which proposes a change
of use from Sui Generis (Ambulance Station) to mixed Class F1 (Place of Worship) and Class F2
(Community Hub), with associated building works.
We support appropriate community facilities in principle. However, we believe this proposal, in this
specific residential location with constrained access, will cause unacceptable harm to local
highway safety, parking, residential amenity, and emergency access unless refused.
Key reasons for objection (material planning grounds)1) School-run pedestrian safety and existing
peak parking stressAvenue Primary Academy has a reported pupil roll of 1,034, meaning the area
experiences very high pedestrian activity (children, parents, buggies) at drop-off/pick-up times,
with additional activity from optional after-school and weekend events. We are concerned that
extra peak-time arrivals, drop-offs and vehicles circulating for parking will reduce visibility at
crossings/junctions and increase risk to children and other pedestrians.
2) Inadequate on-site parking and predictable overspill into residential streetsThe applicant's own
Transport Statement identifies the Friday prayer "changeover" as the busiest period and models a
worst-case overlap scenario of:
* up to 80 vehicles associated with the overlap period;
* only 14 on-site spaces, with an assumption 3 may be used by staff (leaving 11 for attendees);
* therefore 69 vehicles potentially parking on surrounding residential streets in the worst case.We
believe this overspill will lead to obstruction, unsafe manoeuvres, blocked driveways, pavement
parking, reduced visibility and harm to residents' quality of life.
3) Impact on elderly and mobility-impaired residentsOverspill parking and drop-off behaviour can
force pedestrians into the carriageway, block pavements and make it harder for elderly residents,
wheelchair users and those with mobility issues to move safely. These impacts are particularly
serious in a school-run environment and on constrained streets.4) Emergency access and
response resilienceWith constrained streets and single-route reliance, additional on-street
parking/double-parking or queuing traffic increases the risk of delaying emergency vehicles and
refuse collection. This is a highway safety and public safety concern.5) Late operating hours and
residential amenity (noise and disturbance)The applicant's Planning Statement indicates long
operating hours, including community use 07:00-22:00 and prayer use varying and running up to
11pm. Late-evening dispersal (voices outside, car doors, engines idling, vehicles circulating) is a
common source of residential nuisance. We are concerned about sleep disturbance and loss of
peaceful enjoyment of homes, particularly affecting vulnerable and elderly residents.
6) Constrained local road network - single access and cul-de-sac impactsDorset Road effectively
acts as the only vehicular entry/exit to the site and nearby residential streets (including Balmoral
Way) are cul-de-sacs with limited turning space. In such layouts, even short peak surges have
disproportionate impacts: drivers circulate, undertake three-point turns/reversing, and any
obstruction affects the whole area because there is no alternative route.
7) Overreliance on management measures that may not be enforceable long-termThe proposed
mitigation relies heavily on behaviour management (e.g., marshals/management plans) rather
than physical capacity (parking/road space). We are concerned such measures are difficult to
enforce consistently over time, especially as attendance patterns and events change in future
years.
What we ask Sutton Council to doWe ask the Council to refuse DM2025/01591 due to
unacceptable impacts on highway safety, parking, residential amenity and emergency access.

Name: Mr Koteswar Rao Modepalli
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
1. Additional Traffic in an Already Busy Area
The location around the Avenue school is already heavily used at key times of the day, particularly
mornings and afternoons. Introducing a large communityuse building here will bring further traffic
to an area that struggles with flow and visibility. This raises concerns about congestion and the
ability of the road network to cope safely.
2.Parking Demand Exceeding Capacity
The proposal appears to underestimate the number of vehicles that such a facility would attract.
As a result, there is a strong likelihood that visitors will park on nearby residential roads when the
onsite provision is full. This will add pressure to streets that already experience difficulty due to
Avenue schoolrelated parking.
3. Noise Generated by Increased Activity
A premises designed for regular group use is likely to produce noise from large numbers of
arrivals and departures. This includes engine noise, conversations, and general movement. These
repeated disturbances would be particularly noticeable during evenings, weekends, or special
events, affecting the tranquillity of nearby homes.
4. Negative Effect on Residents' Daily Living
The continuous comings and goings associated with the proposed facility would alter the character
of the surrounding residential area. Residents could experience reduced privacy, more foot traffic
outside their homes, and a general increase in daytoday disruption. These effects would materially
impact the comfort and enjoyment of their properties.
5. Combined Pressure with Existing Avenue School Activities
The school already creates predictable periods of intense use, with parents, students, and staff
generating significant activity at certain times. Adding another destination with its own peaks of
attendance could result in overlapping pressure on both the road network and local parking. This
cumulative impact has not been adequately addressed within the application.
6. Light Pollution and Impact on the Surrounding Area
The proposal is likely to introduce additional external lighting for security, access, and evening
activities. Increased artificial light, particularly during darker hours, may spill into neighbouring
gardens and homes, reducing residents' ability to enjoy their outdoor spaces and affecting sleep
patterns. Bright or prolonged lighting can also disrupt local wildlife by interfering with natural
daynight cycles, particularly for birds, insects, and nocturnal mammals. Without a detailed lighting
strategy, it is unclear how the development will prevent glare, overspill, or unnecessary
illumination of the surrounding residential environment.
7.Pressure on Local Infrastructure and Services
The proposed development could place additional strain on the surrounding infrastructure,
including local roads, pedestrian pathways, and public spaces. These facilities are already heavily
used by school families and residents, particularly at peak times. An increase in visitor numbers
may accelerate wear on pavements, create higher maintenance needs, and reduce the overall
capacity of shared community spaces. Without a clear assessment of how these impacts will be
managed, the proposal risks overstretching infrastructure that is already functioning close to its
limits.
8.Increased Pressure on Waste Collection and Refuse Management
The proposal is likely to generate a higher volume of waste than is currently produced in the area,
particularly during events or busy periods. Without a clear, detailed wastemanagement plan, there
is a risk that refuse bins may overflow or accumulate around the site. This can lead to unpleasant
odours, attract pests, and create visual clutter within a residential setting. Additionally, increased
waste output may require more frequent collection, which in turn could add noise, vehicle
movements, and disruption for nearby homes.

Name: Dr Yasser Iqbal
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
To whom it may concern,
I am writing to express my full support of the planning application DM 2025/01591.
As a local resident living a 4-minute walk from the proposed centre, I am very much in favour of
the establishment of a community hub of this kind within the very community that it would serve.
Firstly, there is a significant local Muslim population and currently there is no permanent facility in
the area for their needs, thus putting increased pressure on facilities that are further away for
broader community activities.
I am impressed that the plans have not only aimed to address this shortfall but also deliver value
for the whole local community. The designs include 3 halls - one for prayers, a dedicated and
dignified space for women, and a multi-purpose hall for community use and activities.
As a Doctor and NHS Consultant in Emergency Medicine, I recognise the need for inclusive
community hubs and the immense benefits they can bring in improving personal wellbeing and
health outcomes through health awareness, education and promotion activities, as well as simply
opportunities for direct social interaction. This is especially important for both young people and
elderly residents. I also value the much-needed social and educational benefits that the
community centre would bring to the entire local population through its planned activities -
examples listed in the application include fitness classes, elderly support, inter-faith coffee
mornings, and health promotion. I am particularly pleased that these social activities are a key
feature that the site intends to deliver.
Being a parent of children that attend and have attended Avenue Primary Academy, and living so
close by, I feel that I have good insight and several years direct experience of the traffic situation
around the school. From my observations and perspective, I do not expect there to be any
adverse impact of significance from this centre at peak school travel times.
This is because the only potential clash that anyone could reasonably cite would be on Friday
afternoons where Friday prayers at the centre might coincide with the school pick up time. At all
other peak school travel times, I would expect there to be negligible low intensity use of the facility
and so no impact observed.
However, I expect no adverse impact on Friday afternoons either. The simple reason being that
the current status quo is that BASMA have been holding Friday prayers in staggered sittings only
200m away in St John's Hall for several years now. This has been operated successfully and
responsibly without any noticeable difference or disruption to traffic from any other day of the
week. A significant number of attendees live in close proximity to the hall and so I can confirm that,
like myself and my family members, many already walk to attend Friday prayers there.
Furthermore, the proposal includes the addition of 14 parking spaces, staggered Friday
congregational prayer times away from Friday school pick-up time and support for sustainable
travel through the installation of cycle racks. I therefore feel this could potentially even have a net
positive impact on the current levels of traffic for the area from the status quo. With 2 of the
parking spaces being for disabled parking, this will also improve accessibility and safety for some
of our residents with mobility issues.
Despite the above, the application also lists other measures to try to mitigate potential traffic
issues further still, such as volunteer traffic marshals and parking management initiatives. This
shows responsible and thoughtful consideration for neighbours and local residents in the planning
process, and awareness of potential local concerns about traffic and road safety around the school
during peak travel times with conscientious efforts to address these.
The building designs and appearances look aesthetically pleasing yet still modest without
significant expansion of the current footprint of the building. Together with the preservation of
green spaces, I feel that they aim to nicely maintain the look and character of the local area.
The site will also retain a publicly accessible defibrillator and there are also plans to host training
sessions in Basic Life Support and CPR for the public. All of these would continue the legacy of
the old Ambulance station in helping to save lives of local people.
I therefore feel that such a facility has been much-needed for a long time and would be effectively
used by and beneficial for all of the local population, rather than just serving the currently unmet
needs of the Muslim population alone. Having reviewed the application, I feel BASMA's aims are
aligned with this through the various initiatives they have listed. I feel these are well thought out
and will represent a positive contribution to the area and entire community.
For these reasons, I respectfully request the Council to approve the application.
Your sincerely
Dr Yasser Iqbal

Name: Miss Layla QURESHI
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing in support of the planning application for the proposed Islamic and
Community Centre.
I am a 28 year old local resident, born and raised in Sutton. I attended nursery, primary school,
and secondary school here, and Sutton has always been my home. I am a local resident who
cares deeply about our area and its future. I genuinely believe that our community would greatly
benefit from and is in real need of this centre.
The proposed centre would provide a safe, welcoming space for people of all ages from young
children to the elderly. It would offer youth programmes, wellbeing initiatives, women's support
services, and educational activities that would be open and accessible to the wider community.
These kinds of structured activities are invaluable in fostering positive engagement, personal
development, and social connection.
Importantly, the centre would help reduce isolation and loneliness by creating opportunities for
people to come together, interact, and support one another. In a time when many people feel
disconnected, spaces like this strengthen community bonds and promote understanding across
different backgrounds.
I understand that some residents have concerns about traffic. However, prayer times do not
coincide with school drop-off and pick-up times. Friday prayer times would also be staggered
specifically to avoid conflict with school collection hours. Additionally, the local Muslim community
has been hiring St John's Community Hall, located just down the road from the ambulance station,
every Friday afternoon and every evening during Ramadan for the past five years. I personally
attend St John's Hall for prayers at the moment. This arrangement has not caused significant
disruption, and therefore the proposed change of use is unlikely to create a noticeable difference
in traffic levels.
The site will provide 12 on-site parking spaces, including 2 disabled bays, as well as bicycle racks,
encouraging sustainable travel. The centre is intended to be low-intensity, with no significant light
or noise disturbance. The existing greenery will be maintained, ensuring the character of the area
is preserved.
Overall, I strongly believe that converting the former ambulance station into a Community Centre
would be a positive and meaningful addition to Sutton. It would serve not only the Muslim
community but the wider local community as well, providing support, education, wellbeing
initiatives, and a safe space for connection.
I respectfully ask that the application be approved.
Thank you for your consideration.

Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I am writing as a local resident of, 112 Banstead Road Carshalton SM5 3NH, to formally object to
the planning application DM2025/01591. While I recognize the value of community facilities, this
specific proposal represents an overdevelopment of the site that will negatively impact the local
area. My objection is based on the following material planning considerations:
1. Highways Safety and Traffic Congestion (Policy 37)
The proposed change of use will significantly increase vehicle movements at peak times,
particularly during Friday prayers and community events. Dorset Road is already operating at
capacity. The additional traffic will lead to hazardous road conditions and gridlock, contrary to
Sutton Local Plan Policy 37, which seeks to ensure that development does not adversely affect
highway safety.
2. Inadequate Parking Provision (Policy 37 & Appendix 12)
The application fails to provide sufficient on-site parking for the anticipated number of visitors.
Given the low PTAL rating of this location, visitors will inevitably rely on private vehicles, leading to
substantial overspill parking on surrounding residential streets. This will obstruct emergency
vehicle access and negatively impact existing residents' ability to park near their homes.
3. Impact on Residential Amenity (Policy 29)
The intensified use of the building will cause significant noise and disturbance to immediate
neighbours. The congregational nature of a Place of Worship, combined with potentially early
morning and late evening operating hours, will result in a loss of quiet enjoyment of our homes.
This constitutes a clear breach of Policy 29, which protects residents from unacceptable noise,
vibration, and general disturbance.
4. Character of the Area (Policy 28)
The scale of the proposed "Community Hub" and any associated external alterations [e.g., new
signage, lighting, or structural changes] are out of character with the established
residential/suburban street scene. Under Policy 28, development must be sympathetic to the local
distinctiveness and character of the Sutton neighbourhood, which this proposal fails to achieve.
Conclusion
For the reasons stated above, I urge the Planning Committee to refuse this application. The site is
fundamentally unsuitable for a high-intensity use of this nature without severe detriment to the
local infrastructure and residential environment.
Yours faithfully,
Rajkiran Manda
112 Banstead Road
Carshalton
SM5 3NH

Name: Mr SATHISH KUMAR NATARAJAN
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to the proposal for change of use to mixed Class F1 (place of worship) and
Class F2 (community hub) together with extensions, increased height and associated cycle/refuse
facilities.
My objection is based on material planning considerations, particularly the impact on residential
amenity and the local highway/parking network, given the site's residential context.
1) Highway safety, congestion, and parking stress (peak impacts)
The proposed mixed F1/F2 use is likely to generate high peak arrivals and departures (e.g., Friday
prayers, weekends, evening community events, and festivals). This can create short, intense
pressure on local roads and kerbside space, leading to unsafe manoeuvres, obstruction of
driveways, illegal parking, and congestion at peak times.
Request: The application should be refused unless the applicant provides robust evidence
including:
- a Transport Statement/Assessment addressing peak periods and event days,
- a parking stress survey of surrounding streets at relevant times,
- a Travel Plan and operational measures (marshalling/stewarding, anti-idling, drop-off strategy).
2) Noise and disturbance to neighbouring residents
The use introduces a credible risk of unacceptable disturbance from:
- amplified sound/voice (depending on internal arrangements),
- and crucially noise from congregants arriving/leaving, doors closing, engines, and dispersal-often
outside typical working hours.
Planning Inspectors have previously concluded that worship uses can cause unacceptable
noise/disturbance to neighbours, including noting that sound insulation does not address noise
from people arriving/leaving.
Request: Refusal unless supported by:
- a Noise Impact Assessment,
- a clear plan for hours, capacity, stewarding, dispersal, and complaints handling,
- enforceable conditions limiting hours and the frequency/scale of events.
3) Overdevelopment / design and height increase
The scheme includes a part one/part two-storey extension and increased height.
The Council should be satisfied that the resulting bulk, height and massing do not harm the
streetscape or create undue impacts on neighbouring outlook, daylight, and general amenity.
Request: Refusal unless drawings and assessments demonstrate no unacceptable impact on
neighbouring properties and the character of the area.
4) Uncertainty of operation (mixed F1 + F2)
A mixed worship + community hub use can materially increase total footfall and extend activity
across more days and later hours than a single use. Without firm, enforceable operational
parameters (hours, capacity, event schedule), the proposal is not capable of being properly
assessed.
Request: At minimum, conditions (or a legal agreement where justified) should control:
- maximum occupancy,
- hours of use,
- limits on non-worship events,
- event management and stewarding,
- travel plan measures and review.
5) Refuse/cycle management and local amenity
Refuse/cycle stores must be sized, located, and managed to avoid odour, noise, visual clutter, and
fly-tipping risk.

DM2025/01591.
The proposal to change the use of the former ambulance station into a community hub and place
of worship represents a thoughtful and policy-aligned redevelopment of a currently vacant and
underutilised site. At present, the site is hard-surfaced, inactive, and contributes little to the vitality
or appearance of the surrounding area. Bringing it back into productive use would enhance the
streetscape, improve natural surveillance, and create a focal point for positive community activity -
all on previously developed land, with no loss of greenfield or open space.
From a planning perspective, this is a sustainable form of development. It makes efficient use of
an existing urban site, reduces pressure to develop elsewhere, and introduces a use that is locally
accessible by foot and public transport. Community facilities are most effective when embedded
within neighbourhoods, and this proposal achieves exactly that.
Parking and traffic concerns have been raised, but the evidence submitted does not substantiate
claims of significant harm. The Transport Statement identified 269 parking spaces in the
surrounding area, with only 134 occupied at peak time, demonstrating substantial spare capacity.
The anticipated demand associated with the proposed use is modest in comparison.
It is also important to consider the historical baseline. The former ambulance station had 21 on-
site parking spaces. The proposed redevelopment removes these and, crucially, will also result in
the removal of the existing yellow keep-clear markings near the entrance. This will release
additional kerbside capacity currently unavailable to residents. In practical terms, the kerbside
position is likely to improve rather than worsen.
Some objections have linked the proposal to potential congestion during school-run periods.
However, the timing evidence shows a gap of over two hours between the end of Friday prayer
changeover and the start of the afternoon school run. The assertion of a direct clash is therefore
not supported by the schedule.
Beyond the transport considerations, the social value of this proposal should be given significant
weight. Social isolation is widely recognised as a risk factor for depression, cardiovascular
disease, and cognitive decline, particularly among elderly residents and new mothers. A local,
accessible community hub offering educational activities, wellbeing sessions, youth engagement,
and social support directly addresses these challenges. It would provide a safe and inclusive
space where residents can build connections, access resources, and participate in structured
programmes that enhance overall wellbeing.
This part of the borough is comparatively underserved in terms of multi-functional community
facilities. The application demonstrates clear demand, and the mixed model of faith and broader
community use ensures that the benefits extend beyond any single group. The facility would serve
as an anchor institution within the neighbourhood, encouraging civic participation and fostering
social cohesion.
Importantly, planning decisions must be grounded in material considerations and evidence rather
than perception. On its planning merits, this application is strong. It aligns with local and national
policy supporting the retention and enhancement of community facilities, makes effective use of
brownfield land, and is supported by technical assessments that demonstrate its impacts are
acceptable and manageable.
For these reasons, I respectfully request that the London Borough of Sutton approve planning
application DM2025/01591.

Name: Mrs Kousalya Iyengar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to this proposal on the grounds of Negative Highway Safety & Traffic Impact
and Inadequate Parking Provision.
I regularly travel up and down Dorset Road, Avenue Road, York Road and Holland Avenue. As it
is, during school pick up and drop off times, it takes well over 10 mins to travel from Belmont
Station to the junction of Dorset Road and Holland Road. There are cars stopped on both sides of
the road, kids darting across the traffic. Buses that pass down the road are frequently held up and
delayed. Currently, there is some respite outside pick-up and drop-off times and during term
holidays. The junction of Station Road, Avenue Road and Northdown Road is another chokehold,
especially when there are events held at St John's Church.
With a mosque and a community hall, now the traffic will be near constant. Even with staggered
prayer times, the 12 proposed parking spots are not going to be sufficient for the needs of the
attendees. And where are the attendees going to park? - Up York Road, Holland Avenue,
Balmoral Way - places that already suffer from inconsiderate parking. Just like the school, the
mosque will send out a letter requesting everyone to be considerate to their neighbours - it will
have an impact for a couple of days and then ignored subsequently.
In addition to the school, there are nursing homes and a Bowling club in the vicinity that cater to an
elderly demographic. All the increased traffic and chaos pose a significant risk to the existing users
of the neighbouring roads. It is not sufficient to say that you will increase parking wardens in the
area. What about later in the evenings or the weekends? What will be the impact on the response
times of emergency services that need to pass through these roads?
In response to comments that I see on the planning application:
- The increased footfall and vehicular traffic is unlikely to be confined to Friday afternoons.
- The traffic and noise associated with the Ambulance service was excused because they provided
a valuable life-saving service.
- The increased traffic when the community events are held at St John's Church is definitely visible
and a nuisance for those who live nearby or travel through.
- There is a large mosque on A232, barely a mile from the proposed location, which should be
able to cater to religious needs of the community. There is another on Oakhill Road, and a prayer
hall on Woodstock Road. Compared to other minority faiths, one could say that the Muslims are
well catered to in the provision of prayer and community spaces, in London Borough of Sutton.
- No one wants the site to remain disused, but given the location, it is better suited to a secular
provision that aims to attract a significantly lower number of attendees and is built with a
significantly larger number of parking spaces within the site.
I hope the council recognises the stress placed on the neighbourhood and rejects the proposal.

Comment:
I support planning application DM2025/01591 for the change of use of the former
ambulance station on Dorset Road. This is a thoughtful proposal that addresses a clear local
need.
Preventing health problems is cheaper and more effective than treating them. This proposal
delivers exactly that. Community hubs that offer wellbeing activities, social connection, and
educational programmes are proven to reduce demand on the NHS. In the same vein, neighbours
should welcome this, not object to it. The change from a round-the-clock ambulance station to a
community facility with set operating hours represents a significant reduction in the intensity of use
at this site.
There is simply nothing like this currently available in the Belmont area. Residents who want to
access community or faith facilities have to travel to Sutton town centre or further afield. This
proposal would change that.
If the Council wishes to impose specific conditions around hours of use, the applicant has already
demonstrated willingness to operate within defined limits. The restricted operating hours provide a
sensible framework.
Moreover, it surveyed real conditions at peak time and demonstrated that on-street parking
capacity is more than sufficient. The Transport Statement is evidence-based and thorough. I am
confident that the proposal will not cause a parking problem.
This is not a token addition; it reflects a real gap in local provision that this facility is designed to
fill. The proposal explicitly provides for women's groups, family activities, and provision for elderly
residents.
I'd also add that the concern about school-run congestion has been raised by objectors, but the
timings simply do not overlap. Friday prayers finish well before the afternoon school pickup begins.
The Transport Statement addresses this demonstrably.
For the reasons set out above, I fully support this application and encourage the Council to
approve it.

Comment:
I am writing as a local resident to express my support for planning application
DM2025/01591.
From a planning perspective, I believe this proposal responds positively to a number of important
considerations.
Community benefit
The development would provide valuable community space in line with Sutton's Local Plan
priorities. In addition to its primary use, the inclusion of educational activities and dedicated
provision for women's health and wellbeing would offer meaningful services that many local
residents can benefit from. Facilities like this play an important role in strengthening community
ties and supporting local families.
Design and fit within the area
The building itself is modest in scale and remains in keeping with the surrounding residential
character. The retention of existing greenery helps soften its appearance and ensures it integrates
sensitively into the street scene rather than dominating it.
Parking, highways and thoughtful management
With 12 on-site parking spaces, including two disabled bays, the level of parking appears
proportionate. The commitment to stagger Friday prayer times to avoid overlap with nearby school
pick-up hours is a sensible and responsible measure. It shows a clear intention to manage activity
carefully and minimise any impact on traffic flow and pedestrian safety.
Impact on neighbours
This is not a high-intensity use, and with proper management in place, it should not give rise to
unacceptable levels of noise, light disturbance or traffic. Planning conditions, if required, can
ensure that residential amenity is protected.
Overall, this represents a sustainable reuse of the site that would bring clear community value. I
respectfully ask the Council to approve the application.

Name: Mrs Sowjanya Kalepalli
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally object to the proposed conversion of the former ambulance
station into a centre.
While I fully recognise the importance of community facilities and respect the role they play in
society, I believe that the specific nature and location of this site make the proposed change of use
inappropriate. My concerns are outlined below:
Impact on Residential Amenity (Noise and Disturbance):
The site is located within a quiet residential area. Its previous use as an emergency service base
involved intermittent and generally predictable activity. In contrast, a religious centre is likely to
generate concentrated periods of high activity, particularly during evenings and weekends when
residents reasonably expect peace and quiet.
Noise arising from large gatherings, arrivals and departures, vehicle movements, and any
amplified sound could result in significant disturbance to neighbouring properties.
Highway Safety and Parking Pressure:
The surrounding residential streets are narrow and already experience high parking demand. A
centre typically attracts a substantial number of visitors at the same time, and the site does not
appear to provide sufficient off-street parking to accommodate a full congregation.
This is likely to lead to overspill parking on residential roads, potentially blocking driveways,
restricting access for emergency vehicles, and creating hazards for pedestrians.
Change in the Character of the Area:
The neighbourhood is a quiet, established residential area. The proposed intensification of use -
transforming a low-occupancy service building into a high-capacity public assembly venue -
represents a significant material change of use.
In my view, this would be inconsistent with the tranquil character of the area as defined in the
Sutton Local Plan.
Inadequate Access Arrangements:
The existing access points were designed primarily for ambulance vehicles and may not be
suitable for accommodating a high volume of pedestrian traffic, including families and children.
This raises concerns regarding road safety and general site accessibility.
Given these concerns, I respectfully request that the Planning Committee require a full Noise
Impact Assessment and a comprehensive Transport Statement before any determination is made.
For the reasons outlined above, I urge the Council to refuse this application in order to protect the
residential amenity and safety of local residents.

Address: Flat 3 Glenlyon House 8A Station Road Belmont SM2 6BH
Name: Miss Shaista Naz
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
As someone who lives locally, I want to add my voice in support of planning application
DM2025/01591. I think this proposal will be good for the neighbourhood.
The noise assessment used real-world measurements, not theoretical models. The consultants
recorded actual sound levels from the congregation at their existing venue and assessed how that
noise would transmit through the proposed building fabric. The result: below background noise at
neighbouring properties.
Residents will no longer have emergency vehicles coming and going through the night. The facility
will operate within defined hours, which is a notable improvement over the former ambulance
station that ran 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
I have checked the timings and there is no conflict between Friday prayers and the school run.
The prayer changeover is around 12:30 to 12:55, while school drop-off and pick-up are at 08:30
and 15:00 respectively. These do not coincide.
It is also worth mentioning that but that only works if there are local facilities to refer to. GPs
increasingly refer patients to community activities as part of social prescribing. This proposal
would create one in an area that currently has very little.
This application stands or falls on its planning merits, and on those merits it is strong. I expect the
Council to assess it accordingly. The policy support is clear, the evidence is comprehensive, and
the community benefit is well-documented.
The BNG assessment meets the statutory requirement, and the external greening will improve the
appearance and ecological value of a site that currently offers very little in either regard. I welcome
the environmental improvements included in this proposal.
The modal split for this facility is overwhelmingly sustainable. Half of all attendees at Friday
prayers walk, and fewer than 12% drive alone. The proposed cycle storage will encourage even
more sustainable travel. This is far better than many other developments the Council approves.
It is also worth mentioning that this proposal addresses that directly, with dedicated spaces and
programmes. Women and elderly residents in faith communities can face particular barriers to
accessing community facilities, especially when there is nothing suitable nearby.
I would also note that I think it is important that the planning officer considers the breadth of this
proposal. The educational programmes, wellbeing sessions, and interfaith events are designed to
bring different parts of the community together. This is truly inclusive provision.
Furthermore, the development reduces on-site parking and removes restrictive yellow lines,
actually improving the kerbside situation for residents. The claim that this development will create
a new parking problem ignores the fact that the previous use had 21 parking spaces and
generated regular vehicle movements.
On this point, the Transport Statement is evidence-based and thorough. It surveyed real
conditions at peak time and demonstrated that on-street parking capacity is more than sufficient. I
am satisfied that the proposal will not cause a parking problem.
The proposed extension is not overbearing. It is a part one, part two storey addition that sits
comfortably within the existing site. The inclusion of cycle storage, refuse stores, and external
greening shows that the applicant has thought carefully about how the building sits in its
surroundings.
I would also note that the planning statement provides clear demographic evidence that the local
Muslim community has outgrown the available facilities. But this is not just about faith provision.
The need is real and well-documented. The community hub will serve all residents with
educational, social, and wellbeing activities.
I find it odd that some objectors are concerned about the impact of a community facility when the
previous use was a full-time ambulance station with sirens, heavy vehicles, and overnight
operations. The proposed use is quieter, less disruptive, and operates within defined hours.
I strongly encourage the Council to approve this application. It will bring a unused site back to life
and serve the community for years to come.

Name: Mrs Sandhya Mittatal
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Re: Planning Application DM2025/01591 - Objection
I write to formally object to the above application.
1. Highway Safety & Traffic Impact
The proposed change of use to a Place of Worship and Community Hub will materially increase
vehicle and pedestrian movements, particularly during concentrated service and event times.
Dorset Road and surrounding residential streets already experience congestion during peak
hours. The proposal is likely to result in:
Increased vehicle queuing and unsafe turning movements
Obstructive and overspill parking in residential streets
Reduced visibility and compromised road safety
There is no clear evidence within the application demonstrating that the local highway network can
safely accommodate this intensified use.
2. Impact on Avenue Primary School - Child Safety Risk
Avenue Primary School is located within approximately 1km of the site and shares the same local
road network.
School drop-off and pick-up periods coincide with typical times of worship or community
gatherings. This overlap creates foreseeable conflict between:
Increased vehicle movements
High volumes of young pedestrians
Informal parking and drop-off activity
The application does not provide sufficient mitigation measures to safeguard vulnerable road
users, particularly children.
3. Insufficient Parking & Traffic Management Strategy
The proposal lacks:
A robust Transport Assessment
Clear parking capacity evidence
Managed drop-off and dispersal planning
Event traffic control measures
Without enforceable safeguards, the development would result in unacceptable impacts on
highway safety and residential amenity.
Conclusion
Given the unresolved and significant highway safety concerns - particularly in relation to children
and peak traffic periods - I respectfully request that Sutton Council refuse this application unless a
full and independently verified Transport Assessment clearly demonstrates no adverse impact on
traffic flow or pedestrian safety.

Name: Miss Marion Little

I object to this application due to the traffic chaos it will bring to the local area and roads.

Name: Mr Shailendra Kumar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Objection to Planning Application DM2025/01591
I wish to register my objection to this application on the grounds of highway impact and public
safety.
Traffic and Road Capacity
The proposed use represents a significant intensification compared to the existing use of the
building. A place of worship and community hub typically generates high attendance at specific
times, leading to clustered arrivals and departures.
The surrounding road network, including Dorset Road and adjoining residential streets, is not
designed to accommodate sudden spikes in traffic flow. Increased vehicle movements are likely to
result in:
Congestion at junctions
Informal or illegal parking
Reduced road visibility
Obstruction to residents and emergency vehicles
There is insufficient evidence within the application to demonstrate that these impacts have been
properly assessed or mitigated.
Proximity to a Primary School
Avenue Primary School operates within close proximity to the site and relies on the same local
road network.
School start and finish times already create heightened traffic and pedestrian activity. Introducing
additional event-based traffic in the same area raises clear safety concerns, particularly for young
children navigating crossings and pavements.
The application does not appear to include a detailed strategy addressing:
Safe pedestrian management
Coordinated timing of events
Controlled parking and dispersal arrangements
Lack of Comprehensive Mitigation
Given the scale of potential attendance, a thorough Transport Assessment and structured traffic
management plan should be a minimum requirement. Without enforceable safeguards, the
proposal risks creating ongoing congestion and safety issues for residents and school families
alike.
Request
For the reasons outlined above, I respectfully request that Sutton Council give serious weight to
these highway and safety concerns and refuse the application unless robust, evidence-based
mitigation measures are secured.

Name: Mr Pradeep Panwar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I would like to object to the proposed redevelopment of the former ambulance station.
While community facilities are noted, the scale of this specific proposal is incompatible with the
quiet, residential character of this neighbourhood and the safety requirements of the adjacent
primary school.
Impact on Highway Safety and School Pupils:
The transition to a high-capacity Place of Worship (F1) and Community Hub (F2) will generate
significant vehicular intensification. This residential area already experiences high traffic stress
during school drop-off and pick-up periods. The addition of congregational traffic will create
dangerous bottlenecks, increasing the risk to young pedestrians. The proposed cycle and refuse
stores further suggest a level of occupancy that the narrow residential street infrastructure cannot
safely support.
Loss of Residential Amenity:
The residential environment is highly sensitive to noise. The proposed mixed-use facility will draw
large groups simultaneously. The noise from increased footfall, car doors, and congregational
activity-often during early mornings or late evenings-will cause an unacceptable level of
disturbance to neighbouring homes.
Overdevelopment and Massing:
The part-one, part-two storey extensions and the overall increase in building height represent an
over-intensification of the site. This increased massing will appear overbearing in a residential
context.
The proposal fails to demonstrate that it can operate without compromising the safety of school
children or the fundamental peace of this residential community and hence this objection.

Name: Dr Amir Selim
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I am writing to formally object to the above planning application. I am a resident of [your full
address], located in close proximity to the proposed development, and I have concerns regarding
the impact this proposal may have on the character and functioning of our residential area.
My objection is based on the following planning grounds:
1. Traffic Generation and Highway SafetyThe proposal is likely to significantly increase vehicle
movements, particularly during peak usage times. The surrounding roads already experience
congestion and limited visibility in places, and additional traffic could create safety risks for
pedestrians and drivers.
2. Parking PressureOn-street parking in the area is already heavily constrained. The scale and
anticipated usage of the development are likely to result in overspill parking on nearby residential
streets, leading to inconvenience for residents and potential obstruction of access for emergency
vehicles.
3. Noise and DisturbanceThe increased activity associated with the development, including
arrivals and departures of visitors and general use, has the potential to introduce noise levels
inconsistent with the quiet residential character of the area, particularly during evenings and
weekends.
4. Scale and Impact on Residential AmenityThe size and intensity of the proposed use appear out
of keeping with the surrounding residential context. This may result in a loss of amenity for
neighbouring properties through increased activity, disturbance, and change to the local
environment.
5. Cumulative Impact on Local InfrastructureThe proposal may place additional pressure on local
infrastructure and services without clear mitigation measures, which should be carefully assessed.
In summary, I believe the development, in its current form, would have an adverse impact on
highway safety, residential amenity, and the character of the area. I respectfully request that these
concerns be given full consideration when determining the application.
Thank you for taking the time to consider my representation.
Yours faithfully
Amir Selim

Name: Mrs Mirrette Selim
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I am writing to formally object to the above planning application. I live at 129A Burdon lane very
near to the proposed development, and I am concerned that the proposal would have a significant
adverse impact on highway safety and residential amenity.
My objection is based on the following material planning considerations:
1. Inadequate Road Capacity and Highway Safety
The road is narrow and already experiences constrained vehicle movement, particularly when cars
are parked on both sides. The additional traffic likely to be generated by the proposed use would
intensify congestion and create conflicts between vehicles, cyclists, and pedestrians. This raises
genuine highway safety concerns, especially at peak usage times.
2. Existing Parking Stress and Overspill Risk
On-street parking on the road is already heavily oversubscribed, with residents frequently
struggling to find spaces close to their homes. The proposal does not appear to provide sufficient
on-site parking to accommodate peak demand, making overspill parking onto surrounding
residential streets highly likely. This would worsen access for residents and could impede
emergency or service vehicles.
3. Noise and Disturbance to Residential Amenity
The nature and intensity of the proposed use would introduce increased activity levels, including
vehicle movements, arrivals and departures, and general congregation of visitors. Given the close
proximity of residential properties and the current quiet character of the street, this would result in
noise and disturbance that is materially harmful to the living conditions of nearby residents,
particularly during evenings and weekends.
4. Impact on the Character of the Street
The road functions as a primarily residential environment with limited capacity to absorb higher-
intensity uses. The scale of activity associated with the proposal would be out of keeping with the
existing character of the area and would erode the residential nature of the street.
5. Lack of Demonstrated Mitigation Measures
There does not appear to be clear or robust mitigation to address traffic management, parking
demand, or noise impacts. Without effective controls, the development risks causing ongoing and
cumulative harm to neighbouring amenity and local road conditions.
Conclusion
For the reasons outlined above, I believe the proposal would result in unacceptable impacts in
terms of highway safety, parking pressure, and residential amenity. I respectfully request that the
application be refused, or that significant amendments and enforceable mitigation measures be
required before any approval is considered.
Thank you for your time and consideration of these comments.
Yours faithfully,
Mirrette Selim

Name: Mr Uzair Khalid
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I support the application referenced for the below reasons:
1. Response to Reduction in Local Community Services
Residents have experienced reduced access to structured and affordable community venues due
to wider pressures on public services. The proposed facility will provide a stable, long-term venue
for community activity that is not dependent on fluctuating public provision. By offering additional
capacity for community use, the development will complement existing civic infrastructure and help
maintain continuity of local services.
2. Provision of Affordable Indoor Community Space
There is a recognised shortage of accessible and affordable indoor space suitable for community
activities. The proposed development will provide flexible, bookable halls capable of
accommodating:
- Youth engagement programmes
- Cultural and educational activities
- Community learning and tuition
- Health and wellbeing workshops
The availability of low-cost, multi-functional indoor space will enable a wide range of local groups
to operate within the borough, supporting inclusive participation and efficient use of land and
buildings.
3. Promotion of Mental Health and Social Wellbeing
Accessible local meeting spaces play an important role in promoting social cohesion and
preventative wellbeing. The proposed facility will provide a welcoming and inclusive environment
that supports regular social interaction, particularly benefiting:
- Elderly residents
- New parents
- Young people
Through structured activities, support groups, and community gatherings, the development will
contribute positively to mental wellbeing and help reduce social isolation. These preventative
benefits support wider public health objectives and enhance community resilience.
4. Women-Focused Community Provision
Locally accessible, culturally sensitive provision for women's health, wellbeing, and community
engagement is limited. The proposed development will provide appropriate and inclusive space for
programmes supporting women's participation, learning, and wellbeing. This will promote equality
of access to community facilities and address the needs of under-served groups within the
borough.
5. Provision of Safe and Structured Youth Space
The development will provide a supervised environment for youth activities that supports positive
engagement and personal development. Structured youth provision is widely recognised as
contributing to community safety, social development, and educational support. By offering a
managed venue for young people, the proposal will provide a constructive alternative to
unsupervised gathering in public spaces.
6. Accessible Faith and Community Infrastructure
The proposal will deliver a purpose-designed, properly managed facility that integrates faith use
with wider community functions. This approach will reduce reliance on informal or temporary
arrangements and provide improved accessibility, safety, and organisation of community activities.
The development will function as a shared civic resource that serves both faith-based and wider
community needs.
Planning Balance and Response to Potential Impacts
The proposal has been designed to ensure that any potential impacts are proportionate,
appropriately managed, and outweighed by the significant public benefits delivered.
The use will operate as a managed community facility with structured hours of operation and clear
oversight, ensuring orderly use and minimising disturbance. Activity patterns associated with
places of worship and community facilities are typically time-specific and predictable, allowing
effective management of arrivals, departures, and site use.
In relation to traffic and parking, community and faith-based uses generally generate dispersed
and time-limited movements, often outside peak commuter periods. The site's local accessibility
encourages walking, shared travel, and public transport use. Where necessary, operational
management measures can be implemented to support responsible parking behaviour and
minimise impacts on neighbouring amenity.
Noise and general amenity impacts are expected to be limited due to the indoor nature of the
primary activities and the managed character of the facility. The proposal replaces informal or
temporary arrangements with a properly designed and regulated environment, improving oversight
and reducing the likelihood of unmanaged gatherings.
When assessed in the round, the development delivers substantial and enduring community
benefits, including accessible social infrastructure, improved wellbeing provision, inclusive
community participation, and enhanced local cohesion. These public benefits strongly align with
the social and community objectives of the London Borough of Sutton and materially outweigh any
limited and manageable impacts associated with the use.
Conclusion
The proposed development delivers clear and demonstrable public benefit through the provision of
accessible social infrastructure. It will support community cohesion, promote health and wellbeing,
provide affordable space for local activities, and respond directly to identified gaps in community
provision.
For these reasons, the proposal represents a sustainable and policy-aligned form of development
that will make a positive contribution to the borough and its residents.

Address: Flat 2 469 Gander Green Lane North Cheam SM3 9RA
Name: Mrs Tazeen Rehman
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am a local resident writing in wholehearted support of application DM2025/01591. In
my view this proposal addresses a real need and will bring positive change to a site that currently
sits empty and unused.
The link between community participation and better health outcomes is not disputed. What is
often lacking is the local infrastructure to make participation possible. This proposal provides that
infrastructure in an area where it is currently missing.
I would also note that this proposal will provide a facility that all residents can use, with a
programme of activities designed to be accessible and welcoming to people of all backgrounds
and ages. The Belmont area currently has very limited community space.
The noise assessment addresses the concerns that have been raised by objectors and finds them
unsupported. The building fabric will contain sound effectively, the operating hours are restricted,
and the predicted noise at neighbouring properties is below existing background levels.
The applicant has specifically timetabled Friday prayer changeover to avoid the school-run period.
The evidence shows this has been done successfully, with a clear gap of over two hours between
the two activities.
The proposed use is clearly policy-supported, the technical evidence is thorough, and the
community benefit is substantial. I trust that the Council will assess this application on its planning
merits.
Furthermore, this part of the borough is underserved when it comes to community facilities. The
application establishes a clear need, and the proposed mix of faith and community uses means
the benefits extend well beyond any single group.
It is also worth mentioning that the NPPF requires authorities to plan positively for these uses.
Sutton's own Local Plan identifies community centres and places of worship as essential social
infrastructure under Policy 33. This proposal is exactly what those policies were written to support.
I have read the Transport Statement and the parking evidence is convincing. A survey during the
peak Friday prayer period found on-street parking at less than 50% capacity in the surrounding
area. The worst-case modelling still leaves 66 spaces available and stays well under the 85%
parking stress threshold.
I think this application deserves approval and I respectfully ask the Council to grant planning
permission for DM2025/01591.

Name: Mr CHARAN BAVULURU

Comment: Objection

Comment:
This proposal is about strengthening community life through shared values of respect,
service, compassion, and responsibility. A mosque is not simply a place of worship - it is a
community hub that provides family support, youth engagement, and charity initiatives, while
encouraging members to be considerate neighbours and active contributors to wider society.
The Belmont and Sutton Muslim Association promote the values of good character, service to the
community and care for our neighbours, so I am confident that the mosque will have these values
at its core.
The presence of the mosque can promote social cohesion. It creates opportunities for interfaith
dialogue, charity events, and community that bring people of different backgrounds together.
Rather than dividing communities, such spaces often become bridges that foster understanding
and reduce misconceptions.
For children and young people, a mosque provides activities and mentoring in a safe environment.
This helps promote discipline, moral development, and respectfulness to our community. For
adults and families, it offers a supportive space for connection and community involvement. These
benefits strengthen families, and strong families strengthen neighbourhoods.
Concerns from nearby residents should absolutely be heard and addressed with care. However,
planning conditions and responsible management can ensure that matters such as parking and
any noise are handled respectfully.
We should support a diverse community. Supporting this mosque is a statement that we value
inclusion, mutual respect, and the right of all residents to practise their faith in a safe manner.
I urge the council to consider the social benefits this development would bring to families, young
people, and the broader community.

Name: Mrs Zahida Khalid
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I support the application for the reasons below:
1. Response to Reduction in Local Community Services
Residents have experienced reduced access to structured and affordable community venues due
to wider pressures on public services. The proposed facility will provide a stable, long-term venue
for community activity that is not dependent on fluctuating public provision. By offering additional
capacity for community use, the development will complement existing civic infrastructure and help
maintain continuity of local services.
2. Provision of Affordable Indoor Community Space
There is a recognised shortage of accessible and affordable indoor space suitable for community
activities. The proposed development will provide flexible, bookable halls capable of
accommodating:
- Youth engagement programmes
- Cultural and educational activities
- Community learning and tuition
- Health and wellbeing workshops
The availability of low-cost, multi-functional indoor space will enable a wide range of local groups
to operate within the borough, supporting inclusive participation and efficient use of land and
buildings.
3. Promotion of Mental Health and Social Wellbeing
Accessible local meeting spaces play an important role in promoting social cohesion and
preventative wellbeing. The proposed facility will provide a welcoming and inclusive environment
that supports regular social interaction, particularly benefiting:
- Elderly residents
- New parents
- Young people
Through structured activities, support groups, and community gatherings, the development will
contribute positively to mental wellbeing and help reduce social isolation. These preventative
benefits support wider public health objectives and enhance community resilience.
4. Women-Focused Community Provision
Locally accessible, culturally sensitive provision for women's health, wellbeing, and community
engagement is limited. The proposed development will provide appropriate and inclusive space for
programmes supporting women's participation, learning, and wellbeing. This will promote equality
of access to community facilities and address the needs of under-served groups within the
borough.
5. Provision of Safe and Structured Youth Space
The development will provide a supervised environment for youth activities that supports positive
engagement and personal development. Structured youth provision is widely recognised as
contributing to community safety, social development, and educational support. By offering a
managed venue for young people, the proposal will provide a constructive alternative to
unsupervised gathering in public spaces.
6. Accessible Faith and Community Infrastructure
The proposal will deliver a purpose-designed, properly managed facility that integrates faith use
with wider community functions. This approach will reduce reliance on informal or temporary
arrangements and provide improved accessibility, safety, and organisation of community activities.
The development will function as a shared civic resource that serves both faith-based and wider
community needs.
Conclusion
The proposed development delivers clear and demonstrable public benefit through the provision of
accessible social infrastructure. It will support community cohesion, promote health and wellbeing,
provide affordable space for local activities, and respond directly to identified gaps in community
provision.
For these reasons, the proposal represents a sustainable and policy-aligned form of development
that will make a positive contribution to the borough and its residents.

Name: Mr Mahdi Rahman

Name: Ms Nafisa Amini
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
We respectfully submit this statement in support of the proposed mosque, addressing
the key concerns raised and demonstrating that the development will not result in severe or
unacceptable impacts.
1. Highway Impact
The peak activity associated with Friday prayers is short in duration, predictable, and occurs once
weekly - it is not continuous or daily traffic generation. Importantly:
- There is no overlap with the 8:30am school peak.
- Afternoon school collection times are staggered due to after-school clubs and childcare, reducing
concentrated congestion.
- Friday congregations have been responsibly staggered since 2021, significantly dispersing
arrival and departure times.
- The site has already been operating in real-world conditions for several years without causing
severe highway harm.
The transport impact does not meet the threshold of being "severe," particularly given its limited
frequency and duration.
2. Parking and Overspill
Concerns regarding parking overspill are overstated:
- A significant proportion of attendees live locally and walk to the mosque.
- The proposed on-site parking and improved layout represent an enhancement compared to the
current arrangement.
- Any overspill is short-term, not constant, daily, or continuous.
- Prayer attendance involves brief stays, resulting in quick turnover rather than long-term parking
occupation.
This pattern of use is materially different from high-intensity commercial or all-day venues.
3. Cul-de-Sac and Local Road Network
While the surrounding road network is constrained, activity related to worship is:
- Brief and staggered rather than sustained.
- Comparable to existing weekly use that has operated without demonstrable harm.
- Limited to once-weekly peak activity rather than functioning as a daily high-traffic generator.
Most attendees are local residents who access the mosque on foot, further reducing vehicular
pressure.
4. Late Evening Amenity and Noise
Amenity concerns are limited in scope:
- Worship activities take place predominantly indoors.
- Dispersal is calm, orderly, and short in duration.
- There are no consistent competing peak evening events from nearby venues that would
compound impact.
There is no evidence that the mosque would cause significant or ongoing disturbance to
neighbouring residents.
5. Cumulative Impact
The suggestion of cumulative impact with other local venues does not reflect operational reality:
- Peak activity times do not consistently coincide.
- Friday prayer times vary throughout the year.
- The limited duration of worship significantly reduces any potential overlap effects.
Any cumulative effect would therefore be occasional and brief, not sustained.
6. Demonstrable Local Need
There is a clear and established local need for this facility:
- The mosque provides essential women's services and wider community support.
- Current temporary arrangements are insufficient to meet demand.
- The development primarily serves existing local users - it is not expected to generate a new
influx of visitors from outside the area.
This proposal formalises and improves an already functioning community service.
Conclusion
The evidence demonstrates that the proposed mosque:
- Does not generate continuous or severe highway impacts.
- Improves parking arrangements compared to the current situation.
- Operates on a limited, predictable, and well-managed weekly peak.
- Meets a genuine and growing local community need.
On balance, the social, community, and equality benefits clearly outweigh the limited and short-
duration impacts identified.
We respectfully ask the Council to support this important community development.

Name: Mrs Asal R
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
My objection is based on the following material planning grounds:
1. Lack of Demonstrable Need
There are already several established community centres and halls operating within the SM2
postcode and surrounding Belmont area. These venues provide space for meetings, classes,
worship, and community activities.
No clear evidence has been presented to demonstrate an unmet need for an additional facility of
this scale.The proposal represents avoidable overprovision that would place further strain on local
roads and residential amenity.
2. Traffic and Highway Safety
Dorset Road and the surrounding streets are quiet residential roads that are not designed to
accommodate significant increases in traffic. A place of worship and community hub is likely to
generate concentrated vehicle movements, particularly during evenings and weekends. Events,
gatherings and organised activities may result in simultaneous arrivals and departures, creating
congestion and increased risk to pedestrians.
There are schools in close proximity to the site. Additional traffic during school drop-off and pick-
up times would raise serious highway safety concerns. Overspill parking on surrounding
residential roads is highly likely if on-site provision proves insufficient, potentially obstructing
driveways, narrowing carriageways and restricting emergency access.
3. Impact on Residential Amenity
The proposed use represents a substantial intensification compared to the former ambulance
station. The regular congregation of large groups, combined with evening and weekend activities,
would materially alter the quiet character of the area. Extended hours of use could lead to
disturbance during evenings and weekends, undermining the peaceful residential environment
currently enjoyed.
4. Scale, Bulk and Design
The proposal includes part one- and part two-storey extensions and an increase in height to part
of the building. This raises concerns regarding over development of the site and visual impact on
the street scene. The increased scale and massing may appear out of keeping with the
surrounding residential character and could result in overshadowing or loss of outlook for
neighboring properties.

and beyond ) and they along with their parents/guardians will pass the site twice daily on foot. If
the project goes ahead at this location, it will bring additional road safety concerns to the students,
