Address: 97 Langley Park Road Sutton Sutton SM2 5HF
Name: Ms Yanan Yang
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly object this planning DM2025/01591 as a local resident. My
concerns relate to noise, traffic, parking, safety, and the cumulative
impact of the proposed use - all of which conflict with key policies in the
Sutton Local Plan and the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF).
1. Noise and LateEvening Disturbance
The applicant suggests attendance of around 120 people, but in reality it
is not practical to restrict numbers for Friday prayers. The Council must
therefore consider the true maximum capacity of around 250 people, with
activities running until 11pm.
This is a quiet residential area. The dispersal of up to 250 people at 11pm
would cause unavoidable noise and disturbance.
Relevant policies:
* Sutton Local Plan Policy 29 (Amenity) - requires protection of
residents from noise and disturbance.
* NPPF Paragraphs 130(f) and 185(a) - developments must safeguard
residential amenity and be appropriate for their location.
2. Traffic and Highway Safety
The site sits 100 metres from a primary school with over 1,000 pupils. At
school dropoff and pickup times, the roads are already heavily congested,
with high pedestrian activity and limited visibility.
Adding up to 250 additional visitors - particularly during Friday prayers,
which may overlap with school finishing times - would create serious safety
risks for children and families.
Relevant policies:
* Policy 34 (Transport and Development) - development must not create
or worsen highway hazards.
* NPPF Paragraph 111 - development should be refused if it results in
an unacceptable impact on highway safety.
3. Parking Pressure
Parking in the area is already extremely limited. The application does not
demonstrate how it will accommodate the parking needs of such a large number
of visitors.
Overspill parking would obstruct driveways, reduce visibility, and increase
risks for pedestrians - particularly schoolchildren.
Relevant policies:
* Policy 37 (Parking) - requires adequate onsite parking and
avoidance of harmful overspill.
* NPPF Paragraphs 105-106 - developments must consider local parking
pressures and avoid adverse impacts.
4. Cumulative Impact on the Community
When noise, latenight activity, schoolrelated congestion, parking
pressure, and safety risks are considered together, the cumulative impact is
substantial.
The scale and intensity of the proposed use are not compatible with the
residential character of the area.
Relevant policies:
* Policy 1 (Sustainable Growth) - development must be appropriate to
its context.
* Policy 28 (Character and Design) - requires development to respect
the surrounding area.
* NPPF Paragraph 92 - planning should support safe, healthy, and
inclusive communities.
Conclusion
This application conflicts with multiple Local Plan and NPPF policies. It
would introduce significant noise, traffic, parking stress, and safety risks
- particularly given the proximity of a large primary school.
For these reasons, I respectfully ask the Committee to refuse this
application DM2025/01591.

Name: A Fatima
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application

Name: Mrs Zainab Khan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly support this development.
The proposal goes beyond faith provision to include a genuine community hub. A mosque is not
only a place of worship for many muslims in the area, but it serves as a community space for
children, women, elderly. It also provides educational programmes, family support, youth
engagement, and charity initiatives, while encouraging members to be considerate neighbours and
active contributors to wider society.
The presence of a mosque can actively promote social cohesion. It creates opportunities for
interfaith dialogue, open days, charity events, and community outreach that bring people of
different backgrounds together. Importantly, Islamic teachings emphasise good character,
kindness, and maintaining positive relationships with those who live nearby.
Additionally, a mosque would be incredibly beneficial for residents who live in Cheam/Belmont as
it will provide somewhere in close proximity which is needed for this area. This will be in walking
distance for many Muslims living in the area and will mean we do not need to drive to other
places- it will reduce the need to use cars and other mosques in the area have become very full so
there is a practical need.
Our community is enriched by diversity. Supporting this mosque is a statement that we value
inclusion, mutual respect, and the right of all residents to practise their faith in a safe and dignified
manner. When people feel seen and supported, they are more likely to contribute positively to the
wider society.
I urge the council to consider not only the concerns raised, but also the long-term social benefits
this development would bring to families, young people, and the broader community.

Comment:

Name: Ms Keerti Sethia
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to this application on the following basis:
1. There would be significantly increased traffic and road safety risks, including over crowding with
parking in the neighbouring roads.
2. School safety - the pupils attending nearby Avenue Primary School (and their parents) would be
at risk from reduced parking in the area and reduced visibility around so many extra parked cars
3. Noise and disturbance - especially in the evening from voices, car doors, car engines etc.
4. Disturbance to the local community by increased congestion, especially for nearby care home
and elderly residents.
5. The proposed structure would look out of character in this neighbourhood and would dwarf
nearby properties.
6. It is already difficult to drive during school drop off and pick up times and the proposed use of

Address: Flat 3 Glenlyon House 8A Station Road Belmont SM2 6BH
Name: Mr Rashid Khan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am a local resident and I am writing in support of planning application DM2025/01591
for the former ambulance station on Dorset Road. I have looked at the application and I think this
would be a positive development for the area.
The dedicated provision in this proposal is a thoughtful and practical response to that reality. The
application recognises that women, families, and elderly residents have specific needs that are not
currently being met locally.
This is not just a place of worship. The community hub element is open to all residents and will
provide educational programmes, wellbeing sessions, social drop-ins, and interfaith activities. The
benefits extend well beyond any single community.
Some objectors have suggested parking will be chaotic, but this is not supported by the evidence.
There is ample room for the modest additional demand this facility would generate. The Transport
Statement surveyed actual conditions and found 269 spaces with only 134 occupied at peak time.
It is also worth mentioning that the NPPF, London Plan, and Sutton Local Plan all support this type
of use. I would note that refusing this application would require the Council to justify that decision
against a strong policy presumption in favour of community facility provision.
The submitted noise report confirms that the building fabric will contain sound effectively. Even at
the busiest time, with amplified speech during Friday prayers, the noise reaching neighbouring
properties is predicted to be below ambient background levels. This meets the Local Authority's
criteria.
The travel survey data shows that 50% of Friday prayer attendees travel on foot, only 11. This is a
community that predominantly walks to worship. 7% drive alone, and 20% car-share. The
provision of cycle storage in the application further supports sustainable travel.
Having a local facility where residents can access wellbeing activities and social support is a
genuine health benefit for the neighbourhood. The proposed community hub aligns with the
growing emphasis on preventative health and social prescribing.
In addition, for residents who do not drive, or for families with young children, that distance is a
real barrier. The nearest existing mosque facilities are over two miles away, in Sutton town centre
and Carshalton. A facility on Dorset Road would make community and faith provision genuinely
accessible for the first time.
I would also note that I note that the operating hours can be conditioned by the Council if it sees
fit. The applicant has already proposed restricted hours and a daytime-weighted programme of
activities. I do not see late-night disturbance as a realistic concern.
Compared to the former ambulance station, which ran 24 hours a day with emergency vehicles
and sirens at all hours, the proposed use is much calmer. Operating hours are restricted, there are
no large vehicles, and the activities are community-based rather than emergency-response.
Neighbours will notice the difference.
The concern about school-run congestion has been raised by objectors, but the timings simply do
not overlap. Friday prayers finish well before the afternoon school pickup begins. The Transport
Statement addresses this evidently.
Moreover, the extension is modest, the height increase is limited, and the overall footprint of the
building remains appropriate for the site. This is not a large-scale development. The design
improvements will make the site look better than it has in years.
Planning decisions should be based on material planning considerations: land use, design,
neighbour impact, transport, and environment. I am confident the Council will assess this
application fairly on those grounds.
Moreover, on top of that, the removal of the existing keep-clear lines will actually increase the
amount of on-street parking available to residents. The development will reduce the number of
parking spaces on site compared to the former ambulance station, which had 21.
For all of these reasons, I strongly support planning application DM2025/01591 and I urge the
London Borough of Sutton to grant planning permission.

Name: Mrs Maria Moheb
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to support the proposed development at the former ambulance station on
Dorset Road. Application DM2025/01591 represents a sensible and beneficial use of this vacant
site.
Residents will no longer have emergency vehicles coming and going through the night. The facility
will operate within defined hours, which is a considerable improvement over the former ambulance
station that ran 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
I would ask objectors to consider what they are comparing this proposal against. The site was not
a quiet park. It was an operational ambulance station running 24 hours a day. The proposed use is
calmer, quieter, and more controlled in every respect.
The scale of the proposed works is restrained. The external greening will soften the appearance of
the site. The extension does not dominate the site or the street scene, and the height increase is
limited to improving the internal functionality of the building.
Furthermore, the noise report confirms what common sense suggests: this is a quieter use of the
site. A 24-hour ambulance station with sirens and heavy vehicles was considerably noisier than a
community facility operating within defined hours.
I'd also add that the application demonstrates a clear and growing need for this type of facility in
the area. The demographic evidence is compelling, and the existing provision is simply not
sufficient. This proposal addresses an obvious gap.
It is also worth mentioning that the community hub aspect of this application should not be
overlooked. It provides something that the area currently lacks: a local, accessible space where
residents of all backgrounds can participate in educational, social, and wellbeing activities.
I hope the Council will focus on what matters in planning terms: whether this is the right use for
this site, whether it is well-designed, and whether its impacts are acceptable. The application
documents demonstrate convincingly that the answer to all three is yes.
In addition, the environmental assessments submitted with this application show that the
development has been planned responsibly. The Biodiversity Net Gain requirement is met, flood
risk has been assessed, and the energy strategy has been considered. This is a well-rounded
application.
It is also worth mentioning that a survey during the peak Friday prayer period found on-street
parking at less than 50% capacity in the surrounding area. I have read the Transport Statement
and the parking evidence is convincing. The worst-case modelling still leaves 66 spaces available
and stays well under the 85% parking stress threshold.
In addition, objectors seem to have missed that the previous use had 21 parking spaces on site.
The development reduces this number, not increases it. Additionally, the yellow lines that currently
restrict on-street parking near the entrance will be removed, giving residents more kerbside space
than they have now.
I'd also add that the Transport Statement has addressed this directly and the evidence is clear. I
understand why parents might worry about school-run congestion, but the timings of Friday
prayers simply do not overlap with school drop-off or pick-up.
The link between community participation and better health outcomes is not disputed. What is
often lacking is the local infrastructure to make participation possible. This proposal provides that
infrastructure in an area where it is currently missing.
The inclusion of dedicated women's facilities and family-friendly spaces is an important aspect of
this proposal. This facility would change that. Many women in the area, particularly those with
young children or those who do not drive, currently lack accessible local community provision.
I note that the travel survey data demonstrates a very low rate of single-occupancy car use among
the congregation. Most people walk. The transport impact of this facility is modest. The addition of
cycle storage will support this further.
The NPPF requires planning authorities to plan positively for community infrastructure. This
application is supported at every level of the policy hierarchy, and I would expect the Council to
give that due weight. Sutton's Local Plan echoes this requirement.
This is a 0.12 hectare brownfield site that has been empty since the ambulance service left. The
proposal brings it back into active use for the community. I cannot see any planning reason to
prefer a vacant, deteriorating site over a well-managed community facility.
I respectfully request that the London Borough of Sutton grants planning permission for
DM2025/01591. This is a proposal that will make the neighbourhood better.

Name: Miss Raayan Zafar
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to register my support for planning application DM2025/01591. This is a sensible,
policy-compliant proposal that will make good use of a vacant site and serve the local community.
A community centre with restricted hours versus a 24-hour emergency vehicle depot. The
comparison speaks for itself. The proposed use is a clear improvement in amenity terms for the
surrounding residential streets. Relatedly, this proposal puts accessible provision within walking
distance, with dedicated spaces designed for women and families. Women who do not drive are
effectively cut off from community and faith provision when the nearest facilities are miles away.
The claim that this development will create a new parking problem ignores the fact that the
previous use had 21 parking spaces and generated regular vehicle movements. The development
reduces on-site parking and removes restrictive yellow lines, actually improving the kerbside
situation for residents.
There is no temporal overlap between Friday prayer times and the school run at Avenue Primary
Academy. This concern, while understandable, is not founded on the actual timings. Prayers are at
midday; the school run is morning and mid-afternoon.
I'd also add that the submitted noise report confirms that the building fabric will contain sound
effectively. This meets the Local Authority's criteria. Even at the busiest time, with amplified
speech during Friday prayers, the noise reaching neighbouring properties is predicted to be below
ambient background levels.
I wholeheartedly support this application and ask the Council to grant planning permission. This
facility is needed and will be welcomed.

Address: Flat 7 Hawthorn Court 148 St James' Road Sutton SM1 2TY
Name: Mr shiguang li
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly object this planning DM2025/01591 as a local resident.
My concerns relate to noise, traffic, parking, safety, and the cumulative impact of the proposed use
- all of which conflict with key policies in the Sutton Local Plan and the National Planning Policy
Framework (NPPF).
1. Noise and LateEvening Disturbance
The applicant suggests attendance of around 120 people, but in reality it
is not practical to restrict numbers for Friday prayers. The Council must therefore consider the true
maximum capacity of around 250 people, with activities running until 11pm.
This is a quiet residential area. The dispersal of up to 250 people at 11pm would cause
unavoidable noise and disturbance.
Relevant policies:
* Sutton Local Plan Policy 29 (Amenity) - requires protection of
residents from noise and disturbance.
* NPPF Paragraphs 130(f) and 185(a) - developments must safeguard residential amenity and be
appropriate for their location.
2. Traffic and Highway Safety
The site sits 100 metres from a primary school with over 1,000 pupils. At school dropoff and
pickup times, the roads are already heavily congested, with high pedestrian activity and limited
visibility.
Adding up to 250 additional visitors - particularly during Friday prayers, which may overlap with
school finishing times - would create serious safety risks for children and families.
Relevant policies:
* Policy 34 (Transport and Development) - development must not create or worsen highway
hazards.
* NPPF Paragraph 111 - development should be refused if it results in an unacceptable impact on
highway safety.
3. Parking Pressure
Parking in the area is already extremely limited. The application does not demonstrate how it will
accommodate the parking needs of such a large number of visitors.
Overspill parking would obstruct driveways, reduce visibility, and increase risks for pedestrians -
particularly schoolchildren.
Relevant policies:
* Policy 37 (Parking) - requires adequate onsite parking and
avoidance of harmful overspill.
* NPPF Paragraphs 105-106 - developments must consider local parking pressures and avoid
adverse impacts.
4. Cumulative Impact on the Community
When noise, latenight activity, schoolrelated congestion, parking pressure, and safety risks are
considered together, the cumulative impact is substantial.
The scale and intensity of the proposed use are not compatible with the residential character of
the area.
Relevant policies:
* Policy 1 (Sustainable Growth) - development must be appropriate to its context.
* Policy 28 (Character and Design) - requires development to respect the surrounding area.
* NPPF Paragraph 92 - planning should support safe, healthy, and inclusive communities.
Conclusion
This application conflicts with multiple Local Plan and NPPF policies. It would introduce significant
noise, traffic, parking stress, and safety risks - particularly given the proximity of a large primary
school.
For these reasons, I respectfully ask the Committee to refuse this
application DM2025/01591.

Name: Mr P Chunduru
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Planning Officer,
I wish to formally object to the above planning application for the proposed change of use to Class
F1 (Place of Worship) and Class F2 (Community Hub).
My objection is based on the following material planning considerations:
Traffic and Highway Safety:
The site is located on an already heavily congested road, particularly during school drop-off and
pick-up times. The proposed use is likely to significantly increase vehicular traffic and pedestrian
movement, creating a serious highway safety risk, particularly for children attending the nearby
school.
Inadequate Parking Provision:
The application does not demonstrate sufficient on-site parking. The surrounding residential
streets are already under parking stress, and overspill parking will likely obstruct driveways and
emergency vehicle access.
Noise and Disturbance:
The proposed use may generate significant noise from gatherings, amplified sound, and increased
activity during evenings and weekends, adversely affecting neighbouring residential properties and
the nearby school.
Impact on Residential Amenity:
The scale and intensity of use is not compatible with the predominantly residential character of the
area and would materially harm the quiet enjoyment of neighbouring homes.
For these reasons, I respectfully request that the application be refused.
Thanks.

Name: Mr M Ullah
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment: I whole heartedly support this application as it reflects a need in the community.

Name: Mr Nagendra KOMMURU
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Increased congestion: More vehicles mean longer travel times and bottlenecks at
intersections.
Safety risks: Higher traffic volumes raise the likelihood of accidents, particularly for pedestrians
and cyclists.
Strain on infrastructure: Existing road networks may not be designed for added capacity.
Emergency access concerns: Congested streets can delay emergency services.
Position emphasis: If road capacity and intersection performance are already stretched, additional
traffic can significantly reduce local livability.

Name: Mr Calvert Markham
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Clear benefits have been cited for establishing this new centre, but the issue seems not
to be about its value, but on the impact on the local area.
There are considerable traffic difficulties both in access and parking for the Avenue School. The
addition of a further community centre, which will increase traffic, will exacerbate the current
situation.
More needs to be done to put to rest the concerns of local residents in this development.

Name: Mr Seyit Tolga Ingenc
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
As a local resident, I am writing to support planning application DM2025/01591. I think
this proposal deserves approval and will explain my reasons below.
Objectors have raised concerns about parking, but the evidence does not support them. The
Transport Statement shows 269 on-street spaces within 400 metres of the site, with under 50%
utilisation at peak times. Even adding the maximum predicted visitors, there is still comfortable
headroom before parking stress becomes an issue.
In addition, compared to the former ambulance station, which ran 24 hours a day with emergency
vehicles and sirens at all hours, the proposed use is much calmer. Operating hours are restricted,
there are no large vehicles, and the activities are community-based rather than emergency-
response. Neighbours will notice the difference.
It is also worth mentioning that some objectors have raised concerns about late-night disturbance,
but the operating hours are restricted and the programming shows activities spread across the
daytime hours. This is not a late-night venue.
The application shows a clear and growing need for this type of facility in the area. This proposal
addresses an obvious gap. The demographic evidence is compelling, and the existing provision is
simply not sufficient.
GPs increasingly refer patients to community activities as part of social prescribing. But that only
works if there are local facilities to refer to. This proposal would create one in an area that
currently has very little.
In addition, the proportion driving alone is very low. Based on actual travel surveys of the existing
congregation, the majority of users walk to worship. This means the impact on local parking and
traffic is much smaller than objectors seem to assume.
It is also worth mentioning that I welcome the fact that this proposal goes beyond faith provision to
include a genuine community hub. The wellbeing sessions, drop-ins, and educational programmes
will serve the whole neighbourhood and help build a more connected community.
Moreover, there is no temporal overlap between Friday prayer times and the school run at Avenue
Primary Academy. Prayers are at midday; the school run is morning and mid-afternoon. This
concern, while understandable, is not founded on the actual timings.
I ask the London Borough of Sutton to approve application DM2025/01591. It is well-planned,
policy-compliant, and will bring tangible benefit to the community.

Name: Ms Isra Hasan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application

Name: Ms Kat Haagel
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
With pickups at the Avenue school and a membership at the Downs Lawn Tennis club
(Holland Avenue) I fear the increase in parking needs and overall traffic will be at detriment and
risk to the surrounding areas. To try to get through on the surrounding roads and to get parked it's
already difficult. The local bus often cannot pass safely due to parked cars, so emergency vehicles
will face the same difficulty.

Name: Mr Omer Sen
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I write in support of the above application and wish to make the following observations
for the committee's consideration.
The proposal addresses a demonstrable local need. The Muslim community in this borough has
grown considerably, and existing worship facilities are overcrowded and insufficient. Approval
would provide a sustainable, locally accessible solution - reducing long-distance travel and
improving access for elderly residents and families with young children.
With regard to the objections submitted, I would respectfully note that the former ambulance use
involved 24-hour operations with large emergency vehicles - a level of traffic and noise that
significantly exceeds what a community facility would generate. The claim that this proposal
represents an intensification of harmful activity is therefore not well-founded when compared to the
previous lawful use of the site.
The proposed development is consistent with planning policy supporting community facilities and
the efficient re-use of previously developed land. I urge the committee to grant permission.

Name: Mrs Zekiye Sen
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing in strong support of this planning application for the conversion of the
former ambulance station into a Masjid and Community Centre.
This proposal would meet a genuine and growing need. Muslim residents in this borough currently
lack sufficient local worship and community space, meaning families, the elderly, and young
people must travel unnecessarily long distances. A facility of this kind would change that.
I would also like to address some of the concerns raised in objections. On traffic: the site
previously operated as a 24-hour emergency ambulance station with large heavy vehicles
responding at all hours. A community and worship facility will generate a fraction of that activity,
concentrated at predictable times. This is an improvement, not a deterioration, for local residents
and the school nearby.
On noise and amenity: mosques and Islamic centres across the UK are well-established
community institutions known for their calm and orderly congregations. They are not sources of
antisocial noise. The concerns raised do not reflect the reality of how these facilities operate in
practice.
Converting a vacant building into an active, positive community hub - offering prayer, education,
youth activities, and support services open to all - is exactly the kind of development our area
needs. I urge the planning committee to approve this application.

Name: Mr Mehmet Sen
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to support this planning application. A Masjid and Community Centre in our
neighbourhood would be a genuinely positive addition - providing a local place of worship,
education, and community support for families who currently have to travel significant distances.
The site is a vacant former ambulance station. Converting it into an active community facility is a
sensible use of the building, and I believe it will benefit residents of all backgrounds, not only those
who worship there.
I urge the committee to approve this application.

Name: Mr C Pulger
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to the proposed redevelopment of the former ambulance station
to a Place of Worship and Community Hub.
While the inclusion of community facilities is acknowledged, the scale and intensity of this proposal
are fundamentally incompatible with the quiet, residential character of the surrounding
neighbourhood and the safety requirements of the adjacent primary school. The proposal conflicts
with national planning policy objectives that seek to promote safe, healthy, and well-functioning
places.
Impact on Highway Safety and School Pupils
The proposed change to a high-capacity Place of Worship (Use Class F1) and Community Hub
(Use Class F2) will result in a significant increase in vehicular movements. The surrounding
residential streets already experience considerable congestion and traffic stress during school
drop-off and pick-up periods. The addition of congregational traffic would exacerbate these
conditions, creating unsafe bottlenecks and increasing risks to young pedestrians.
This is contrary to the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF), which requires development
to provide safe and suitable access for all users and to ensure that highway safety is not
compromised. Paragraphs 110 and 111 of the NPPF make clear that development should only be
permitted where safe access can be achieved and where the residual cumulative impacts on the
road network would not be severe. In this case, the proposal fails to demonstrate that it would not
result in unacceptable risks to highway safety, particularly for schoolchildren.
Furthermore, the provision of cycle storage and refuse facilities indicates a level of occupancy that
the existing narrow residential road infrastructure is not designed to safely accommodate.
Loss of Residential Amenity
The residential environment in this location is particularly sensitive to noise and disturbance. The
proposed mixed-use development would attract large numbers of users simultaneously. Noise
arising from increased footfall, vehicle movements, car doors, and congregational activity-
potentially occurring during early mornings and late evenings-would result in an unacceptable loss
of residential amenity for neighbouring properties.
This directly conflicts with paragraph 135 of the NPPF, which states that development should be
sympathetic to local character and should not create unacceptable impacts on the living conditions
of existing residents, including from noise and disturbance. The proposal fails to demonstrate how
these impacts would be adequately mitigated.
Overdevelopment and Massing
The proposed part-one, part-two storey extensions and overall increase in building height
represent an over-intensification of the site. The increased scale and massing would appear
visually overbearing and out of keeping with the surrounding residential context.
This is inconsistent with paragraph 130 of the NPPF, which requires development to respond
positively to local character, maintain a strong sense of place, and ensure that buildings are
visually attractive and appropriate to their surroundings. The proposal fails to achieve these
objectives.
Failure to Achieve Sustainable Development
Taken as a whole, the proposal does not meet the NPPF's core objective of achieving sustainable
development. Paragraph 8 of the NPPF makes clear that social sustainability includes supporting
health, safety, and quality of life. A development that increases risk to children, harms residential
amenity, and undermines community cohesion cannot be considered socially sustainable.
Overall, the proposal fails to demonstrate that it can operate without compromising the safety of
schoolchildren or the reasonable enjoyment and tranquillity of nearby homes. For these reasons,
the development should be refused.
It is also evident that a significant number of local residents have raised objections to this
proposal, indicating a clear lack of community support. Should planning permission be granted
despite this overwhelming opposition, there is a strong likelihood that the decision-making process
and consultation procedure would be subject to scrutiny. Such an outcome risks causing
substantial disappointment, undermining confidence in the planning system, and damaging
community cohesion.

Name: Mr mustafa cayir
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Here is a shorter and slightly reworded version:
---
I am writing to express my strong support for the proposal to convert the former ambulance station
into a Masjid and Community Centre.
There is a clear and growing need for adequate worship and community space for Muslim
residents in the borough. At present, many families, elderly residents, and young people must
travel significant distances to access suitable facilities. This development would provide an
important local solution.
Concerns about traffic and noise should be viewed in context. The site previously operated as a
24-hour ambulance station with heavy vehicle movements at all hours. A community and worship
centre would generate far less activity, at predictable times, representing an overall improvement
for the area.
Transforming a vacant building into a positive community hub - offering prayer, education, and
support services - would bring clear social benefits. I respectfully urge the planning committee to
approve the application.

Address: 200 Church Hill Road North Cheam SM3 8LA
Name: Mr Ertugrul Duran
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application

Name: Mr MONAF ROSIN
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application

Address: 5 Buckingham Court 9 Dorset Road Belmont SM2 6JJ
Name: Ms EMMA NATALIE CHOULES
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
As a neighbouring resident of the former ambulance station, I wish to object to the
proposed development on the grounds of highway safety, parking impact, residential amenity,
noise disturbance, overdevelopment, and harm to local character.
There is already a pending application at Buckingham Court (Ref: DM2025/00363) for six
additional residential units, and local residents have raised concerns about cumulative parking
stress from that proposal. The combined impact of both schemes would worsen existing
congestion and should be assessed together, as required under cumulative impact considerations.
1. Parking and Highway Safety
The proposal has the potential to generate significant vehicle movements, particularly for Friday
gatherings where attendance could exceed 70 vehicles. The surrounding residential streets
already experience peaktime congestion associated with Avenue Primary Academy, which has an
early Friday finish. The additional influx of vehicles would create conflict between school traffic,
residents, and visitors to the site. Dorset Road is a nothrough road with limited turning space, and
the area does not have the capacity to safely accommodate eventbased traffic. Despite claims that
most attendees will travel on foot, consultation comments show many supporters are from beyond
walking distance, making this assumption unrealistic.
2. Impact on School Safety
The site is situated very close to Avenue Primary Academy (over 1,000 pupils). Additional traffic
during peak school times would worsen congestion, increase unsafe manoeuvres, reduce visibility,
and elevate risks to pupils and families. This represents an avoidable intensification of vehicle
movements in an already pressured school zone.
3. Noise and LateEvening Disturbance
The proposed lateevening use (up to 11pm) and the dispersal of up to 250 attendees would cause
unavoidable noise from conversations, engines, car doors, and vehicle movements. This is
incompatible with a quiet residential location.
Claims that the previous ambulance station operated 24 hours are misleading. As a resident for
nine years, I experienced no comparable noise disturbance, and its historic use generated minimal
latenight traffic. The proposed use represents a significant intensification.
4. Overdevelopment and Visual Impact
The proposed extensions, including an increased roof height, would create a visually dominant
structure inconsistent with the existing domestic scale of surrounding homes. This would adversely
affect the character and appearance of the neighbourhood.
In combination with the proposed Buckingham Court development, the raised roof would also
create a sense of enclosure for neighbouring residents, contributing to an overdeveloped
appearance.
Conclusion
The proposal represents an overintensive use of a constrained residential site, resulting in
unacceptable harm to:
highway and pedestrian safety
residential amenity
local character
schooladjacent safety and congestion
For these reasons, I respectfully request that planning permission be refused.

Name: Mrs Susan Chard
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment: Housing is needed, there's a very big mosque in Morden. Parking would be horrendous.

Name: Mr David Halili
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am in support of this application. There is nothing wrong with having a place of worship
in this area. This will only bring together a community, which is much needed. Nothing bad will
come of this.

Name: Mr Yigit Agabeyli
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I express my full support for this planning application to transform the former ambulance
station into a Masjid and Community Centre.
This development would address a clear and increasing need within the borough. At present,
Muslim residents do not have adequate local space for worship or community activities, which
forces many families, elderly individuals, and young people to travel considerable distances.
Establishing this facility would provide accessible, local provision and greatly benefit the
community.
I would also like to respond to some of the concerns raised in the objections. Regarding traffic: the
site previously functioned as a 24-hour ambulance station, with large emergency vehicles
operating at all times of day and night. In comparison, a place of worship and community centre
would produce significantly less traffic, occurring mainly at set and predictable times. This
represents an overall reduction in disruption for nearby residents and the neighbouring school.
In terms of noise and general amenity: mosques and Islamic centres throughout the UK are long-
standing, respected community institutions. They are typically peaceful, well-managed
environments and are not associated with antisocial noise or disturbance. The objections raised
do not accurately reflect the way such facilities operate in practice.
Bringing a vacant building back into meaningful use as a welcoming community hub - providing
prayer facilities, educational programmes, youth engagement, and support services accessible to
all - would be a positive step for the area. I strongly encourage the planning committee to grant
approval for this application.

Name: Mr Iain Carnegie
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
As a resident of Holland Avenue, living in particularly close proximity to the site of the
proposed building, I am opposed to these plans for the following reasons:
1. The road infrastructure of Dorset Road, Holland Avenue, Avenue Road and other adjoining
roads was never designed to accommodate the volume of traffic and parking caused by the
significant expansion of The Avenue Primary Academy and the daily occurrence of illegal parking
on red lines, yellow lines and zig zags has led to serious traffic jams, congestion and verbal
confrontations on a daily basis. On some occasions, these roads have become gridlocked due to
no places for cars to pull in. Adding a place of worship with completely inadequate parking
facilities in the middle of this is therefore inappropriate.
2. The proposal is in direct opposition to the council's Sutton School Streets Scheme, which aims
to address the rising incidence of asthma and related illnesses in children and others due to
increased traffic volumes around school sites.
3. The proposed building will be in use from 7am - 11pm. This will cause significant disturbance
and noise nuisance with no respite for local residents.
4. Exhaust fumes will emit directly into the gardens of our neighbouring properties, given the citing
of the few parking spaces.
For these reasons and others, this site is totally inappropriate for the proposals detailed in the
application and will have a significantly negative impact on the local residents and also further
compromise the health and safety of local school children if implemented. I urge you to reject.

Name: Ms Aysha Jamil
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to the Council to express my support for the planning application at the
former ambulance station on Dorset Road (DM2025/01591). I think this is a good proposal that
merits approval.
A community centre with restricted hours versus a 24-hour emergency vehicle depot. The
comparison speaks for itself. The proposed use is a clear improvement in amenity terms for the
surrounding residential streets.
On this point, I have checked the timings and there is no conflict between Friday prayers and the
school run. The prayer changeover is around 12:30 to 12:55, while school drop-off and pick-up are
at 08:30 and 15:00 respectively. These do not coincide.
Additionally, I hope the Council will focus on what matters in planning terms: whether this is the
right use for this site, whether it is well-designed, and whether its impacts are acceptable. The
application documents demonstrate convincingly that the answer to all three is yes.
The planning statement provides clear demographic evidence that the local Muslim community
has outgrown the available facilities. But this is not just about faith provision. The need is real and
well-documented. The community hub will serve all residents with educational, social, and
wellbeing activities.
The mix of uses in this application is what makes it so valuable. It is not a single-use building. The
community hub element will host activities for all residents, making it a shared neighbourhood
resource rather than a facility for one group.
I hope the Council will give this application the positive decision it merits. I fully support the grant
of planning permission for DM2025/01591.

Name: Mrs Carole Smith
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
My first objection is the effect this will have on the already difficult parking situation
which is already dangerous for motorists and pedestrians and it is also a bus route which makes
for chaos at busy times in York Road.
My second objection is that it will change our tranquil neighbourhood and I have already seen the
chaos Friday prayers causes with inconsiderate parking.
My third objection is that local residents will be excluded from the new "community" area.
Some of the people supporting this new venture are from out of the area and will not be affected
by the extra traffic.

Name: Maham Taimuri
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I have been a local resident in Sutton for the best part of 20 years. Growing up there
was a lack of youth activities and I felt there was not that much of a community. I got introduced to
BASMA about 5 years ago and have been attending regular congregations since. BASMA brings
about a sense of community in the locality and over the last 20 years this was something that was
lacking. They regularly host discussions, festivals, get togethers, careers talks, and do activities
which support the youth and the most vulnerable in our society. The community came together to
fund the purchase of the Ambulance centre and this is largely a community led project by local
residents, for the local residents. This project is to create a central location where BASMA can
base themselves and continue to provide benefit to the local community. Whilst BASMA has put in
permission for a place of worship, it keeps its doors open to people of all faiths and also to those
that do not follow a particular faith. Should the permission be granted, the developed
accomodation can host youth clubs, recreational halls and community get togethers.
The NPPF and current Government agenda is
to promote the development of brownfield sites and this application for the ambulance centre fits
this category. The proposed plans makes good majority of the existing structure and building
footprint suggesting the environmental impact of the development will be minimal as opposed to
keeping a derelict site in the locality. A small extension has been proposed, however this is
towards the rear of the building and has been incorporated in the design to maintain a female
friendly use of the space encouraging diversity and inclusion. The planning application has taken
into consideration parking provisions by incorporating 12 onsite parking provisions and 2 disabled
parking spaces. The operational times have factored in school pick up timings thereby not causing
unnecessary strain on local infrastructure. The biodiversity of the site has been factored in
maintaining green spaces, and a cycle storage facility is also incorporated within the design. The
location is such where local residents can walk to the site. This suggests that the design and
location has great consideration for the environment and general wellbeing of individuals.
One similar development in the area which comes to mind is the Church at the bottom end of
Northey Avenue. Regular congregations occur here and on street and off street parking is
incorporated however this use of their site is welcomed by locals and has not had a severe impact
on local traffic. This Church does bring together community and I am very confident that should
the application for the ambulance centre be granted, a similar positive effect would take place in
the area.
In conclusion, I feel the application for the ambulance centre is a proposed development which I
feel will greatly benefit the local community and help promote Sutton, Belmont, and Cheam as a
welcoming, diverse, and considerate neighbourhood. I therefore humbly request that this
application is given the positive light that it deserves and that it is granted over an overwhelming
support from the locals in the area.

Name: Mrs Prathima Gummadi
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Avenue and Dorset Roads' residential areas are already congested with active schools,
senior citizen homes and housing that all require access to Belmont Village. Buses are already
having difficulty travelling through the roads and nearby residential roads are not designed to
accommodate such levels of vehicular activity.

Name: Mrs Kathleen Wright
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I do not think it is sensible to have this change. I have noted the number of cars trying to
find spaces while the services have been held at St John's Community hall. If this number were to
try to find spaces so near to the Avenue School just at the time the nursery children are coming
and going, I can imagine chaos. The parents of the school children already use all of the nearby
streets for parking.

Name: Mr Patrick Rayfield
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The development is located in a residential area, a commercial development, is not in
keeping with in keeping with the streetscape of the area. it is out of character.
The development will result in a massive increase in traffic movements for the area. The proposed
development is in close proximity to a school and in very close proximity to 2 sports clubs. At
certain times of the day the road network is already log jammed, notwithstanding a frequently used
bus route.
The development will result in a massive increase in required car parking bays. The area is
residential with next to no "spare" parking bays.
Where are the cars going to park??? Random cars being parked illegally will result in significant
road safety concerns given the close proximity of a school and sports clubs.

Name: Mrs Debra Upot
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to submit a formal objection to planning application DM2025/01591 for the
proposed use of the former ambulance station on Dorset Road as a place of worship.
While community and faith facilities play an important role within the borough, the proposed
development raises serious concerns regarding highway safety, residential amenity and the
suitability of this location for a potentially high-attendance destination use.
1. Highway Safety and Cumulative Impact on an already Sensitive Street
Dorset Road already accommodates sensitive community uses, including a primary school and a
residential care home. These uses generate predictable peak periods involving children, elderly
residents, carers, delivery vehicles and school-related traffic.
The introduction of a place of worship is likely to create additional concentrated traffic movements
at similar peak times, particularly:
- weekday evening gatherings
- special religious events attracting large numbers of visitors
This cumulative activity risks significantly increasing congestion, unsafe parking and vehicle
manoeuvres on a road already serving vulnerable users.
The presence of school children and elderly residents heightens the need for a precautionary
approach to highway safety. Increased visitor traffic, informal drop-offs and overspill parking could
materially compromise pedestrian safety and safe access for emergency and care services.
2. Impact on Vulnerable Road Users
Planning decisions must consider the needs of vulnerable groups. The proximity of both a primary
school and a care home means Dorset Road functions as a sensitive environment requiring calm
traffic conditions, clear sightlines and reliable access.
A destination venue attracting large numbers of visitors may lead to:
- inappropriate parking near crossings and junctions
- obstructions of pavements
- increased noise and activity during sensitive periods
Such impacts would be inconsistent with maintaining a safe and appropriate environment for
children, elderly residents and care home operations.
3. Residential Amenity and Noise Disturbance
The proposed use represents a significant intensification compared with the former ambulance
station. A place of worship involves simultaneous arrivals and departures, social gatherings before
and after services and potential evening and weekend activity.
Given the residential nature of Dorset Road, these patterns of use risk causing ongoing
disturbance through noise, congregation outside the building and traffic-related impacts extending
beyond normal residential expectations.
4. Suitability of the Site
Although the previous use was institutional, it did not operate as a public assembly venue
attracting regular large gatherings. The constrained nature of the site and surrounding residential
context raise doubts as to whether it is an appropriate location for a high-attendance community
destination.
Alternative locations better served by main roads or commercial areas may be more suitable for
such a use.
5. Insufficient Operational Information
The application does not appear to provide sufficient clarity regarding:
- expected congregation size
- service frequency
- hours of operation
- management of large events
Without this information, it is not possible to conclude that unacceptable impacts would be
avoided.
Conclusion
For the reasons outlined above, particularly the highway safety implications arising from the
proximity of a primary school and care home, I respectfully request that the Council refuse
planning permission.

Comment: I support as it would bring a positive impact to the communities

Much needed community centre for the surrounding area for the usage of all
communities . For the elderly , the youth , mother and toddler groups , health and fitness and

Name: Ryan Sharp
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing as a local resident to support this application.
Our area genuinely benefits from accessible community spaces and this is an excellent
opportunity to bring a disused building back into active, positive use. Community hubs strengthen
neighbourhoods, reduce isolation, and provide vital gathering spaces for residents of all
backgrounds.
The concerns raised about traffic and parking are disproportionate. The Avenue already
experiences its heaviest congestion during school drop-off and pick-up at Avenue Primary
Academy - far exceeding what occasional community gatherings would generate. Additionally,
Belmont church already serves a regular congregation nearby without causing the predicted
disruption to parking or road safety. The evidence simply does not support the level of concern
being expressed.
The noise and disturbance objections similarly appear speculative. Community halls and places of
worship operate successfully throughout residential areas across the borough without the
problems described.
Repurposing a former ambulance station into a thriving community hub is exactly the kind of
sensitive, purposeful development that should be encouraged. I urge the council to assess this
application on its planning merits and approve it.

Name: Ms Jing Guo

It will generate a significant increase in traffic, which would impact the surrounding

Name: Mrs Jacqueline Jablonski

Comment:
There's already an enormous mosque in Morden, Sutton does not need another.
There's already a huge amount of traffic on A217 without adding more and there's no adequate
