Name: Mr Edgar Ter Danielyan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I write to register my objection to the above planning application for the change of use of
the former Sutton Ambulance Station at 18 Dorset Road, Belmont, from Sui Generis (Ambulance
Station) to mixed use Classes F1 (Places of Worship) and F2 (Community Hub), together with the
proposed physical extensions and increase in building height. I am a local resident whose home
and daily life would be directly affected by this proposal.
My objection is that this specific site, by reason of its location, access constraints, parking context,
and proximity to sensitive receptors, is unsuitable for the proposed use, and that the proposal
conflicts with adopted development plan policy at national, regional, and local level.
The material planning grounds for my objection are set out below under eight headings. In each
case, I identify the relevant development plan policies that are engaged.
1. Traffic Generation and Highway Safety
The proposed change of use would result in a dramatic and unacceptable intensification of
vehicular traffic on Dorset Road and at its junction with the A217 (Belmont Rise). The former
ambulance station generated modest, predictable vehicle movements associated with a small
professional crew and emergency call-outs. By contrast, a place of worship and community hub
attracting congregations would generate concentrated peak-hour traffic of an entirely different
order of magnitude.
The critical characteristic of this site is that Dorset Road has a long-established traffic closure that
prevents through-traffic, meaning the road functions as a cul-de-sac with vehicular access only
from the A217 end. This closure has been in place for many years and is valued by residents
precisely because it protects the quiet, residential character of the street. Every vehicle visiting the
proposed development must enter and exit via a single junction with the A217. The arrival and
departure of a large congregation within a short period - particularly for Friday prayers, weekend
events, weddings, funerals, and community gatherings - would concentrate all vehicle movements
at this single pinch-point, creating queuing, congestion, and conflict with traffic on the A217.
The proposal would also generate traffic at times that are entirely alien to the existing residential
character of the street. Daily prayers, evening classes, weekend events, and seasonal
observances such as Ramadan would produce vehicle movements early in the morning, late at
night, and throughout weekends and public holidays. These are precisely the periods when
residential amenity is most sensitive and when residents have a legitimate expectation of quiet
enjoyment.
This ground of objection is supported by Paragraph 115(d) of the NPPF (December 2024), which
requires that any significant impacts from the development on the transport network, in terms of
capacity and congestion, or on highway safety, can be cost-effectively mitigated to an acceptable
degree. Paragraph 116 provides that development should only be refused on highways grounds
where there would be an unacceptable impact on highway safety, or the residual cumulative
impacts on the road network would be severe; it is submitted that both thresholds are met here.
London Plan Policy T4 requires that development proposals should not increase road danger.
Sutton Local Plan Policy 24 requires transport assessments and the promotion of sustainable
transport. The proposed development fundamentally conflicts with all of these policies.
2. Parking Pressure on Surrounding Residential Streets
Belmont already has a Controlled Parking Zone (CPZ), which is concrete evidence that parking
pressure is a recognised and documented problem in this area. The London Borough of Sutton
operates only a small number of CPZs across the entire borough, and the fact that Belmont
requires one confirms that on-street parking demand already exceeds available capacity. The
council's own borough-wide parking review has acknowledged that parking remains one of the
issues of most concern to residents, and active consultations specifically for the Belmont area
were conducted throughout 2023-2024.
The site has a Public Transport Accessibility Level (PTAL) rating of just 1b-2, which is among the
lowest ratings in the transport accessibility scale. This means the area is heavily car-dependent,
and the overwhelming majority of visitors to any facility on this site would arrive by private car. A
congregation of 240-250 people, even allowing for car-sharing, would generate demand for well
over 100 parking spaces per session. The site is approximately 0.3 acres (0.12 hectares) in area.
Even after the proposed extensions, the amount of on-site parking that can realistically be
provided is a small fraction of the demand that would be generated.
The inevitable consequence is that the vast majority of visitors would park on surrounding
residential streets - Dorset Road, Overton Road, Linden Road, Chiltern Road, and adjoining roads
- displacing residents from parking near their own homes. This overspill parking would occur both
within and outside CPZ operational hours. On Fridays, weekends, evenings, and during religious
festivals, parking demand would be at its highest, and much of this would fall outside CPZ
enforcement hours, leaving residents with no protection.
The London Plan provides no specific maximum parking standard for F1 or F2 uses. Policy T6 and
Paragraph 10.6.5 state that parking levels for such uses must be determined on a case-by-case
basis, taking into account PTAL, local connectivity, and the transport context of the site. In an area
with PTAL 1b-2, adequate on-site parking provision is essential. The failure to provide it is contrary
to policy and would cause demonstrable harm to residential amenity.
3. Noise and Disturbance to Residential Amenity
The proposed change of use would fundamentally alter the noise environment experienced by
neighbouring residential properties. A place of worship and community hub generates sustained,
regular, and predictable noise from multiple sources: amplified speech, recitation, or chanting
within the building; the gathering and dispersal of large congregations outside the building before
and after services; the simultaneous starting, manoeuvring, and departure of dozens of vehicles;
conversations and social interaction in outdoor areas; deliveries; and celebrations associated with
weddings, festivals, and community events.
The site is flanked by residential properties on all sides. It is also in close proximity to Overton
Park (a public recreation ground used by families and children), a primary school, Overton Grange
secondary school, Sutton Bowling Club, and Belmont House Nursing Home (a 60-bed residential,
nursing, and dementia care facility rated "Good" by the Care Quality Commission). The density of
noise-sensitive receptors around this site is unusually high. Residents in the nursing home, many
of whom are elderly or living with dementia, are particularly vulnerable to disturbance from
sustained and unpredictable noise.
This ground of objection is supported by Sutton Local Plan Policy 29 (Protecting Amenity), which
requires development to avoid unacceptable impacts on neighbouring amenity including noise and
disturbance. London Plan Policy D14 (Noise) requires development to avoid significant adverse
noise impacts on health and quality of life. London Plan Policy D13 (Agent of Change) places the
onus on new noise-generating development to design in mitigation to protect existing residents.
NPPF Paragraph 135(f) requires development to create places with a high standard of amenity for
existing and future users. The proposed development conflicts with all of these policies.
4. Hours of Operation
Places of worship and community hubs, by their nature, operate across a wide range of hours that
extend well beyond the typical working day. Daily prayers may begin before dawn and continue
after dark. Evening study circles, youth groups, community meetings, and social events extend
activity into late evening. Seasonal observances, particularly Ramadan, involve pre-dawn and
post-sunset gatherings that would generate vehicle movements and pedestrian activity at the most
sensitive hours of the night and early morning.
In addition, weddings, funerals, festivals, and special community events may take place at
weekends and on public holidays, when residents have a heightened expectation of quiet
enjoyment of their homes and gardens. The former ambulance station, despite being a 24-hour
facility, generated no comparable external footfall, congregational noise, or visitor traffic during
these sensitive periods.
Hours of operation are a specifically recognised material planning consideration. If the council is
minded to approve this application - which for the reasons set out in this letter I submit it should
not - then strictly enforceable conditions on hours of operation would be essential. However, it is
submitted that the nature of the proposed use is such that the hours of activity cannot realistically
be conditioned in a way that would adequately protect residential amenity, given the frequency
and breadth of the activities proposed.
5. Scale, Massing, and Impact on Local Character
The proposal involves the erection of a part one, part two storey front and side extension and an
increase in the height of part of the building. These physical alterations would transform the form,
scale, and visual appearance of the building from a low-key operational garage and yard into an
institutional-scale structure. The resulting building would be out of keeping with the prevailing
character of Dorset Road, which is defined by semi-detached and detached family homes of a
consistent domestic scale.
Belmont is a quiet, suburban, overwhelmingly residential village. Census data confirms that over
60 per cent of the land area in the immediate locality is domestic gardens, which is more than
double the London average. The residential character is one of spaciousness, greenery, and low-
density family housing. An institutional building with increased height and massing, together with
associated signage, lighting, boundary treatment, cycle stores, and refuse enclosures, would
represent an incongruous intrusion into this established residential streetscape.
NPPF Paragraph 135(c) requires development to be sympathetic to local character and history,
including the surrounding built environment. Paragraph 139 states that development which is not
well designed should be refused, especially where it fails to reflect local design policies and
government guidance on design. Sutton Local Plan Policy 28 (Character and Design) requires
development to respect and enhance the surrounding character. The council's Design of
Residential Extensions Supplementary Planning Document (2006) provides further guidance on
extensions in residential areas. It is submitted that the proposed extensions conflict with these
policies.
6. Loss of Blue Light Emergency Service Infrastructure
The former Sutton Ambulance Station is classified as Sui Generis - a specialist use that served the
community as blue light emergency service infrastructure. Its loss raises specific policy concerns
at all three tiers of the development plan.
NPPF Paragraph 101 gives significant weight to the need to create, expand, or alter public service
infrastructure, including the provision of facilities for blue light services. This policy direction clearly
favours the retention and re-use of blue light sites for public service purposes, rather than their
conversion to unrelated uses. NPPF Paragraph 98(c) directs planning authorities to guard against
the unnecessary loss of valued facilities and services, particularly where this would reduce the
community's ability to meet its day-to-day needs.
London Plan Policy S1 (Developing London's Social Infrastructure) states that development
proposals that result in a loss of social infrastructure in an area of defined need should be refused,
unless realistic re-provision plans exist or the loss is part of a wider public service transformation
plan demonstrating that the facility is surplus to requirements. The applicant should be required to
provide robust evidence that the London Ambulance Service has permanently confirmed the site is
surplus, that no alternative emergency or social infrastructure use of the building is viable, and that
no equivalent re-provision is needed for the local area.
Sutton Local Plan Policies 20-23 (Social Infrastructure) similarly protect existing social
infrastructure from loss. The Sui Generis ambulance station use classification could accommodate
a range of alternative social infrastructure uses - for example, community healthcare, emergency
response training, or other public services. The automatic loss of this potential to accommodate
future social infrastructure needs should not be accepted without compelling justification.
7. Pedestrian Safety and Conflict
Large congregations arriving and departing simultaneously generate significant pedestrian footfall
on a street that was not designed for institutional-scale visitor numbers. Dorset Road serves as a
route to a primary school and is within the catchment area of Overton Grange secondary school.
Families with children walk along this road daily to access these schools and Overton Park. The
bowling club also attracts elderly pedestrians.
The concentration of all vehicle movements at the single Dorset Road/A217 junction, combined
with large numbers of pedestrians walking to and from the proposed facility along the same narrow
section of road, creates unacceptable pedestrian-vehicle conflict. This risk is heightened during
periods of peak congregation attendance (Fridays, weekends, evenings, and festivals), which
coincide with times when children and families are most likely to be using the park and walking to
school or community activities.
NPPF Paragraph 117 requires development to minimise the scope for conflicts between
pedestrians, cyclists, and vehicles, and to avoid unnecessary street clutter. NPPF Paragraph 96(b)
requires places to be safe and accessible. NPPF Paragraph 102 and Footnote 44 explicitly list
places of worship among locations where large numbers of people congregate, requiring
heightened public safety consideration. London Plan Policy T4 requires that development
proposals should not increase road danger. It is submitted that the proposed development would
create unacceptable pedestrian safety risks contrary to these policies.
8. Refuse, Waste Management, and Servicing
A community hub and place of worship with regular large gatherings will generate substantially
different and larger waste streams than a former ambulance station. Community meals,
celebrations, weddings, and events produce significant volumes of food waste, packaging, and
general refuse. The storage, containment, and collection of this waste on a constrained residential
street raises material amenity concerns, including odour, vermin attraction, and visual impact for
neighbouring residential properties.
The application proposes the provision of refuse stores, but no detail has been provided as to the
capacity, frequency of collection, or access arrangements for collection vehicles. Given that Dorset
Road is traffic-calmed and functions as a cul-de-sac, the manoeuvring of large refuse collection
vehicles at the site and at the road's turning area raises practical concerns. Inadequate refuse
provision would cause nuisance to neighbouring properties and is contrary to Sutton Local Plan
Policy 29 (Protecting Amenity).
Conclusion and Request
For all of the reasons set out above, I submit that the proposed development is contrary to the
adopted development plan at all three tiers. It conflicts with NPPF Paragraphs 96, 98, 101, 102,
115, 116, 117, 135, and 139; London Plan Policies S1, D13, D14, T4, and T6; and Sutton Local
Plan Policies 20-23, 24, 28, and 29.
The combination of a traffic-calmed cul-de-sac, an existing Controlled Parking Zone confirming
pre-existing parking stress, a PTAL rating of 1b-2 ensuring high car dependency, and a dense
cluster of sensitive receptors - including a primary school, a secondary school, a nursing home, a
GP surgery, and a public park - renders this specific location fundamentally unsuitable for a use of
this nature and intensity.
I would note that planning appeal decisions support this assessment. In the Windsor Muslim
Association appeal (2021), Inspector Pipkin dismissed a proposal for a prayer hall accommodating
just 30 families, finding that cumulative planning harms including highway safety and residential
amenity impacts outweighed even the substantial weight given to equality considerations. In the
Bearwood Road, Smethwick appeal (2020), an inspector dismissed a proposal for a mosque and
community centre for approximately 250 people, citing inadequate on-site parking, unacceptable
highway safety risks, parking overspill, and noise nuisance. In Ikram v Secretary of State
(Wembley, 2019), the High Court quashed an inspector's decision allowing a mosque and
community centre, finding fundamental defects in the assessment of residential amenity, parking,
noise, food waste, and light pollution impacts.
I request that the council refuse this application. In the alternative, if the council is minded to
approve, I request that the application be referred to the council's Planning Committee for
determination rather than being decided under delegated powers, so that the significant local
concerns can be heard and considered in a public forum.

Name: Mr Mirza Jibraan Taimuri
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to express my strong support for the proposal to convert the dormant
ambulance station into a mosque and community facility. This project represents a thoughtful
approach to urban renewal, transforming an underutilized, deteriorating asset into a vibrant center
for social and spiritual cohesion.
1. Social Impact & Community Inclusivity
The primary driver for this support is the urgent need for adequate space. Currently, local demand
for prayer and community services far outstrips the capacity of existing infrastructure.
Relieving Infrastructure Stress: Existing facilities are frequently over-capacity, leading to cramped
conditions that hinder the quality of worship and community engagement.
Support for Vulnerable Groups: Crucially, this additional square footage is not just about numbers;
it is about dignity and safety. For children with sensory vulnerabilities, neurodivergent conditions,
or physical disabilities, "cramped" environments can be overwhelming or inaccessible.
A Safe Haven: The proposed layout allows for dedicated, quieter spaces. This ensures that
vulnerable community members-and those with underlying health conditions-can participate in
communal life without the physical stress and health risks associated with overcrowded
environments.
2. Domestic & Local Amenity Benefits
A common concern with any conversion is the impact on the immediate neighbourhood. This
proposal proactively addresses these issues:
Dedicated Off-Street Parking: By incorporating on-site parking, the plan mitigates the risk of street
congestion. This is a significant improvement over the current "dormant" state, which often attracts
unauthorized parking or anti-social behaviour.
Managed Use: Unlike the unpredictable sirens and high-intensity activity of an active emergency
station, a community center operates on a predictable schedule, offering a more harmonious
"neighbour" to local residents.
3. Economic Vitality & Urban Regeneration
Leaving a public building dormant is an economic drain on the area. Bringing it back into use
provides several financial upsides:
Asset Preservation: Regular maintenance and private investment will prevent the building from
falling into further disrepair, which protects the property values of the surrounding area.
Local Spending: The presence of a community hub brings footfall to local businesses and
services, providing a subtle but consistent "micro-economic" boost to nearby shops.
Job Creation: The construction phase and the subsequent facility management offer opportunities
for local employment and volunteerism.
4 Enhanced Traffic & Local Synergy Strategy
Strategic Alignment with 'The Avenue' School A key strength of this proposal is its proactive
approach to local traffic management and its relationship with nearby educational institutions.
Mitigating Peak-Hour Congestion: The management of the proposed mosque has committed to a
flexible scheduling model. Where religiously permissible, prayer times and community events have
been strategically adjusted to avoid overlap with the drop-off and pick-up windows of The Avenue
school. This "staggered" approach ensures that the local road network is not overwhelmed,
maintaining a smooth flow of traffic for parents, residents, and commuters alike.
Cultural and Practical Synergy: It is worth noting that 'The Avenue' already demonstrates an
inclusive ethos by catering to Halal dietary requirements. This indicates a significant overlap
between the school's demographic and the mosque's congregation. By providing a place of
worship within walking distance of an institution that already serves these families, we reduce the
need for multi-trip car journeys, encouraging a "park once" or "walk-to-both" culture.
Improving Safety for Vulnerable Students: As 'The Avenue' serves a diverse student body, the
reduction in street-side congestion directly contributes to a safer environment for children.
Combined with the mosque's transition to off-street parking, this proposal represents a net
improvement in pedestrian safety compared to the current unmanaged state of the dormant
station.
Conclusion
The conversion of this site is a "common-sense" solution to a pressing social problem. It takes a
site that is currently serving no purpose and repurposes it to serve the most vulnerable among us-
providing a spacious, safe, and modern environment for children and adults alike.
I urge the planning committee to recognize the multifaceted benefits of this application and grant
approval for this much-needed community resource.

Name: Mr Robert Kearey
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to object to the proposed Mosque scheme under the headings described below:
Increased Traffic & Road Safety Risks
The proposed site is located in an area already experiencing substantial traffic congestion and
parking issues, particularly during the Avenue Primary School drop-off and pick-up periods when
vehicle queues, limited manoeuvring space and restricted visibility are common. I have observed
these conditions when collecting my grandchildren from the School. Access for emergency
vehicles to Dorset Road, York Road, Holland Avenue and Avenue Road is already difficult.
Additional vehicle movements to and from the proposed Mosque scheme will only exacerbate
existing congestion and increase road safety and community risks.
School Safety
Avenue Primary School is in close proximity to the proposed development and has a pupil
capacity of up to 1,050 students. At morning drop-off and afternoon collection times, there are
exceptionally high numbers of pedestrian and vehicle movements. Large numbers of children,
parents, pushchairs, and cyclists use the surrounding pavements and crossings, creating a very
crowded environment. Any further vehicle and pedestrian movements would materially increase
risks to child safety.
Impact on Local Recreational Facilities and Facilities for the Elderly
In addition to the school, the area is served by recreational facilities including tennis courts and an
elderly residents' facility. These facilities generate steady vehicle circulation and parking demand
throughout the day and into the evening. The presence of elderly persons, some with reduced
mobility, adds to safety concerns and must be considered when assessing the impact of the
proposed Mosque scheme.
Noise and Night-Time Disturbance
The proposed introduction of a community hall, temple, mosque or similar facility would result in
concentrated user arrival and departure patterns throughout the day, particularly during evenings,
weekends, and special events. Such additional congestion would inevitably create noise nuisance
and disturbance to local residents and detract from what is presently a quiet residential
neighbourhood.
Exterior Design Proposal
The proposed Mosque scheme elevations, with increased building height compared with the
existing building would create a structure that is visually dominant and in sharp contrast to the
domestic scale of neighbouring properties. There do not appear to be any details of the first floor
of the proposed scheme to enable public comment at this planning application stage.
Summary
In summary, the Mosque scheme proposal would, if adopted:
- Materially increase highway safety risk to pedestrians and other road users
- Diminish existing residential amenity
- Detract visually from the character of the local neighbourhood

contribute to poor mental and physical health, and local, accessible community facilities are one of

It makes no sense for a brownfield site in the heart of a residential neighbourhood to remain
vacant when there is a clear demand for community facilities. This proposal makes efficient use of
the land without encroaching on any greenfield or protected areas.

The facility will operate within defined hours, which is a significant improvement over the former
ambulance station that ran 24 hours a day, seven days a week. Residents will no longer have
emergency vehicles coming and going through the night.
I encourage the London Borough of Sutton to approve application DM2025/01591. It is properly
planned, policy-compliant, and will bring genuine benefit to the community.

This facility will generate far less car traffic than its attendance numbers might suggest. The cycle

I'd also add that the proposed works are modest: a part one, part two storey extension with a
limited height increase to the existing building. There is no new upper floor that would create

improved refuse arrangements, all of which improve the appearance of the site. In the same vein,
a 24-hour ambulance station with sirens and heavy vehicles was considerably noisier than a
community facility operating within defined hours. The noise report confirms what common sense

The Belmont area currently has very limited community space. This proposal will provide a facility
that all residents can use, with a programme of activities designed to be accessible and welcoming
to people of all backgrounds and ages.
I am fully behind this application. It is good planning, it meets a real need, and I urge the Council
to approve it.

Name: Mr Aguilane Periasoamy
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Planning Objection to Proposed Prayer and Community Hall Development
To: The Local Planning Authority
Re: Objection to Proposed Prayer and Community Hall Development in Residential Area
Application Reference: DM2025/01591
We, the undersigned residents, wish to formally object to the above planning application on the
basis of material planning considerations under UK planning law.
1. Impact on Residential Amenity
The proposed development is located within a predominantly residential area intended to provide
a quiet and low-traffic living environment. The scale and nature of a prayer and community hall,
including regular gatherings and events, are likely to result in increased noise, disturbance, and
general activity levels that would adversely affect neighbouring residential amenity.
This appears contrary to the principles set out in the National Planning Policy Framework (NPPF),
which seeks to ensure developments provide a high standard of amenity for existing and future
occupants.
2. Traffic Generation and Highway Safety
The proposed use is expected to generate significant additional vehicle movements, particularly
during peak prayer times and community events. This raises concerns regarding:
- Traffic congestion on residential streets
- Insufficient parking provision
- Obstruction to access routes
- Increased risk to pedestrians
These concerns are heightened due to the proximity of a nearby school and an elderly care home,
where vulnerable road users are present. The proposal may therefore conflict with local highway
safety policies and NPPF guidance promoting safe and suitable access for all users.
3. Impact on Local Infrastructure and Environment
The development may lead to environmental impacts including:
- Increased noise levels
- Deterioration in local air quality due to higher vehicle emissions
- Pressure on existing local infrastructure within a residential setting
Such impacts should be carefully assessed through appropriate transport, environmental, and
noise impact assessments in accordance with planning policy requirements.
4. Suitability of Location and Land Use Compatibility
Given the intensity of use associated with a prayer and community hall, the proposed location may
be incompatible with the established residential character of the area. Alternative sites within
mixed-use or town-centre locations may be more appropriate and consistent with sustainable
planning principles encouraged by the NPPF.
Conclusion
Our objection is based solely on material planning considerations, including residential amenity,
highway safety, environmental impact, and land-use compatibility. It is not related to the nature of
the religious or community activities themselves.
We respectfully request that the Local Planning Authority carefully consider these planning
concerns when determining the application.

Comment:
I support the planning application for prayer hall and community hub. This will be
beneficial for everyone in the local community.

Name: Mr Dave Slater
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object this proposal because the amount of extra traffic
during prayer times will be unacceptable and dangerous due to it being in close proximity to a very
busy junior school ( which has its own parking issues during school drop off and pick up times
already).

Name: Mrs Elizabeth Joyce
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The traffic in the area surrounding the proposed development is already currently a
difficult issue. Currently, at peak times there is a safety concern when crossing Avenue Road,
Holland Avenue, York Road, and Dorset Road. It is difficult currently for pedestrians who require
additional time when crossing the roads (the elderly, parents/guardians with young children and/or
pushchairs). With the proposed increase of 70+ cars with the new development and no
consideration to local road safety, the petition is not suitable to the local area. Especially
considering the safety of school children who attend the Avenue primary school (1034 children)
but also to the children commuting to the local secondary schools. In terms of safety, the petition is
not feasible.
Currently, parking in the area at peak times causes many problems from the residents of the
surrounding roads and bus routes of the proposed development. During peak hours, residents
face parked cars obstructing their driveways as well as obstruction when pulling out of driveways.
The hail and ride sections of the S4 and S2 bus routes often are restricted when pulling into to
stop as peak parking blocks dropped curbs. This is a particular issue for our vulnerable residents
of the surrounding roads around the proposed development. The roads close to the proposed
development have additional parking issues as there are already many social centres including the
church on north down road (excessive parking at peak times including the weekends for additional
services), the downs social club (which holds tennis, other exercises classes, and social events)
and the Sutton bowling club. These social centres often have cars parked inappropriately (on
corners, over dropped curbs, yellow lines etc). Therefore the addition of 70+ cars with only 12
parking spaces at the proposed development would be dangerous.
The current condition of the road surfaces is appalling and is regularly patched. Currently there are
approximately 20 pot holes in Holland avenue with the current throughput of traffic. Therefore the
increase of cars would only add to the current problem. The surrounding roads of the proposed
development are already used a cut through from the a217 to the Brighton road and without better
road infrastructure, the condition of the road surfaces will only escalate.
Previously there was very little noise pollution when the site was an ambulance station and as a
resident of over 50 years, it rarely bothered myself. However, in recent years the noise pollution
comes from the local social centres. As with the plans of potential dispersal of up to 250 people up
to 11pm at night, the noise pollution for the residents of the surrounding roads would increase
dramatically which affects the quality of life to the residents. Especially the vulnerable residents
that live locally (many nursing homes and elderly residents).

Name: Dr Fui Mee Quek
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly object to this development as the area is already really busy and this will
cause further traffic congestion. With the local school there may be risks related to the extra
parking and a reduced visibility for pedestrians and drivers alike. The proposed extension and
building height would create a building out of keeping with the residential neighbourhood. There
might also be more disturbance to the elderly at a care home near by. The proposal is likely to
make a negative impact on vulnerable residents.

Name: Mrs Julie Watt
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
As someone who lives within 350 yards of the proposed building extension I am
unhappy for this to go ahead on the grounds of increased traffic parking and noise from 7am until
11pm. This is a residential street so it would not be appropriate for more cars trying to park at all
hours . It's bad enough with the local school but add this into the mix would be disastrous for local
residences.

Name: Ms J Morgan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Another mosque is not needed in this area, there are already 4 mosques in Sutton,
Greater London and 10 mosques with a 4-5 mile radius of Sutton, Greater London. The road is
residential and unsuitable for this kind of building which will bring much traffic and disruption to the
residents.

Name: Mr Tariq Abbas
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I support application DM2025/01591 as I would like to eventually use the facility as a
place of worship and a place to develop community cohesion, tolerance and understanding.
Having looked at the development proposals, I am of the view that the proposal is scale
appropriate, in keeping with the visual appearance of the area and an improvement compared to
the existing classification of use.
The increased softscaping will improve the local streetscape and be welcome addition in terms of
urban greening factor and biodiversity. As a Muslim myself, who is committed to strengthening
community ties and having due high regard for neighbourly assistance and care in the community,
I believe that our Muslim community can and will be a benefit to the area.
Our Muslim community is diverse and in need of a dedicated facility to serve local residents of the
Muslim faith.
The Transport Statement highlights 269 street parking spaces in close proximity to the proposed
site which has capacity at peak times for approximately 134 parking spaces, more than sufficient
to meet the needs of the proposed development without being a burden on local parking needs.
In terms of noise, the proposed development as a place of worship and community hub, operating
within defined hours to be agreed with the council, is confirmed by the noise report as an
improvement compared to the current use as a 24-hour ambulance station.
The proposed use classification is for daytime and early evening use, rather than a 24-hour noise
generating facility as is the current permitted type of use classification. Enabling local community
educational classes, wellbeing classes, interfaith sessions so we can learn from each other, drop-
ins and community events can only be a positive for Belmont.
In the comments, there is mention of a potential for conflict with the school run, however our
prayer times do not clash with the morning / afternoon school drop off and pick up times and
facility operators are aware to not conflict with school timings.
The environmental assessment documents have fully addressed Drainage and flood risk, energy
conservation, biodiversity improvements and tree preservation.
As a son, and carer for my elderly mother, having a place of worship and community hub is
important for both our health and wellbeing, sense of belonging in a tolerant and respectful
society. The ability for both of us to enjoy community events within the facility would greatly
improve are wellbeing.
Looking at comments associated with this application, and knowing existing worshippers of the
God of Abraham, the God of Moses, the God of Noah, within the local area, there is a legitimate
need for such a facility to address demand, increase community wellbeing and tolerance and
alleviate overspill elsewhere. It is significant in this respect that the provision of additional
community space in the Belmont would bring an increase is Social Value which must be taken into
consideration, now more than ever given the rise in geopolitical tension and the fracturing of
religious and racial harmony by some in our society.
I am of the view that this proposal is in keeping with planning policy, indeed it is a positive addition,
benefiting the whole local area, not just Muslims.
I support this application as it meets all the relevant grounds for planning consent to be provided
and therefore request the Council to grant planning permission.

Name: Miss Holly Sweetman
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
We have a quiet road full of elderly people who are easily disturbed, we have a puppy at
home who uses the front garden to go do her business and we are not happy for this to go through
as it poses a risk. It also can be an issue during school runs, getting to work and coming home as I
work late shifts. My little brothers play football outside on OUR driveway and garden so I do not
support for this to go through.

Name: Mr G Morgan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Sutton has four mosques at the moment. Therefore I don't see the need for another
religous building.

Name: Ms Taksh Arora
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment: There is a mosque in proximity

Name: Mr Joseph Mcmanus
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This proposal to substantially intensify the use of this site would be out of keeping with
the area and would materially increase traffic in what is a busy area already.
There are schools adjacent to the proposal and the traffic would unquestionably pose a greater
safety .
Undoubtedly traffic and parking would spill over to adjacent areas including Belmont ,Sutton and
south Cheam which would be an unwelcome development.

Name: Mr Chandra Mannur
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Concerns below.
- Not in keeping with the character of a residential zone
- Sets a precedent for further commercial or non-residential development
- Increased vehicle movements, especially at peak worship times
- Insufficient on-site parking leading to overflow onto residential streets
- There's a school nearby and that would cause more chaos.

Name: Mr Sanjeev Gulati
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Sutton has four mosques at the moment. Therefore I don't see the need for another
religious building. Considering the percentage of population vs. the overall population of the area
the number of existing worship areas for this community are already in abundance. The number of
places of worship areas for any religion should be proportionate to the population following that
religion.

Name: Mr Sangan Patil
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
We don't need any more new religious monuments in our area.
Ours is a peaceful community.
We want our kids to grow with a free and open mind than with no religious tag.
There is one such structure under construction near high street already, which is barely 2 miles
from the proposed building.
There is a primary school very close to proposed religious structure, which is not a good influence
on young minds.
Traffic, cleanliness, crime and safety of young, elderly, women and properties is already a huge
issue.
Peace loving citizens of this society should not be taken for granted by allowing this religious
structure to come up.

Name: Mrs ELIZABETH MCMANUS
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This proposed building is far too big in a very small and narrow road. During school
times with the nursery, it is already incredibly busy. With this building, which is significant in size
and with people from outside the area being bussed in, it will grind to a halt and the impact on the
road and surrounding areas will be intolerable.

Name: Mr Nishant Ingale
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Tx for this opportunity. I strongly object with following reasons.
1. Current heavy traffic - it is heavy traffic and rush situation each school day around school start
and finish time. Otherwise also it's a busy road.
2. Pedestrian safety- with traffic jams and honking Pedestrian safety is at stake.
3. Blocked driveway -Many times my driveway is blocked by either parents or guardians if I don't
park my car on my driveway white lines. This is reported to school many times and still no
viewabke actions from school other than sending emails out.
4. Noise- heavy noise with all listed above
5. Friends and relatives- they need to park cars far away when they visit us.
Considering above we don't have any scope to accommodate massive/demanding place of
worship/community hub. There must be reason why it was not F1 F2 type place before and hence
that needs to be respected and adhered to. Tx again

Name: Ms Shikha Wadhwa
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
We have far more mosques than Londoners need. What a country needs is something
that serves wider good for the overall neighborhood. On the top of it- it is not going to help with the
traffic situation in the area. There are already many mosques around - one who needs a place to
worship can easily go there.

Name: Mrs Leilani Tizard-Varcoe
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Logistically it is not suitable as it's a close residential area. It's already busy with the
Avenue school and the dispersal of up to 250 people at 11pm can mean voices, engines, door
slamming and cars circling the residential area. The noise will affect the vulnerable in the nursing
home nearby and us residents who live just a few metres away. Avenue Primary has over a 1000
pupils and extra traffic increases risk at drop/pick up and reduces visibility. I already have near
misses of accidents going in and out of our road which is opposite the proposed site/extensions.
The proposed extensions and increased building height would create a structure that is visually
dominant and entirely out of keeping with the domestic scale of the neighbourhood. Please object
the build as it really is not suitable for the neighbourhood. People who live outside the area
shouldn't have a say as they're not the ones impacted. The residents in Belmont who is going to
be affected should be heard first.

Name: Bruce Tizard-Varcoe
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
That area is already too congested with traffic due to dense housing, a cross road, the
primary school, tennis club, bowling club, nursing home, the bus service and situated in a cul de
sac. To have such a facility there would further increase the traffic problem.

Name: Mr Geof Haseler
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
From comments made, people coming to the mosque will not only be local but also from
a wider area than indicated. More people will travel by car and park in local roads as the number
of car park spaces provided at the site is very limited. This extra traffic and parking will acerbate
the already dreadful situation in that part of Belmont.

Name: Dr Swee Lip Quek
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I want to lodge my vehement objection to this development proposal for the following
reasons:
The area is already hugely congested and what is being applied for will without doubt significantly
exacerbate this situation.
The increase in traffic will put additional pressure on the already serious lack of parking spaces
and add to toxic air pollution.
I have serious concerns about the safety of school children attending the Avenue Primary
Academy due to the combination of increased traffic, blocked pavements and parked cars which
will hinder the visibility of children crossing roads to drivers.
Elderly and vulnerable residents of the nearby care home as well as others living nearby will find
the (particularly late-night & early morning) activities and noise of the proposed development
disturbing and stressful.
The extension and building height would create a building completely out of keeping with the
residential vicinity. This will also very unfairly & negatively impact the value of properties in the
neighbourhood.
I am certain that my objections will be shared by the majority of presence in this area

Name: Pooja Padmanabhan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This area has more inflow and outflow because of a primary school. It is not appropriate
to have any religious structure near a school which will increase coming in and out, of unknown
people. There is an elderly people's home as well. This whole area is residential and no outsiders
should be allowed considering safety of residents, especially children, women and elderly. Also,
there is already a religious praying space for the same community just 1 mile or so away, at high
street. There is no justification for having one more at such a short distance.

Name: Mr Paul Sarfaty
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The proposal will place significant unacceptable strains on a quiet residential location.
In acquiring the site, the applicant has failed to recognise the extremely small outside space for
visitor parking, and seriously limited street parking in the immediate vicinity.
Roads in the area are generally narrow (Avenue Road and Holland Avenue) and there is a traffic
barrier on Dorset Road. The community currently uses a nearby church hall for Friday prayers
which results in significant parking congestion in Belmont, and this proposal simply transfers the
problem to residential streets which are already congested on weekdays by parking related to
Avenue Primary Academy, which has over 1,000 students. The S4 bus route is already impacted
by this issue on weekdays.
It is not just a case of measuring the available kerb space in nearby roads: this is already
stretched to breaking point during school hours, with regular cases of anti-social parking
behaviour. Further capacity simply does not exist, and takes no account of the additional air
pollution it will produce as a result of poor transport links.
The proposal to increase the building height is questionable in relation to neighbouring structures,
as is the accuracy of the anticipated attendance figures at gatherings in the building. There are
other mosques within easy reach of this location, which is not suitable for such a development.

Name: Mr Subramaniam Kasi
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
1. Existing Traffic Congestion and Peak-Time Pressure
The proposed site is located in an area already experiencing substantial traffic congestion and
acute peak-time pressure. The surrounding road network frequently operates at or near capacity,
particularly during school drop-off and pick-up hours, when vehicle queues, limited manoeuvring
space, and restricted visibility are common.
2.Impact of Nearby Primary School
The immediate vicinity includes a primary school with a pupil capacity exceeding 1,000 students.
This generates exceptionally high volumes of pedestrian and vehicular activity during morning and
afternoon peak periods. Large numbers of children, parents, pushchairs, and cyclists use the
surrounding pavements and crossings, creating a highly sensitive highway environment.
Any further intensification of traffic would materially increase risks to child safety and exacerbate
existing congestion.
3.Cumulative Impact from Recreational and Elderly Facilities
In addition to the school, the area is served by recreational facilities including tennis courts and an
elderly residents' golf facility. These uses generate steady vehicle circulation and parking demand
throughout the day and into the evening.
Importantly, the presence of elderly users increases the vulnerability profile of the area, as many
may have reduced mobility or slower crossing speeds, requiring heightened highway safety
considerations.
4.Traffic Generation from Assembly-Based Use
The proposed introduction of a community hall, temple, mosque, church, or similar assembly use
would result in concentrated arrival and departure patterns, particularly during evenings,
weekends, and special events.
Unlike residential development, assembly uses typically create simultaneous peak traffic
movements, leading to:
Intensified congestion
Increased on-street parking stress
Obstructive and unsafe parking practices
Reduced visibility at junctions
Impeded access for emergency vehicles
This pattern of traffic generation would compound existing pressures rather than operate
independently of them.
5.Highway Safety and Residential Amenity
When considered cumulatively, the existing high pedestrian activity, vulnerable users (children and
elderly residents), and established congestion levels demonstrate that the site is unsuitable for
further high-intensity assembly use.
The proposal would:
Materially worsen highway safety conditions
Increase risks to pedestrians
Diminish residential amenity
Undermine the safe operation of the surrounding road network'

Name: Mrs Kanthimathi Sivan Pillai
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The proposed site is situated within an area already subject to substantial traffic
congestion and acute peak-time pressure.
The immediate vicinity includes a primary school with a pupil capacity in excess of 1,000,
generating exceptionally high volumes of pedestrian and vehicular movement during morning
drop-off and afternoon pick-up periods. These times are characterised by congestion, limited
parking availability, reduced visibility at junctions, and increased vulnerability for children and
accompanying parents.
In addition, nearby recreational facilities, including tennis courts and an elderly residents' golf
facility, contribute to sustained vehicle circulation throughout the day and evening. These uses
attract both regular visitors and older residents, many of whom may have reduced mobility,
thereby heightening the sensitivity of the area in terms of highway safety and pedestrian
protection.
The introduction of a community hall, temple, mosque, church, or any comparable assembly use
would inevitably result in concentrated arrival and departure patterns, particularly during evenings,
weekends, and religious or community events. Such uses typically generate peak, overlapping
traffic flows rather than dispersed movements. This would significantly intensify existing
congestion, exacerbate on-street parking stress, increase instances of obstructive or unsafe
parking, compromise emergency vehicle access, and materially reduce road safety for
schoolchildren, elderly residents, and other pedestrians.
When assessed cumulatively, the existing pressures and the sensitive character of surrounding
uses clearly demonstrate that the site is unsuitable for further high-intensity, assembly-based
development. The proposal would materially worsen highway safety conditions and adversely
affect residential amenity.

Address: 21 Glyndale Grange Stanley Road Sutton SM2 6NA
Name: Ms Deepti Maheshwari
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to the proposed religious centre at due to its immediate
proximity to a busy primary school. The area already experiences significant congestion during
drop off and pick up times, and the additional traffic, parking demand, and footfall are likely to
create safety risks for children and restrict emergency access. My objection is based solely on
planning and public safety grounds, particularly the suitability of this location next to a busy
primary school.

Name: Mr Rohit Arora
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment: Trying if one user can submit multiple

Name: Mr Ramanan Shanmuganathan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to this development as it would increase traffic and contribute to additional air
and noise pollution. School-time parking pressures must also be carefully considered. The road is
already served by the S4 bus route and the nearby S2 route, which contribute to existing
congestion.
In addition, there is a care home around the corner, and the potential impact on its residents
should be taken into account. The rear gate of Avenue School is located nearby, and the pick-up
and drop-off of younger children already create challenges. Additional vehicles parking on
surrounding roads would further complicate access and safety in the area.

Name: Mr Hari babu Kunapaneni
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
While I recognise that a community centre may provide benefits to local residents, this
area already experiences significant traffic congestion. The surrounding roads are consistently
overcrowded, particularly during peak hours, causing delays, safety concerns, and disruption to
residents. The proposed development is likely to substantially increase traffic volume and further
exacerbate these existing problems. For these reasons, I strongly object to this application.

Name: Mr Zahid Ataullah
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I will be most grateful for this facility in this area.
I will primarily attend as a place of worship but the association have been thoughtful about the use
of the building and it has the potential to serve the wider community well.
For example a multi use hall that can be used by people of any or no faith can become a useful
community hub in the area.
I will be moving closer to the ambulance station in due course, withing walking distance, so I will
be able to benefit from the facility without inconveniencing the neighbours in terms of parking for
example.

Name: Miss Huma Malik
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application

Name: Mrs Samantha Labbadia
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
It will cause extra unnecessary congestion. We already struggle with the Avenue school
congestion, we do not need more.

Name: Mr S Labbadia
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Congestion in this area is already bad due to the Avenue school being literally across
the road from the proposed mosque so this would cause severe congestion, stress and major
inconvenience to local residents and disruption. From a road safety aspect, the increased traffic
would cause a danger to the pedestrians and school children going to and from the school.

Address: 42 Kew Crescent North Cheam Sutton SM3 9RT
Name: Mrs Anjum Bukhari
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I fully support the Ambulance Station Conversion to a Mosque . I am a regular daily
visitor during the Holy month of Ramadan. I go weekly for Friday prayers to Saint John's Church ,
which is my nearest venue for Worship . The venue is an ideal location for other Non Muslim
people to use also after the full conversion to a Local Community Centre and Mosque. There will
be plenty of parking available on the premises for the visitors who are coming by car or bikes. It
will be an ideal Hub for the local residents for Excercise and other activities. It will complement the
Saint John's Church that we use at present for the local Non Muslim Residents in the area.

Name: Mr James Wilson
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I was the first home owner in Balmoral Way more than 40 years ago. I was a police
officer for 30 years retiring as a senior officer. I am therefore very familiar with the area and during
my service worked closely with local authorities dealing with various issues involving
neighbourhood policing. When Balmoral Way was constructed road safety was a major issue, the
homes had double garages and decent sized drives. Dorset Road was subject of serious
discussion and it was decided to prohibit vehicle access to Brighton Road to minimise traffic flow
as it could prove a dangerous cut through'. The local school, The Avenue, was a small infant
school and had parking on site for staff. Since then however the school has increased 4 times and
has now limited parking on its premises because of extra buildings on the grounds. This has
increased parking congestion and is now become a serious road safety issue. I would be surprised
if London Transport had not objected to the likelihood of disruption to the bus route via The
Avenue. If I can give an example of a recent incident when I was asked by a delivery driver if it
was my vehicle parked across my driveway and he couldn't pass causing congestion behind him, I
explained the car belonged to a parent collecting her child from school. The driver and two of his
colleagues physically bumped the car onto the pavement to allow his and the other vehicles to
pass. I spoke to the car driver on her return and explained what her inconsiderate parking had
caused but met with the usual excuse, "I was only 20 minutes". Whilst I'm not adverse to dealing
with confrontation my in my 80s I've become more wary of doing so. I hope you will take my
concerns into consideration when deciding this application. I would be willing to be contacted if I
can be of further assistance.

Name: Ms Gail Hewitt
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I am objecting to the change of use from ambulance station to the building of a mosque.
This would greatly increase the traffic and parking on Holland Avenue. It is already congested due
to the Avenue School's need for parking and some of the Friday Mosque services would be at the
same time as the school parking making the situation impossible . I anticipate that this congestion
will not be only be restricted to Fridays as there will be other activities at the mosque. The mosque
would also be out of character with the domestic scale of neighbouring properties and could
devalue our houses . It would also be a serious noise disturbance in the area . In addition people
visiting the mosques without cars would have to use the S4/S2 buses - this is not viable for the
volume of anticipate increase of people and would cause problems for the local people using the
bus now.

Name: Mrs Lynn hilder
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Build up of congestion resulting in parking over drives and heavy flow of traffic in a
residential area

Name: Mrs Susanne HAYLOCK
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
My objections are as follows
1. Traffic Disruption caused by increased traffic for both the school traffic and bus traffic .
2. Increased noise disruption for local residents and especially the local care homes caused by the
anticipated increase of visitors to the area. This will be especially disruptive in the evenings as we
currently live in a quiet residential area.
3. School safety - there are many children and parents walking to the Avenue school and an
increase in traffic would severely increase risk of accidents and their safety.
4. Parking - as a local resident I am very concerned about the potential increase in parked cars on
my road by the visitors to the mosque . We already have issues during school hours and this
already causes us a problem and this would increased by more visitors. Also this is a bus route so
the parking already causes a problem as all the roads here are narrow residential roads.
5. I am concerned that the proposed extension and increased height would create a structure that
is out of character with the local residential surroundings and in particular would become visually
dominant .
Overall I am very concerned that these disruptions would not be restricted to just Fridays but
would become the everyday norm as the proposal is for a mosque and community centre that
would be open every day .

Name: Miss Sabina Hassan
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Sutton has a significant and growing Muslim population who currently have limited
dedicated space for worship. Like churches, a mosque provides a recognised place for religious
practice, community support, and education. Equal access to places of worship is part of a fair and
inclusive society.
A mosque is not only a place of prayer. It can serve as a hub for charitable work, youth
engagement, educational programmes, and interfaith dialogue - all of which strengthen community
cohesion and benefit the wider Sutton community.
Members of the Muslim community have been attending St John's Church for religious activities
for several years, which is located very close to the proposed site. This indicates that worship-
related footfall already exists in the area. A purpose-built facility would not introduce a new
population to the area, but rather provide appropriate accommodation for an existing one.
It is important that they are provided with a suitable and permanent place of worship.

Name: Mrs Shobadevi Marudamuthu
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Traffic in this area is already heavy because of the school and busy bus routes, and
adding further congestion raises safety concerns for both children and parents. This is a
residential neighbourhood chosen by people who prefer to live away from busy, hightraffic zones
and are willing to pay a premium for that environment. Weekend activities will generate
considerable noise, which will disturb families and prevent working people from getting adequate
rest. To minimise disruption to this residential community, the proposed facility could be placed in
a more suitable location-such as a commercial zone, an existing community hub, or areas where
similar facilities already operate and infrastructure is better equipped to handle increased traffic
and activities.

Name: Mr Joseph Hebrard
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly object to this proposal on the basis that it will inevitably exacerbate the
present parking and access situation in and around Balmoral Way. The poor visibility between
Dorset Road and Holland Avenue /Avenue Road at peak times also creates dangerous conditions
for the children attending the Avenue school, motorists and pedestrians alike.

Name: Mr Soobramanien Marudamuthu
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I have been in this area for a while now, and I can see how the local residents bear with
the heavily congested period considering we have The Avenue school in the close proximity, this
proposed development, from a road safety aspect, the increased traffic would cause a danger to
the pedestrians and school children going to and from the school.
This proposal would cause severe congestion, stress and major inconvenience to local residents
and disruption. Roadside parking will overflow to the roads in the vicinity, with all the
inconveniences this will have a negative impact on the local community and potentially on the
price of local properties.
I strongly recommend that the committee do not approve this proposed development.

Name: Mrs Pooja Gupta
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to the proposed change of use from Sui Generis (Ambulance
Station) to mixed use Classes F1 (Place of Worship) and F2 (Community Hub), including the
proposed extensions and increase in height.
While I appreciate the value of community facilities, this proposal raises several significant
concerns.
1. School Safety: The proposed use would invite many people and strangers to visit nearby areas
of the Avenue Primary school and there will be risk of school children getting exposed to strangers
and unknown people. Recently there are communications from School reporting the incidents in
Sutton, pupils are being followed and stalked by random people, thereby increasing the risks of
incidents against school pupil. Children safety should be of priority.
2. Increased traffic, Parking and Road Safety: The proposed use is likely to generate substantially
higher visitor numbers, particularly during evenings and weekends. The area already suffers from
limited on-street parking, and there is insufficient evidence that the site can accommodate peak
demand. Overspill parking would likely cause congestion, obstruct driveways and junctions, and
compromise access for emergency vehicles.
3. We truly acknowledge the feelings of the muslim community to have their regular prayers. It is
to be noteworthy that this area is already have multiple mosques (in Sutton) and big mosque in
Morden.
Following are couple of mosques and Islamic Centres within 5 miles of the proposed property-
1) Sutton Central Masjid, 25 Carshalton Rd, Sutton SM1 4LF. 1.8 miles
2) Sutton Islamic Centre, 62 Oakhill Rd, Sutton SM1 3AG. 2.6 miles
3) Muslim Cultural & Welfare Association of Sutton (MCWAS), Wentworth Hall, 80 Ruskin Rd,
Carshalton SM5 3DH. 3.2 miles
4) The Baitul Futuh Mosque, 181 London Rd, Morden SM4 5PT. (Biggest mosque in London). 4.4
miles
5) Morden Islamic Community Centre, 116 London Rd, Morden SM4 5AX. 4.6 miles
These mosque and centre should serve the community and the followers to conduct prayers and
other activities.
4. Noise and Residential Amenity: Religious services, meetings, events, and gatherings are likely
to generate ongoing noise, particularly in the evenings and at weekends. The application lacks
sufficient detail regarding operating hours, capacity limits, and noise mitigation measures to
safeguard neighbouring amenity.
In summary, the proposal represents a material intensification of use that would adversely affect
parking, highway and pedestrian safety, residential amenity, and the character of this
predominantly residential area. I therefore respectfully request that the application be refused.

Name: Mr Khaled Fahad
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
Representation in Support of Application DM2025/01591
I am writing to strongly support application DM2025/01591. As a local resident and a Muslim, I
believe this center is essential for fostering community cohesion, tolerance, and mutual
understanding in our neighborhood.
1. Design and Visual Impact
The proposed development is scaled appropriately for the site. It fits the local character and offers
a clear visual improvement over the current site classification.
2. Environmental Benefits & Softscaping
The inclusion of softscaping will greatly improve the streetscape. The focus on biodiversity and
urban greening is a welcome change that benefits everyone in the area.
3. Community Value
As a Muslim committed to neighborly care, I know our community will be a dedicated asset to
Belmont. We are a diverse group in urgent need of a local space to serve our religious and social
needs.
4. Transport and Parking
The Transport Statement confirms 269 street parking spaces nearby. With a peak capacity of 134
spaces, the site can easily accommodate visitors without placing a burden on local residents.
5. Noise and Operating Hours
Unlike the current 24-hour ambulance station, this center will operate within defined daytime and
early evening hours. This transition represents a significant reduction in noise pollution for
neighbors.
6. Local Services
The center will host educational classes, wellbeing sessions, and interfaith events. These services
provide a positive "social anchor" for Belmont, moving the site from an industrial feel to a
community-focused one.
7. School Traffic Coordination
Prayer times naturally fall outside of the morning and afternoon school run windows. The facility
operators are committed to scheduling events to ensure there is no conflict with local school traffic.
8. Environmental Standards
The application fully addresses all technical requirements, including drainage, flood risk, energy
conservation, and the preservation of local trees.
9. Health and Wellbeing
Having a local place of worship is vital for our mental health and sense of belonging. It provides a
safe, respectful space for the elderly to socialize and stay active.
10. Social Value and Global Context
There is a legitimate, unmet demand for a facility dedicated to the followers of the Abrahamic
faiths in this area. In a time of rising geopolitical tension, providing a space for harmony and social
value is more important than ever.
Why this Mosque is Necessary for the Area
Beyond the technical merits, this facility is a necessity for the following reasons:
Local Accessibility: Currently, local Muslims especially the elderly and those without cars must
travel long distances to worship. A local facility reduces travel time and carbon footprints.
Youth Engagement: The area lacks dedicated spaces for minority youth. This center will provide a
safe environment for mentorship and positive community participation.
Crisis of Loneliness: Many residents in Belmont face social isolation. A community hub provides a
"third space" outside of work and home to build friendships across different backgrounds.
Integration: A visible, open, and transparent community center is the best tool for dispelling myths
and building a truly integrated, peaceful neighborhood.
I believe this proposal aligns perfectly with planning policy and offers a net positive for all
residents. I urge the Council to grant planning permission.

Name: Mrs Claire Nicoll
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly object to this application on highway safety, parking, residential amenity and
design grounds.
The surrounding roads are already heavily congested, particularly during school drop-off and pick-
up times at Avenue Primary School. Parking regularly overspills into nearby roads including
Burdon Lane and Northey Avenue, and buses experience obstruction. The Dorset Road/Belmont
Rise junction has a history of accidents and cannot safely accommodate a significant increase in
vehicle movements.
The proposed building would attract a large number of attendees arriving and leaving within short
time periods. The lack of adequate on-site parking is likely to result in overspill parking on
residential streets, increasing congestion, reducing visibility at junctions, and creating additional
risks for pedestrians - particularly school children.
There is also concern about noise and disturbance from vehicle movements and dispersal,
especially in the evening, which would harm the amenity of neighbouring residents and nearby
care facilities.
In addition, the proposed extension and increased height appear out of keeping with the
residential character of the area and would create a visually dominant structure in a domestic
setting.
For these reasons, the proposal would have an unacceptable impact on highway safety,
residential amenity and local character, and planning permission should be refused.

Local resident

consideration.

Name: Mr Dominic Howard
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I wish to formally object to the above planning application on the following planning and
infrastructure grounds.
1. Insufficient Parking Provision
The proposed development is likely to generate a significant increase in vehicle movements,
particularly during peak worship periods and community events.
The surrounding residential streets already experience parking pressure, and the application does
not demonstrate adequate off-street parking provision to accommodate anticipated visitor
numbers. This risks:
- Increased congestion,
- Obstruction to emergency access,
- Displacement parking affecting local residents.
2. Impact on Local Highway Infrastructure
The local road network is primarily residential in character and not designed to accommodate
large volumes of visitors arriving simultaneously.
High-frequency gathering times may result in traffic build-up, unsafe manoeuvring, and reduced
pedestrian safety, particularly during evenings and weekends.
3. Pressure on Local Infrastructure
Under planning policy, developments must be supported by appropriate infrastructure capacity.
The proposal may place additional strain on:
- Local transport routes,
- Waste collection services,
- Residential amenity,
- Public realm and noise environment.
The application does not sufficiently demonstrate how these impacts will be mitigated.
4. Residential Amenity and Noise
Regular large gatherings have the potential to increase noise levels, vehicle activity, and general
disturbance inconsistent with the established residential nature of the area.
5. Community Integration and Inclusive Use
While community facilities are welcomed in principle, developments should demonstrate broad
accessibility and inclusive benefit for the whole local community. The proposal provides limited
evidence of how the facility would serve the wider Sutton population beyond a single user group.
6. Cumulative Development Impact
Sutton is already experiencing significant development growth. The cumulative impact of
additional high-intensity community buildings should be carefully assessed to ensure the
borough's suburban character and residential balance are preserved, consistent with objectives
within the Sutton Local Plan.
