Name: Mr Sakthivel Kumar
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dear Sir/Madam,
I am writing to object to the above planning proposal on the grounds that it conflicts with several
key policies of the London Plan, specifically GG3, D14, T2, T4, and SL1. These policies aim to
ensure that development protects residential amenity, promotes healthy and safe streets, and
avoids harmful environmental and transport impacts.
The proposal, in its current form, fails to meet these requirements for the reasons set out below.
1. Policy GG3 - Creating a Healthy City
Policy GG3 requires developments to protect the health, wellbeing, and quality of life of existing
communities by reducing noise, pollution, overcrowding and preventing harm to residential
amenity.
Contravention:
The development will bring large volumes of people into a quiet residential area, especially during
peak events such as Friday prayers where the council notes that up to 250 attendees may leave
the site late in the evening (up to 11pm), causing significant disturbance to neighbouring homes.
Such lateevening footfall and vehicle activity will harm peace, wellbeing, and sense of safety for
nearby residents, contrary to GG3's expectations for safe, inclusive, lowdisturbance
neighbourhoods.
This directly undermines the policy intent of supporting healthy, peaceful communities.
2. Policy D14 - Noise and Vibration
Policy D14 requires all new developments to:
Avoid generating unacceptable noise
Protect existing residents from additional noise sources
Provide effective mitigation measures
Contravention:
The council's preapplication feedback already raised concerns that vehicle movements, people
arriving and departing, and the operation of the site until 11pm will create significant noise and
disturbance within a predominantly residential setting.
The scale of activity-especially with a maximum capacity of 250 people-presents a clear risk of
unacceptable noise exposure to surrounding homes, including those with gardens immediately
adjacent to the site.
No meaningful noise mitigation strategy has been presented that could realistically offset this level
of disturbance.
The proposal therefore fails to comply with D14's requirement to protect residents from harmful
noise impacts.
3. Policy T2 - Healthy Streets
Policy T2 seeks to ensure streets are safe, pedestrianfriendly, and not dominated by vehicles.
Developments must avoid worsening conditions for people walking, cycling, or living in residential
streets.
Contravention:
Dorset Road, Balmoral Way, and surrounding residential streets are narrow, heavily parked, and
not designed for surges of traffic or parking demand.
The anticipated influx of cars during peak times will create unsafe conditions for pedestrians,
including children walking to and from nearby residential properties.
Increased turning movements at the site's vehicular access further undermines street safety.
The proposal conflicts with T2 because it makes local streets less safe and less accessible.
4. Policy T4 - Assessing and Mitigating Transport Impacts
Policy T4 requires developments to:
Undertake a full assessment of transport impacts
Demonstrate no severe impact on congestion or road safety
Provide effective mitigation
Contravention:
The site has a PTAL rating of 1a, among the lowest possible, meaning almost all attendees would
rely on private cars.
The council has stated that Friday prayers alone could generate demand for ~21 additional
onstreet spaces even after using all onsite spaces-creating severe parking stress in an already
constrained area.
This level of parking overspill and congestion represents a severe transport impact, failing the T4
policy test.
The development does not provide realistic mitigation capable of addressing these impacts.
As such, the proposal falls significantly short of T4 requirements.
5. Policy SL1 - Social Infrastructure
Policy SL1 supports community facilities only when they:
Are appropriately located
Do not negatively impact neighbours
Are accessible via sustainable modes
Provide benefits without harming amenity or safety
Contravention:
While community facilities are supported in principle, this site is not suitable due to its very poor
transport accessibility and residential context.
The proposed intensity of use will negatively impact neighbouring homes, particularly through
noise, traffic, nighttime activity, and parking overspill.
The location fails SL1's requirement for accessibility and for avoiding adverse impact on
residential amenity.
Therefore, the proposal cannot be considered acceptable under SL1.
Conclusion
The development is fundamentally incompatible with key London Plan policies governing health,
noise, transport, and residential amenity. It would introduce:
Excessive noise and disturbance
Severe parking and traffic pressures
Unsafe and unhealthy street conditions
Disproportionate impact on surrounding residential properties
For these reasons, and given clear conflicts with policies GG3, D14, T2, T4, and SL1, I
respectfully request that the planning authority refuse this application.
Yours faithfully,
Sakthivel Kumar

Name: Mr Robin Brodie
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
We are objecting to this planning application on the grounds that the area cannot cope
with an increase in either the number of pedestrians or cars particularly at the drop-off and pick-up
times for the children attending the nearby Avenue Primary Academy School which has over 1,000
pupils on roll.
Our road, Balmoral Way, is a very narrow road and at times, it is already difficult to drive through
due to the number of parked cars. The prospect of more is frankly not only dangerous but also
outrageous !
We are both elderly and my wife is in a wheel chair. Recently, on a Friday lunch time, we came
home from Sutton on the S4 bus. We had wanted to get off at the junction of Dorset Road and
Holland Avenue but, due to the sheer number of cars parked along the road and even on the
corners, the driver could not stop. Consequently, he took us much further along Avenue Road in
front of the school entrance. It was much too much for me to push the wheelchair home and we
both found it a real struggle.
The congestion in this area, especially on a Friday between 12.30 and 14.30 is just intolerable.

Name: Mr Himanshu Porwal
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I strongly object to the proposed development at the former Sutton Ambulance Station
on Dorset Road. The scale, intensity, and nature of this proposal make it wholly inappropriate for
its setting and would cause significant, lasting, and unacceptable harm to the surrounding
residential community.
1. A fundamentally incompatible use in a 100% residential area
This is a quiet neighbourhood made up entirely of homes, flats, and gardens. Introducing a
highcapacity place of worship and community hub-operating long hours, hosting highturnover
events, and attracting large volumes of people-would radically and permanently disrupt the
tranquillity of the area. This type of facility simply does not belong in such a location.
2. Visually overbearing and completely out of character
The proposed extensions and increased building height would create a structure that is visually
dominant and entirely out of keeping with the domestic scale of neighbouring properties. It would
erode the established look, feel, and rhythm of the neighbourhood and introduce a
commercialscale presence in a suburban street.
3. No unmet need-this development is unjustified and excessive
Multiple similar facilities already exist within 1-1.5 miles of this site, including those used by the
applicant. There is no shortfall in provision and no evidence to justify positioning a highintensity
community centre in a suburban location with extremely poor public transport accessibility (PTAL
1a). This development is unnecessary, overconcentrated, and in the wrong place.
4. Serious noise and latenight disturbance
The Council's own report acknowledges that the proposal could result in raised noise and
disturbance, including the dispersal of up to 250 people at 11pm. This level of activity is wholly
unsuitable for a residential street and would severely undermine neighbours' ability to enjoy
peace, privacy, and sleep. This is a direct threat to residential amenity.
5. Unacceptable parking pressure and traffic impact
The proposal relies heavily on onstreet parking, with demand expected to exceed onsite capacity
and spill into surrounding roads. Combined with poor public transport links, this will cause
congestion, reduce highway safety, and significantly inconvenience residents, particularly during
peak event times.
6. Damaging cumulative impact on community character
Taken together, the increased noise, traffic, footfall, lateevening activity, and intense use would
fundamentally alter the character of the neighbourhood. This development would replace a calm,
suburban residential environment with a highintensity activity hub, harming the area's cohesion,
identity, and quality of life.
Conclusion
This proposal is unnecessary, inappropriate, and harmful.
It threatens residential amenity, undermines the established suburban character of the area, and
introduces a scale and intensity of use that the location simply cannot support.
For these reasons, I strongly urge the Council to refuse the proposed development.

Name: Ms P P
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
1. Traffic generation and highway safety
The proposed use will result in a significant increase in traffic movements compared to the former
ambulance station use, particularly:
regular daily visits, large peaktime arrivals and departures, intensified activity during evenings,
weekends and special events.
The surrounding road network is not suitable to accommodate this increase. The proposal risks:
congestion on already busy local roads, unsafe vehicle manoeuvres, increased conflict between
vehicles, pedestrians and cyclists.
The application fails to demonstrate that the local highway network can safely absorb the
additional traffic.
2. Inadequate parking provision and overspill parking
The proposal does not provide sufficient onsite parking for the scale and intensity of the proposed
use.
This will inevitably lead to:
overspill parking on nearby residential streets, obstruction of driveways and dropped kerbs,
reduced access for residents, delivery vehicles and emergency services.
This would cause ongoing inconvenience and harm to local residents and is contrary to good
planning practice.
3. Noise, disturbance and loss of residential amenity
The proposed use is likely to generate significantly higher noise levels than the former use,
including:
large groups arriving and leaving at the same time, noise from gatherings, meetings and events,
evening and weekend activity.
This would result in an unacceptable level of disturbance to nearby residents, particularly given the
residential nature of the surrounding area.
No adequate noise impact assessment has been provided to demonstrate how residential amenity
would be protected.
4. Overdevelopment and inappropriate scale
The proposed part one, part twostorey extensions and increased building height represent
overdevelopment of the site.
The scale, bulk and massing of the development would:
appear out of keeping with surrounding buildings, result in visual harm to the street scene, create
an overly dominant and intrusive structure.
The proposal does not respect the character or appearance of the area.
5. Change in intensity of use:
The former use as an ambulance station had controlled, functional activity.
The proposed mixed use will introduce:
a much higher intensity of occupation, prolonged hours of use, frequent large gatherings.
This represents a material intensification of use that is unsuitable for this location and incompatible
with nearby residential properties.
6. Insufficient servicing, refuse and management details
The application does not adequately address:
refuse storage capacity and collection arrangements, servicing and delivery times, crowd
management and dispersal.
Without a clear and enforceable management plan, the development risks causing ongoing
disruption and disorder.
7. Lack of robust supporting evidence
The application is not supported by sufficient technical evidence, including:
a detailed transport assessment, a parking stress survey,
a noise impact assessment, a clear operational management plan.
In the absence of this information, the local planning authority cannot properly assess the full
impact of the proposal.
Conclusion
For the reasons set out above, the proposal would result in harm to highway safety, residential
amenity, and the character of the area.
The development is therefore unsuitable for this location and should be refused.

Name: Mr M Mansuri
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I am writing to formally state my support for this Planning Application. As a local
resident since 2017, I believe this proposal will be a significant asset and bring vital benefits to our
community.
The proposal represents an excellent conversion of a currently unused building into a thriving
communal hub. It is particularly commendable that this is a self-funded facility supported entirely
by the community.
The building is modest in scale, in keeping with local character, and represents a low-intensity use
of the site compared to other potential developments. I am pleased to see the focus on community
wellbeing, including inclusive facilities, educational programs for children, and dedicated space for
women's activities. The project will provide a dedicated facility for the religious needs of the local
Muslim population and offer a space for youth engagement and wider community improvement.
Regarding infrastructure, the provision of 12 on-site parking spaces, two disabled bays, and cycle
racks demonstrates a clear plan for sustainable travel. Many residents are within walking distance,
which will help reduce the local carbon footprint. Any potential traffic or noise concerns are
effectively addressed by implementing staggered Friday prayer sessions. As Avenue School
closes at 2:10 PM on Fridays, there is minimal overlap with prayer times, avoiding conflict with
school pickups.
I do not believe this application will cause significant issues regarding traffic, light, or noise. I
respectfully ask the Council to approve this application.

Name: Mr JOSE Varela
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The parking is already restricted, particularly during the school run times and this will
increase the traffic and further restrict parking for much of the day and into the night. The current
parking works because the space is clear by the time most people return from work but the
proposed planning application will destroy this arrangement and residents will be restricted at all
times.
The increase in traffic and people coming and going will add to the noise and change the
character of the area.

Name: Miss Zara Ahmed
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
We really need a place of worship and community in this area. We have Eid in the park
right around the corner, and it is always packed. Clearly there is a need. The Muslim community
will work well supporting community cohesion and tolerance, especially through voluntary work.
It's a shame that the letter delivered notifying us of this proposal only presented reasons for
objection rather than demonstrating all the positives that come with having this community worship
place.

Name: Mr Mervyn Desouza
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I object to the use of the ambulance site as a mosque.
1. The area is primarily a residential area, and the mosque is out of character with the local
demography.
2. The mosque will attract additional traffic to the area, and the road is already congested with
parked cars. This is evident during the school drop-off and pick-up times.
3. Increased activity in the area will lead to an increase in traffic and noise pollution.
4. This development will compromise the safety of pedestrians.

Name: Mr Kamran Ahmed
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application

Name: Mrs Saida Uddin
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
I live in the area but currently have to travel far for my Quran classes. I have mobility

also help younger generations bond within the community

Name: Mr David Wilton
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This is another instance of the building for multiple occupants with no regard to the
parking needs. The parking chaos especially on school days would be even more dangerous than
it is at present.

Name: Mrs Kristina Foster
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This development will materially impact pupil safety, congestion at pick up / drop off
times, increasing the risk of collisions and unsafe crossing conditions. I can't imagine how

Name: Mrs Sue Murrell

Comment:
The proposed development will increase traffic in an already congested location. The
impact of which are unacceptably harmful and not realistically mitigable. As a nearby resident we
are already impacted by the nearby school, and access at certain points in the day is very difficult.
Parking is now a problem and sometimes access to our homes can be restricted due to poor
parking (which in itself is due to the lack of available parking in the area). The proposed
development by its very nature will only increase traffic flow to the area, further negatively
impacting nearby residents.

Name: Mrs Bindu Paruchuri
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I seriously object for the above proposal.
It will create problems in every aspect especially school round the corner.
Will be more chaotic.

Name: Mrs Jodie Albrow
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
My reason for objection is due to the volume of traffic / parking (at certain times) that the
Dorset Road / Holland Avenue / Avenue Road area already struggles to cope with.
Avenue Primary Academy has over 1,000 pupils and the safety of these pupils needs to be a
consideration. At drop off / pick up times Dorset Road becomes chaotic / dangerous and I believe
the proposed plans would only add to this.
People already don't observe road markings in the area. Often parking on double yellows, double
reds, zig-zag yellows and blocking driveways. This makes the roads in the areas incredibly
dangerous to cross as a pedestrian / cyclist at times. I believe the proposed plans would add to
this issue further.

Name: Mr James Liddle
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I formally object to this application on the following material planning grounds:
1. Highways and Parking (NPPF 2023, Para. 115)
The site provides only 14 on-site spaces against peak surges of 250 persons, with 69 vehicles
already displacing onto surrounding streets. This ratio represents a severe and demonstrable
impact on the local road network, meeting the NPPF threshold for refusal on highway grounds. A
full Transport Assessment and Travel Plan have not been submitted, without which the LPA
cannot lawfully make an informed determination. The statutory Highway Authority consultee
should be asked to formally assess and report on this deficiency.
2. Child Safety (NPPF Para. 100)m a nearby school means that overflow parking of up to 69
vehicles on surrounding streets will reduce sightlines and visibility for children and families. The
NPPF requires particular priority to be given to vulnerable road users, including children. No road
safety assessment addressing this risk has been submitted.
3. Late-Night Noise and Amenity (NPPF Para. 191; Noise Policy Statement for England 2010):
Regular dispersal of up to 250 people at approximately 11:00 PM, with associated vehicle
movements and noise, constitutes a serious and recurring amenity harm in a residential setting.
No Noise Impact Assessment or Management Plan has been submitted. Conditions cannot
adequately address what is a fundamental and foreseeable pattern of harm.
4. Vulnerable Residents (NPPF Chapter 8): A nearby care home houses residents with heightened
sensitivity to noise and disturbance. Late-night dispersal, headlight intrusion, and vehicle noise will
directly harm their amenity and wellbeing. This has not been addressed in the application.
5. Cumulative Highway Impact (NPPF Para. 115): The surrounding road network already serves
multiple venues. Overlapping peak periods will compound congestion and parking displacement
beyond what any individual use generates alone. No cumulative impact assessment has been
provided, rendering the application materially incomplete.
The unaddressed impacts go directly to the LPA's statutory obligations on highway safety,
amenity, and the protection of vulnerable residents.

Name: Mr Peter Foster
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Belmont is a small and quiet residential area and the creation of a place of worship will
make the area unliveable for the residents.
The build up of traffic will be unbearable for residents in an already bad hotspot due to the local
school. The level of noise and pollution will increase significantly and will reduce the quality of
residential living in that area.
The place of worship will also mean a significant increase in noise especially at weekends, early
morning and evenings which is unfair for families / religions that have lived in the area for many
generations.
The area doesn't need to have refuse stores and will lead to increased rubbish in the area with
people using the area to dump unwanted refuse especially at night. This will lead to additional
anxiety for current residents with animals, vermin increasing in numbers.
Belmont does not need another place of worship in the area. Please do not spoil a quiet area by
encouraging 1000's of people to a very small community with limited parking in the area, that will
block up roads, cul du sac's and make the area a volatile area to live.
Bringing this place of worship to Belmont will, almost certainly, lead to problems in the community.

Name: Mr Matthew Gosling
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The ambulance station is right next door to my grandmother's house! She has been
diagnosed with early-onset dimentia! This will become a health and safety risk!

Address: Beechcroft 9 Golf Side Cheam Sutton SM2 7HA
Name: Mr Noble Mathew
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment: Reasons: Increased traffic and associated road safety.

Name: Mr Ian Cooper
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I cannot support any initiative that will inevitably add to the congestion and parking
issues already faced by residents in the area due to Avenue School drop off and pick up.
The roads around the proposed place of worship are congested and heavily parked at school drop
off and collection times, leading to frustration for residents when wanting to leave or return home.
It is impossible to park on Holland Avenue within 200-250m of home if arriving during this time.
Furthermore, residents often suffer inconsiderate parking with driveways sometimes partially or
fully blocked.
The effect is worst during afternoon pick up where frequent bottlenecks occur caused by
congestion and excessive parking. I have observed numerous incidents of road rage and
harassment on Holland Avenue. This will only get worse with an increase in vehicles circulating in
the area looking for somewhere to park.
Supporters, some from far away are suggesting parking will not be an issue, but without any real
justification of why they feel this way. On the contrary, objections from residents on Queens Road
state that they suffer with haphazard parking by some of those attending Friday prayer at St Johns
Church. I have experienced significant delay and frustration caused by inconsiderate parking on
Northdown Road which had created an entirely avoidable bottleneck. Unfortunately, I have no
reason to believe this behaviour will change when switching to the new venue, and this will
regrettably divide rather than unite the community.
Well-meaning but ultimately flawed Transport Statement and Travel Plan documents have been
submitted. The Transport Statement mentions three Traffic Marshals, among their responsibilities
are to "ensure that visitors' cars do not block the driveways of nearby residents" and "ensure that
visitors to the place of worship do not park off-street in the driveways of nearby residents". This is
alarming and implies the applicant is well aware of the potential parking problems the proposed
use of this site will raise.
The Travel Plan mentions that around 250 people will attend the site for Friday prayers, resulting
in at least 80 cars associated with the site and leading to a 76% parking occupancy on all roads
within a 400m radius according to a survey. I note that the parking occupancy survey was
undertaken at 13:00 on a Friday, before many parents had turned up to collect their children, yet
prayers will take place until 13:35 or 13:40 depending on time of year. This overlaps with parents
parking in advance of school pick up, when Holland Avenue is usually 100% parked to at least
200m from my address. Hence the 76% occupancy rate is likely to be far higher in practice, and
certainly higher than the Council's own definition of parking stress of 85%. A visit to the area
during term time at school pick up is strongly recommended so that decision makers may
experience for themselves the current situation.
I would like to ask the planning committee to listen carefully to the views of the residents who live
nearest to the site and who will be most affected by the proposal, the majority of whom do not
support it for the reasons given in their objections. I feel that this application should be refused
until proper thought has been applied to how congestion and parking issues will be managed, and
practical solutions rather than targets and initiatives are put forward to address them.

Name: Mr Peter Goodwin
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment: I object to this planning to install a mosque in our area.

Name: Mr Nikhil Ramakrishnan
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Object there is already a lack of parking in the area, my drive nearby is frequently
blocked by cars - I think this issue will get worse.

Name: Mr Dhiran Mistry
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
I don't believe this site would be appropriate as a place of worship due to the limited
parking and adverse impact to local residents and the nearby school.

Name: Ms Shumaila Aziz
Stance: Customer made comments in support of the Planning Application
Comment:
The growing Muslim population currently have limited dedicated spaces for worship.
Equal access to places of worship is part of a fair and inclusive society and will help contribute to
interfaith dialogue, strengthening community relations.
Members of the Muslim community have been attending St John's Church for religious activities
for several years, which is located very close to the proposed site. This indicates that worship-
related visitor volume already exists in the area and does not pose a problem.

Name: Mr Mike De Silva
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Dorset Rd is far too small and congested as it is. A large community based religious
gathering would place an impossible strain on a small suburban road. It is wholly out of keeping
with the available space and will put addition strain on the relevant road, which is already over
burdened by Belmont Station traffic.

Name: Mr Chris O'Dea
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Any proposal for the site should be brought forward in a respectful, sensible and above
all safe manner.
The proposed change of use raises significant concerns relating to traffic, parking availability,
congestion and pedestrian safety, particularly given the proximity of the local school and the
narrow residential streets surrounding the site.
The plans do not appear to take into account the impact on residents, school staff, parents,
children or bus services at peak times. Many parents and young children use these roads daily,
and with the potential for increased vehicle numbers - especially at times when children finish
early or walk with preschool siblings - the risk of accidents would increase.
As a resident of York Road, I am very familiar with the congestion already present during school
pickup and dropoff times, especially in the roads being suggested for parking. The two bus routes
serving the area already become delayed or blocked due to traffic, and any increase in vehicles
would worsen both congestion and pollution.
This raises a fundamental question: can the site be converted while accommodating the additional
traffic it would generate at peak times, without causing unacceptable risks to pedestrian and road
safety? Based on current evidence, this does not appear to be the case.
This is not an emotional argument, but one rooted in public safety - particularly the safety of school
children. If there is any uncertainty about the extent of the risk, the council should undertake a
public traffic and highwaysafety study to properly assess it.
If the site were to be converted in a way that significantly increases traffic, substantial mitigation
would be required, such as dedicated onsite parking (potentially including underground provision)
and appropriate trafficflow controls to prevent large numbers of vehicles entering or exiting
simultaneously. Additional parking restrictions or a residents' permit scheme would also be
needed to protect the surrounding streets from overflow.
In its current form, and due to the unresolved questions and challenges outlined above, the
proposal should be rejected unless and until these issues are fully addressed and satisfactorily
resolved.

Name: Irene Fitzsimons-Bell
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
The road in question is very narrow with restrictions already in place which will force
people to park everywhere else and anywhere causing congestion for residents in the vicinity. The
proposal is wholly untenable given the state of the road and surrounding residential area.

Name: Mr Robert Duneclift
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Those who are unfortunate to live in or around the Avenue school and surrounding
roads , will no doubt agree that in recent years the traffic congestion has gone from the surreal to
the ridiculous. At drop off and collect times common sense and adherence to the road traffic act
has clearly evaporated. The red route lines are ignored as are the school safety zig zags.
Driveways of local residents are blocked and busses ,lorries and indeed emergency vehicles are
severely hampered. Adding to this problem by allowing this application and inviting a further influx
of vehicles into the very heart of the congested area , will be no less than a disaster and will affect
further the quality of life for those living in the area.
I also object to the initial information sent out that this was a intended to be a ' community centre'.
Clearly the devil is in the detail but there seems to me a world of difference between a community
centre and a mosque.

Name: Mrs Gillian McCay
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This is a quiet neighbourhood and that is why people have bought their homes here.
Residents with young children and the elderly cannot have late night noise. If a place of worship
and a community hub is built, who will police the noise of service users leaving the proposed
buildings? What sort of events will take place in the community hub? Who will police what time the
events finish? Who will oversee that the community hub is quiet and closed by 6pm? Will the
caretaker make sure that the church is closed and locked by 6pm? My occupation was managing
a place of worship and community hub, and therefore I am qualified to know that the public will not
arrive nor leave quietly. Am happy for you to contact me as I have considerable experience in this
area. If this goes ahead, Sutton Council will be bombarded with constant complaints from
residents as I was bombarded with complaints when I managed similar buildings.

Name: Mr Robert Duneclift
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
Having had time to browse through the support or objections on this application. I can't
help but notice that considerable amount of support comes from residents who live no where near
the intended site. They are unlikely therefore to be subjected to the disruption it will cause.

Name: Mr Barry James
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This is a wholey inappropriate site for a mosque and community centre. It will create

Name: Mrs Sridevi Sura
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This area is already having severe parking issues having already school and bowing

near by .

Name: Mrs Laura James
Stance: Customer objects to the Planning Application
Comment:
This is ridiculous there is already a parking issue the noise the extra pollution right near

Name: Mr Paris Cowley

This would be detrimental to the public safety of the neighbourhood. The proposed build

The proposed build will attract a large community of people throughout the day which interferes

The location has poor public transport so most people will drive and the proposed new build will

where the streets and planning dont have the capacity. It will totally break the already fracturing

The proposed insued chaos will ultimately lead to the childrens safety be put at risk... potentially
costing a childs life as a consequence of peoples disarray and distracted focus.
Do not make this mistake.

Name: Mr Raiah Niranjana Murthy Hosahally Chandrashekha

That place have too much traffic now when I goto drop my son for school. It is difficult to
